meta.author: 鲁迅 · Lu Xun
meta.tagline: 十四篇小说，与一句自白：「聊以慰藉那在寂寞里奔驰的猛士，使他不惮于前驱。」——鲁迅第一部小说集全本，附自序。
about: 《呐喊》是鲁迅的第一部小说集，一九二三年由北京新潮社初版，收一九一八至一九二二年间所作小说十四篇，并冠以《自序》。从《狂人日记》——中国现代文学史上第一篇白话小说——到《阿Q正传》《孔乙己》《故乡》《药》，这些篇章以冷峻的笔、滚烫的心，写出一个民族在世纪转折处的沉默、麻木与挣扎。
鲁迅说，他做小说，多采自病态社会的不幸的人们中，「意思是在揭出病苦，引起疗救的注意」。题名「呐喊」，便是要为那在寂寞里奔驰的猛士助几声呐喊，使他不惮于前驱——纵然不免在《药》的坟上，凭空添一个花环。
中文为鲁迅原著，属公共领域，据维基文库校录、转为简体；英文译文为本站逐段译出，志在信而能读，专有名词从拼音。
meta.howto: 读到动心处，轻点那一段，留下你的「心动指数」（1–5）。所有读者的心动汇聚成「心动榜」——让这部呐喊，由今天的读者共同写下批注。

chapters.label: 序

segs.zh: 我在年青时候也曾经做过许多梦，后来大半忘却了，但自己也并不以为可惜。所谓回忆者，虽说可以使人欢欣，有时也不免使人寂寞，使精神的丝缕还牵着已逝的寂寞的时光，又有什么意味呢，而我偏苦于不能全忘却，这不能全忘的一部分，到现在便成了呐喊的来由。
segs.en: In my youth, I too dreamed many dreams; most of them I have since forgotten, yet I do not feel this is a pity. For while it is said that memories can bring joy, they may at times also bring a sense of desolation; and what meaning is there in letting the threads of one’s spirit remain tethered to a lonely, bygone time? Yet I am burdened by the inability to forget entirely, and this part I cannot cast off has become the source of my call to arms.

segs.zh: 我有四年多，曾经常常，——几乎是每天，出入于质铺和药店里，年纪可是忘却了，总之是药店的柜台正和我一样高，质铺的是比我高一倍，我从一倍高的柜台外送上衣服或首饰去，在侮蔑里接了钱，再到一样高的柜台上给我久病的父亲去买药。回家之后，又须忙别的事了，因为开方的医生是最有名的，以此所用的药引也奇特：冬天的芦根，经霜三年的甘蔗，蟋蟀要原对的，结子的平地木，……多不是容易办到的东西。然而我的父亲终于日重一日的亡故了。
segs.en: For more than four years, I was a frequent—almost daily—visitor to the pawnshop and the pharmacy. I have forgotten my exact age, but I remember that the pharmacy counter was exactly as tall as I was, while the one at the pawnshop was twice my height. From outside that towering counter, I would hand over clothes or jewelry, then, clutching the money tossed down with such contempt, I would hurry to the counter that stood level with my chin to buy medicine for my chronically ill father. Back home, there were other chores to attend to, for the doctor who prescribed the medicine was a man of great renown, and consequently, the required "adjuvants" were often bizarre: winter reed roots, sugarcane that had been frosted for three years, a pair of crickets—the original pair—or stalk-bearing *Ardisia*... none of them easy things to come by. Yet, in spite of it all, my father’s condition grew worse day by day, until he finally passed away.

segs.zh: 有谁从小康人家而坠入困顿的么，我以为在这途路中，大概可以看见世人的真面目；我要到Ｎ进Ｋ学堂去了，仿佛是想走异路，逃异地，去寻求别样的人们。我的母亲没有法，办了八元的川资，说是由我的自便；然而伊哭了，这正是情理中的事，因为那时读书应试是正路，所谓学洋务，社会上便以为是一种走投无路的人，只得将灵魂卖给鬼子，要加倍的奚落而且排斥的，而况伊又看不见自己的儿子了。然而我也顾不得这些事，终于到Ｎ去进了Ｋ学堂了，在这学堂里，我才知道世上还有所谓格致、算学、地理、历史、绘图和体操。生理学并不教，但我们却看到些木版的全体新论和化学卫生论之类了。我还记得先前的医生的议论和方药，和现在所知道的比较起来，便渐渐的悟得中医不过是一种有意的或无意的骗子，同时又很起了对于被骗的病人和他的家族的同情；而且从译出的历史上，又知道了日本维新是大半发端于西方医学的事实。
segs.en: Is there anyone who has fallen from a comfortable family life into destitution? I believe that along such a path, one can truly see the face of the world. I was headed for the K Academy in N, as if seeking an alternative path, a place far away, in search of a different kind of life. My mother, having no choice, provided me with eight dollars for travel expenses and told me to do as I pleased. Yet, she wept—which was only natural, for in those days, studying for the civil service examinations was the only respectable path. To study "Western learning" was considered the resort of those with nowhere left to turn, who had no choice but to sell their souls to the foreign devils; such people were met with redoubled ridicule and ostracism. Moreover, she would no longer see her son. But I could not concern myself with such matters. In the end, I went to N and entered the K Academy. It was there that I learned for the first time of subjects like physics, mathematics, geography, history, drawing, and physical education. Physiology was not taught, but we managed to see some woodblock-printed books like *A New Treatise on Anatomy* and *Discourses on Chemistry and Hygiene*. I still remember the pronouncements and prescriptions of the doctors of the past; comparing them with what I now knew, I gradually realized that traditional Chinese medicine was nothing more than an intentional or unintentional fraud. At the same time, I began to feel a deep sympathy for the patients and their families who were being deceived. Furthermore, from translated histories, I learned the fact that the Meiji Restoration in Japan had, for the most part, been sparked by Western medicine.

segs.zh: 因为这些幼稚的知识，后来便使我的学籍列在日本一个乡间的医学专门学校里了。我的梦很美满，预备卒业回来，救治像我父亲似的被误的病人的疾苦，战争时候便去当军医，一面又促进了国人对于维新的信仰。我已不知道教授微生物学的方法，现在又有了怎样的进步了，总之那时是用了电影，来显示微生物的形状的，因此有时讲义的一段落已完，而时间还没有到，教师便映些风景或时事的画片给学生看，以用去这多馀的光阴。其时正当日、俄战争的时候，关于战事的画片自然也就比较的多了，我在这一个讲堂中，便须常常随喜我那同学们的拍手和喝采。有一回，我竟在画片上忽然会见我久违的许多中国人了，一个绑在中间，许多站在左右，一样是强壮的体格，而显出麻木的神情。据解说，则绑着的是替俄国做了军事上的侦探，正要被日军砍下头颅来示众，而围着的便是来赏鉴这示众的盛举的人们。
segs.en: Because of this rudimentary knowledge, I later found myself enrolled in a rural medical college in Japan. My dream was a beautiful one: upon graduation, I would return home to relieve the suffering of patients like my father who had been misdiagnosed, or, in the event of war, serve as an army surgeon, while simultaneously promoting among my countrymen a faith in reform. I have no idea how the methods for teaching microbiology have progressed since then, but in those days, they used motion pictures to illustrate the forms of microbes. Often, when a segment of a lecture ended before the period was up, the teacher would screen slides of scenery or current events to fill the remaining time. As this was during the Russo-Japanese War, there were naturally many slides related to the conflict, and in that lecture hall, I was frequently obliged to join my classmates in their clapping and cheering. Then one day, I suddenly caught sight of a number of my long-unseen countrymen on the screen. One was bound in the center, while many others stood around him; they were all equally robust in physique, yet their expressions were utterly vacant. According to the commentary, the man being bound was a spy for the Russians, about to have his head severed by the Japanese military as a public warning, while those standing around him were the onlookers who had come to savor this grand spectacle of public execution.

segs.zh: 这一学年没有完毕，我已经到了东京了，因为从那一回以后，我便觉得医学并非一件紧要事，凡是愚弱的国民，即使体格如何健全，如何茁壮，也只能做毫无意义的示众的材料和看客，病死多少是不必以为不幸的。所以我们的第一要著，是在改变他们的精神，而善于改变精神的是，我那时以为当然要推文艺，于是想提倡文艺运动了。在东京的留学生很有学法政理化以至警察工业的，但没有人治文学和美术；可是在冷淡的空气中，也幸而寻到几个同志了，此外又邀集了必须的几个人，商量之后，第一步当然是出杂志，名目是取『新的生命』的意思，因为我们那时大抵带些复古的倾向，所以只谓之新生。
segs.en: Before the academic year had concluded, I had already arrived in Tokyo. Ever since that incident, I had come to feel that medicine was not a matter of primary importance. For a foolish and weak populace, no matter how sound or robust their physiques might be, they could serve only as fodder for meaningless public spectacles or as spectators to them; how many of them died of illness was of no great consequence. Therefore, our most urgent task was to transform their spirit, and at the time, I believed that the best medium for this transformation was, naturally, literature and art. Thus, I resolved to promote a literary movement. Among the students in Tokyo, many were studying law, political science, physics, chemistry, or even police work and industry, but no one was concerned with literature or the fine arts. Yet, amidst that indifferent atmosphere, I was fortunate enough to find a few like-minded comrades. We gathered a few other necessary people, and after some deliberation, we decided that the first step must be to publish a magazine. We chose a title that conveyed the meaning of a new life; as we were all inclined toward a certain archaism at the time, we simply called it *New Life*.

segs.zh: 新生的出版之期接近了，但最先就隐去了若干担当文字的人，接着又逃走了资本，结果只剩下不名一钱的三个人。创始时候既已背时，失败时候当然无可告语，而其后却连这三个人也都为各自的运命所驱策，不能在一处纵谈将来的好梦了，这就是我们的并未产生的新生的结局。
segs.en: The publication date of our new journal was drawing near, yet first a number of our contributors vanished, and then the capital took flight, leaving nothing behind but three penniless individuals. Since the venture had been ill-fated from its inception, its failure was, of course, beyond lament. In the end, even those three were driven by the dictates of their own destinies, unable to remain together to indulge in grand dreams of the future. Such was the conclusion of our stillborn enterprise.

segs.zh: 我感到未尝经验的无聊，是自此以后的事。我当初是不知其所以然的；后来想，凡有一人的主张，得了赞和，是促其前进的，得了反对，是促其奋斗的，独有叫喊的生人中，而生人并无反应，既非赞同，也无反对，如置身毫无边际的荒原，无可措手的了，这是怎样的悲哀呵，我于是以我所感到者为寂寞。
segs.en: It was from this time on that I began to feel a boredom I had never known before. At first, I did not understand why; later, I came to think that if a man’s ideas meet with approval, it spurs him onward; if they meet with opposition, it spurs him to struggle. But to be among people who are shouting, only to find that there is no response—neither approval nor opposition—is like being cast into a boundless wilderness, where there is nothing to lay one’s hands upon. What a profound sorrow that is! And so, I gave the name of loneliness to what I felt.

segs.zh: 这寂寞又一天一天的长大起来，如大毒蛇，缠住了我的灵魂了。
segs.en: This loneliness grew greater day by day, like a giant venomous snake, coiling itself around my soul.

segs.zh: 然而我虽然自有无端的悲哀，却也并不愤懑，因为这经验使我反省，看见自己了：就是我决不是一个振臂一呼应者云集的英雄。
segs.en: Yet, though I harbored my own inexplicable sorrow, I felt no resentment, for this experience forced me to reflect and see myself for what I truly was: I am by no means a hero at whose call the masses would rise in response.

segs.zh: 只是我自己的寂寞是不可不驱除的，因为这于我太痛苦。我于是用了种种法，来麻醉自己的灵魂，使我沈入于国民中，使我回到古代去，后来也亲历或旁观过几样更寂寞、更悲哀的事，都为我所不愿追怀，甘心使他们和我的脑一同消灭在泥土里的，但我的麻醉法却也似乎已经奏了功，再没有青年时候的慷慨激昂的意思了。
segs.en: Yet I had to dispel my own loneliness, for it was too painful to bear. I resorted to various methods to anaesthetize my soul, forcing myself to merge into the masses, to retreat into the ancient past. Later, I experienced or witnessed a few things even more lonely and sorrowful, which I have no desire to recall, willing as I am to let them vanish with my own brain into the dust. But it seems my anaesthetic has worked; I no longer possess the fervent, impassioned spirit of my youth.

segs.zh: Ｓ会馆里有三间屋，相传是往昔曾在院子里的槐树上缢死过一个女人的，现在槐树已经高不可攀了，而这屋还没有人住；许多年，我便寓在这屋里钞古碑。客中少有人来，古碑中也遇不到什么问题和主义，而我的生命却居然暗暗的消去了，这也就是我惟一的愿望。夏夜，蚊子多了，便摇着蒲扇坐在槐树下，从密叶缝里看那一点一点的青天，晚出的槐蚕又每每冰冷的落在头颈上。
segs.en: In the S Guildhall there are three rooms which, according to legend, were once the site where a woman hanged herself from the locust tree in the courtyard. The tree has now grown too tall to climb, and the rooms remain unoccupied. For many years, I have lodged in these rooms, transcribing ancient stone inscriptions. Few visitors come to see me, and in the old inscriptions I encounter no problems or isms; yet, my life has been quietly slipping away, which is, in fact, my only wish. On summer nights, when the mosquitoes grow numerous, I sit beneath the locust tree waving a palm-leaf fan, peering through the gaps in the dense foliage at the tiny, scattered patches of blue sky, while the late-emerging locust worms often drop, cold and clammy, onto the nape of my neck.

segs.zh: 那时偶或来谈的是一个老朋友金心异，将手提的大皮夹放在破桌上，脱下长衫，对面坐下了，因为怕狗，似乎心房还在怦怦的跳动。
segs.en: Sometimes, a friend of mine, Jin Xinyi, would drop by for a chat. He would set his heavy leather briefcase down on the rickety table, take off his long gown, and sit down opposite me, his heart still thumping, it seemed, from his fear of the dogs.

segs.zh: 『你钞了这些有什么用？』有一夜，他翻着我那古碑的钞本，发了研究的质问了。
segs.en: "What’s the use of copying all these?" one night, as he leafed through my transcripts of ancient steles, he asked with the air of a researcher.

segs.zh: 『没有什么用。』
segs.en: It’s no use.

segs.zh: 『那么，你钞他是什么意思呢？』
segs.en: Then what was your point in copying his?

segs.zh: 『没有什么意思。』
segs.en: There is no meaning in it.

segs.zh: 『我想，你可以做点文章……』
segs.en: I think you might be able to write something about it...

segs.zh: 我懂得他的意思了，他们正办新青年，然而那时仿佛不特没有人来赞同，并且也还没有人来反对，我想，他们许是感到寂寞了，但是说：
segs.en: I understood what he meant. They were running *New Youth* at the time, and it seemed as though not only was there no one to support them, but there was no one to oppose them either. I imagine they must have felt a sense of desolation, yet he said:

segs.zh: 『假如一间铁屋子，是绝无窗户而万难破毁的，里面有许多熟睡的人们，不久都要闷死了，然而是从昏睡入死灭，并不感到就死的悲哀。现在你大嚷起来，惊起了较为清醒的几个人，使这不幸的少数者来受无可挽救的临终的苦楚，你倒以为对得起他们么？』
segs.en: Suppose there were an iron house without windows, absolutely indestructible, with many people fast asleep inside who would soon die of suffocation. Since they are in a deep slumber, they would pass from this state directly into death without feeling the sorrow of their impending end. Now, if you were to raise a great shout and awaken a few of the more conscious among them, causing these unfortunate few to suffer the agony of an irredeemable death, could you really say you had done them a kindness?

segs.zh: 『然而几个人既然起来，你不能说决没有毁坏这铁屋的希望。』
segs.en: Yet, since a few have risen, you cannot say that there is absolutely no hope of destroying this iron house.

segs.zh: 是的，我虽然自有我的确信，然而说到希望，却是不能抹杀的，因为希望是在于将来，决不能以我之必无的证明，来折服了他之所谓可有，于是我终于答应他也做文章了，这便是最初的一篇狂人日记。从此以后，便一发而不可收，每写些小说模样的文章，以敷衍朋友们的嘱托，积久就有了十馀篇。
segs.en: True, though I have my own convictions, as for hope, I cannot blot it out; for hope lies in the future, and I could never use the proof of my own certainty that it is groundless to refute what he calls the possibility of its existence. And so, in the end, I agreed to write for him, and that was how the first piece, A Madman’s Diary, came to be. From then on, there was no stopping me; I would write these story-like pieces to appease the requests of my friends, and over time, they added up to more than ten.

segs.zh: 在我自己，本以为现在是已经并非一个切迫而不能已于言的人了，但或者也还未能忘怀于当日自己的寂寞的悲哀罢，所以有时候仍不免呐喊几声，聊以慰藉那在寂寞里奔驰的猛士，使他不惮于前驱。至于我的喊声是勇猛或是悲哀，是可憎或是可笑，那倒是不暇顾及的；但既然是呐喊，则当然须听将令的了，所以我往往不恤用了曲笔，在药的瑜儿的坟上平空添上一个花环，在明天里也不叙单四嫂子竟没有做到看见儿子的梦，因为那时的主将是不主张消极的，至于自己，却也并不愿将自以为苦的寂寞，再来传染给也如我那年青时候似的正做着好梦的青年。
segs.en: For my own part, I had assumed that I was no longer a man compelled by an irrepressible urge to speak, yet perhaps I have not truly managed to forget the lonely sorrow of those days. And so, from time to time, I cannot help but let out a few cries, if only to comfort those brave warriors galloping through the wilderness, that they might not falter in their charge. As for whether my cries are fierce or mournful, detestable or ridiculous, I have no time to consider. But since it is a call to arms, I must, of course, obey the commander’s orders. Thus, I have often felt no qualms about using a deflective brush: I added a wreath to the grave of Little Yu in "Medicine," and in "Tomorrow," I refrained from recounting how Fourth Sister Shan failed to see her son even in her dreams—for the commander at the time did not advocate for pessimism. As for myself, I have no desire to infect the young, who are dreaming their own bright dreams much as I once did, with the loneliness I have come to find so bitter.

segs.zh: 这样说来，我的小说和艺术的距离之远，也就可想而知了，然而到今日还能蒙着小说的名，甚而至于且有成集的机会，无论如何总不能不说是一件徼幸的事，但徼幸虽使我不安于心，而悬揣人间暂时还有读者，则究竟也仍然是高兴的。
segs.en: This being the case, the distance between my stories and true art can well be imagined; yet the fact that they are still called stories today, and have even reached the point of being collected into a volume, is something I cannot help but regard as a stroke of good fortune. And while this good fortune makes me uneasy, the surmise that there are still readers in this world, at least for the time being, remains, in the final analysis, a source of pleasure.

segs.zh: 所以我竟将我的短篇小说结集起来，而且付印了，又因为上面所说的缘由，便称之为呐喊。
segs.en: And so I finally gathered my short stories together and sent them to be printed; and for the reasons mentioned above, I called the collection Call to Arms.

segs.zh: 一九二二年十二月三日，鲁迅记于北京。
segs.en: December 3, 1922. Recorded by Lu Xun in Beijing.

segs.zh: 这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may also be in the public domain in countries and regions where the copyright term is based on the life of the author plus an additional term, but which apply a shorter term rule to foreign works.

chapters.note: 鲁迅自述弃医从文的缘起，与那个著名的「铁屋子」之问：万难破毁的铁屋里，倘若惊起较为清醒的几个人，叫他们眼睁睁看着无可挽救的临终，又是否对得起他们？这篇自序，是理解全书的钥匙。

chapters.label: 一

segs.zh: 今天晚上，很好的月光。
segs.en: Tonight, the moonlight is very fine.

segs.zh: 我不见他，已是三十多年；今天见了，精神分外爽快。才知道以前的三十多年，全是发昏；然而须十分小心。不然，那赵家的狗，何以看我两眼呢？
segs.en: I have not seen him for over thirty years; seeing him today, I feel an extraordinary lightness of spirit. I realize now that those thirty-odd years were nothing but a daze. Still, I must be extremely careful. Otherwise, why would that dog of the Zhao family have glared at me twice?

segs.zh: 我怕得有理。
segs.en: My fear was not without reason.

segs.zh: 今天全没月光，我知道不妙。早上小心出门，赵贵翁的眼色便怪：似乎怕我，似乎想害我。还有七八个人，交头接耳的议论我。又怕我看见。一路上的人，都是如此。其中最凶的一个人，张着嘴，对我笑了一笑；我便从头直冷到脚跟，晓得他们布置，都已妥当了。
segs.en: There is no moonlight tonight; I know this is a bad sign. When I stepped out cautiously this morning, Zhao Guiweng’s expression was strange: he seemed afraid of me, yet also intent on harming me. Seven or eight others were huddled together, whispering about me, then pretending not to see me when they noticed I was looking. Everyone I met along the way was the same. One of them, the most vicious-looking, opened his mouth and flashed a grin at me; I felt a chill run from the top of my head to my heels, and I knew their trap had been set and was ready.

segs.zh: 我可不怕，仍旧走我的路。前面一伙小孩子，也在那里议论我；眼色也同赵贵翁一样，脸色也都铁青。我想我同小孩子有什么仇，他也这样。忍不住大声说：『你告诉我！』他们可就跑了。
segs.en: I was not afraid, however, and kept on my way. Ahead, a group of children were also discussing me; their eyes held the same look as Zhao Guiweng’s, and their faces were just as livid. I wondered what grudge these children could possibly have against me to treat me this way. Unable to restrain myself, I shouted, "Tell me!" At that, they scattered and ran.

segs.zh: 我想：我同赵贵翁有什么仇，同路上的人又有什么仇；只有廿年以前，把古久先生的陈年流水簿子，踹了一脚，古久先生很不高兴。赵贵翁虽然不认识他，一定也听到风声，代抱不平；约定路上的人，同我作冤对。但是小孩子呢？那时候，他们还没有出世，何以今天也睁着怪眼睛，似乎怕我，似乎想害我。这真教我怕，教我纳罕而且伤心。
segs.en: I reflected: what grudge could I possibly bear against Zhao Guiweng, or for that matter, against the people on the street? The only thing I could think of was that twenty years ago I stepped on Mr. Gujiu’s ancient, dusty ledger, which had greatly displeased him. Although Zhao Guiweng did not know him, he must have heard whispers of it and decided to take up his cause, conspiring with the people on the street to make me their enemy. But what about the children? They weren’t even born back then. Why, today, do they also stare at me with such strange eyes, as if they fear me, or perhaps, as if they want to do me harm? This truly terrifies me; it leaves me bewildered and heartbroken.

segs.zh: 我明白了。这是他们娘老子教的！
segs.en: I understood. This is what their own parents taught them!

segs.zh: 晚上总是睡不着。凡事须得研究，才会明白。
segs.en: I cannot sleep at night. Everything requires scrutiny before it can be understood.

segs.zh: 他们——也有给知县打枷过的，也有给绅士掌过嘴的，也有衙役占了他妻子的，也有老子娘被债主逼死的；他们那时候的脸色，全没有昨天这么怕，也没有这么凶。
segs.en: They—some had been pilloried by the magistrate, some had been slapped by the gentry, some had had their wives taken by yamen runners, and some had had their parents driven to their deaths by creditors; yet in those days, the expressions on their faces were neither as fearful nor as ferocious as they were yesterday.

segs.zh: 最奇怪的是昨天街上的那个女人，打他儿子，嘴里说道，『老子呀！我要咬你几口才出气！』他眼睛却看着我。我出了一惊，遮掩不住；那青面獠牙的一伙人，便都哄笑起来。陈老五赶上前，硬把我拖回家中了。
segs.en: The strangest thing was that woman on the street yesterday. As she beat her son, she kept crying out, "You little wretch! I’ll take a few bites out of you to vent my spleen!" Yet, all the while, her eyes were fixed on me. I was startled, and could not hide my alarm; the whole gang of them, with their green faces and bared fangs, burst into mocking laughter. Chen Laowu rushed forward and dragged me home by force.

segs.zh: 拖我回家。家里的人都装作不认识我；他们的眼色，也全同别人一样。进了书房，便反扣上门，宛然是关了一只鸡鸭。这一件事，越教我猜不出底细。
segs.en: They dragged me home. My own family pretended they did not know me; the looks in their eyes were exactly like everyone else's. Once I was inside the study, they bolted the door behind me, just as one would pen up a chicken or a duck. This whole affair left me more bewildered than ever.

segs.zh: 前几天，狼子村的佃户来告荒，对我大哥说，他们村里的一个大恶人，给大家打死了；几个人便挖出他的心肝来，用油煎炒了吃，可以壮壮胆子。我插了一句嘴，佃户和大哥便都看我几眼。今天才晓得他们的眼光，全同外面的那伙人一模一样。
segs.en: A few days ago, a tenant farmer from Wolf Cub Village came to report a poor harvest. He told my elder brother that a notorious villain in their village had been beaten to death by the crowd; some of them had even carved out his heart and liver to fry in oil and eat, believing it would bolster their courage. I put in a word, and both the tenant and my brother turned to stare at me. Only today do I realize that their eyes were exactly the same as those of the people outside.

segs.zh: 想起来，我从顶上直冷到脚跟。
segs.en: At the thought of it, a chill ran from the crown of my head down to my heels.

segs.zh: 他们会吃人，就未必不会吃我。
segs.en: If they can eat people, they are just as likely to eat me.

segs.zh: 你看那女人『咬你几口』的话，和一伙青面獠牙人的笑，和前天佃户的话，明明是暗号。我看出他话中全是毒，笑中全是刀，他们的牙齿，全是白厉厉的排着，这就是吃人的家伙。
segs.en: Look at how that woman speaks of “taking a few bites out of you,” and the laughter of that pack of green-faced, fanged monsters, and what the tenant farmer said the day before yesterday—it is all clearly code. I can see that his words are filled with poison, his laughter with daggers, and their teeth, all lined up white and sharp, are nothing but man-eating tools.

segs.zh: 照我自己想，虽然不是恶人，自从踹了古家的簿子，可就难说了。他们似乎别有心思，我全猜不出。况且他们一翻脸，便说人是恶人。我还记得大哥教我做论，无论怎样好人，翻他几句，他便打上几个圈；原谅坏人几句，他便说『翻天妙手，与众不同』。我那里猜得到他们的心思，究竟怎样；况且是要吃的时候。
segs.en: As for me, while I am no villain, I suppose it has been hard to say otherwise ever since I trampled on the Gu family’s ledger. They seem to harbor ulterior motives, though I cannot for the life of me guess what they are. Moreover, the moment they turn hostile, they label a man a villain. I still remember how my elder brother used to teach me to write essays: no matter how upright a person was, if I managed to pick a few faults in him, he would draw circles of approval; yet if I offered a few words of leniency toward a scoundrel, he would praise me as a "master of subversion, quite extraordinary." How could I possibly fathom their motives? And besides, this was all happening just when I was about to be eaten.

segs.zh: 凡事总须研究，才会明白，古来时常吃人，我也还记得，可是不甚清楚。我翻开历史一查，这历史没有年代，歪歪斜斜的每叶上都写着『仁义道德』几个字。我横竖睡不着，仔细看了半夜，才从字缝里看出字来，满本都写着两个字是『吃人』！
segs.en: Everything must be examined before it can be understood. From ancient times, people have often eaten one another; I still remember this, though not very clearly. I opened a history book to look into it, but the book had no dates, and across every line, scrawled crookedly, were the words: "Benevolence, Righteousness, and Morality." Since I could not sleep anyway, I examined the pages carefully for half the night until I finally began to see words between the lines; the entire book was filled with only two words: "Eat people!"

segs.zh: 书上写着这许多字，佃户说了这许多话，却都笑吟吟的睁着怪眼睛看我。
segs.en: The books were filled with all these words, and the tenant farmers had spoken all these things, yet they all looked at me with strange, wide eyes, smiling all the while.

segs.zh: 我也是人，他们想要吃我了！
segs.en: I am a human being, too, and they are out to eat me!

segs.zh: 早上，我静坐了一会。陈老五送进饭来，一碗菜，一碗蒸鱼；这鱼的眼睛，白而且硬，张着嘴，同那一伙想吃人的人一样。吃了几筷，滑溜溜的不知是鱼是人，便把他兜肚连肠的吐出。
segs.en: In the morning, I sat quietly for a while. Chen Laowu brought in my meal: a bowl of vegetables and a bowl of steamed fish. The fish's eyes were white and hard, its mouth agape, just like that pack of people who want to eat men. I took a few bites, but unable to tell whether the slippery mass was fish or human, I spat it all out, guts and all.

segs.zh: 我说：『老五，对大哥说，我闷得荒，想到园里走走。』老五不答应，走了，停一会，可就来开了门。
segs.en: I said, "Old Wu, tell your master I’m feeling restless and would like to take a walk in the garden." Old Wu didn't answer and walked away, but a moment later, he came back and opened the gate.

segs.zh: 我也不动，研究他们如何摆布我；知道他们一定不肯放松。果然！我大哥引了一个老头子，慢慢走来；他满眼凶光，怕我看出，只是低头向着地，从眼镜横边暗暗看我。大哥说：『今天你彷佛很好。』我说：『是的。』大哥说：『今天请何先生来，给你诊一诊。』我说：『可以！』其实我岂不知道这老头子是刽子手扮的！无非借了看脉这名目，揣一揣肥瘠：因这功劳，也分一片肉吃。我也不怕；虽然不吃人，胆子却比他们还壮。伸出两个拳头，看他如何下手。老头子坐着，闭了眼睛，摸了好一会，呆了好一会；便张开他鬼眼睛说：『不要乱想。静静的养几天，就好了。』
segs.en:
I did not move, studying how they intended to handle me; I knew they would never let me go. And sure enough! My elder brother led an old man slowly toward me. The man’s eyes were full of malice, yet for fear I might notice, he kept his head bowed, stealing glances at me from the corners of his spectacles.

"You seem much better today," my brother said.

"Yes," I replied.

"I’ve asked Mr. He to come and examine you today," my brother continued.

"Very well!" I said.

In truth, how could I not know that this old man was an executioner in disguise? He was merely using the pretext of feeling my pulse to gauge how fat I had grown, so that he, too, might claim a slice of meat for his trouble. I felt no fear; though I do not eat people, my courage is greater than theirs. I thrust out both my fists to see how he would proceed. The old man sat down, closed his eyes, and fumbled for a long while, remaining motionless for an even longer while. Then, he opened his ghostly eyes and said, "Do not let your mind wander. Just rest quietly for a few days, and you will be fine."

segs.zh: 不要乱想，静静的养！养肥了，他们是自然可以多吃；我有什么好处，怎么会『好了』？他们这群人，又想吃人，又是鬼鬼祟祟，想法子遮掩，不敢直捷下手，真要令我笑死。我忍不住，便放声大笑起来，十分快活。自己晓得这笑声里面，有的是义勇和正气；老头子和大哥，都失了色，被我这勇气正气镇压住了。
segs.en: Stop these wild thoughts and just rest quietly! Once I’m fattened up, they’ll naturally have more to feast on; what benefit is it to me, how could I ever get "better"? This pack of people, they want to eat human flesh, yet they slink about, desperately trying to cover their tracks, not daring to strike directly—it’s enough to make me die laughing. I couldn’t help myself, and I burst into loud, hearty laughter. I knew that in this laughter there was nothing but courage and righteousness; the old man and my big brother both turned pale, completely cowed by my bold, righteous spirit.

segs.zh: 但是我有勇气，他们便越想吃我，沾光一点这勇气。老头子跨出门，走不多远，便低声对大哥说道，『赶紧吃罢！』大哥点点头。原来也有你！这一件大发见，虽似意外，也在意中：合伙吃我的人，便是我的哥哥！
segs.en: But I have courage, and the more they want to eat me, to get a taste of this courage for themselves. The old man stepped out the door and, after going only a short distance, whispered to my elder brother, "Hurry up and eat!" My elder brother nodded. So, you are in on it too! This great discovery, though it seemed unexpected, was actually within reason: the very person who conspired to eat me is my own brother!

segs.zh: 吃人的是我哥哥！
segs.en: It is my elder brother who is the man-eater!

segs.zh: 我是吃人的人的兄弟！
segs.en: I am the brother of a man-eater!

segs.zh: 我自己被人吃了，可仍然是吃人的人的兄弟！
segs.en: I myself have been eaten, and yet I remain the brother of those who eat men!

segs.zh: 这几天是退一步想：假使那老头子不是刽子手扮的，真是医生，也仍然是吃人的人。他们的祖师李时珍做的『本草什么』上，明明写着人肉可以煎吃；他还能说自己不吃人么？
segs.en: In the last few days, I have had to step back and reconsider: even if that old man wasn't posing as an executioner, even if he were truly a doctor, he would still be a man-eater. In the *Ben Cao* whatever-it-is written by their patron saint, Li Shizhen, it is clearly stated that human flesh can be boiled and eaten; how, then, could he claim that he himself does not eat people?

segs.zh: 至于我家大哥，也毫不冤枉他。他对我讲书的时候，亲口说过可以『易子而食』；又一回偶然议论起一个不好的人，他便说不但该杀，还当『食肉寝皮』。我那时年纪还小，心跳了好半天。前天狼子村佃户来说吃心肝的事，他也毫不奇怪，不住的点头。可见心思是同从前一样狠。既然可以『易子而食』，便什么都易得，什么人都吃得。我从前单听他讲道理，也糊涂过去；现在晓得他讲道理的时候，不但唇边还抹着人油，而且心里满装着吃人的意思。
segs.en: As for my big brother, there is no injustice in saying this of him. Back when he was tutoring me, he once said quite plainly that it was acceptable to "exchange children and eat them." Another time, when we happened to be discussing some wicked person, he remarked that not only should the man be put to death, but one ought to "eat his flesh and sleep on his hide." I was quite young then, and my heart pounded for a long while after. The day before yesterday, when the tenant farmer from Wolf Cub Village came to talk about eating human hearts and livers, my brother showed no surprise at all; he just kept nodding his head. It is clear that his frame of mind remains as ruthless as ever. If one can justify "exchanging children to eat them," then anything can be exchanged, and anyone can be eaten. In the past, I would simply listen to his moralizing and let it muddle my mind; now I realize that while he was preaching morality, his lips were not only smeared with human fat, but his heart was also brimming with the intent to devour people.

segs.zh: 黑漆漆的，不知是日是夜。赵家的狗又叫起来了。
segs.en: It was pitch black; I could not tell if it was day or night. The Zhao family’s dog began to bark again.

segs.zh: 狮子似的凶心，兔子的怯弱，狐狸的狡猾，……
segs.en: A lion’s ferocity, a rabbit’s timidity, a fox’s cunning...

segs.zh: 我晓得他们的方法，直捷杀了，是不肯的，而且也不敢，怕有祸祟。所以他们大家连络，布满了罗网，逼我自戕。试看前几天街上男女的样子，和这几天我大哥的作为，便足可悟出八九分了。最好是解下腰带，挂在梁上，自己紧紧勒死；他们没有杀人的罪名，又偿了心愿，自然都欢天喜地的发出一种呜呜咽咽的笑声。否则惊吓忧愁死了，虽则略瘦，也还可以首肯几下。
segs.en: I know their methods: they would never dare to kill me outright, nor are they willing to, fearing the retribution of ghosts. Instead, they have joined forces, spreading their nets everywhere to drive me to self-destruction. One only needs to observe the expressions of the men and women on the street these past few days, or consider my elder brother’s recent behavior, to grasp eight or nine parts of the truth. The best outcome for them would be for me to unfasten my belt, hang it from the rafters, and strangle myself tight. Then, without the stain of murder on their hands, and with their hearts' desire fulfilled, they would naturally burst into a chorus of jubilant, whimpering laughter. Failing that, if I were to die of fright or sorrow, though I might be a little gaunt, they would still be able to nod their heads in satisfaction.

segs.zh: 他们是只会吃死肉的！——记得什么书上说，有一种东西，叫『海乙那』的，眼光和样子都很难看；时常吃死肉，连极大的骨头，都细细嚼烂，咽下肚子去，想起来也教人害怕。『海乙那』是狼的亲眷，狼是狗的本家。前天赵家的狗，看我几眼，可见他也同谋，早已接洽，老头子眼看着地。岂能瞒得我过。
segs.en: They only know how to eat carrion! I remember reading in some book about a creature called a "hyena," with a hideous gaze and an even more hideous appearance. They are always eating carrion, and they even chew up the biggest bones, grinding them fine before swallowing them down. The very thought of it is enough to make one shudder. The hyena is a relative of the wolf, and the wolf is of the same breed as the dog. The day before yesterday, the Zhao family’s dog took a few looks at me; it is clear that he is in on the conspiracy, that they have long been in league, and that the old man just looks down at the ground. How could they possibly hope to hoodwink me?

segs.zh: 最可怜的是我的大哥。他也是人，何以毫不害怕；而且合伙吃我呢？还是历来惯了，不以为非呢？还是丧了良心，明知故犯呢？
segs.en: The most pathetic of all is my big brother. He is a human being too, so why is he not afraid at all? And why does he join in to eat me? Is it because he has been accustomed to it for so long that he no longer sees the wrong in it? Or has he lost his conscience and is committing the crime while knowing full well what he is doing?

segs.zh: 我诅咒吃人的人，先从他起头；要劝转吃人的人，也先从他下手。
segs.en: I curse the man-eaters, starting with him; and if I am to persuade the man-eaters to turn back, I must also begin with him.

segs.zh: 其实这种道理，到了现在，他们也该早已懂得，……
segs.en: In truth, this kind of logic is something they ought to have understood long ago by now, ...

segs.zh: 忽然来了一个人；年纪不过二十左右，相貌是不很看得清楚，满面笑容，对了我点头，他的笑也不像真笑。我便问他：『吃人的事，对么？』他仍然笑着说：『不是荒年，怎么会吃人。』我立刻就晓得，他也是一伙，喜欢吃人的；便自勇气百倍，偏要问他。
segs.en: Suddenly, someone approached. He was not more than twenty years old. His features were not easy to make out, but his face was wreathed in smiles as he nodded at me—though his smile did not look like a genuine one. I asked him, "Is it right to eat people?" He kept on smiling and replied, "How could anyone eat people when there is no famine?" I knew at once that he was one of their lot, someone who delighted in eating people. My courage surged a hundredfold, and I insisted on questioning him further.

segs.zh: 『对么？』
segs.en: Is that right?

segs.zh: 『这等事问他甚么。你真会……说笑话。……今天天气很好。』
segs.en: Why ask him about such things? You really are… a joker. … The weather is very fine today.

segs.zh: 天气是好，月色也很亮了。可是我要问你，『对么？』
segs.en: The weather is fine, and the moonlight is bright, too. But I must ask you: "Is that right?"

segs.zh: 他不以为然了。含含胡胡的答道，『不……』
segs.en: He did not agree. Mumbling, he replied, "No..."

segs.zh: 『不对？他们何以竟吃！』
segs.en: Not right? Then how is it they eat them!

segs.zh: 『没有的事……』
segs.en: Nothing of the sort...

segs.zh: 『没有的事？狼子村现吃；还有书上都写着，通红斩新！』
segs.en: Never happened? It’s happening right now in Langzi Village; and it’s all written down in the books, plain as day, in bright, fresh ink!

segs.zh: 他便变了脸，铁一般青。睁着眼说，『也许有的，这是从来如此……』
segs.en: His face darkened, turning the color of iron. Staring me straight in the eye, he said, "Perhaps there are, it has always been so..."

segs.zh: 『从来如此，便对么？』
segs.en: Just because it has always been so, does that make it right?

segs.zh: 『我不同你讲这些道理；总之你不该说，你说便是你错！』
segs.en: I’m not going to argue these points with you; the point is you shouldn’t have said it, and if you say it, you’re wrong!

segs.zh: 我直跳起来，张开眼，这人便不见了。全身出了一大片汗。他的年纪，比我大哥小得远，居然也是一伙；这一定是他娘老子先教的。还怕已经教给他儿子了；所以连小孩子，也都恶狠狠的看我。
segs.en: I bolted upright, eyes wide open, but the man was already gone. My whole body was drenched in a cold sweat. He was much younger than my elder brother, yet he was part of that same pack; his parents must have taught him their ways long ago. I fear they have already begun teaching his son, too; that is why even the children look at me with such venomous eyes.

segs.zh: 自己想吃人，又怕被别人吃了，都用着疑心极深的眼光，面面相觑。……
segs.en: They want to eat people, yet they are afraid of being eaten themselves, and so they eye one another with the deepest suspicion.

segs.zh: 去了这心思，放心做事走路吃饭睡觉，何等舒服。这只是一条门槛，一个关头。他们可是父子、兄弟、夫妇、朋友、师生、仇敌和各不相识的人，都结成一伙，互相劝勉，互相牵掣，死也不肯跨这一步。
segs.en: Once you set that worry aside—to go about your work, your walking, your eating, and your sleeping with an easy mind—how comfortable that is. Yet this is but a threshold, a single turning point. And they—fathers and sons, brothers, husbands and wives, friends, teachers and students, enemies, and utter strangers—all band together, exhorting one another, holding one another back, refusing even in death to take that step across.

segs.zh: 大清早，去寻我大哥；他立在堂门外看天，我便走到他背后，拦住门，格外沈静，格外和气的对他说：
segs.en: Early in the morning, I went to look for my elder brother. He was standing outside the hall door, gazing at the sky. I walked up behind him, blocked the doorway, and with unusual calmness and gentleness, said to him:

segs.zh: 『大哥，我有话告诉你。』
segs.en: Big Brother, I have something to tell you.

segs.zh: 『你说就是。』他赶紧回过脸来，点点头。
segs.en: "Just say so, then." He turned his head back in a hurry and gave a nod.

segs.zh: 『我只有几句话，可是说不出来。大哥，大约当初野蛮的人，都吃过一点人。后来因为心思不同，有的不吃人了，一味要好，便变了人，变了真的人。有的却还吃，——也同虫子一样，有的变了鱼、鸟、猴子，一直变到人。有的不要好，至今还是虫子。这吃人的人比不吃人的人，何等惭愧。怕比虫子的惭愧猴子，还差得很远很远。
segs.en: I have only a few words to say, but I cannot get them out. Big Brother, it is likely that in the beginning, all primitive people ate a little human flesh. Later, because their minds developed differently, some stopped eating people and strove only for the good; they became human beings—true human beings. Others, however, kept on eating—just like insects, some of which turned into fish, birds, or monkeys, and eventually into men. But some refused to be good, and to this day they remain insects. These man-eaters, compared to those who do not eat people, should feel such shame. I fear they are as far removed from the shame of a man as a monkey is from an insect, perhaps even further.

segs.zh: 『易牙蒸了他儿子，给桀纣吃，还是一直从前的事。谁晓得从盘古开辟天地以后，一直吃到易牙的儿子；从易牙的儿子，一直吃到徐锡林；从徐锡林，又一直吃到狼子村捉住的人。去年城里杀了犯人，还有个生痨病的人，用馒头蘸血舐。
segs.en: Yi Ya steamed his own son to feed Jie and Zhou; that was long, long ago. Who knows how many people have been eaten from the time Pangu cleaved heaven and earth apart down to the son of Yi Ya? And from the son of Yi Ya down to Xu Xilin, and from Xu Xilin down to the man they caught in Langzi Village? Last year, when they executed the prisoners in the city, there was even a man with consumption who bought a steamed bun dipped in blood to lick.

segs.zh: 『他们要吃我，你一个人，原也无法可想；然而又何必去入伙。吃人的人，什么事做不出；他们会吃我，也会吃你，一伙里面，也会自吃。但只要转一步，只要立刻改了，也就人人太平。虽然从来如此，我们今天也可以格外要好，说是不能！大哥，我相信你能说，前天佃户要减租，你说过不能。』
segs.en: They want to eat me, and you, all by yourself, could hardly stop them; but why must you join their ranks? People who eat others are capable of anything. If they can eat me, they can eat you too, and among their own kind, they will even eat each other. But if you would just take a step back, if you would only change right now, then everyone could live in peace. Even though it has always been this way, we could still be better than that today, couldn't we? Big brother, I know you have the power to speak up. The day before yesterday, when the tenant farmers asked for a rent reduction, you were the one who said it couldn't be done.

segs.zh: 当初，他还只是冷笑，随后眼光便凶狠起来，一到说破他们的隐情，那就满脸都变成青色了。大门外立着一伙人，赵贵翁和他的狗，也在里面，都探头探脑的挨进来。有的是看不出面貌，似乎用布蒙着；有的是仍旧青面潦牙，抿着嘴笑。我认识他们是一伙，都是吃人的人。可是也晓得他们心思很不一样，一种是以为从来如此，应该吃的；一种是知道不该吃，可是仍然要吃，又怕别人说破他，所以听了我的话，越发气愤不过，可是抿着嘴冷笑。
segs.en: At first, he would only sneer, but then his eyes would turn fierce; once their secret was laid bare, his whole face would turn a ghastly green. Outside the main gate stood a crowd, Zhao Guiweng and his dog among them, all craning their necks to edge their way in. Some faces were indistinguishable, as if veiled by cloth; others were still green-faced with bared fangs, smirking as they stood there. I recognized them as one gang: they were all man-eaters. Yet I also knew their minds were far from identical. One sort believed that because things had always been this way, eating people was only right; the other sort knew it was wrong, yet insisted on eating them anyway, and because they feared having their secret exposed, my words only made them more resentful, though they still stood there, smirking with pursed lips.

segs.zh: 这时候，大哥也忽然显出凶相，高声喝道：
segs.en: At this moment, my elder brother’s expression suddenly turned ferocious, and he shouted harshly:

segs.zh: 『都出去！疯子有什么好看！』
segs.en: Get out, all of you! What’s there to see in a madman?

segs.zh: 这时候，我又懂得一件他们的巧妙了。他们岂但不肯改，而且早已布置；预备下一个疯子的名目罩上我。将来吃了，不但太平无事，怕还会有人见情。佃户说的大家吃了一个恶人，正是这方法。这是他们的老谱！
segs.en: At this moment, I grasped another of their cunning tricks. Not only are they unwilling to change, but they have long since laid their plans, ready to slap the label of "madman" on me. If they eventually devour me, it will not only be perfectly fine, but some might even thank them for it. The tenant farmer’s talk of everyone eating a villain is precisely this method. It is their old, worn-out game!

segs.zh: 陈老五也气愤愤的直走进来。如何按得住我的口，我偏要对这伙人说，
segs.en: Chen Laowu also stormed in, fuming. How could they ever seal my lips? I would speak to this lot, and I would do it out of spite.

segs.zh: 『你们可以改了，从真心改起！要晓得将来容不得吃人的人，活在世上。
segs.en: You can change, change from the bottom of your hearts! You must realize that there will be no place in the world to come for man-eaters.

segs.zh: 『你们要不改，自己也会吃尽。即使生得多，也会给真的人除灭了，同猎人打完狼子一样！——同虫子一样！』
segs.en: If you don't change, you will eventually devour yourselves. Even if you multiply, you will be exterminated by truly human beings—just as hunters wipe out litters of wolves! Just as one wipes out vermin!

segs.zh: 那一伙人，都被陈老五赶走了。大哥也不知那里去了。陈老五劝我回屋子里去。屋里面全是黑沈沈的。横梁和椽子都在头上发抖；抖了一会，就大起来，堆在我身上。
segs.en: Chen Laowu drove the whole lot of them away. My eldest brother had disappeared, I don’t know where. Chen Laowu urged me to go back inside. The room was pitch-black. The crossbeams and rafters above my head were trembling; they trembled for a moment, then grew larger and larger, and came crashing down upon me.

segs.zh: 万分沉重，动弹不得；他的意思是要我死。我晓得他的沉重是假的，便挣扎出来，出了一身汗。可是偏要说，
segs.en: It was immensely heavy, and I could not move; his intention was for me to die. Knowing that his weight was feigned, I struggled free, breaking into a sweat. Yet, I felt compelled to say,

segs.zh: 『你们立刻改了，从真心改起！你们要晓得将来是容不得吃人的人，……』
segs.en: Change yourselves at once, from the bottom of your hearts! You must realize that there will be no place for man-eaters in the future...

segs.zh: 太阳也不出，门也不开，日日是两顿饭。
segs.en: The sun never came out, the doors never opened, and day after day there were only two meals.

segs.zh: 我捏起筷子，便想起我大哥；晓得妹子死掉的缘故，也全在他。那时我妹子才五岁，可爱可怜的样子，还在眼前。母亲哭个不住，他却劝母亲不要哭；大约因为自己吃了，哭起来不免有点过意不去。如果还能过意不去，……
segs.en: I picked up my chopsticks and was at once reminded of my elder brother; it was entirely because of him that my little sister died. She was only five years old then, and her sweet, pitiable face still rises before my eyes. Mother wept incessantly, yet he kept urging her not to cry—probably because, having eaten the child himself, he felt a little uneasy. If he were still capable of feeling uneasy...

segs.zh: 妹子是被大哥吃了，母亲知道没有，我可不得而知。
segs.en: My sister was eaten by our eldest brother; whether my mother knew about it or not, I have no way of knowing.

segs.zh: 母亲想也知道；不过哭的时候，却并没有说明，大约也以为应当的了。记得我四五岁时，坐在堂前乘凉，大哥说爷娘生病，做儿子的须割下一片肉来，煮熟了请他吃，才算好人；母亲也没说不行。一片吃得，整个的自然也吃得。但是那天的哭法，现在想起来，实在还教人伤心，这真是奇极的事！
segs.en: Mother probably knew as well, though she did not say so while she was crying; I suppose she, too, thought it was only right. I remember when I was four or five, sitting in the main hall to escape the heat, my elder brother said that when parents fall ill, a son must slice off a piece of his own flesh, boil it, and serve it to them; only then is he a good son. Mother did not say that was wrong. If one could eat a slice, naturally one could eat the whole. Yet, thinking back on the way she cried that day, it is truly heartbreaking—it is the strangest thing!

segs.zh: 不能想了。
segs.en: I cannot think about it anymore.

segs.zh: 四千年来时时吃人的地方，今天才明白，我也在其中混了多年；大哥正管着家务，妹子恰恰死了，他未必不和在饭菜里，暗暗给我们吃。
segs.en: A place that has been devouring people for four thousand years—only today do I realize that I, too, have been drifting along in its midst for all these years. My elder brother is currently managing the household affairs; since my little sister died just at the right time, he may well have mixed her into our food for us to eat on the sly.

segs.zh: 我未必无意之中，不吃了我妹子的几片肉，现在也轮到我自己，……
segs.en: 暫無回應或API格式不符

segs.zh: 有了四千年吃人履历的我，当初虽然不知道，现在明白，难见真的人！
segs.en: With four thousand years of man-eating history behind me, though I was unaware of it at the time, I now realize that it is hard to catch sight of a real human being!

segs.zh: 没有吃过人的孩子，或者还有？
segs.en: Perhaps there are still children who haven’t eaten human flesh?

segs.zh: 救救孩子……
segs.en: Save the children...

segs.zh: （一九一八年四月。）
这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may also be in the public domain in countries and regions where the copyright term is longer for domestic works but shorter for foreign ones, in accordance with the rule of the shorter term.

segs.zh: 据《中华人民共和国著作权法释义》，法人作品应满足三点要求：（1）由法人（或其他组织，以下简称“法人”）主持创作，而非工作人员自发进行；（2）创作思想及表达方式体现法人意志；（3）由法人负责，而非执笔者。（详情）
segs.en: According to the Interpretation of the Copyright Law of the People's Republic of China, a work created by a legal entity must satisfy three requirements: (1) it must be created under the direction of the legal entity (or other organization, hereinafter referred to as "legal entity"), rather than being the spontaneous work of its staff; (2) the creative intent and manner of expression must embody the will of the legal entity; and (3) the legal entity must bear responsibility for the work, rather than the individual authors who penned it. (Details)

chapters.note: 中国现代文学史上第一篇白话小说。一个「狂人」在字缝里读出「吃人」二字——结尾一句「救救孩子……」，喊了整整一个世纪。

chapters.label: 二

segs.zh: 鲁镇的酒店的格局，是和别处不同的：都是当街一个曲尺形的大柜台，柜里面豫备着热水，可以随时温酒。做工的人，傍午傍晚散了工，每每花四文铜钱，买一碗酒——这是二十多年前的事，现在每碗要涨到十文，——靠柜外站着，热热的喝了休息；倘肯多花一文，便可以买一碟盐煮笋，或者茴香豆，做下酒物了，如果出到了十几文，那就能买一样荤菜，但这些顾客，多是短衣帮，大抵没有这样阔绰。只有穿长衫的，才踱进店面隔壁的房子里，要酒要菜，慢慢地坐喝。
segs.en: The layout of the taverns in Lu Town is unlike that of anywhere else: each features a large, L-shaped counter facing the street, where hot water is always kept ready to warm the wine at a moment’s notice. When the laborers finish their shifts at noon or dusk, they often spend four coppers on a bowl of wine—this was over twenty years ago, of course; the price has since risen to ten coppers a bowl—and stand outside the counter, sipping it hot to rest their weary limbs. If they are willing to spend one extra copper, they can buy a small dish of salt-boiled bamboo shoots or fennel beans to go with their drink. If they spend upwards of a dozen coppers, they can even order a meat dish, but these customers are mostly of the short-jacketed crowd, and rarely that extravagant. Only those who wear long gowns pace into the room partitioned off from the main tavern, where they order wine and dishes, sitting down to drink at their leisure.

segs.zh: 我从十二岁起，便在镇口的咸亨酒店里当伙计，掌柜说，样子太儍，怕侍候不了长衫主顾，就在外面做点事罢。外面的短衣主顾，虽然容易说话，但唠唠叨叨缠夹不清的也很不少。他们往往要亲眼看着黄酒从坛子里舀出，看过壶子底里有水没有，又亲看将壶子放在热水里，然后放心：在这严重监督之下，羼水也很为难。所以过了几天，掌柜又说我干不了这事。幸亏荐头的情面大，辞退不得，便改为专管温酒的一种无聊职务了。
segs.en: I have been a waiter at the Xianheng Tavern at the edge of town since I was twelve. The proprietor said I looked too foolish to wait on the long-gowned customers, so he told me to work outside instead. The short-coated customers who patronized the outer counter were easier to deal with, though many of them were tiresome, rambling, and prone to endless quibbling. They insisted on watching with their own eyes as the yellow wine was ladled from the jar, checking the bottom of the pot to ensure there was no water in it, and then watching closely as the pot was set into the boiling water. Only then would they be satisfied. Under such strict supervision, it was indeed difficult to water down the wine. After a few days, the proprietor said I could not handle that job either. Fortunately, the person who had recommended me carried enough weight that I could not be dismissed, so I was relegated to the dull task of warming the wine.

segs.zh: 我从此便整天的站在柜台里，专管我的职务。虽然没有什么失职，但总觉有些单调，有些无聊。掌柜是一副凶脸孔，主顾也没有好声气，教人活泼不得；只有孔乙己到店，才可以笑几声，所以至今还记得。
segs.en: From then on, I spent my entire day standing behind the counter, attending to my duties. Though I never neglected my work, I found it monotonous and dull. The proprietor had a fierce face, and the customers were rarely pleasant, which made it impossible for anyone to be lively. Only when Kong Yiji came to the tavern could I have a few laughs, which is why I remember him to this day.

segs.zh: 孔乙己是站着喝酒而穿长衫的唯一的人。他身材很高大；青白脸色，皱纹间时常夹些伤痕；一部乱蓬蓬的花白的胡子。穿的虽然是长衫，可是又脏又破，似乎十多年没有补，也没有洗。他对人说话，总是满口之乎者也，教人半懂不懂的。因为他姓孔，别人便从描红纸上的『上大人孔乙己』这半懂不懂的话里，替他取下一个绰号，叫作孔乙己。孔乙己一到店，所有喝酒的人便都看着他笑，有的叫道：『孔乙己，你脸上又添上新伤疤了！』他不回答，对柜里说：『温两碗酒，要一碟茴香豆。』便排出九文大钱。他们又故意的高声嚷道：『你一定又偸了人家的东西了！』孔乙己睁大眼睛说：『你怎么这样凭空污人清白……』『什么清白？我前天亲眼见你偸了何家的书，吊着打。』孔乙己便涨红了脸，额上的青筋条条绽出，争辩道：『窃书不能算偸……窃书！……读书人的事，能算偸么？』接连便是难懂的话，什么『君子固穷』，什么『者乎』之类，引得众人都哄笑起来；店内外充满了快活的空气。
segs.en: Kong Yiji was the only person who stood at the bar to drink while wearing a long gown. He was a tall man with a pale, sallow complexion, his wrinkles often marked by fresh scars, and he sported a ragged, greying beard. Though he wore a long gown, it was dirty and tattered; it seemed not to have been patched or washed in over a decade. When he spoke to others, he would spout archaic phrases like "zhi, hu, zhe, ye," leaving people only half-understanding what he meant. Because his surname was Kong, others took the nonsensical line from the children’s copybook—"The Great Man Kong Yiji"—and dubbed him with the nickname Kong Yiji. Whenever he arrived at the tavern, all the drinkers would look at him and laugh, some calling out, "Kong Yiji, you’ve got a new scar on your face!" He would not answer, but instead say to the counterman, "Warm two bowls of wine and give me a plate of aniseed beans." He would then lay out nine coppers. They would deliberately shout even louder, "You must have stolen something again!" Kong Yiji would widen his eyes and retort, "How can you slander an innocent man’s character out of thin air..." "Innocent character? I saw you stealing books from the He family the day before yesterday, and you were strung up and beaten for it." Kong Yiji’s face would flush, the veins on his forehead standing out, as he argued, "Stealing books cannot be called stealing... Stealing books! ... Can the affairs of a scholar be called stealing?" This would be followed by more unintelligible jargon about "a gentleman remaining steadfast in poverty" and suchlike, which would set everyone off into peals of laughter; the tavern, both inside and out, was filled with an air of merriment.

segs.zh: 听人家背地里谈论，孔乙己原来也读过书，但终于没有进学，又不会营生；于是愈过愈穷，弄到将要讨饭了。幸而写得一笔好字，便替人家钞钞书，换一碗饭吃。可惜他又有一样坏脾气，便是好喝懒做。坐不到几天，便连人和书籍纸张笔砚，一齐失踪。如是几次，叫他钞书的人也没有了。孔乙己没有法，便免不了偶然做些偸窃的事。但他在我们店里，品行却比别人都好，就是从不拖欠；虽然间或没有现钱，暂时记在粉板上，但不出一月，定然还清，从粉板上拭去了孔乙己的名字。
segs.en: According to what people said in private, Kong Yiji had once been a scholar, but he had never passed the examinations and had no way to make a living; consequently, he grew steadily poorer until he was on the verge of begging. Fortunately, he wrote a fine hand, so he would copy manuscripts for people in exchange for a meal. Unfortunately, he had a bad habit: he was fond of a drink and lazy. He would not last more than a few days before he, along with the books, paper, brushes, and inkstones, would all vanish. After this had happened several times, no one would hire him to copy manuscripts anymore. With no other options, Kong Yiji could not help but occasionally resort to petty theft. Yet, in our tavern, his conduct was better than anyone else’s; he never fell behind in his payments. Even when he lacked ready cash, he would have it temporarily tallied on the slate, but within a month, he would always pay it off and have his name wiped from the board.

segs.zh: 孔乙己喝过半碗酒，涨红的脸色渐渐复了原，旁人便又问道，『孔乙己，你当真认识字么？』孔乙己看着问他的人，显出不屑置辩的神气。他们便接着说道：『你怎的连半个秀才也捞不到呢？』孔乙己立刻显出颓唐不安模样，脸上笼上了一层灰色，嘴里说些话；这回可是全是之乎者也之类，一些不懂了。在这时候，众人也都哄笑起来：店内外充满了快活的空气。
segs.en: After Kong Yiji had drunk half a bowl of wine, the flush on his face gradually faded, and the others would ask again, "Kong Yiji, do you really know how to read?" Kong Yiji would look at the man who had asked, showing a look of disdain for such an argument. They would then follow up with, "How is it that you didn't even manage to scrape together a half-title of 'scholar'?" Kong Yiji would immediately look dejected and uneasy, a grey pallor spreading across his face, and start muttering to himself; this time, it was all "zhi, hu, zhe, ye" and such, completely unintelligible. At this, everyone would roar with laughter, and the tavern, inside and out, would be filled with a cheerful atmosphere.

segs.zh: 在这些时候，我可以附和着笑，掌柜是决不责备的。而且掌柜见了孔乙己，也每每这样问他，引人发笑。孔乙己自己知道不能和他们谈天，便只好向孩子说话。有一回对我说道，『你读过书么？』我略略点一点头。他说，『读过书，……我便考你一考。茴香豆的茴字，怎样写的？』我想，讨饭一样的人，也配考我么？便回过脸去，不再理会。孔乙己等了许久，很恳切的说道：『不能写罢？……我教给你，记着！这些字应该记着。将来做掌柜的时候，写帐要用。』我暗想我和掌柜的等级还很远呢，而且我们掌柜也从不将茴香豆上帐；又好笑，又不耐烦，懒懒的答他道：『谁要你教，不是草头底下一个来回的回字么？』孔乙己显出极高兴的样子，将两个指头的长指甲敲着柜台，点头说：『对呀对呀！……回字有四样写法，你知道么？』我愈不耐烦了，努着嘴走远。孔乙己刚用指甲蘸了酒，想在柜上写字，见我毫不热心，便又叹一口气，显出极惋惜的样子。
segs.en: At such times, I could join in the laughter, and the tavern keeper would never rebuke me. Indeed, whenever the keeper saw Kong Yiji, he would often ask him these same questions to provoke amusement. Kong Yiji knew he could not converse with the others, so he had to settle for talking to the children. Once, he said to me, "Have you been to school?" I nodded slightly. He continued, "Been to school, eh? ...Well, I shall test you. How do you write the *hui* in *huixiangdou*?" I thought to myself, a beggar like him, is he fit to test me? So I turned my face away and ignored him. Kong Yiji waited for a long time, then said quite earnestly, "Can't write it, eh? ...I will teach you. Remember! These characters are worth remembering. When you become a tavern keeper one day, you will need them for your accounts." I mused that I was still a long way from being a tavern keeper, and besides, our keeper never put *huixiangdou* on the ledger. Feeling both amused and impatient, I answered him sluggishly, "Who needs you to teach me? Isn't it just the character *hui* meaning 'return' with a grass radical on top?" Kong Yiji looked extremely pleased; he tapped on the counter with his long fingernails and nodded, saying, "That's it, that's it! ...But there are four ways to write the character *hui*. Did you know that?" My impatience grew, and I pursed my lips and walked away. Kong Yiji had just dipped his finger into his wine, intending to write on the counter, but seeing my utter lack of interest, he heaved a sigh and looked thoroughly disappointed.

segs.zh: 有几回，邻舍孩子听得笑声，也赶热闹，围住了孔乙己。他便给他们茴香豆吃，一人一颗。孩子吃完豆，仍然不散，眼睛都望着碟子。孔乙己着了慌，伸开五指将碟子罩住，弯腰下去说道：『不多了，我已经不多了。』直起身又看一看豆，自己摇头说：『不多不多！多乎哉？不多也。』于是这一群孩子都在笑声里走散了。
segs.en: Several times, when the neighborhood children heard the laughter, they would come running to join in the fun, crowding around Kong Yiji. He would give them each a fennel bean. When the children had finished their beans, they would not disperse, but kept their eyes fixed on the dish. Flustered, Kong Yiji would spread his five fingers over it, bend down, and say, "There aren't many left. I really don't have many left." He would straighten up, take another look at the beans, shake his head, and mutter, "Not many, not many! Are there many? No, not many." And so, amidst the laughter, the cluster of children would scatter.

segs.zh: 孔乙己是这样的使人快活，可是没有他，别人也便这么过。
segs.en: Kong Yiji was such a source of amusement, yet without him, life for the others went on just the same.

segs.zh: 有一天，大约是中秋前的两三天，掌柜正在慢慢的结帐，取下粉板，忽然说：『孔乙己长久没有来了。还欠十九个钱呢！』我才也觉得他的确长久没有来了。一个喝酒的人说道，『他怎么会来？……他打折了腿了。』掌柜说：『哦！』『他总仍旧是偸。这一回，是自己发昏，竟偸到丁举人家里去了。他家的东西，偸得的么？』『后来怎么样？』『怎么样？先写服辩，后来是打，打了大半夜，再打折了腿。』『后来呢？』『后来打折了腿了。』『打折了怎样呢？』『怎样？……谁晓得？许是死了。』掌柜也不再问，仍然慢慢的算他的帐。
segs.en:
One day, perhaps two or three days before the Mid-Autumn Festival, the proprietor was slowly settling his accounts. He took down the tally board and suddenly remarked, "Kong Yiji hasn’t been here for a long time. He still owes nineteen coppers!" It was only then that I, too, realized he really hadn't been around for quite a while.

A drinker spoke up: "How could he come? ...His legs were broken."

"Oh!" the proprietor said.

"He was still stealing, as usual. But this time, he must have lost his mind—he actually went to steal from the household of Scholar Ding. Is that the kind of place you can steal from?"

"And what happened then?"

"What happened? First they made him sign a confession, then they beat him. They beat him for most of the night, and finally, they broke his legs."

"And then?"

"Then his legs were broken."

"And after they were broken, what happened?"

"What happened? ...Who knows? Perhaps he’s dead."

The proprietor did not ask anything further, but went on slowly totting up his accounts.

segs.zh: 中秋过后，秋风是一天凉比一天，看看将近初冬；我整天的靠着火，也须穿上棉袄了。一天的下半天，没有一个顾客，我正合了眼坐着。忽然间听得一个声音，『温一碗酒。』这声音虽然极低，却很耳熟。看时又全没有人。站起来向外一望，那孔乙己便在柜台下对了门槛坐着。他脸上黑而且瘦，已经不成样子；穿一件破夹袄，盘着两腿，下面垫一个蒲包，用草绳在肩上挂住；见了我，又说道，『温一碗酒。』掌柜也伸出头去，一面说：『孔乙己么？你还欠十九个钱呢！』孔乙己很颓唐的仰面答道，『这……下回还清罢。这一回是现钱，酒要好。』掌柜仍然同平常一样，笑着对他说，『孔乙己，你又偸了东西了！』但他这回却不十分分辩，单说了一句『不要取笑！』『取笑？要是不偸，怎么会打断腿？』孔乙己低声说道：『跌断，跌，跌……』他的眼色，很像恳求掌柜，不要再提。此时已经聚集了几个人，便和掌柜都笑了。我温了酒，端出去，放在门槛上。他从破衣袋里摸出四文大钱，放在我手里，见他满手是泥，原来他便用这手走来的。不一会，他喝完酒，便又在旁人的说笑声中，坐着用这手慢慢走去了。
segs.en:
After the Mid-Autumn Festival, the autumn wind grew chillier with each passing day, and with early winter fast approaching, I had to spend the whole day by the stove, eventually needing to put on a padded jacket. One afternoon, when there were no customers, I sat with my eyes closed. Suddenly, I heard a voice: "Warm a bowl of wine." The voice, though very low, was familiar. I looked, but there was no one there. I stood up and looked out; Kong Yiji was sitting on the threshold, facing the door. His face was dark and gaunt—he was a wretched sight. He wore a ragged, lined jacket, his legs crossed, resting on a straw mat tied over his shoulders with a straw rope. Seeing me, he repeated, "Warm a bowl of wine."

The proprietor also poked his head out and said, "Kong Yiji? You still owe nineteen copper coins!"

Kong Yiji looked up, utterly dejected, and replied, "This... I'll pay it off next time. This time I have cash; the wine must be good."

The proprietor, as usual, laughed and said, "Kong Yiji, you’ve been stealing again, haven't you!"

This time, he didn't put up much of a defense, merely saying, "Stop mocking me!"

"Mocking? If you hadn't stolen anything, how did you get your legs broken?"

Kong Yiji whispered, "Fell... broke... fell, fell..." His eyes seemed to plead with the proprietor not to bring it up again. By now, a few people had gathered, and they all joined the proprietor in laughter.

I warmed the wine, carried it out, and set it on the threshold. He fished four coppers out of his tattered pocket and placed them in my hand. I saw that his hands were covered in mud; it turned out he had been crawling on them. Before long, he finished his wine and, amidst the laughter and taunts of the others, began to crawl away slowly on those same hands.

segs.zh: 自此以后，又长久没有看见孔乙己。到了年关，掌柜取下粉板说：『孔乙己还欠十九个钱呢！』到第二年的端午，又说：『孔乙己还欠十九个钱呢！』到中秋可是没有说，再到年关也没有看见他。
segs.en: After that, I did not see Kong Yiji for a long time. When the end of the year came, the tavern keeper took down the tally board and said, "Kong Yiji still owes nineteen coppers!" By the Dragon Boat Festival of the following year, he said again, "Kong Yiji still owes nineteen coppers!" But by the Mid-Autumn Festival, he said nothing more, and when the end of the year arrived once again, he was still nowhere to be seen.

segs.zh: 我到现在终于没有见——大约孔乙己的确死了。
segs.en: I have never seen him since—it is likely that Kong Yiji is indeed dead.

segs.zh: （一九一九年三月。）
这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
