segs.en: According to the Explanation of the Copyright Law of the People’s Republic of China, a work by a legal entity must satisfy three requirements: (1) it must be created under the auspices of a legal entity (or other organization, hereinafter referred to as "legal entity"), rather than spontaneously by its staff members; (2) its creative concept and mode of expression must embody the will of the legal entity; and (3) the legal entity, rather than the individual author, must bear responsibility for it. (Details)

segs.zh: 我从乡下跑到京城里，一转眼已经六年了。其间耳闻目睹的所谓国家大事，算起来也很不少；但在我心里，都不留什么痕迹，倘要我寻出这些事的影响来说，便只是增长了我的坏脾气，——老实说，便是教我一天比一天的看不起人。
segs.en: I have been in the capital for six years now, having come here from the countryside, and in the blink of an eye, it is already over. In that time, I have heard and seen a fair share of what they call "national affairs," but they have left no mark upon me. If I were forced to describe their impact, I would say they have only served to make me more ill-tempered—to be plain, they have taught me to hold people in greater contempt day by day.

segs.zh: 但有一件小事，却于我有意义，将我从坏脾气里拖开，使我至今忘记不得。
segs.en: Yet there was one small incident, significant to me, that pulled me out of my foul mood and which, to this day, I cannot forget.

segs.zh: 这是民国六年的冬天，大北风刮得正猛，我因为生计关系，不得不一早在路上走。一路几乎遇不见人，好容易才雇定了一辆人力车，教他拉到S门去。不一会，北风小了，路上浮尘早已刮净，剩下一条洁白的大道来，车夫也跑得更快。刚近S门，忽而车把上带著一个人，慢慢地倒了。
segs.en: It was the winter of the sixth year of the Republic. A fierce north wind was howling, and, forced by the necessity of earning a living, I had to be out on the road at daybreak. I hardly encountered a soul along the way, and it was only with great difficulty that I managed to hire a rickshaw, instructing the puller to take me to S Gate. Before long, the north wind eased. The dust had been swept clean from the road, leaving a pristine, white path, and the rickshaw man quickened his pace. Just as we neared S Gate, the shafts of the rickshaw suddenly caught a person, who slowly toppled over.

segs.zh: 跌倒的是一个女人，花白头发，衣服都很破烂。伊从马路边上突然向车前横截过来；车夫已经让开道，但伊的破棉背心没有上扣，微风吹著，向外展开，所以终于兜著车把。幸而车夫早有点停步，否则伊定要栽一个大斤斗，跌到头破血出了。
segs.en: The person who fell was a woman, with graying hair and ragged clothes. She had darted suddenly from the side of the road right into the path of the rickshaw. The puller had already swerved to avoid her, but her tattered padded vest, unbuttoned, was caught by a light breeze and flared open, snagging on the shaft of the rickshaw. Fortunately, the puller had slowed down in time; otherwise, she would certainly have taken a nasty spill and ended up with a broken head and bloodied face.

segs.zh: 伊伏在地上；车夫便也立住脚。我料定这老女人并没有伤，又没有别人看见，便很怪他多事，要自己惹出是非，也误了我的路。
segs.en: She lay sprawled on the ground, and the rickshaw puller came to a halt. I was certain that the old woman was not hurt, and since no one else was around to see, I thought him a meddlesome fool for inviting trouble and delaying my journey.

segs.zh: 我便对他说，‘没有什么的。走你的罢！’
segs.en: I said to him, "It’s nothing. Be on your way!"

segs.zh: 车夫毫不理会，——或者并没有听到，——却放下车子，扶那老女人慢慢起来，搀着臂膊立定，向伊说：
segs.en: The rickshaw puller paid no heed—or perhaps he had not heard—but lowered the shafts, helped the old woman slowly to her feet, steadied her by the arm, and said to her:

segs.zh: ‘你怎么啦？’
segs.en: What’s the matter with you?

segs.zh: ‘我摔坏了。’
segs.en: I've broken it.

segs.zh: 我想，我眼见你慢慢倒地，怎么会摔坏呢，装腔作势罢了，这真可憎恶。车夫多事，也正是自讨苦吃，现在你自己想法去。
segs.en: I thought to myself, I saw you sink to the ground slowly; how could you have possibly hurt yourself? It’s nothing but a pose, and it’s truly detestable. As for the rickshaw puller, he was just being meddlesome, asking for trouble—now he can figure out how to deal with you himself.

segs.zh: 车夫听了这老女人的话，却毫不踌躇，仍然搀著伊的臂膊，便一步一步的向前走。我有些诧异，忙看前面，是一所巡警分驻所，大风之后，外面也不见人。这车夫扶著那老女人，便正是向那大门走去。
segs.en: The rickshaw puller, hearing the old woman’s words, did not hesitate for a moment; he simply kept hold of her arm and continued to walk forward, step by step. I was somewhat taken aback and hurried to look ahead. It was a police substation, standing deserted in the aftermath of the gale. The puller, still supporting the old woman, was heading directly toward its gate.

segs.zh: 我这时突然感到一种异样的感觉，觉得他满身灰尘的后影，刹时高大了，而且愈走愈大，须仰视才见。而且他对于我，渐渐的又几乎变成一种威压，甚而至于要榨出皮袍下面藏著的‘小’来。
segs.en: At this moment, a strange sensation suddenly came over me. It seemed as if his dust-covered back had grown taller in an instant, and as he walked away, it loomed larger and larger, until I had to look up to see him. Furthermore, he began to exert a kind of pressure upon me, almost as if he were intent on squeezing the "smallness" hidden beneath my own fur-lined gown out into the open.

segs.zh: 我的活力这时大约有些凝滞了，坐着没有动，也没有想，直到看见分驻所里走出一个巡警，才下了车。
segs.en: My vitality seemed to have congealed by then; I sat there motionless, my mind a blank, and only when I saw a policeman emerge from the local station did I climb down from the rickshaw.

segs.zh: 巡警走近我说，‘你自己雇车罢，他不能拉你了。’
segs.en: The policeman walked over to me and said, "You’d better hire another rickshaw; he can’t pull you anymore."

segs.zh: 我没有思索的从外套袋里抓出一大把铜元，交给巡警，说，‘请你给他……’
segs.en: Without a second thought, I reached into my overcoat pocket, grabbed a handful of copper coins, and handed them to the policeman. "Please," I said, "give these to him..."

segs.zh: 风全住了，路上还很静。我走着，一面想，几乎怕敢想到我自己。以前的事姑且搁起，这一大把铜元又是什么意思？奖他么？我还能裁判车夫么？我不能回答自己。
segs.en: The wind had died down completely, and the road remained deathly still. I walked on, lost in thought, afraid to even contemplate my own self. Putting aside the events of the past for a moment, what was the meaning of this handful of coppers? Was I rewarding him? Was I even in a position to judge the rickshaw puller? I could not answer myself.

segs.zh: 这事到了现在，还是时时记起。我因此也时时熬了苦痛，努力的要想到我自己。几年来的文治武力，在我早如幼小时候所读过的‘子曰诗云’一般，背不上半句了。独有这一件小事，却总是浮在我眼前，有时反更分明，教我惭愧，催我自新，并且增长我的勇气和希望。
segs.en: Even now, I recall this incident from time to time. Because of it, I often endure a sense of bitterness, striving to reflect upon myself. The political and military upheavals of these past few years have become to me like the classical texts I recited in my childhood—I cannot recall a single line of them. Only this one small event remains, always floating before my eyes. At times, it appears even more vivid, shaming me, urging me toward self-renewal, and imbuing me with newfound courage and hope.

segs.zh: （一九二〇年七月。）初次发表时无此文，为《呐喊》编集时所加。
segs.en: (July, 1920.) This piece was not included in the original publication; it was added when compiling Call to Arms.

segs.zh: 这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may also be in the public domain in countries and regions where the copyright term is longer for domestic works but shorter for foreign works.

segs.zh: 星期日的早晨，我揭去一张隔夜的日历，向着新的那一张上看了又看的说：
segs.en: On Sunday morning, I tore off a page of yesterday’s calendar and, gazing intently at the fresh one underneath, said:

segs.zh: ‘阿，十月十日，——今天原来正是双十节。这里却一点没有记载！’
segs.en: Ah, the tenth of October—so today is actually the Double Tenth Festival. And yet, there isn’t a single mention of it here!

segs.zh: 我的一位前辈先生Ｎ，正走到我的寓里来谈闲天，一听这话，便很不高兴的对我说：
segs.en: An elder of mine, Mr. N, who happened to be visiting my lodgings for a bit of idle talk, heard this and, looking quite displeased, said to me:

segs.zh: ‘他们对！他们不记得，你怎样他；你记得，又怎样呢！’
segs.en: They are right! They don't remember how you treated them; but you—you remember, and what does that change!

segs.zh: 这位Ｎ先生本来脾气有点乖张，时常生些无谓的气，说些不通世故的话。当这时候，我大抵任他自言自语，不赞一辞；他独自发完议论，也就算了。
segs.en: This Mr. N was by nature a bit eccentric, often flying into pointless rages and spouting remarks that showed a complete lack of worldly wisdom. Whenever he carried on like this, I generally left him to talk to himself, not offering a single word of agreement; once he had finished venting his opinions, that was usually the end of it.

segs.zh: 他说：
segs.en: He said:

segs.zh: ‘我最佩服北京双十节的情形。早晨，警察到门，吩咐道“挂旗。”“是，挂旗！”各家大半懒洋洋的踱出一个国民来，撅起一块斑驳陆离的洋布。这样一直到夜，——收了旗关门；几家偶然忘却的，便挂到第二天的上午。
segs.en: What I admire most is the way the Double Tenth is celebrated in Beijing. In the morning, the police come to the door and announce, "Hoist the flags!" "Yes, hoist the flags!" comes the reply. From most houses, a citizen will saunter out listlessly and hoist a piece of mottled, foreign-made bunting. And so it goes until nightfall, when the flags are taken down and the doors are shut. For those few who happen to forget, the flags simply remain hanging until the following morning.

segs.zh: ‘他们忘却了纪念，纪念也忘却了他们！
segs.en: They have forgotten the commemoration, and the commemoration has forgotten them!

segs.zh: ‘我也是忘却了纪念的一个人。倘使纪念起来，那第一个双十节前后的事，便都上我的心头，使我坐立不稳了。
segs.en: I, too, am one who has forgotten how to commemorate. Yet, if I were to begin remembering, all that happened around the time of that first Double Tenth would flood my mind, leaving me unable to sit or stand in peace.

segs.zh: ‘多少故人的脸，都浮在我眼前。几个少年辛苦奔走了十多年，暗地里一颗弹丸要了他的性命；几个少年一击不中，在监牢里身受一个多月的苦刑；几个少年怀着远志，忽然踪影全无，连尸首也不知那里去了。——
segs.en: So many faces of old friends drift before my eyes. There were those youths who toiled and struggled for more than a decade, only to have their lives snuffed out by a single secret bullet; others who failed in a single strike and endured over a month of torture in prison; and still others, fired by lofty ideals, who suddenly vanished without a trace, their very corpses lost to the world.

segs.zh: ‘他们都在社会的冷笑恶骂迫害倾陷里过了一生；现在他们的坟墓也早在忘却里渐渐平塌下去了。
segs.en: They lived out their lives amidst the sneers, abuse, persecution, and entrapment of society; now, their graves have long since begun to crumble away into oblivion.

segs.zh: ‘我不堪纪念这些事。
segs.en: I cannot bear to commemorate these things.

segs.zh: ‘我们还是记起一点得意的事来谈谈罢。’
segs.en: Let us recall something to our credit and talk about that instead.

segs.zh: Ｎ忽然现出笑容，伸手在自己头上一摸，高声说：
segs.en: N suddenly broke into a smile, reached up to stroke his own head, and said in a loud voice:

segs.zh: ‘我最得意的是自从第一个双十节以后，我在路上走不再被人笑骂了。
segs.en: What pleased me most was that, ever since the first Double Tenth Festival, I was no longer laughed at or cursed at when I walked along the street.

segs.zh: ‘老兄，你可知道头发是我们中国人的宝贝和冤家，古今来多少人在这上头吃些毫无价值的苦呵！
segs.en: My dear fellow, surely you know that hair is both the treasure and the bane of us Chinese? Think of all the people, past and present, who have suffered so pointlessly on its account!

segs.zh: ‘我们的很古的古人，对于头发似乎也还看轻。据刑法看来，最要紧的自然是脑袋，所以大辟是上刑；次要便是生殖器了，所以宫刑和幽闭也是一件吓人的罚；至于髡，那是微乎其微了；然而推想起来，正不知道曾有多少人们因为光着头皮便被社会践踏了一生世。
segs.en: Our very ancient ancestors, it seems, did not set much store by hair. To judge by the penal code, the most vital part was naturally the head, which is why decapitation was the supreme punishment; the next in importance were the genitals, which is why castration and sequestration were also terrifying penalties; as for shaving the head, that was a trifle of the smallest consequence. Yet, when one stops to reflect, there is no telling how many people have had their entire lives trampled into the dust by society simply for being bareheaded.

segs.zh: ‘我们讲革命的时，候，大谈什么扬州十日，嘉定屠城，其实也不过一种手段；老实说：那时中国人的反抗，何尝因为亡国，只是因为拖辫子。
segs.en: When we talk about revolution, we make a great show of discussing the Ten Days of Yangzhou or the massacre at Jiading, but in truth, that is merely a rhetorical device. To be perfectly honest, the resistance of the Chinese people at that time had nothing to do with the fall of their country; it was solely a matter of their queues.

segs.zh: ‘顽民杀尽了，遗老都寿终了，辫子早留定了，洪、杨又闹起来了。我的祖母曾对我说，那时做百姓才难哩，全留着头发的被官兵杀，还是辫子的便被长毛杀！
segs.en: The obstinate subjects have all been slaughtered, the remnants of the old regime have all died of old age, the queue has long since been mandated, and now the Hong and Yang rebels are stirring again. My grandmother once told me that back then it was truly difficult to be a commoner: those who kept their hair full-length were killed by the government troops, and those who wore queues were killed by the Long-haired rebels!

segs.zh: ‘我不知道有多少中国人只因为这不痛不痒的头发而吃苦，受难，灭亡。’
segs.en: I do not know how many Chinese people have suffered, been tormented, or perished, all on account of this trivial, inconsequential queue.

segs.zh: Ｎ两眼望着屋梁，似乎想些事，仍然说：
segs.en: N stared at the rafters, as if lost in thought, and said once more:

segs.zh: ‘谁知道头发的苦轮到我了。
segs.en: Who would have thought it was my turn to suffer the wheel of fate regarding my hair.

segs.zh: ‘我出去留学，便剪掉了辫子，这并没有别的奥妙，只为他太不便当罢了。不料有几位辫子盘在头顶上的同学们便很厌恶我，监督也大怒，说要停了我的官费，送回中国去。
segs.en: When I went abroad to study, I cut off my queue. There was no profound mystery in this; it was simply too inconvenient. To my surprise, several fellow students who wore their queues coiled atop their heads were deeply offended, and the supervisor flew into a rage, threatening to revoke my government stipend and ship me back to China.

segs.zh: ‘不几天，这位监督却自己被人剪去辫子逃走了。去剪的人们里面，一个便是做革命军的邹容，这人也因此不能再留学，回到上海来，后来死在西牢里。你也早已忘却了罢？
segs.en: A few days later, however, this supervisor himself had his queue cut off and fled. Among those who did the cutting was Zou Rong, who had joined the revolutionary army. Because of this, he could no longer continue his studies abroad; he returned to Shanghai and later died in a Western prison. You have long since forgotten that, haven't you?

segs.zh: ‘过了几年，我的家景大不如前了，非谋点事做便要受饿，只得也回到中国来。我一到上海，便买定一条假辫子，那时是二元的市价，带着回家。我的母亲倒也不说什么，然而旁人一见面，便都首先研究这辫子，待到知道是假，就一声冷笑，将我拟为杀头的罪名；有一位本家，还豫备去告官，但后来因为恐怕革命党的造反或者要成功，这才中止了。
segs.en: A few years later, my family’s fortunes had declined significantly; unless I found some way to make a living, I would face starvation, so I had no choice but to return to China. The moment I arrived in Shanghai, I bought a fake queue—the market price was two dollars at the time—and wore it home. My mother didn’t say anything, but whenever others saw me, their first order of business was to examine this queue. Once they discovered it was fake, they would let out a cold sneer and judge me as worthy of decapitation. One of my kinsmen even prepared to report me to the authorities, though he eventually desisted, fearing that the revolutionaries might actually succeed in their uprising.

segs.zh: ‘我想，假的不如真的直截爽快，我便索性废了假辫子，穿着西装在街上走。
segs.en: I thought that a fake is never as straightforward or satisfying as the real thing, so I simply discarded the fake queue and walked down the street in a Western suit.

segs.zh: ‘一路走去，一路便是笑骂的声音，有的还跟在后面骂：“这冒失鬼！”“假洋鬼子！”
segs.en: As I walked along, the sound of jeers and curses followed me the whole way. Some even trailed behind, shouting, "You reckless fool!" and "Fake foreign devil!"

segs.zh: ‘我于是不穿洋服了，改了大衫，他们骂得更厉害。
segs.en: So I stopped wearing Western-style clothes and switched to a long gown, which only made their insults more venomous.

segs.zh: ‘在这日暮途穷的时候，我的手里才添出一支手杖来，拼命的打了几回，他们渐渐的不骂了。只是走到没有打过的生地方还是骂。
segs.en: It was only when I reached this dead end that I finally added a walking stick to my arsenal. After I had thrashed them a few times for all I was worth, they gradually stopped their cursing. It was only when I wandered into unfamiliar territory where I hadn’t yet struck anyone that they would start again.

segs.zh: ‘这件事很使我悲哀，至今还时时记得哩。我在留学的时候，曾经看见日报上登载一个游历南洋和中国的本多博士的事；这位博士是不懂中国和马来语的，人问他，你不懂话，怎么走路呢？他拿起手杖来说，这便是他们的话，他们都懂！我因此气愤了好几天，谁知道我竟不知不觉的自己也做了，而且那些人都懂了。……
segs.en: This affair causes me great sorrow, and even now I remember it from time to time. When I was studying abroad, I once saw a report in a daily newspaper about a Dr. Honda who had traveled through the South Seas and China. This doctor understood neither Chinese nor Malay. When people asked him, "If you don't understand the language, how do you get around?" he picked up his walking stick and said, "This is their language; they all understand it!" I was indignant about this for several days, yet who could have known that, all unawares, I would end up doing the very same thing, and that those people would understand it too. ...

segs.zh: ‘宣统初年，我在本地的中学校做监学，同事是避之惟恐不远，官僚是防之惟恐不严，我终日如坐在冰窖子里，如站在刑场旁边，其实并非别的，只因为缺少了一条辫子！
segs.en: In the early years of the Xuantong reign, I served as a proctor at a local middle school. My colleagues shunned me as if I were a plague, and the bureaucrats guarded against me as if I were a hardened criminal. All day long, I felt as though I were sitting in an ice cellar or standing at the edge of an execution ground. And the reason for all this? It was nothing more than the lack of a pigtail.

segs.zh: ‘有一日，几个学生忽然走到我的房里来，说：“先生，我们要剪辫子了。”我说，“不行！”“有辫子好呢，没有辫子好呢？”“没有辫子好……”“你怎么说不行呢？”“犯不上，你们还是不剪上算，——等一等罢。”他们不说什么，撅着嘴唇走出房去；然而终于剪掉了。
segs.en:
One day, several students suddenly came into my room and said, "Sir, we are going to cut off our queues."

"No, you mustn't!" I said.

"Which is better, having a queue or not having one?"

"Not having one is better..."

"Then why do you say we mustn't?"

"It’s not worth the trouble. You’d be better off not cutting them—just wait a while."

They said nothing, but walked out of the room with their lips pursed. Yet, in the end, they cut them off anyway.

segs.zh: ‘呵！不得了了，人言啧啧了；我却只装作不知道，一任他们光着头皮，和许多辫子一齐上讲堂。
segs.en: Ha! It’s become intolerable, the gossip is incessant; yet I simply pretend to be unaware, letting them go about bare-headed, right into the lecture hall alongside all those queues.

segs.zh: ‘然而这剪辫病传染了；第三天，师范学堂的学生忽然也剪下了六条辫子，晚上便开除了六个学生。这六个人，留校不能，回家不得，一直挨到第一个双十节之后又一个多月，才消去了犯罪的火烙印。
segs.en: However, this hair-cutting contagion spread; on the third day, six students from the normal school suddenly cut off their queues, and by that evening, all six had been expelled. These six men, unable to remain at school and forbidden from returning home, lingered in limbo until more than a month after the first Double Tenth festival, when at last the incriminating brand of their crime began to fade.

segs.zh: ‘我呢？也一样，只是元年冬天到北京，还被人骂过几次，后来骂我的人也被警察剪去了辫子，我就不再被人辱骂了；但我没有到乡间去。’
segs.en: And I? It was much the same. I only went to Beijing in the winter of the first year of the Republic, where I was abused a few times myself. Later, the man who abused me had his queue cut off by the police, and after that, I was no longer insulted; but I never did go to the countryside.

segs.zh: Ｎ显出非常得意模样，忽而又沉下脸来：
segs.en: N assumed a look of great self-satisfaction, then suddenly let his face fall.

segs.zh: ‘现在你们这些理想家，又在那里嚷什么女子剪发了，又要造出许多毫无所得而痛苦的人！
segs.en: And now you idealists are back at it again, clamoring about women cutting their hair, just to create more miserable souls who gain absolutely nothing for their trouble!

segs.zh: ‘现在不是已经有剪掉头发的女人，因此考不进学校去，或者被学校除了名么？
segs.en: Aren't there women nowadays who have cut their hair, only to be denied admission to schools or expelled from them as a result?

segs.zh: ‘改革么，武器在那里？工读么，工厂在那里？
segs.en: As for reform, where are the weapons? As for work-study, where are the factories?

segs.zh: ‘仍然留起，嫁给人家做媳妇去：忘却了一切还是幸福，倘使伊记着些平等自由的话便要苦痛一生世！’
segs.en: Still, let her keep it, and marry into someone’s family to be a daughter-in-law: to forget everything is still a form of happiness; if she were to remember any of those words about equality and freedom, she would be miserable for the rest of her life!

segs.zh: ‘我要借了阿尔志跋绥夫的话问你们：你们将黄金时代的出现豫约给这些人们的子孙了，但有什么给这些人们自己呢？
segs.en: I shall borrow the words of Artsybashev to ask you: you have promised the arrival of a golden age to the descendants of these people, but what have you given to the people themselves?

segs.zh: ‘阿，造物的皮鞭没有到中国的脊梁上时，中国便永远是这一样的中国，决不肯自己改变一枝毫毛！
segs.en: Ah, until the lash of the Creator falls upon the spine of China, China will remain forever the same China, never willing to change so much as a single hair!

segs.zh: ‘你们的嘴里既然并无毒牙，何以偏要在额上贴起“蝮蛇”两个大字，引乞丐来打杀？……’
segs.en: Since you have no fangs in your mouths, why must you insist on plastering the characters for 'Viper' on your foreheads, only to invite every beggar to come and beat you to death?...

segs.zh: Ｎ愈说愈离奇了，但一见到我不很愿听的神情，便立刻闭了口，站起来取帽子。
segs.en: N went on with increasingly bizarre tales, but the moment he caught the look of reluctance on my face, he fell silent at once and stood up to reach for his hat.

segs.zh: 我说，‘回去么？’
segs.en: I asked, “Are you going back?”

segs.zh: 他答道，‘是的，天要下雨了。’
segs.en: He replied, “Yes, it is going to rain.”

segs.zh: 我默默的送他到门口。
segs.en: I saw him to the door in silence.

segs.zh: 他戴上帽子说：
segs.en: He put on his hat and said:

segs.zh: ‘再见！请你恕我打搅，好在明天便不是双十节，我们统可以忘却了。’
segs.en: Goodbye! Pray forgive my intrusion; fortunately, tomorrow is no longer Double Tenth, and we can all afford to forget it.

segs.zh: （一九二〇年十月。）
segs.en: (October, 1920.)

segs.zh: 这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may still be under copyright in its country or region of origin where the copyright term is longer, but it is in the public domain in those countries and regions that apply the rule of the shorter term to foreign works.

segs.zh: 据《中华人民共和国著作权法释义》，法人作品应满足三点要求：（1）由法人（或其他组织，以下简称“法人”）主持创作，而非工作人员自发进行；（2）创作思想及表达方式体现法人意志；（3）由法人负责，而非执笔者。（详情）
segs.en: According to the Interpretation of the Copyright Law of the People’s Republic of China, a work produced by a legal entity must satisfy three requirements: (1) it is created under the direction of a legal entity (or other organization, hereinafter referred to as "legal entity"), rather than being the spontaneous endeavor of individual staff members; (2) the creative ideas and modes of expression reflect the will of the legal entity; and (3) the legal entity, rather than the individual author, assumes responsibility for the work. (Details)

segs.zh: 临河的土场上，太阳渐渐的收了他通黄的光线了。场边靠河的乌桕树叶，干巴巴的才喘过气来，几个花脚蚊子在下面哼着飞舞。面河的农家的烟突里，逐渐减少了炊烟，女人孩子们都在自己门口的土场上泼些水，放下小桌子和矮凳；人知道，这已经是晚饭时候了。
segs.en: On the threshing ground by the river, the sun was gradually withdrawing its golden light. The leaves of the tallow trees lining the bank, parched and brittle, seemed finally to catch their breath, while a few mottled mosquitoes hummed as they danced beneath them. From the chimneys of the farmhouses facing the water, the cooking smoke thinned and faded. Women and children began splashing water onto the earth before their doorways, setting out small tables and low stools; everyone knew it was time for the evening meal.

segs.zh: 老人男人坐在矮凳上，摇着大芭蕉扇闲谈，孩子飞也似的跑，或者蹲在乌桕树下赌玩石子。女人端出乌黑的蒸干菜和松花黄的米饭，热篷蓬冒烟。河里驶过文人的酒船，文豪见了，大发诗兴，说：『无思无虑，这真是田家乐呵！』
segs.en: Old men sat on low stools, waving large plantain-leaf fans and chatting idly, while children darted about like arrows or crouched beneath the tallow trees, gambling with pebbles. Women brought out bowls of steaming, coal-black preserved vegetables and pine-pollen-yellow rice, the heat rising in thick, fragrant clouds. A scholar’s pleasure boat glided down the river; seeing this, the man of letters was overcome by a sudden poetic urge and remarked, "Carefree and untroubled—what a true pastoral delight!"

segs.zh: 但文豪的话有些不合事实，就因为他们没有听到九斤老太的话。这时候，九斤老太正在大怒。拿破芭蕉扇敲着凳脚说：
segs.en: But the words of the great writers were not entirely in accordance with the facts, simply because they had never heard the words of Old Lady Jiujin. At that moment, Old Lady Jiujin was in a towering rage. Thumping the leg of her stool with a tattered palm-leaf fan, she said:

segs.zh: 『我活到七十九岁了，活够了，不愿意眼见这些败家相，——还是死的好。立刻就要吃饭了，还吃炒豆子，吃穷了一家子！』
segs.en: I’ve lived to be seventy-nine, and I’ve lived long enough. I have no desire to witness such ruinous behavior—it would be better to die. Dinner is about to be served, yet you’re still eating fried beans! You’ll eat this whole family into poverty!

segs.zh: 伊的曾孙女儿六斤捏着一把豆，正从对面跑来，见这情形，便直奔河边，藏在乌桕树后，伸出双丫角的小头，大声说，『这老不死的！』
segs.en: Her great-granddaughter, Liujin, clutching a handful of beans, came running from across the way. Seeing this, she bolted straight for the riverbank, ducked behind a Chinese tallow tree, and, poking her little head out with its two tufts of hair, shouted at the top of her voice, "The old bag of bones!"

segs.zh: 九斤老太虽然高寿，耳朵却还不很聋，但也没有听到孩子的话，仍旧自己说，『这真是一代不如一代！』
segs.en: Although Old Mrs. Jiujin was of a great age, her hearing was not yet very dull; yet she did not hear the child’s words, and continued to mutter to herself, "It is true—each generation is worse than the last!"

segs.zh: 这村庄的习惯有点特别，女人生下孩子，多喜欢用秤称了轻重，便用斤数当作小名。九斤老太自从庆祝了五十大寿以后，便渐渐的变了不平家，常说伊年青的时候，天气没有现在这般热，豆子也没有现在这般硬：总之现在的时世是不对了。何况六斤比伊的曾祖，少了三斤，比伊父亲七斤，又少了一斤，这真是一条颠扑不破的实例。所以伊又用劲说，『这真是一代不如一代！』
segs.en: The customs of this village were somewhat peculiar: when a woman gave birth, she liked to weigh the infant and use the number of jin as a nickname. Ever since Old Mrs. Jiujin celebrated her fiftieth birthday, she had gradually become a malcontent. She would often say that when she was young, the weather was never as hot as it was now, and the beans were never as hard: in short, the present times were all wrong. What was more, Liujin weighed three jin less than her great-grandmother, and one jin less than her father, Qijin—this was, to her, an irrefutable example. And so she would insist, with great vehemence, "It is truly a case of each generation being worse than the last!"

segs.zh: 伊的儿媳七斤嫂子正捧着饭篮走到桌边，便将饭篮在桌上一摔，愤愤的说，『你老人家又这么说了。六斤生下来的时候，不是六斤五两么？你家的秤又是私秤，加重称，十八两秤；用了准十六，我们的六斤该有七斤多哩。我想便是太公和公公，也不见得正是九斤八斤十足，用的秤也许是十四两。……』
segs.en: Qijin’s wife, his daughter-in-law, was just carrying a rice basket to the table when she slammed it down and said indignantly, "There you go again, old man. When Liujin was born, didn’t she weigh six catties and five ounces? And your scale is a private one, a heavy-duty eighteen-ounce scale; if we used a standard sixteen-ounce one, our Liujin would weigh more than seven catties. I suspect that even in the days of your grandfather and father, people weren't exactly nine or eight catties to the mark—they probably used fourteen-ounce scales back then, too..."

segs.zh: 『一代不如一代！』
segs.en: Each generation is worse than the last!

segs.zh: 七斤嫂还没有答话，忽然看见七斤从小巷口转出，便移了方向，对他嚷道，『你这死尸怎么这时候才回来，死到那里去了！不管人家等着你开饭！』
segs.en: Before Qijin’s wife could answer, she suddenly caught sight of Qijin turning the corner from the alleyway. Shifting her focus, she shrieked at him, "You walking corpse, why are you only just getting back? Where the devil have you been dying all this time? Don't you care that people are waiting for you to open the rice pot!"

segs.zh: 七斤虽然住在农村，却早有些飞黄腾达的意思。从他的祖父到他，三代不捏锄头柄了；他也照例的帮人撑着航船，每日一回，早晨从鲁镇进城，傍晚又回到鲁镇，因此很知道些时事：例如什么地方，雷公劈死了蜈蚣精；什么地方，闺女生了一个夜叉之类。他在村人里面，的确已经是一名出场人物了。但夏天吃饭不点灯，却还守着农家习惯，所以回家太迟，是该骂的。
segs.en: Although Qijin lived in the countryside, he had long shown signs of rising in the world. For three generations, from his grandfather down to him, no one had touched a hoe handle. He, too, followed the custom of plying a ferryboat for others, making the trip once a day: into the city from Lu Town in the morning, and back to Lu Town by evening. Because of this, he was quite well-informed about current events—such as where a thunderbolt had struck a centipede spirit dead, or where a girl had given birth to a yaksha. Among the villagers, he was indeed a person of some standing. Yet, in the summer, he still followed the peasant habit of eating dinner without a lamp, so if he returned home too late, he was bound to be scolded.

segs.zh: 七斤一手捏着象牙嘴白铜斗六尺多长的湘妃竹烟管，低着头，慢慢地走来，坐在矮凳上。六斤也趁势溜出，坐在他身边，叫他爹爹。七斤没有应。
segs.en: Qijin walked over slowly, head bowed, clutching a six-foot-long Xiangfei bamboo pipe with an ivory mouthpiece and a white-copper bowl. He sat down on a low stool. Liujin seized the opportunity to slip out and sit beside him, calling him "Father." Qijin did not answer.

segs.zh: 『一代不如一代！』九斤老太说。
segs.en: "Each generation is worse than the last!" said Nine-Pounder.

segs.zh: 七斤慢慢地抬起头来，叹一口气说，『皇帝坐了龙庭了。』
segs.en: Qijin slowly raised his head, sighed, and said, "The Emperor is back on the Dragon Throne."

segs.zh: 七斤嫂呆了一刻，忽而恍然大悟的道，『这可好了，这不是又要皇恩大赦了么！』
segs.en: Seventh Sister-in-law stood dazed for a moment, then suddenly, as if enlightened, she exclaimed, "Well, isn't this wonderful! Doesn't this mean there’s going to be another imperial amnesty?"

segs.zh: 七斤又叹一口气，说，『我没有辫子。』
segs.en: Qijin sighed again and said, "I have no queue."

segs.zh: 『皇帝要辫子么？』
segs.en: Does the Emperor want pigtails?

segs.zh: 『皇帝要辫子。』
segs.en: The Emperor wants pigtails.

segs.zh: 『你怎么知道呢？』七斤嫂有些着急，赶忙的问。
segs.en: "How do you know that?" Mrs. Qijin asked, her voice tight with anxiety.

segs.zh: 『咸亨酒店里的人，都说要的。』
segs.en: Everyone at the Xianheng Tavern says it must be done.

segs.zh: 七斤嫂这时从直觉上觉得事情似乎有些不妙了，因为咸亨酒店是消息灵通的所在。伊一转眼瞥见七斤的光头，便忍不住动怒，怪他恨他怨他；忽然又绝望起来，装好一碗饭，搡在七斤的面前道，『还是赶快吃你的饭罢！哭丧着脸，就会长出辫子来么？』
segs.en: Mrs. Qijin sensed intuitively that something was amiss, for the Xianheng Tavern was a place where news traveled fast. Catching sight of Qijin’s shaven head, she could not help but flare up with resentment, blaming and loathing him. Then, suddenly overcome by despair, she dished out a bowl of rice, shoved it in front of him, and snapped, "Just hurry up and eat your rice! Will pulling a long face grow your queue back?"

segs.zh: 太阳收尽了他最末的光线了，水面暗暗地回复过凉气来；土场上一片碗筷声响，人人的脊梁上又都吐出汗粒。七斤嫂吃完三碗饭，偶然抬起头，心坎里便禁不住突突地发跳。伊透过乌桕叶，看见又矮又胖的赵七爷正从独木桥上走来，而且穿着宝蓝色竹布的长衫。
segs.en: The sun had withdrawn its final rays, and a cool dampness began to rise from the water. On the threshing floor, the clatter of bowls and chopsticks filled the air, and beads of sweat broke out once more along everyone’s spine. After finishing her third bowl of rice, Mrs. Qijin happened to look up, and her heart gave an involuntary jolt. Through the leaves of the tallow tree, she saw Master Zhao—short and stout—striding across the footbridge, wearing a long gown of sapphire-blue nankeen.

segs.zh: 赵七爷是邻村茂源酒店的主人，又是这三十里方圆以内的唯一的出色人物兼学问家；因为有学问，所以又有些遗老的臭味。他有十多本金圣叹批评的三国志，时常坐着一个字一个字的读；他不但能说出五虎将姓名，甚而至于还知道黄忠表字汉升和马超表字孟起。革命以后，他便将辫子盘在顶上，像道士一般；常常叹息说，倘若赵子龙在世，天下便不会乱到这地步了。七斤嫂眼睛好，早望见今天的赵七爷已经不是道士，却变成光滑头皮，乌黑发顶；伊便知道这一定是皇帝坐了龙庭，而且一定须有辫子，而且七斤一定是非常危险。因为赵七爷的这件竹布长衫，轻易是不常穿的，三年以来，只穿过两次；一次是和他呕气的麻子阿四病了的时候，一次是曾经砸烂他酒店的鲁大爷死了的时候；现在是第三次了，这一定又是于他有庆，于他的仇家有殃了。
segs.en: Seventh Master Zhao was the proprietor of the Maoyuan Tavern in the neighboring village and, within a radius of thirty li, the only person of any real standing or erudition. Because he was a man of learning, he possessed a certain stench of the old imperial loyalist. He owned a dozen or so volumes of the *Romance of the Three Kingdoms* with commentaries by Jin Shengtan, which he would often sit and read, word by word. Not only could he recite the names of the Five Tiger Generals, he even knew that Huang Zhong’s courtesy name was Hansheng and that Ma Chao’s was Mengqi. After the revolution, he had coiled his queue atop his head like a Daoist priest, frequently sighing that if Zhao Zilong were still alive, the world would never have fallen into such chaos. Qijin’s wife, having sharp eyes, spotted from afar that today’s Seventh Master Zhao was no longer a Daoist; his scalp was smooth and his pate glistening black. She knew at once that the Emperor must have ascended the dragon throne, that queues were once again mandatory, and that Qijin was in grave danger. For Seventh Master Zhao did not wear his bamboo-cloth gown lightly; in the past three years, he had worn it only twice: once when Pockmarked Asi, with whom he had quarreled, fell ill, and once when Master Lu, who had once smashed up his tavern, died. This was the third time; it surely meant a joyous occasion for him, and calamity for his enemies.

segs.zh: 七斤嫂记得，两年前七斤喝醉了酒，曾经骂过赵七爷是『贱胎』，所以这时便立刻直觉到七斤的危险，心坎里突突地发起跳来。
segs.en: Mrs. Qijin remembered that two years ago, when Qijin was drunk, he had called Master Zhao a "bastard." The realization of Qijin’s peril struck her instantly, and her heart began to thump against her ribs.

segs.zh: 赵七爷一路走来，坐着吃饭的人都站起身，拿筷子点着自己的饭碗说，『七爷，请在我们这里用饭！』七爷也一路点头，说道『请请』，却一径走到七斤家的桌旁。七斤们连忙招呼，七爷也微笑着说『请请』，一面细细的研究他们的饭菜。
segs.en: As Seventh Master Zhao walked by, the diners all rose from their seats, pointing at their own bowls with their chopsticks and calling out, "Seventh Master, do us the honor of dining with us!" Seventh Master nodded all the way, murmuring, "Please, please," but he walked straight to the table where Qijin’s family sat. The Qijins hurried to greet him, and Seventh Master, still smiling, said, "Please, please," while peering closely at the food on their table.

segs.zh: 『好香的干菜，——听到了风声了么？』赵七爷站在七斤的后面七斤嫂的对面说。
segs.en: "What fragrant dried vegetables—have you heard the latest news?" Seventh Master Zhao said, standing behind Qijin and facing Qijin’s wife.

segs.zh: 『皇帝坐了龙庭了。』七斤说。
segs.en: "The Emperor has ascended the Dragon Throne," Qijin said.

segs.zh: 七斤嫂看着七爷的脸，竭力陪笑道，『皇帝已经坐了龙庭，几时皇恩大赦呢？』
segs.en: Seventh Sister-in-law peered into Seventh Master’s face, forcing a smile as she asked, "Now that the Emperor has ascended the Dragon Throne, when will the imperial amnesty be declared?"

segs.zh: 『皇恩大赦？——大赦是慢慢的总要大赦罢。』七爷说到这里，声色忽然严厉起来，『但是你家七斤的辫子呢，辫子？这倒是要紧的事。你们知道：长毛时候，留发不留头，留头不留发，……』
segs.en: A general amnesty? Well, an amnesty will come eventually, I suppose." At this point, Seventh Master’s tone suddenly sharpened. "But what about your Qijin’s queue, his queue? That is the real matter of importance. You know the saying: in the days of the Long-hairs, it was 'keep your hair and lose your head, or keep your head and lose your hair'...

segs.zh: 七斤和他的女人没有读过书，不很懂得这古典的奥妙，但觉得有学问的七爷这么说，事情自然非常重大，无可挽回，便仿佛受了死刑宣告似的，耳朵里嗡的一声，再也说不出一句话。
segs.en: Qijin and his wife were illiterate and did not quite grasp the profound mysteries of the classics, but since Master Seventh, a man of learning, spoke with such authority, they felt the matter must be grave and beyond remedy. It was as if a death sentence had been passed upon them; a buzzing filled their ears, and they could not utter another word.

segs.zh: 『一代不如一代，——』九斤老太正在不平，趁这机会，便对赵七爷说，『现在的长毛，只是剪人家的辫子，僧不僧，道不道的。从前的长毛，这样的么？我活到七十九岁了，活够了。从前的长毛是——整匹的红缎子裹头，拖下去，拖下去，一直拖到脚跟；王爷是黄缎子，拖下去，黄缎子；红缎子，黄缎子，——我活够了，七十九岁了。』
segs.en: Each generation is worse than the last," Old Lady Jiujin grumbled, taking this opportunity to say to Seventh Master Zhao, "These present-day Longhairs do nothing but snip off people’s queues, making them look like neither monks nor Taoists. Was it like this in the days of the old Longhairs? I’ve lived to be seventy-nine, and I’ve lived long enough. The old Longhairs—they wrapped their heads in whole bolts of red satin, trailing down, trailing down, all the way to their heels; the princes used yellow satin, trailing down, yellow satin; red satin, yellow satin—I’ve lived long enough, seventy-nine years.

segs.zh: 七斤嫂站起身，自言自语的说，『这怎么好呢？这样的一班老小，都靠他养活的人，……』
segs.en: Mrs. Qijin stood up and muttered to herself, "What is to be done now? A whole houseful of young and old, all of them depending on him for their keep..."

segs.zh: 赵七爷摇头道，『那也没法。没有辫子，该当何罪，书上都一条一条明明白白写着的。不管他家里有些甚么人。』
segs.en: Seventh Master Zhao shook his head and said, "That can't be helped. The penalty for not having a queue is written down clearly, point by point, in the books. It doesn't matter who he might have at home."

segs.zh: 七斤嫂听到书上写着，可真是完全绝望了；自己急得没法，便忽然又恨到七斤。伊用筷子指着他的鼻尖说，『这死尸自作自受！造反的时候，我本来说，不要撑船了，不要上城了。他偏要死进城去，滚进城去，进城便被人剪去了辫子。从前是绢光乌黑的辫子，现在弄得僧不僧道不道的。这囚徒自作自受，带累了我们又怎么说呢？这活死尸的囚徒……』
segs.en: When Seventh Sister-in-law heard what was written in the books, she fell into absolute despair. Frantic and helpless, she suddenly turned her hatred toward Seventh Pounder. Pointing a chopstick at the tip of his nose, she cried, "This corpse brought this on himself! When the uprising started, I told him, 'Don't go punting the boat, don't go into town.' But he had to go die in town, had to go roll into town, and once there, he got his queue cut off. He used to have a thick, glossy black queue, and now he looks like neither monk nor priest. This prisoner brought it on himself, but what about the trouble he’s dragged us into? This living corpse, this prisoner..."

segs.zh: 村人看见赵七爷到村，都赶紧吃完饭，聚在七斤家饭桌的周围。七斤自己知道是出场人物，被女人当大众这样辱骂，很不雅观，便只得抬起头，慢慢地说道：
segs.en: When the villagers saw Seventh Master Zhao arrive, they hurried through their meals and gathered around the table in Qijin’s house. Qijin, well aware that he was the center of attention and finding it rather unseemly to be berated by his wife in front of the whole crowd, had no choice but to lift his head and say slowly:

segs.zh: 『你今天说现成话，那时你……』
segs.en: You’re talking big now, but back then you...

segs.zh: 『你这活死尸的囚徒……』
segs.en: You living corpse of a prisoner...

segs.zh: 看客中间，八一嫂是心肠最好的人，抱着伊的两周岁的遗腹子，正在七斤嫂身边看热闹；这时过意不去，连忙解劝说，『七斤嫂，算了罢。人不是神仙，谁知道未来事呢？便是七斤嫂，那时不也说，没有辫子倒也没有什么丑么？况且衙门里的大老爷也还没有告示。……』
segs.en: Among the onlookers, Sister Bayi was the kindest-hearted; she was standing beside Sister Qijin, holding her two-year-old posthumous son and watching the excitement. Feeling a bit uneasy, she hurriedly stepped in to mediate: "Let it be, Sister Qijin. People aren't gods; who can foresee the future? Why, even you yourself said back then that there was nothing so ugly about not having a queue. Besides, the bigwigs at the yamen haven't issued any notices yet..."

segs.zh: 七斤嫂没有听完，两个耳朵早通红了；便将筷子转过向来，指着八一嫂的鼻子，说，『阿呀，这是什么话呵！八一嫂，我自己看来倒还是一个人，会说出这样昏诞胡涂话么？那时我是，整整哭了三天，谁都看见；连六斤这小鬼也都哭，……』六斤刚吃完一大碗饭，拿了空碗，伸手去嚷着要添。七斤嫂正没好气，便用筷子在伊的双丫角中间，直扎下去，大喝道，『谁要你来多嘴！你这偷汉的小寡妇！』
segs.en: Before Seven-pounds’ wife had even finished listening, her ears were already burning red. She turned her chopsticks around, pointed them at Eight-one’s wife’s nose, and cried, "My goodness, what kind of talk is that! Eight-one’s wife, I still consider myself a decent human being; would I ever say such muddled, nonsensical things? Back then, I cried for three whole days; everyone saw me. Even that little brat, Six-pounds, was crying..." Six-pounds had just finished a large bowl of rice and was holding out the empty vessel, clamoring for more. Seven-pounds’ wife, already in a foul mood, jabbed her chopsticks straight into the space between the child’s two pigtails and shouted, "Who asked you to butt in! You little widow-in-the-making!"

segs.zh: 扑的一声，六斤手里的空碗落在地上了，恰巧又碰着一块砖角，立刻破成一个很大的缺口。七斤直跳起来，检起破碗，合上了检查一回，也喝道，『入娘的！』一巴掌打倒了六斤。六斤躺着哭，九斤老太拉了伊的手，连说着『一代不如一代』，一同走了。
segs.en: With a sharp crack, the empty bowl in Liujin’s hands hit the ground. It happened to strike the corner of a brick, and a large piece immediately chipped off. Qijin leaped up, picked up the broken bowl, fitted the pieces together to inspect the damage, and then roared, "Damn your mother!" He slapped Liujin, knocking her to the floor. As Liujin lay there crying, Grandmother Jiujin took her by the hand, muttering "Each generation is worse than the last," and led her away.

segs.zh: 八一嫂也发怒，大声说，『七斤嫂，你「恨棒打人」。……』
segs.en: Auntie Eight-One grew angry too, and shouted, "Auntie Qijin, you’re just ‘beating a dog with a stick’—you’re spoiling for a fight!..."

segs.zh: 赵七爷本来是笑着旁观的；但自从八一嫂说了『衙门里的大老爷没有告示』这话以后，却有些生气了。这时他已经绕出桌旁，接着说，『「恨棒打人」，算什么呢。大兵是就要到的。你可知道，这回保驾的是张大帅，张大帅就是燕人张翼德的后代，他一支丈八蛇矛，就有万夫不当之勇，谁能抵挡他？』他两手同时捏起空拳，仿佛握着无形的蛇矛模样，向八一嫂抢进几步道，『你能抵挡他么！』
segs.en: Master Zhao had been looking on with a smile, but ever since Sister Bayi remarked that "the grand masters at the yamen have issued no proclamation," he had grown rather annoyed. He now stepped out from behind the table and continued, "So what if he 'hits people with a stick'? The troops are arriving any moment. Do you know who is guarding the Emperor this time? It is Commander Zhang! Commander Zhang is a descendant of Zhang Yide of Yan, and with his single eighteen-foot serpent-spear, he possesses the courage of ten thousand men—who could possibly stand against him?" He clenched both empty fists as if gripping an invisible spear and lunged a few paces toward Sister Bayi, shouting, "Could you stand against him!"

segs.zh: 八一嫂正气得抱着孩子发抖，忽然见赵七爷满脸油汗，瞪着眼，准对伊冲过来，便十分害怕，不敢说完话，回身走了。赵七爷也跟着走去，众人一面怪八一嫂多事，一面让开路，几个剪过辫子重新留起的便赶快躲在人丛后面，怕他看见。赵七爷也不细心察访，通过人丛，忽然转入乌桕树后，说道『你能抵挡他么！』跨上独木桥，扬长去了。
segs.en: Sister Bayi was trembling with rage as she clutched her child, when suddenly she saw Seventh Master Zhao, his face slick with sweat and grease, eyes bulging, charging straight toward her. Terrified, she broke off mid-sentence and turned to flee. Seventh Master Zhao followed in her wake. While the onlookers grumbled about Sister Bayi’s meddling, they cleared a path for him; those who had once cut off their queues and were now growing them back again scrambled to hide behind the crowd, fearful of being spotted. Seventh Master Zhao did not bother to investigate further. Pushing through the throng, he suddenly veered behind a tallow tree, barked, “Do you think you can stand up to him!” and strode off across the footbridge, head held high.

segs.zh: 村人们呆呆站着，心里计算，都觉得自己确乎抵不住张翼德，因此也决定七斤便要没有性命。七斤既然犯了皇法，想起他往常对人谈论城中的新闻的时候，就不该含着长烟管显出那般骄傲模样，所以对于七斤的犯法，也觉得有些畅快。他们也仿佛想发些议论，却又觉得没有什么议论可发。嗡嗡的一阵乱嚷，蚊子都撞过赤膊身子，闯到乌桕树下去做市；他们也就慢慢地走散回家，关上门去睡觉。七斤嫂咕哝着，也收了家伙和桌子矮凳回家，关上门睡觉了。
segs.en: The villagers stood there blankly, calculating in their minds, and all concluded that they certainly could not withstand a Zhang Yide, and therefore decided that Qijin’s life was as good as forfeit. Since Qijin had broken the Imperial Law, they recalled how he used to talk about news from the city, and felt he shouldn't have held his long pipe with such an arrogant air; thus, they even felt a certain satisfaction at his transgression. They seemed to want to offer some commentary, yet felt there was nothing left to be said. After a chaotic buzz of shouting, the mosquitoes bumped against their bare chests and swarmed off to the tallow trees to hold their own market; the villagers then slowly dispersed to their homes, shut their doors, and went to sleep. Qijin’s wife grumbled to herself, gathered up the utensils, the table, and the low stools, went home, shut the door, and went to sleep.

segs.zh: 七斤将破碗拿回家里，坐在门槛上吸烟；但非常忧愁，忘却了吸咽，象牙嘴六尺多长湘妃竹烟管的白铜斗里的火光，渐渐发黑了。他心里但觉得事情似乎十分危急，也想想些方法，想些计画，但总是非常模糊，贯穿不得：『辫子呢辫子？丈八蛇矛。一代不如一代！皇帝坐龙庭。破的碗须得上城去钉好。谁能抵挡他？书上一条一条写着。入娘的！』
segs.en: Qijin brought the broken bowl home and sat on the threshold to smoke; but he was so profoundly troubled that he forgot to draw on his pipe, and the glow in the white-copper bowl of his six-foot-long mottled bamboo pipe gradually faded to ash. He felt that the situation was critical, and he tried to think of some way out, some plan, but his thoughts remained blurred and disconnected: "The queue, what about the queue? Zhang Fei’s serpent-headed spear. Each generation is worse than the last! The Emperor sits on the dragon throne. The broken bowl must be taken to the city to be riveted. Who can stand against him? It’s all written there, line by line, in the books. Damn it all!"

segs.zh: 第二日清晨，七斤依旧从鲁镇撑航船进城，傍晚回到鲁镇，又拿着六尺多长的湘妃竹烟管和一个饭碗回村。他在晚饭席上，对九斤老太说，这碗是在城内钉合的，因为缺口大，所以要十六个铜钉，三文一个，一总用了四十八文小钱。
segs.en: The next morning, Qijin set off as usual by boat from Lu Town for the city, returning to Lu Town by dusk, clutching a six-foot-long mottled bamboo pipe and a rice bowl as he made his way back to the village. At the dinner table, he told Old Granny Jiujin that he had had the bowl riveted in the city; because the crack was so large, it had taken sixteen copper rivets at three cash apiece, costing him a total of forty-eight copper coins.

segs.zh: 九斤老太很不高兴的说，『一代不如一代，我是活够了。三文钱一个钉；从前的钉，这样的么？从前的钉是……我活了七十九岁了，——』
segs.en: Old Lady Nine-Pound was very displeased. "Each generation is worse than the last," she said. "I've lived long enough. Three cash for a nail; were the nails of the past like this? The nails of the past were... I have lived seventy-nine years, and—"

segs.zh: 此后七斤虽然是照例日日进城，但家景总有些黯淡，村人大抵回避着，不再来听他从城内得来的新闻。七斤嫂也没有好声气，还时常叫他『囚徒』。
segs.en: After this, although Qijin continued to go into town every day as usual, the atmosphere at home remained somewhat gloomy. The villagers generally avoided him, no longer coming to hear the news he brought back from the city. Qijin’s wife, too, had no kind words for him, often calling him a “jailbird.”

segs.zh: 过了十多日，七斤从城内回家，看见他的女人非常高兴，问他说，『你在城里可听到些什么？』
segs.en: Ten days or so later, Qijin returned home from the town. Seeing him, his wife was overjoyed and asked, "Did you hear any news while you were in town?"

segs.zh: 『没有听到些什么。』
segs.en: I haven't heard anything.

segs.zh: 『皇帝坐了龙庭没有呢？』
segs.en: Has the Emperor ascended the Dragon Throne yet?

segs.zh: 『他们没有说。』
segs.en: They didn't say.

segs.zh: 『咸亨酒店里也没有人说么？』
segs.en: And does nobody at the Xianheng Tavern have anything to say about it?

segs.zh: 『也没人说。』
segs.en: Nobody said anything, either.

segs.zh: 『我想皇帝一定是不坐龙庭了。我今天走过赵七爷的店前，看见他又坐着念书了，辫子又盘在顶上了，也没有穿长衫。』
segs.en: I suspect the Emperor must have abdicated his throne. I walked past Master Zhao’s shop today and saw him sitting there reading his books again, his queue coiled once more on top of his head, and he wasn't wearing his long gown.

segs.zh: 『你想，不坐龙庭了罢？』
segs.en: Tell me, you don't think he's going to sit on the Dragon Throne anymore, do you?

segs.zh: 『我想，不坐了罢。』
segs.en: "I think I’d best be going," I said.

segs.zh: 现在的七斤，是七斤嫂和村人又都早给他相当的尊敬，相当的待遇了。到夏天，他们仍旧在自家门口的土场上吃饭；大家见了，都笑嘻嘻的招呼。九斤老太早已做过八十大寿，仍然不平而且康健。六斤的双丫角，已经变成一支大辫子了；伊虽然新近裹脚，却还能帮同七斤嫂做事，捧着十八个铜钉的饭碗，在土场上一瘸一拐的往来。
segs.en: As for Qijin, both his wife and the villagers now treated him with the respect and consideration he deserved. When summer came, they still ate their meals on the dirt patch in front of their house, and everyone who passed by would greet them with a smile. Old Lady Jiujin had long since celebrated her eightieth birthday and remained as cantankerous and healthy as ever. Liujin’s double buns had grown into a single thick braid; though she had recently bound her feet, she was still able to help her mother, hobbling back and forth across the dirt patch while clutching a rice bowl studded with eighteen copper rivets.

segs.zh: （一九二〇年十月。）
segs.en: (October, 1920.)

chapters.label: 八

segs.zh: 我冒了严寒，回到相隔二千馀里，别了二十馀年的故乡去。
segs.en: I braved the bitter cold to return to my hometown, a place over two thousand li away that I had not seen for more than twenty years.

segs.zh: 时候既然是深冬；渐近故乡时，天气又阴晦了，冷风吹进船舱中，呜呜的响，从篷隙向外一望，苍黄的天底下，远近横着几个萧索的荒村，没有一些活气。我的心禁不住悲凉起来了。
segs.en: It was the depth of winter. As we drew near my hometown, the weather turned overcast again, and a cold wind whistled into the boat cabin, howling. Peering out through the gaps in the bamboo awning, I saw beneath a sallow, dreary sky a few desolate villages scattered across the landscape, devoid of any sign of life. My heart could not help but sink with melancholy.

segs.zh: 阿！这不是我二十年来时时记得的故乡？
segs.en: Ah! Is this not the hometown I have carried in my memory for the past twenty years?

segs.zh: 我所记得的故乡全不如此。我的故乡好得多了。但要我记起他的美丽，说出他的佳处来，却又没有影像，没有言辞了。仿佛也就如此。于是我自己解释说：故乡本也如此，——虽然没有进步，也未必有如我所感的悲凉，这只是我自己心情的改变罢了，因为我这次回乡，本没有什么好心绪。
segs.en: The home I remember was nothing like this. My home was far better. Yet, if I were to try to recall its beauty or describe its charms, no images would surface, no words would come. Perhaps it was always like this. I tried to explain it to myself: my hometown was always just as it is—and while it may not have progressed, it could hardly be as desolate as I felt it to be. It was simply a change in my own state of mind, for I had come back this time in no mood for anything.

segs.zh: 我这次是专为了别他而来的。我们多年聚族而居的老屋，已经公同卖给别姓了，交屋的期限，只在本年，所以必须赶在正月初一以前，永别了熟识的老屋，而且远离了熟识的故乡，搬家到我在谋食的异地去。
segs.en: I had come this time specifically to bid it farewell. Our old family house, where we had lived together for generations, had been sold to a stranger. The deadline for vacating the premises was set for this very year, so I had to hurry back before the first day of the Lunar New Year to bid a final goodbye to the familiar old house and turn my back on my familiar hometown, moving my household to the distant place where I made my living.

segs.zh: 第二日清早晨我到了我家的门口了。瓦楞上许多枯草的断茎当风抖着，正在说明这老屋难免易主的原因。几房的本家大约已经搬走了，所以很寂静。我到了自家的房外，我的母亲早已迎着出来了，接着便飞出了八岁的侄儿宏儿。
segs.en: The next morning, I arrived at the gate of my ancestral home. On the eaves, the broken stalks of withered grass trembled in the wind, a silent testament to why this old house was destined to change hands. The various branches of the family had likely already moved away, for everything was deathly quiet. As I reached my own quarters, my mother came out to greet me, followed closely by my eight-year-old nephew, Hong'er, who came flying out to meet me.

segs.zh: 我的母亲很高兴，但也藏着许多凄凉的神情，教我坐下，歇息，喝茶，且不谈搬家的事。宏儿没有见过我，远远的对面站着只是看。
segs.en: My mother was overjoyed, yet a shadow of deep melancholy lingered in her expression. She bade me sit, rest, and drink some tea, deliberately avoiding any mention of moving house. Hong'er, who had never seen me before, stood at a distance across the room, simply staring.

segs.zh: 但我们终于谈到搬家的事。我说外间的寓所已经租定了，又买了几件家具，此外须将家里所有的木器卖去，再去增添。母亲也说好，而且行李也略已齐集，木器不便搬运的，也小半卖去了，只是收不起钱来。
segs.en: In the end, however, we turned to the subject of moving. I told her that I had already rented a place elsewhere and bought a few pieces of furniture; furthermore, we would need to sell off all the wooden items from the house and replace them with new ones. My mother agreed, and since our luggage was already more or less packed and we had sold off a small portion of the furniture that was inconvenient to move, the only remaining problem was that we hadn't actually collected the money for it.

segs.zh: 『你休息一两天，去拜望亲戚本家一回，我们便可以走了。』母亲说。
segs.en: "Rest for a day or two, pay a visit to your relatives and kin, and then we can be on our way," my mother said.

segs.zh: 『是的。』
segs.en: Yes.

segs.zh: 『还有闰土，他每到我家来时，总问起你，很想见你一回面。我已经将你到家的大约日期通知他，他也许就要来了。』
segs.en: And Runtu—whenever he comes to my house, he always asks after you; he is very eager to see you again. I have already sent word to him about the approximate date of your arrival; he may be here any moment.

segs.zh: 这时候，我的脑里忽然闪出一幅神异的图画来：深蓝的天空中挂着一轮金黄的圆月，下面是海边的沙地，都种着一望无际的碧绿的西瓜，其间有一个十一二岁的少年，项带银圈，手捏一柄钢叉，向一匹猹尽力的刺去，那猹却将身一扭，反从他的胯下逃走了。
segs.en: At that moment, a strange and vivid picture flashed through my mind: a golden full moon hanging in a deep blue sky, and beneath it, a stretch of seaside sand planted with endless rows of emerald-green watermelons. In their midst stands a boy of eleven or twelve, a silver ring around his neck and a steel pitchfork in his hand, thrusting with all his might at a zha. But the creature, with a nimble twist of its body, slips through his legs and escapes.

segs.zh: 这少年便是闰土。我认识他时，也不过十多岁，离现在将有三十年了；那时我的父亲还在世，家景也好，我正是一个少爷。那一年，我家是一件大祭祀的值年。这祭祀，说是三十多年才能轮到一回，所以很郑重；正月里供祖像，供品很多，祭器很讲究，拜的人也很多，祭器也很要防偸去。我家只有一个忙月（我们这里给人做工的分三种：整年给一定人家做工的叫长年；按日给人做工的叫短工；自己也种地，只在过年过节以及收租时候来给一定的人家做工的称忙月），忙不过来，他便对父亲说，可以叫他的儿子闰土来管祭器的。
segs.en: This boy was Runtu. When I first knew him, he was no more than ten or so, and that was nearly thirty years ago. My father was still alive then, our family circumstances were comfortable, and I was, in every sense, a young master. That year, it fell to our household to take charge of a major ancestral sacrifice. It was a rite held only once every thirty years or so, and was therefore treated with great solemnity. During the first lunar month, we displayed the ancestral portraits; the offerings were plentiful, the sacrificial vessels exquisite, and there were many people coming to pay their respects—so many that the vessels had to be guarded against theft. We had only one seasonal laborer (in our parts, those who worked for others were divided into three types: those who worked for a single family year-round were called "long-term laborers"; those who worked by the day were "short-term laborers"; and those who farmed their own land but came to work for a specific family only during the holidays or at rent-collection time were called "seasonal laborers"). Being unable to cope with the workload, he suggested to my father that he could send his son, Runtu, to keep watch over the sacrificial vessels.

segs.zh: 我的父亲允许了；我也很高兴，因为我早听到闰土这名字，而且知道他和我仿佛年纪，闰月生的，五行缺土，所以他的父亲叫他闰土。他是能装弶捉小鸟雀的。
segs.en: My father gave his consent; and I was delighted, for I had long since heard the name Runtu. I knew, too, that he was about my own age, and because he had been born in a leap month and his horoscope lacked the element of earth, his father had named him Runtu. He was a boy who knew how to set traps to catch small birds.

segs.zh: 我于是日日盼望新年，新年到，闰土也就到了。好容易到了年末，有一日，母亲告诉我，闰土来了，我便飞跑的去看。他正在厨房里，紫色的圆脸，头戴一顶小毡帽，颈上套一个明晃晃的银项圈，这可见他的父亲十分爱他，怕他死去，所以在神佛面前许下愿心，用圈子将他套住了。他见人很怕羞，只是不怕我，没有旁人的时候，便和我说话，于是不到半日，我们便熟识了。
segs.en: I began to look forward to the New Year each day, for when the New Year came, Runtu would arrive. At long last the year drew to a close, and one day my mother told me that Runtu had come. I flew to see him. He was in the kitchen, his face round and purple, wearing a small felt cap on his head and a gleaming silver neck-ring. It was clear that his father loved him dearly and, fearing he might die, had made a vow before the gods to tether him to life with that ring. He was quite shy with strangers, but not with me; when no one else was about, he would talk to me, and within half a day, we had become fast friends.

segs.zh: 我们那时候不知道谈些什么，只记得闰土很高兴，说是上城之后，见了许多没有见过的东西。
segs.en: We did not know what to talk about in those days; I only remember that Runtu was very happy, saying that after coming to the city, he had seen many things he had never seen before.

segs.zh: 第二日，我便要他捕鸟。他说：
segs.en: The next day, I asked him to catch some birds for me. He said:

segs.zh: 『这不能。须大雪下了才好。我们沙地上，下了雪，我扫出一块空地来，用短棒支起一个大竹匾，撒下秕谷，看鸟雀来吃时，我远远地将缚在棒上的绳子只一拉，那鸟雀就罩在竹匾下了。什么都有：稻鸡、角鸡、鹁鸪、蓝背……』
segs.en: That won’t do. It has to be after a heavy snowfall. On our sandy flats, once the snow falls, I sweep a patch of ground clear, prop up a large bamboo dustpan with a short stick, and scatter some chaff beneath it. When I see the birds come to feed, I stand far off and give the string tied to the stick a quick tug, and the birds are trapped under the dustpan. There’s everything: rice buntings, horned pheasants, wood pigeons, blue-backs...

segs.zh: 我于是又很盼望下雪。
segs.en: And so, I once again found myself longing for snow.

segs.zh: 闰土又对我说：
segs.en: Runtu spoke to me again:

segs.zh: 『现在太冷，你夏天到我们这里来。我们日里到海边检贝壳去，红的绿的都有，鬼见怕也有，观音手也有，晚上我和爹管西瓜去，你也去。』
segs.en: It’s too cold now. Come visit us in the summer. During the day, we go down to the beach to collect shells; there are red ones and green ones, and some called 'ghost-scare-me' and 'Guanyin’s hands.' At night, when I go with my father to watch over the watermelons, you can come too.

segs.zh: 『管贼么？』
segs.en: Are you going to look after the thief?

segs.zh: 『不是。走路的人口渴了摘一个瓜吃，我们这里是不算偸的。要管的是獾猪、刺猬、猹。月亮地下，你听，啦啦的响了，猹在咬瓜了。你便捏了胡叉，轻轻地走去……』
segs.en: No, it’s not. If a passerby is thirsty and picks a melon to eat, we don't call that stealing around here. The ones you have to watch out for are badgers, hedgehogs, and chā. When the moon is out, listen—you’ll hear a rustling sound; that’s a chā gnawing on the melons. Then you take your pitchfork, tread softly, and go...

segs.zh: 我那时并不知道这所谓猹的是怎么一件东西——便是现在也没有知道——只是无端的觉得状如小狗而很凶猛。
segs.en: At that time, I had no idea what this creature called a zha was—I do not know even now—I only felt, for no reason at all, that it resembled a small dog, yet was exceedingly fierce.

segs.zh: 『他不咬人么？』
segs.en: Doesn't he bite?

segs.zh: 『有胡叉呢。走到了，看见猹了，你便刺。这畜生很伶俐，倒向你奔来，反从胯下窜了。他的皮毛是油一般的滑。……』
segs.en: You have a pitchfork. When you arrive and see a zha, you thrust it. The beast is very clever; instead of running away, it charges straight at you, slipping right between your legs. Its fur is as slick as oil...

segs.zh: 我素不知道天下有这许多新鲜事：海边有如许五色的贝壳；西瓜有这样危险的经历，我先前单知道他在水果店里出卖罢了。
segs.en: I had never known that there were so many strange things in the world: that by the seaside there were such multicolored seashells, and that watermelons had such perilous adventures—I had previously supposed they were simply things sold in fruit shops.

segs.zh: 『我们沙地里，潮汛要来的时候，就有许多跳鱼儿只是跳，都有青蛙似的两个脚。……』
segs.en: In our sandy tracts, whenever the tide is about to come in, there are always plenty of mudskippers leaping about; they even have two little legs just like frogs’...

segs.zh: 阿！闰土的心里有无穷无尽的希奇的事，都是我往常的朋友所不知道的。他们不知道一些事，闰土在海边时，他们都和我一样只看见院子里高墙上的四角的天空。
segs.en: Ah! Runtu’s mind was a treasure trove of endless, wondrous things, all unknown to my circle of friends. They knew nothing of these things; while Runtu was by the sea, they, like me, saw only the square patch of sky above the high walls of our courtyards.

segs.zh: 可惜正月过去了，闰土须回家里去，我急得大哭，他也躲到厨房里，哭着不肯出门，但终于被他父亲带走了。他后来还托他的父亲带给我一包贝壳和几枝很好看的鸟毛，我也曾送他一两次东西，但从此没有再见面。
segs.en: It was a pity that the first month of the lunar year came to an end, and Runtu had to return home. I was so distressed that I wept bitterly, and he hid himself in the kitchen, crying and refusing to leave, though in the end, he was taken away by his father. Later on, he entrusted his father to bring me a package of seashells and several beautiful bird feathers, and I, too, sent him a few gifts once or twice, but we never met again.

segs.zh: 现在我的母亲提起了他，我这儿时的记忆，忽而全都闪电似的苏生过来，似乎看到了我的美丽的故乡了。我应声说：
segs.en: Now that my mother has brought him up, my childhood memories suddenly flash back to life, and I seem to see my beautiful hometown once again. I answer:

segs.zh: 『这好极！他，——怎样？……』
segs.en: That’s excellent! And him—how is he?...

segs.zh: 『他？……他景况也很不如意……』母亲说着，便向房外看，『这些人又来了。说是买木器，顺手也就随便拿走的，我得去看看。』
segs.en: Him? ... His circumstances are not very good either..." Mother said, glancing toward the door. "These people are here again. They claim they're here to buy woodenware, but they just help themselves to whatever they can get their hands on. I’d better go and keep an eye on them.

segs.zh: 母亲站起身，出去了。门外有几个女人的声音，我便招宏儿走近面前，和他闲话：问他可会写字，可愿意出门。
segs.en: Mother stood up and went out. I could hear the voices of several women outside, so I beckoned Hong'er to come closer and made idle conversation with him, asking if he knew how to write and whether he would like to go away.

segs.zh: 『我们坐火车去么？』
segs.en: Shall we go by train?

segs.zh: 『我们坐火车去。』
segs.en: Let's go by train.

segs.zh: 『船呢？』
segs.en: And the boat?
First, take a boat...

segs.zh: 『哈！这模样了！胡子这么长了！』一种尖利的怪声突然大叫起来。
segs.en: "Ha! Look at you now! Your beard’s grown so long!" a shrill, strange voice suddenly shrieked.

segs.zh: 我吃了一吓，赶忙抬起头，却见一个凸颧骨，薄嘴唇，五十岁上下的女人站在我面前，两手搭在髀间，没有系裙，张着两脚，正像一个画图仪器里细脚伶仃的圆规。
segs.en: I was startled and quickly looked up, only to see a woman of about fifty standing before me—high cheekbones, thin lips, her hands resting on her hips. She wore no apron, and stood with her legs splayed, looking for all the world like a pair of spindly compasses from a geometry set.

segs.zh: 我愕然了。
segs.en: I was stunned.

segs.zh: 『不认识了么？我还抱你咧！』
segs.en: Don't you recognize me? I used to hold you in my arms!

segs.zh: 我愈加愕然了。幸而我的母亲也就进来，从旁说：
segs.en: I was even more astonished. Fortunately, my mother came in at that moment and said from the side:

segs.zh: 『他多年出门，统忘却了。你该记得罢，』便向着我说，『这是斜对门的杨二嫂，……开豆腐店的。』
segs.en: He has been away for so many years that he has forgotten everything. You should remember her, though," he said, turning to me, "this is Sister Yang from across the street... the one who keeps the beancurd shop.

segs.zh: 哦，我记得了。我孩子时候，在斜对门的豆腐店里确乎终日坐着一个杨二嫂，人都叫伊『豆腐西施』。但是擦着白粉，颧骨没有这么高，嘴唇也没有这么薄。而且终日坐着，我也从没有见过这圆规式的姿势。那时人说：因为伊，这豆腐店的买卖非常好。但这大约因为年龄的关系，我却并未蒙着一毫感化，所以竟完全忘却了。然而圆规很不平，显出鄙夷的神色，仿佛嗤笑法国人不知道拿破仑，美国人不知道华盛顿似的，冷笑说：
segs.en: Oh, I remember now. When I was a child, there was indeed a Mrs. Yang who sat all day in the bean curd shop diagonally across from us, known to everyone as the "Bean Curd Beauty." But she wore face powder, her cheekbones were not so high, and her lips were not so thin. Besides, as she sat there all day, I had never seen this pose of hers, like a pair of compasses. People used to say that it was because of her that business at the bean curd shop was so good. But perhaps due to my age, I had never been in the least bit influenced by her, and so I had completely forgotten her. Yet the compasses looked very displeased, wearing an expression of contempt, as if mocking a Frenchman for not knowing Napoleon or an American for not knowing Washington, and sneered:

segs.zh: 『忘了？这真是贵人眼高。……』
segs.en: Forgotten? That is truly the way of the high and mighty...

segs.zh: 『那有这事……我……』我惶恐着，站起来说。
segs.en: "Nothing of the sort... I..." I said, standing up in a panic.

segs.zh: 『那么，我对你说。迅哥儿，你阔了，搬动又笨重，你还要什么这些破烂木器，让我拿去罢。我们小户人家，用得着。』
segs.en: Well then, let me tell you. Brother Xun, now that you’ve come into money and are moving such heavy, cumbersome things, why would you still want all this broken-down furniture? Let me have it. We folks of humble means can still make use of it.

segs.zh: 『我并没有阔哩。我须卖了这些，再去……』
segs.en: I haven't grown rich, you see. I must sell these, and then I'll...

segs.zh: 『阿呀呀，你放了道台了，还说不阔？你现在有三房姨太太；出门便是八抬的大轿，还说不阔？吓，什么都瞒不过我。』
segs.en: Oh, oh, oh! So you’ve been promoted to daotai, and you still claim you aren't rich? You’ve got three concubines now; you travel everywhere in an eight-bearer sedan chair, and still you say you aren't rich? Hah! You can't hide anything from me.

segs.zh: 我知道无话可说了，便闭了口，默默的站着。
segs.en: I knew there was nothing left to say, so I held my tongue and stood there in silence.

segs.zh: 『阿呀阿呀，真是愈有钱，便愈是一毫不肯放松，愈是一毫不肯放松，便愈有钱……』圆规一面愤愤的回转身，一面絮絮的说，慢慢向外走，顺便将我母亲的一副手套塞在裤腰里，出去了。
segs.en: "My, my, the wealthier they get, the less they’re willing to let go of even a hair’s breadth; and the less they’re willing to let go of a hair’s breadth, the wealthier they become..." The Compass muttered indignantly to herself as she turned, rambling on as she slowly made her way out, taking the opportunity to tuck my mother’s pair of gloves into the waistband of her trousers before she left.

segs.zh: 此后又有近处的本家和亲戚来访问我。我一面应酬，偸空便收拾些行李，这样的过了三四天。
segs.en: After that, some relatives and members of my own clan from the neighborhood came to visit me. I had to receive them, but I snatched every spare moment to pack my things, and in this way, three or four days passed.

segs.zh: 一日是天气很冷的午后，我吃过午饭，坐着喝茶，觉得外面有人进来了，便回头去看。我看时，不由的非常出惊，慌忙站起身，迎着走去。
segs.en: It was a cold afternoon. I had just finished my midday meal and was sitting there drinking tea when I sensed someone enter. I turned to look, and the sight gave me such a start that I scrambled to my feet and hurried forward to greet him.

segs.zh: 这来的便是闰土。虽然我一见便知道是闰土，但又不是我这记忆上的闰土了。他身材增加了一倍；先前的紫色的圆脸，已经变作灰黄，而且加上了很深的皱纹；眼睛也像他父亲一样，周围都肿得通红，这我知道，在海边种地的人，终日吹着海风，大抵是这样的。他头上是一顶破毡帽，身上只一件极薄的棉衣，浑身瑟索着；手里提着一个纸包和一枝长烟管，那手也不是我所记得的红活圆实的手，却又粗又笨而且开裂，像是松树皮了。
segs.en: The person who had arrived was Runtu. Although I knew at once it was Runtu, he was no longer the Runtu of my memory. He had grown to twice his former size; his once round, purplish face had turned a sallow grey and was etched with deep wrinkles; his eyes, too, were rimmed with the same swollen redness as his father’s—this I understood, as those who farm by the sea, exposed to the salt winds all day, are generally like this. He wore a tattered felt hat and only a thin, flimsy cotton-padded jacket, shivering all over. In his hand he carried a paper parcel and a long pipe. His hand was no longer the plump, ruddy, firm hand I remembered, but was now coarse, clumsy, and chapped, looking for all the world like pine bark.

segs.zh: 我这时很兴奋，但不知道怎么说才好，只是说：
segs.en: I was very excited at this moment, but I did not know how best to express it, and could only say:

segs.zh: 『阿！闰土哥，——你来了？……』
segs.en: Ah! Brother Runtu—you’ve come?

segs.zh: 我接着便有许多话，想要连珠一般涌出：角鸡、跳鱼儿、贝壳、猹，……但又总觉得被什么挡着似的。单在脑里面回旋，吐不出口外去。
segs.en: A torrent of words then welled up within me, ready to spill out in a rush: horned pheasants, jumping fish, seashells, chā... but I felt as if something were blocking them. They merely circled in my brain, unable to find their way to my lips.

segs.zh: 他站住了，脸上现出欢喜和凄凉的神情；动着嘴唇，却没有作声。他的态度终于恭敬起来了，分明的叫道：
segs.en: He stopped, his face a mixture of joy and desolation; his lips moved, but no sound came out. At last, his manner became respectful, and he called out clearly:
Master!...

segs.zh: 我似乎打了一个寒噤；我就知道，我们之间已经隔了一层可悲的厚障壁了。我也说不出话。
segs.en: I felt a sudden shudder; I knew then that a pitiable, impenetrable barrier had risen between us. I could not find a word to say.

segs.zh: 他回过头去说：『水生，给老爷磕头。』便拖出躲在背后的孩子来，这正是一个二十年前的闰土，只是黄瘦些，颈子上没有银圈罢了。『这是第五个孩子，没有见过世面，躲躲闪闪。……』
segs.en: He turned his head and said, "Shuisheng, kowtow to the master." With that, he pulled out the child who had been hiding behind him; it was the Runtu of twenty years ago, only yellower and thinner, and without the silver neck ring. "This is my fifth, he’s never seen the world, always so shy and skittish..."

segs.zh: 母亲和宏儿下楼来了，他们大约也听到了声音。
segs.en: Mother and Hong'er came downstairs; they must have heard the noise too.

segs.zh: 『老太太。信是早收到了。我实在喜欢的了不得，知道老爷回来……』闰土说。
segs.en: "Old Madam, the letter was received some time ago. I was truly overjoyed to hear that the Master was returning..." Runtu said.

segs.zh: 『阿，你怎的这样客气起来。你们先前不是哥弟称呼么？还是照旧：迅哥儿。』母亲高兴的说。
segs.en: "Ah, why this sudden formality? Weren't you two always like brothers before? Just call him as you used to: Brother Xun," my mother said, beaming.

segs.zh: 『阿呀，老太太真是……这成什么规矩。那时是孩子，不懂事……』闰土说着，又叫水生上来打拱，那孩子却害羞，紧紧的只贴在他背后。
segs.en: "Oh, my, ma'am, really... what kind of manners are these? Back then, he was just a child, he didn't know any better..." Runtu said, then told Shuisheng to come forward and make a bow, but the boy was shy and kept pressed tight against his father’s back.

segs.zh: 『他就是水生？第五个？都是生人，怕生也难怪的；还是宏儿和他去走走。』母亲说。
segs.en: "So this is Shuisheng? The fifth one? They’re all strangers to each other, so it’s no wonder he’s shy. Hong'er, why don’t you go and show him around?" Mother said.

segs.zh: 宏儿听得这话，便来招水生，水生却松松爽爽同他一路出去了。母亲叫闰土坐，他迟疑了一回，终于就了坐，将长烟管靠在桌旁，递过纸包来，说：
segs.en: Hong’er heard this and went to beckon Shuisheng, who followed him out, easy and carefree. Mother told Runtu to sit down; he hesitated for a moment, then finally took a seat, leaned his long pipe against the table, and handed over a paper package, saying:

segs.zh: 『冬天没有什么东西了。这一点干青豆倒是自家晒在那里的，请老爷……』
segs.en: There’s nothing much to be had in winter. These few dried green beans I dried myself—please, sir...

segs.zh: 我问问他的景况。他只是摇头。
segs.en: I asked him about his circumstances. He only shook his head.

segs.zh: 『非常难。第六个孩子也会帮忙了，却总是吃不够……又不太平……什么地方都要钱，没有定规……收成又坏。种出东西来，挑去卖，总要捐几回钱，折了本；不去卖，又只能烂掉。……』
segs.en: It’s dreadfully hard. The sixth child is old enough to help out now, but there’s never enough to eat... and things are so unsettled... they demand money everywhere, with no rhyme or reason... and the harvests are poor. If you grow something and carry it to market, you’re hit with so many levies you lose your shirt; but if you don't take it to market, it just rots away...

segs.zh: 他只是摇头；脸上虽然刻着许多皱纹，却全然不动，仿佛石像一般。他大约只是觉得苦，却又形容不出，沉默了片时，便拿起烟管来默默的吸烟了。
segs.en: He merely shook his head. Though his face was etched with deep wrinkles, it remained utterly impassive, like a stone statue. He seemed to feel only bitterness, yet he could not find the words to express it. After a brief silence, he picked up his pipe and began to smoke in silence.

segs.zh: 母亲问他，知道他的家里事务忙，明天便得回去；又没有吃过午饭，便叫他自己到厨下炒饭吃去。
segs.en: His mother asked him if he knew that things were busy at home and that he would have to return tomorrow; and since he had not yet had lunch, she told him to go to the kitchen and fry some rice for himself.

segs.zh: 他出去了；母亲和我都叹息他的景况：多子、饥荒、苛税、兵、匪、官、绅，都苦得他像一个木偶人了。母亲对我说，凡是不必搬走的东西，尽可以送他，可以听他自己去拣择。
segs.en: He went out. My mother and I both sighed over his plight: with many children, famine, exorbitant taxes, soldiers, bandits, officials, and the landed gentry all weighing upon him, he had been crushed into a mere wooden puppet. Mother said to me that anything not strictly necessary for us to move could be given to him, and he should be left to choose for himself what he wanted.

segs.zh: 下午，他拣好了几件东西：两条长桌，四个椅子，一副香炉和烛台，一杆抬秤。他又要所有的草灰（我们这里煮饭是烧稻草的，那灰，可以做沙地的肥料），待我们启程的时候，他用船来载去。
segs.en: In the afternoon, he finished selecting a few items: two long tables, four chairs, a set of incense burner and candlesticks, and a steelyard. He also asked for all the straw ash (we use rice straw for cooking here, and the ash makes good fertilizer for the sandy soil), and when we set off, he brought a boat to carry it all away.

segs.zh: 夜间，我们又谈些闲天，都是无关紧要的话；第二天早晨，他就领了水生回去了。
segs.en: That night we chatted a while longer about this and that, nothing of any consequence; the next morning, he took Shuisheng and left.

segs.zh: 又过了九日，是我们启程的日期。闰土早晨便到了，水生没有同来，却只带着一个五岁的女儿管船只。我们终日很忙碌，再没有谈天的工夫。来客也不少，有送行的，有拿东西的，有送行兼拿东西的。待到傍晚我们上船的时候，这老屋里的所有破旧大小粗细东西，已经一扫而空了。
segs.en: Nine days later, the day of our departure arrived. Runtu came early in the morning, though he did not bring Shuisheng with him, having only his five-year-old daughter along to mind the boat. We were busy all day and had no time for further talk. There were many visitors, too—some to see us off, some to take things, and some to do both. By the time we boarded the boat at dusk, every last item in the old house, whether broken or whole, large or small, fine or coarse, had been swept clean away.

segs.zh: 我们的船向前走，两岸的青山在黄昏中，都装成了深黛颜色，连着退向船后梢去。
segs.en: Our boat moved forward, and the green hills on both banks, in the gathering dusk, took on a shade of deep, dark indigo, receding one after another toward the stern.

segs.zh: 宏儿和我靠着船窗，同看外面模糊的风景，他忽然问道：
segs.en: Hong'er and I leaned against the cabin window, watching the blurred scenery drift by, when he suddenly asked:

segs.zh: 『大伯！我们甚么时候回来？』
segs.en: Uncle! When are we coming back?

segs.zh: 『回来？你怎么还没有走就想回来了。』
segs.en: Coming back? How can you be thinking of coming back before you've even left?

segs.zh: 『可是，水生约我到他家玩去咧……』他睁着大的黑眼睛，痴痴的想。
segs.en: "But Shuisheng invited me to his place to play..." He stared with his large, dark eyes, lost in a dazed reverie.

segs.zh: 我和母亲也都有些惘然，于是又提起闰土来。母亲说：那豆腐西施的杨二嫂，自从我家收拾行李以来，本是每日必到的，前天伊在灰堆里，掏出十多个碗碟来，议论之后，便定说是闰土埋着的，他可以在运灰的时候，一齐搬回家里去；杨二嫂发见了这件事，自己很以为功，便拿了那狗气杀（这是我们这里养鸡的器具，木盘上面有着栅栏，内盛食料，鸡可以伸进颈子去啄，狗却不能，只能看着气死），飞也似的跑了，亏伊装着这么高底的小脚，竟跑得这样快。
segs.en: My mother and I were both somewhat bewildered, and so we began to speak of Runtu again. Mother said: That "Bean-Curd Beauty," Mrs. Yang, who had been a daily visitor ever since we started packing, had dug up over a dozen bowls and dishes from the ash heap the day before yesterday. After some deliberation, she insisted that Runtu must have buried them there, intending to smuggle them home under the cover of hauling away the ashes. Having made this discovery, Mrs. Yang took great credit for herself and, seizing our "dog-gasper"—a contrivance we use here for feeding chickens, consisting of a wooden tray with a slatted cover that allows a chicken to poke its neck through to peck at the feed while a dog can only look on in frustration—she scurried off as if in flight. It was remarkable how fast she could run, encumbered as she was by those tiny, high-heeled bound feet of hers.

segs.zh: 老屋离我愈远了；故乡的山水也都渐渐远离了我，但我却并不感到怎样的留恋。我只觉得我四面有看不见的高墙，将我隔成孤身，使我非常气闷；那西瓜地上的银项圈的小英雄的影象，我本来十分清楚，现在却忽地模糊了，又使我非常的悲哀。
segs.en: The old house grew ever more distant, and the landscapes of my hometown gradually receded from me, yet I felt no particular sense of attachment. I felt only that I was surrounded by invisible high walls, isolating me in my solitude and stifling my spirit; the image of that young hero with the silver necklet in the watermelon patch, once so vivid in my mind, had suddenly become blurred, which filled me with a profound sorrow.

segs.zh: 母亲和宏儿都睡着了。
segs.en: Mother and Hong'er were both asleep.

segs.zh: 我躺着，听船底潺潺的水声，知道我在走我的路，我想：我竟与闰土隔绝到这地步了，但我们的后辈还是一气，宏儿不是正在想念水生么。我希望他们不再像我，又大家隔膜起来……然而我又不愿意他们因为要一气，都如我的辛苦展转而生活，也不愿意他们都如闰土的辛苦麻木而生活，也不愿意都如别人的辛苦恣睢而生活。他们应该有新的生活，为我们所未经生活过的。
segs.en: I lay there, listening to the murmuring of the water against the hull, knowing I was on my way. I thought: to think that Runtu and I have grown so estranged. Yet our children are still close; isn't Hong'er already missing Shuisheng? I hope they will not end up like me, drifting into such mutual isolation... And yet, I do not wish for them to endure the weary, restless lives I have led just to remain close, nor do I wish for them to live the weary, numbing lives of Runtu, nor the weary, rapacious lives of others. They should have a new life, a life we have never yet experienced.

segs.zh: 我想到希望，忽然害怕起来了。闰土要香炉和烛台的时候，我还暗地里笑他，以为他总是崇拜偶像，什么时候都不忘却。现在我所谓希望，不也是我自己手制的偶像么？只是他的愿望切近，我的愿望茫远罢了。
segs.en: I thought of hope, and suddenly I felt afraid. When Runtu asked for the incense burner and candlesticks, I had secretly laughed at him, thinking that he was forever worshiping idols, never forgetting them for a moment. But is the hope I speak of not also an idol of my own making? It is just that his hopes are near and tangible, while mine are vast and remote.

segs.zh: 我在朦胧中，眼前展开一片海边碧绿的沙地来，上面深蓝的天空中挂着一轮金黄的圆月。我想：希望是本无所谓有，无所谓无的。这正如地上的路，其实地上本没有路；走的人多了，也便成了路。
segs.en: In a haze, I saw before my eyes a stretch of emerald beach by the sea, and suspended in the deep blue sky above it was a golden round moon. I thought: hope cannot be said to exist, nor can it be said not to exist. It is much like the paths upon the earth; for in truth, there were no paths to begin with, but when many people pass the same way, a path is made.

segs.zh: （一九二一年一月。）
segs.en: (January, 1921.)

segs.zh: 这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may also be in the public domain in countries and regions where the copyright term is longer for domestic works but applies a shorter term to foreign or non-local works.

segs.zh: 据《中华人民共和国著作权法释义》，法人作品应满足三点要求：（1）由法人（或其他组织，以下简称“法人”）主持创作，而非工作人员自发进行；（2）创作思想及表达方式体现法人意志；（3）由法人负责，而非执笔者。（详情）

(Details)

chapters.note: 「其实地上本没有路；走的人多了，也便成了路。」少年闰土与中年闰土之间，隔着一层「可悲的厚障壁」。

chapters.label: 九
chapters.en: The True Story of Ah Q

segs.zh: 我要给阿Q做正传，已经不止一两年了。但一面要做，一面又往回想，这足见我不是一个『立言』的人，因为从来不朽之笔，须传不朽之人，于是人以文传，文以人传——究竟谁靠谁传，渐渐的不甚了然起来，而终于归结到传阿Q，仿佛思想里有鬼似的。
segs.en: It has been more than a year or two since I first thought of writing a true biography of Ah Q. Yet, every time I set out to write, I find myself hesitating and looking back. This is proof enough that I am not the sort of man meant to "achieve immortality through the written word." For it is said that immortal pens must record immortal deeds, so that the man is remembered for his writing, and the writing for the man—though in the end, it becomes rather unclear who is relying on whom for posterity. I always find myself returning to the idea of writing about Ah Q, as if there were a ghost in my thoughts.

segs.zh: 然而要做这一篇速朽的文章，才下笔，便感到万分的困难了。第一是文章的名目。孔子曰：『名不正则言不顺。』这原是应该极注意的。传的名目很繁多：列传、自传、内传、外传、别传、家传、小传，……而可惜都不合。『列传』么，这一篇并非和许多阔人排在『正史』里；『自传』么，我又并非就是阿Q。说是『外传』，『内传』在那里呢？倘用『内传』，阿Q又决不是神仙。『别传』呢，阿Q实在未曾有大总统上谕宣付国史馆立『本传』——虽说英国正史上并无『博徒列传』，而文豪迭更司也做过博徒别传这一部书，但文豪则可，在我辈却不可的。其次是『家传』，则我既不知与阿Q是否同宗，也未曾受他子孙的拜托；或『小传』，则阿Q又更无别的『大传』了。总而言之，这一篇也便是『本传』，但从我的文章着想，因为文体卑下，是『引车卖浆者流』所用的话，所以不敢僭称，便从不入三教九流的小说家所谓『闲话休题言归正传』这一句套话里，取出『正传』两个字来，作为名目，即使与古人所撰书法正传的『正传』字面上很相混，也顾不得了。
segs.en: However, when I set pen to paper to compose this ephemeral piece, I immediately felt the utmost difficulty. The first problem is the title. Confucius said, "If names are not correct, language is not in accordance with the truth of things." This, of course, is a matter that demands the greatest attention. The variety of biographical titles is vast: *liezhuan* (standard biography), *zizhuan* (autobiography), *neizhuan* (inner biography), *waizhuan* (outer biography), *biezhuan* (unofficial biography), *jiazhuan* (family biography), *xiaozhuan* (short biography)... yet, unfortunately, none of them fit. As for *liezhuan*, this piece is not being ranked alongside the lives of great men in any "official history." As for *zizhuan*, I am certainly not Ah Q. If I call it *waizhuan*, where is the *neizhuan*? And if I were to use *neizhuan*, Ah Q is most certainly no immortal. As for *biezhuan*, Ah Q has never had an imperial decree from a President ordering the State History Bureau to compile his "standard biography"—though it is true that while the official history of England contains no "Biography of Gamblers," the literary giant Dickens did write a *biezhuan* of a gambler. But what a literary giant may do, I cannot. Next, there is *jiazhuan*, but I do not know if I share a clan with Ah Q, nor have I been entrusted by his descendants to write it. Or *xiaozhuan*—but Ah Q has no other "great biography" to speak of. In short, this piece is, in fact, his "standard biography," but considering my own writing style—which is lowly and uses the language of "those who pull carts and sell sauces"—I dare not presume to use such a term. Thus, I have simply plucked the two characters *zhengzhuan* (True Story) from that hackneyed phrase used by novelists of the lower orders: "Enough idle talk, let us return to the True Story." Even if this might be confused with the "True Story" of those calligraphic treatises written by the ancients, I can no longer concern myself with that.

segs.zh: 第二，立传的通例，开首大抵该是『某，字某，某地人也』，而我并不知道阿Q姓什么。有一回，他似乎是姓赵，但第二日便模糊了。那是赵太爷的儿子进了秀才的时候，锣声铛铛的报到村里来，阿Q正喝了两碗黄酒，便手舞足蹈的说，这于他也很光采，因为他和赵太爷原来是本家，细细的排起来他还比秀才长三辈呢。其时几个旁听人倒也肃然的有些起敬了。那知道第二天，地保便叫阿Q到赵太爷家里去；太爷一见，满脸溅朱，喝道：
segs.en: Second, by the common convention of biography, one should begin with: "So-and-so, style-name such-and-such, a native of such-and-such a place." But I do not even know Ah Q’s surname. Once, it seemed to be Zhao, but by the next day that had grown blurred. That was when Master Zhao’s son passed the prefectural examination; the gongs clanged into the village to announce the news, and Ah Q, having downed two bowls of yellow rice wine, began to dance and gesticulate, declaring that this was a great honor for him too, since he and Master Zhao were originally of the same clan, and if one traced the lineage carefully, he was actually three generations senior to the new licentiate. At the time, the few bystanders were even struck with a certain solemn respect. Who could have known that the very next day, the village bailiff would summon Ah Q to Master Zhao’s house; the moment the Master saw him, his face flushed crimson and he roared:

segs.zh: 『阿Q，你这浑小子！你说我是你的本家么？』
segs.en: Ah Q, you wretched rascal! Do you really think you're of the same clan as me?

segs.zh: 阿Q不开口。
segs.en: Ah Q said nothing.

segs.zh: 赵太爷愈看愈生气了，抢进几步说：『你敢胡说！我怎么会有你这样的本家？你姓赵么？』
segs.en: The more Master Zhao looked, the angrier he became. He lunged forward a few steps and snapped, "How dare you spout such nonsense! How could I possibly have a clansman like you? Are you even surnamed Zhao?"

segs.zh: 阿Q不开口，想往后退了；赵太爷跳过去，给了他一个嘴巴。
segs.en: Ah Q said nothing and tried to back away; Master Zhao leapt forward and slapped him across the face.

segs.zh: 『你怎么会姓赵！——你那里配姓赵！』
segs.en: How could you possibly be surnamed Zhao!—You aren't fit to be surnamed Zhao!

segs.zh: 阿Q并没有抗辩他确凿姓赵，只用手摸着左颊，和地保退出去了；外面又被地保训斥了一番，谢了地保二百文酒钱。知道的人都说阿Q太荒唐，自己去招打；他大约未必姓赵，即使真姓赵，有赵太爷在这里，也不该如此胡说的。此后便再没有人提起他的氏族来，所以我终于不知道阿Q究竟什么姓。
segs.en: Ah Q did not dispute that he was indeed surnamed Zhao; he merely touched his left cheek and withdrew with the bailiff. Outside, he was lectured once more by the bailiff, and had to pay him two hundred cash for wine by way of thanks. Those in the know all said that Ah Q was preposterous, inviting a beating upon himself; in all likelihood, he was not surnamed Zhao, and even if he truly were, with Master Zhao present, he had no business talking such nonsense. From then on, no one ever mentioned his clan name again, and so, to this day, I still do not know what Ah Q’s surname actually was.

segs.zh: 第三，我又不知道阿Q的名字是怎么写的。他活着的时候，人都叫他阿Quei，死了以后，便没有一个人再叫阿Quei了，那里还会有『著之竹帛』的事。若论『著之竹帛』，这篇文章要算第一次，所以先遇着了这第一个难关。我曾经仔细想：阿Quei，阿桂还是阿贵呢？倘使他号叫月亭，或者在八月间做过生日，那一定是阿桂了；而他既没有号——也许有号，只是没有人知道他，——又未尝散过生日征文的帖子：写作阿桂，是武断的。又倘若他有一位老兄或令弟叫阿富，那一定是阿贵了；而他又只是一个人：写作阿贵，也没有佐证的。其馀音Quei的偏僻字样，更加凑不上了。先前，我也曾问过赵太爷的儿子茂才先生，谁料博雅如此公，竟也茫然，但据结论说，是因为陈独秀办了新青年提倡洋字，所以国粹沦亡，无可查考了。我的最后的手段，只有托一个同乡去查阿Q犯事的案卷，八个月之后才有回信，说案卷里并无与阿Quei的声音相近的人。我虽不知道是真没有，还是没有查，然而也再没有别的方法了。生怕注音字母还未通行，只好用了『洋字』，照英国流行的拼法写他为阿Quei，略作阿Q。这近于盲从新青年，自己也很抱歉，但茂才公尚且不知，我还有什么好办法呢。
segs.en: Thirdly, I do not know how to write Ah Q’s name. When he was alive, everyone called him "Ah Quei," but after he died, no one ever called him that again, so there was certainly no question of "committing it to bamboo and silk."
