If we speak of "committing it to bamboo and silk," this essay would be the first instance, and thus I have immediately encountered this first obstacle. I have thought about it carefully: is it Ah Gui (桂, laurel) or Ah Gui (贵, precious)? If he had a courtesy name like Yueting, or had celebrated his birthday in the eighth lunar month, it would certainly be Ah Gui (桂); but he had no courtesy name—or perhaps he did, only no one knew it—nor had he ever sent out invitations for a birthday celebration: to write it as Ah Gui (桂) would be arbitrary. Again, if he had an elder or younger brother named Ah Fu (Rich), it would certainly be Ah Gui (贵); but he was all alone: to write it as Ah Gui (贵) lacks evidence. As for other rare characters with the sound "Quei," they are even less fitting. I once asked Mr. Maocai, the son of Old Master Zhao. Who would have thought that such a learned gentleman would be at a loss? His conclusion, however, was that because Chen Duxiu’s *New Youth* had promoted foreign letters, the national essence had perished, and there was nowhere to look it up. My final recourse was to ask a fellow villager to check the court records of Ah Q’s case. Eight months later, the reply came that there was no one in the records with a name sounding like "Ah Quei." I do not know if that is because no such person existed or because the records were not checked, but I have no other way. Fearing that the phonetic alphabet was not yet in common use, I had no choice but to use "foreign letters," spelling it according to common English usage as "Ah Quei," and abbreviating it as "Ah Q." This borders on blindly following *New Youth*, for which I am quite apologetic, but when even Mr. Maocai does not know, what other choice do I have?

segs.zh: 第四，是阿Q的籍贯了。倘他姓赵，则据现在好称郡望的老例，可以照郡名百家姓上的注解，说是『陇西天水人也』，但可惜这姓是不甚可靠的，因此籍贯也就有些决不定。他虽然多住未庄，然而也常常宿在别处，不能说是未庄人，即使说是『未庄人也』，也仍然有乖史法的。
segs.en: Fourth, there is the matter of Ah Q’s ancestral home. If his surname were Zhao, then, following the current fad of boasting about one’s clan origins, one could cite the annotations in the *Hundred Family Surnames* and declare him a "native of Tianshui, Longxi." Unfortunately, however, his surname is not entirely reliable, which makes his ancestral home somewhat difficult to determine. Although he lived mostly in Weizhuang, he often spent his nights elsewhere, so he could not strictly be called a man of Weizhuang; and even if one were to label him a "native of Weizhuang," it would still violate the principles of historiography.

segs.zh: 我所聊以自慰的，是还有一个『阿』字非常正确，绝无附会假借的缺点，颇可以就正于通人。至于其馀，却都非浅学所能穿凿，只希望有『历史癖与考据癖』的胡适之先生的门人们，将来或者能够寻出许多新端绪来，但是我这阿Q正传到那时却又怕早经消灭了。
segs.en: The one thing I can console myself with is that the character "Ah" is entirely accurate, free from any defects of forced association or borrowing, and I would be happy to have it corrected by any man of learning. As for the rest, it is not something my shallow scholarship could ever hope to fathom; I can only hope that the disciples of Mr. Hu Shizhi—that man with a penchant for history and textual research—might one day uncover many new clues. Yet, I fear that by then, my True Story of Ah Q will long since have vanished.

segs.zh: 以上可以算是序。
segs.en: The above may serve as a preface.

segs.zh: 阿Q不独是姓名籍贯有些渺茫，连他先前的『行状』也渺茫。因为未庄的人们之于阿Q，只要他帮忙，只拿他玩笑，从来没有留心他的『行状』的。而阿Q自己也不说，独有和别人口角的时候，间或瞪着眼睛道：
segs.en: Not only were Ah Q’s surname and place of origin somewhat obscure, but his previous history was equally vague. This was because the people of Weizhuang only ever made use of him when they needed help or made him the butt of their jokes; no one had ever paid the slightest attention to his past. As for Ah Q himself, he never spoke of it, except when he happened to get into a dispute with someone, at which point he would occasionally glare and declare:

segs.zh: 『我们先前——比你阔的多啦！你算是什么东西！』
segs.en: We were much better off than you—once upon a time! Who do you think you are!

segs.zh: 阿Q没有家，住在未庄的土谷祠里；也没有固定的职业，只给人家做短工，割麦便割麦，舂米便舂米，撑船便撑船。工作略长久时，他也或住在临时主人的家里，但一完就走了。所以，人们忙碌的时候，也还记起阿Q来，然而记起的是做工，并不是『行状』；一闲空，连阿Q都早忘却，更不必说『行状』了。只是有一回，有一个老头子颂扬说：『阿Q真能做！』这时阿Q赤着膊，懒洋洋的瘦伶仃的正在他面前，别人也摸不着这话是真心还是讥笑，然而阿Q很喜欢。
segs.en: Ah Q had no home; he lived in the Earth Temple of Weizhuang. He had no steady occupation either, only odd jobs for whoever would hire him: reaping wheat when it was time to reap, husking rice when it was time to husk, or punting a boat when there was a boat to punt. When a job lasted a little longer, he might stay at his temporary employer’s house, but as soon as the work was finished, he would be off. Thus, when people were busy, they would remember Ah Q, though it was his labor they remembered, not his “biography.” Once the work was done and they were idle, they would forget Ah Q altogether, let alone his “biography.” Only once, an old man remarked in praise, “Ah Q is a real worker!” At the time, Ah Q was standing before him, stripped to the waist, looking listless and scrawny. No one could tell if the old man was being sincere or mocking, but Ah Q was very pleased.

segs.zh: 阿Q又很自尊，所有未庄的居民，全不在他眼睛里，甚而至于对于两位『文童』也有以为不值一笑的神情。夫文童者，将来恐怕要变秀才者也；赵太爷、钱太爷大受居民的尊敬，除有钱之外，就因为都是文童的爹爹，而阿Q在情神上独不表格外的崇奉，他想：我的儿子会阔得多啦！加以进了几回城，阿Q自然更自负，然而他又很鄙薄城里人，譬如用三尺长三寸宽的木板做成的凳子，未庄叫『长凳』，他也叫『长凳』，城里人却叫『条凳』，他想：这是错的，可笑！油煎大头鱼，未庄都加上半寸长的葱叶，城里却加上切细的葱丝，他想：这也是错的，可笑！然而未庄人真是不见世面的可笑的乡下人呵，他们没有见过城里的煎鱼！
segs.en:
Ah Q was also a man of great self-esteem. He held all the residents of Weizhuang in complete contempt, and even regarding the two "literary scholars" in the village, he maintained an air of disdain, finding them hardly worth a laugh. Now, a literary scholar is someone who, in all likelihood, will eventually become a certified xiucai. If Master Zhao and Master Qian were held in such high esteem by the villagers, it was not merely because of their wealth, but because they were the fathers of these scholars. Yet, Ah Q alone refused to grant them any special reverence in his heart, thinking to himself: "My own son will be far wealthier than that!"

Furthermore, having visited the city a few times, Ah Q naturally felt even more smug. And yet, he held the city folk in deep disdain. For instance, a bench made of a wooden plank three feet long and three inches wide—the people of Weizhuang called it a "long bench," and so did he; but the city people called it a "strip bench." He thought: "That’s wrong, and laughable!" Or take pan-fried bighead carp: in Weizhuang, they always added half-inch-long green onion leaves, but in the city, they added finely shredded onion slivers. He thought: "That’s wrong, too, and laughable!" Yet, the people of Weizhuang were truly such narrow-minded, ridiculous bumpkins; they had never even seen how they fried fish in the city!

segs.zh: 阿Q『先前阔』，见识高，而且『真能做』，本来几乎是一个『完人』了，但可惜他体质上还有一些缺点，最恼人的是在他头皮上，颇有几处不知于何时的癞疮疤。这虽然也在他身上，而看阿Q的意思，倒也似乎以为不足贵的，因为他讳说『癞』以及一切近于『赖』的音，后来推而广之，『光』也讳，『亮』也讳，再后来，连『灯』『烛』都讳了。一犯讳，不问有心与无心，阿Q便全疤通红的发起怒来，估量了对手，口讷的他便骂，气力小的他便打；然而不知怎么一回事，总还是阿Q吃亏的时候多，于是他渐渐的变换了方针，大抵改为怒目而视了。
segs.en: Ah Q had once been "well-to-do," was a man of high discernment, and was, moreover, "truly capable," making him, by all accounts, nearly a "perfect man." It was a pity, however, that he suffered from certain physical shortcomings; most vexing of all were the several ringworm scars on his scalp, of unknown origin. Although these were a part of him, Ah Q himself seemed to consider them nothing to be proud of, for he avoided the word "ringworm" and any sounds that came close to it. Later, he extended this taboo to the words "light" and "bright," and eventually even to "lamp" and "candle." Whenever someone tripped over these taboos, intentionally or otherwise, Ah Q’s entire scalp would flush a fiery red with rage. He would size up his opponent: if the person was slow of speech, he would curse them; if they were physically weak, he would strike them. Yet, for some reason, it was almost always Ah Q who came off the worse, and so, he gradually shifted his strategy, settling mostly for glaring at his adversaries with baleful eyes.

segs.zh: 谁知道阿Q采用怒目主义之后，未庄的闲人们便愈喜欢玩笑他，一见面，他们便假作吃惊的说：
segs.en: Who would have guessed that after Ah Q adopted his "glaring-eye" policy, the idlers of Weizhuang would only find it more amusing to tease him? Whenever they met him, they would feign astonishment and say:

segs.zh: 『哙，亮起来了。』
segs.en: Hey, it's lighting up.

segs.zh: 阿Q照例的发了怒，他怒目而视了。
segs.en: Ah Q, as was his custom, flew into a rage and glared at him with eyes full of fury.

segs.zh: 『原来有保险灯在这里！』他们并不怕。
segs.en: "So, there’s a safety lamp here after all!" They were not afraid.

segs.zh: 阿Q没有法，只得另外想出报复的话来：
segs.en: Ah Q was at his wit's end and had no choice but to rack his brains for some other way to get his own back.

segs.zh: 『你还不配……』这时候，又仿佛在他头上的是一种高尚的光荣的癞头疮，并非平常的癞头疮了；但上文说过，阿Q是有见识的，他立刻知道和『犯忌』有点抵触，便不再往底下说。
segs.en: "You're not worthy..." At this moment, it was as if the ringworm on his head were a mark of noble distinction, no longer the common affliction it once was. But, as mentioned before, Ah Q was a man of insight; he realized at once that this might clash with "taboo," and so he said no more.

segs.zh: 闲人还不完，只撩他，于是终而至于打。阿Q在形式上打败了，被人揪住黄辫子，在壁下碰了四五个响头，闲人这才心满意足的得胜的走了，阿Q站了一刻，心里想，『我总算被儿子打了，现在的世界真不像样……』于是也心满意足的得胜的走了。
segs.en: The idlers would not let him be; they kept goading him until it finally came to blows. Ah Q was defeated in form, his yellow pigtail seized and his head knocked against the wall four or five times with a loud thud. Only then did the idlers walk away, satisfied and triumphant. Ah Q stood there for a moment, thinking to himself, "I have been beaten by my own son, the world really is going to the dogs..." And with that, he too walked away, satisfied and triumphant.

segs.zh: 阿Q想在心里的，后来每每说出口来，所以凡有和阿Q玩笑的人们，几乎全知道他有这一种精神上的胜利法，此后每逢揪住他黄辫子的时候，人就先一着对他说：
segs.en: What Ah Q kept in his mind, he would sooner or later let slip from his lips. As a result, almost everyone who joked with him became familiar with this "spiritual victory method" of his. From then on, whenever they grabbed him by his yellow pigtail, they would preempt him by saying:

segs.zh: 『阿Q，这不是儿子打老子，是人打畜生。自己说，人打畜生！』
segs.en: Ah Q, this isn't a son beating his father; it's a man beating a beast. Say it yourself: a man beating a beast!

segs.zh: 阿Q两只手都捏住了自己的辫根，歪着头，说道：
segs.en: Ah Q clutched the roots of his pigtail with both hands, tilted his head, and said:

segs.zh: 『打虫豸，好不好？我是虫豸——还不放么？』
segs.en: Beat the vermin, how about that? I am the vermin—won’t you let me go yet?

segs.zh: 但虽然是虫豸，闲人也并不放，仍旧在就近什么地方给他碰了五六个响头，这才心满意足的得胜的走了，他以为阿Q这回可遭了瘟。然而不到十秒钟，阿Q也心满意足的得胜的走了，他觉得他是第一个能够自轻自贱的人，除了『自轻自贱』不算外，馀下的就是『第一个』。状元不也是『第一个』么？『你算是什么东西』呢！？
segs.en: Yet, though they treated him like a maggot, the idlers were not about to let him off. They made him knock his head against some nearby object five or six times, and only then did they walk away, satisfied and triumphant, convinced that Ah Q had truly been struck by a plague this time. Within ten seconds, however, Ah Q also walked away, satisfied and triumphant. He felt that he was the first person in the world to be able to belittle himself; and if you set aside the "belittling," what remained was being the "first." Was the top scholar in the imperial examinations not also the "first"? "What kind of thing do you think you are!"

segs.zh: 阿Q以如是等等妙法克服怨敌之后，便愉快的跑到酒店里喝几碗酒，又和别人调笑一通，口角一通，又得了胜，愉快的回到土谷祠，放倒头睡着了。假使有钱，他便去押牌宝，一堆人蹲在地面上，阿Q即汗流满面的夹在这中间，声音他最响：
segs.en: After Ah Q had vanquished his enemies with such ingenious methods, he would trot off to the tavern in high spirits to down a few bowls of wine. There, he would trade barbs and jests with the others, and having once again emerged victorious, he would return happily to the Tutu Temple, flop down, and fall fast asleep. If he happened to have money, he would go to gamble on the card game. A crowd would be huddled on the ground, and Ah Q, face streaming with sweat, would squeeze into the thick of it, his voice the loudest of all:

segs.zh: 『青龙四百！』
segs.en: Four hundred on the Azure Dragon!

segs.zh: 『咳～～开～～啦！』桩家揭开盒子盖，也是汗流满面的唱。『天门啦～～角回啦！人和穿堂空在那里啦～～！阿Q的铜钱拿过来～～！』
segs.en: Right—here—we—go!" The banker lifted the lid of the box, his face streaming with sweat as he chanted. "Heaven’s Gate is open—the Corner is back! Man and the Passage are left wide open! Bring over Ah Q’s copper coins!

segs.zh: 『穿堂一百——一百五十！』
segs.en: A hundred and fifty—a hundred and fifty-five!

segs.zh: 阿Q的钱便在这样的歌吟之下，渐渐的输入别个汗流满面的人物的腰间。他终于只好挤出堆外，站在后面看，替别人着急，一直到散场，然后恋恋的回到土谷祠，第二天，肿着眼睛去工作。
segs.en: To the accompaniment of such chants, Ah Q’s money would gradually find its way into the pockets of other men, who stood there streaming with sweat. In the end, he had no choice but to squeeze his way out of the crowd and watch from behind, fretting on behalf of the winners until the game broke up. Then, casting lingering looks behind him, he would return to the Tutu Temple, only to go to work the next day with swollen eyes.

segs.zh: 但真所谓『塞翁失马安知非福』罢，阿Q不幸而赢了一回，他倒几乎失败了。
segs.en: But as the old saying goes, "The loss of the horse may be a blessing in disguise"—or so it seemed, for Ah Q, having had the misfortune of winning once, very nearly ended up the loser.

segs.zh: 这是未庄赛神的晚上。这晚上照例有一台戏，戏台左近，也照例有许多的赌摊。做戏的锣鼓，在阿Q耳朵里仿佛在十里之外；他只听得桩家的歌唱了。他赢而又赢。铜钱变成角洋，角洋变成大洋，大洋又成了叠。他兴高采烈得非常：
segs.en: It was the evening of the village festival in Weizhuang. As usual, there was a theatrical performance, and as usual, there were many gambling stalls set up near the stage. To Ah Q’s ears, the gongs and drums of the play sounded as if they were ten miles away; he could hear nothing but the rhythmic chanting of the banker. He won and won again. Copper coins turned into silver dimes, silver dimes into silver dollars, and silver dollars into a growing stack. He was in an ecstasy of delight.

segs.zh: 『天门两块！』
segs.en: Two coins for the heavenly gate!

segs.zh: 他不知道谁和谁为什么打起架来了。骂声、打声、脚步声，昏头昏脑的一大阵，他才爬起来，赌摊不见了，人们也不见了，身上有几处很似乎有些痛，似乎也挨了几拳几脚似的，几个人诧异的对他看。他如有所失的走进土谷祠，定一定神，知道他的一堆洋钱不见了。赶赛会的赌摊多不是本村人，还到那里去寻根柢呢？
segs.en: He had no idea who was fighting whom, or why. Amidst a dizzying blur of shouting, scuffling, and the thud of running footsteps, he finally scrambled to his feet. The gambling stall was gone; the crowd had vanished. He felt a few dull aches in his body, as if he had indeed taken a few blows and kicks, and several people were staring at him with bewildered expressions. He walked into the Earth God Temple, feeling as if he had lost something vital. Once he regained his composure, he realized his pile of silver coins was gone. The gambling stalls at a temple fair were rarely run by locals; where in the world was he supposed to go to track them down?

segs.zh: 很白很亮的一堆洋钱！而且是他的——现在不见了！说是算被儿子拿去了罢，总还是忽忽不乐；说自己是虫豸罢，也还是忽忽不乐：他这回才有些感到失败的苦痛了。
segs.en: A pile of silver dollars, so bright, so white! And they had been his—now they were gone! Even if he told himself they had simply been taken by his son, he still felt a hollow gloom; even if he called himself a lowly worm, he still felt that same hollow gloom: for the first time, he truly tasted the bitterness of defeat.

segs.zh: 但他立刻转败为胜了。他擎起右手，用力的在自己脸上连打了两个嘴巴，热刺刺的有些痛；打完之后，便心平气和起来，似乎打的是自己，被打的是别一个自己，不久也就仿佛是自己打了别个一般，——虽然还有些热刺刺，——心满意足的得胜的躺下了。
segs.en: But he immediately turned his defeat into victory. He raised his right hand and dealt himself two resounding slaps across the face; it stung with a hot, sharp pain. Once finished, he felt calm and composed, as if the one who had struck was one person, and the one struck was another—and soon, it was as if he had actually struck someone else. Though his face still burned, he lay down, satisfied and triumphant.

segs.zh: 他睡着了。
segs.en: He had fallen asleep.

segs.zh: 然而阿Q虽然常优胜，却直待蒙赵太爷打他嘴巴之后，这才出了名。
segs.en: Yet for all Ah Q’s frequent triumphs, it was not until he had been slapped in the face by Old Master Zhao that he truly made a name for himself.

segs.zh: 他付过地保二百文酒钱，忿忿的躺下了，后来想：『现在的世界太不成话，儿子打老子……』于是忽而想到赵太爷的威风，而现在是他的儿子了，便自己也渐渐的得意起来，爬起身，唱着小孤孀上坟到酒店去。这时候，他又觉得赵太爷高人一等了。
segs.en: He paid the village head two hundred cash for the wine and lay down, fuming. Presently, he thought to himself, "The world today is a complete disgrace, a son beating his own father..." Then, suddenly, he recalled the formidable prestige of Mr. Zhao, and since he was now Mr. Zhao’s son, he gradually began to feel quite pleased with himself. He scrambled up and headed off toward the tavern, singing the tune of *The Young Widow Visits the Grave*. By then, he felt once more that Mr. Zhao was a man a cut above the rest.

segs.zh: 说也奇怪，从此之后，果然大家也仿佛格外尊敬他。这在阿Q，或者以为因为他是赵太爷的父亲，而其实也不然。未庄通例，倘如阿七打阿八，或者李四打张三，向来本不算一件事，必须与一位名人如赵太爷者相关，这才载上他们的口碑。一上口碑，则打的既有名，被打的也就托庇有了名。至于错在阿Q，那自然是不必说。所以者何？就因为赵太爷是不会错的。但他既然错，为什么大家又仿佛格外尊敬他呢？这可难解，穿凿起来说，或者因为阿Q说是赵太爷的本家，虽然挨了打，大家也还怕有些真，总不如尊敬一些稳当。否则，也如孔庙里的太牢一般，虽然与猪羊一样，同是畜生，但既经圣人下箸，先儒们便不敢妄动了。
segs.en:
Strange to say, from that day on, everyone really did seem to treat him with a newfound respect. Ah Q, for his part, may have attributed this to his newfound status as Old Master Zhao’s father, but in reality, that was not the case at all. In Weizhuang, it was the custom that if Ah Qi beat Ah Ba, or Li Si beat Zhang San, it was never considered an event of any consequence. It had to involve a person of standing, like Old Master Zhao, to be worthy of becoming the talk of the town. Once it reached the status of local gossip, the beater became famous by association, and the beaten, by extension, shared in that reflected glory. As for the fault being Ah Q’s—that, of course, went without saying. And why? Simply because Old Master Zhao could never be in the wrong. 

But if he was in the wrong, why did everyone seem to treat him with such extra respect? This is hard to fathom. If one were to force an explanation, perhaps it was because Ah Q claimed to be a clansman of Old Master Zhao; even though he had been beaten, everyone feared there might be some truth to the claim, and it was safer to treat him with a degree of deference. Otherwise, it was much like the sacrificial ox in the Confucian Temple: though it was a beast just like any pig or sheep, once the Sage had deigned to use his chopsticks upon it, the scholars of later generations dared not lay a finger on it.

segs.zh: 阿Q此后倒得意了许多年。
segs.en: After that, Ah Q actually enjoyed a good many years of triumph.

segs.zh: 有一年的春天，他醉醺醺的在街上走，在墙根的日光下，看见王胡在那里赤着膊捉虱子，他忽然觉得身上也痒起来了。这王胡，又癞又胡，别人都叫他王癞胡，阿Q却删去了一个癞字，然而非常渺视他。阿Q的意思，以为癞是不足为奇的，只有这一部络腮胡子，实在太新奇，令人看不上眼。他于是并排坐下去了，倘是别的闲人们，阿Q本不敢大意坐下去。但这王胡旁边，他有什么怕呢？老实说：他肯坐下去，简直还是抬举他。
segs.en: One spring, he was walking down the street, drunk, when he saw Wang Hu sitting by the base of a wall in the sunshine, stripped to the waist and catching lice. Suddenly, he felt itchy himself. This Wang Hu was both mangy and bearded, so everyone called him Wang the Mangy-Bearded. Ah Q, however, dropped the "mangy" part, though he held the man in utter contempt. In Ah Q’s view, mange was nothing to write home about; it was that thick, bristly beard that was truly outlandish and an eyesore. He sat down right beside him. Had it been any of the other idlers, Ah Q would never have dared to sit down so carelessly. But beside this Wang Hu, what did he have to fear? To be honest, the very fact that he was willing to sit down was, if anything, doing the man a favor.

segs.zh: 阿Q也脱下破夹袄来，翻检了一回，不知道因为新洗呢还是因为粗心，许多工夫，只捉到三四个。他看那王胡，却是一个又一个，两个又三个，只放在嘴里毕毕剥剥的响。
segs.en: Ah Q also took off his ragged lined jacket and searched through it, but whether because it had been freshly washed or because he was simply careless, he spent a long time catching only three or four. He looked over at Wang Hu, who was catching them one after another—two, then three—popping them into his mouth with a series of sharp, crackling snaps.

segs.zh: 阿Q最初是失望，后来却不平了：看不上眼的王胡尚且那么多，自己倒反这样少，这是怎样的大失体统的事呵！他很想寻一两个大的，然而竟没有，好容易才捉到一个中的，恨恨的塞在厚嘴唇里，狠命一咬，劈的一声，又不及王胡响。
segs.en: At first, Ah Q was disappointed, but soon he felt indignant: if even Wang Hu, whom he held in such contempt, could have so many, why should he have so few? What a scandalous lapse in decorum! He longed to find one or two large ones, but there were none to be had. After much effort, he managed to catch a medium-sized one, which he shoved spitefully between his thick lips and bit down on with all his might. *Snap!* It did not even make as loud a sound as Wang Hu’s.

segs.zh: 他癞疮疤块块通红了，将衣服摔在地上，吐一口唾沫，说：
segs.en: The scabs on his head turned a fiery red, and he flung his clothes to the ground, spat, and said:

segs.zh: 『这毛虫！』
segs.en: That caterpillar!

segs.zh: 『癞皮狗，你骂谁？』王胡轻蔑的抬起眼来说。
segs.en: "Mangy dog, who are you cursing?" Wang Hu asked, raising his eyes with a look of disdain.

segs.zh: 阿Q近来虽然比较的受人尊敬，自己也更高傲些，但和那些打惯的闲人们见面还胆怯，独有这回却非常武勇了。这样满脸胡子的东西，他敢出言无状么？
segs.en: Although Ah Q had been treated with more respect of late, and had himself grown somewhat more arrogant, he still felt a tremor of timidity whenever he met those idlers with whom he was accustomed to trading blows. Yet this time, he was exceptionally valiant. This fellow with the face full of whiskers—did he dare to be insolent?

segs.zh: 『谁认便骂谁！』他站起来，两手叉在腰间说：
segs.en: "Whoever acknowledges it, that's who I'm cursing!" He stood up, hands on his hips, and said:

segs.zh: 『你的骨头痒了么？』王胡也站起来，披上衣服说：
segs.en: "Are your bones itching for a beating?" Wang Hu also stood up, threw his jacket over his shoulders, and said:

segs.zh: 阿Q以为他要逃了，抢进去就是一拳。这拳头还未达到身上，已经被他抓住了，只一拉，阿Q跄跄踉踉的跌进去，立刻又被王胡扭住了辫子，要撞到墙上照例去碰头。
segs.en: Ah Q, thinking he was about to flee, lunged forward and threw a punch. But before his fist could land, it was caught; with a single tug, Ah Q went stumbling forward, only to have his queue instantly seized by Wang Hu, who prepared to knock his head against the wall in the customary fashion.

segs.zh: 『「君子动口不动手！」』阿Q歪着头说：
segs.en: "A gentleman uses his tongue, not his fists!" Ah Q said, tilting his head.

segs.zh: 王胡似乎不是君子，并不理会，一连给他碰了五下，又用力的一推，至于阿Q跌出六尺多远，这才满足的去了。
segs.en: Wang Hu, seemingly no gentleman, paid him no heed. After bumping into him five times in a row, he gave him a vigorous shove that sent Ah Q reeling more than six feet away, only then walking off, satisfied.

segs.zh: 在阿Q的记忆上，这大约要算是生平第一件的屈辱，因为王胡以络腮胡子的缺点，向来只被他奚落，从没有奚落他，更不必说动手了。而他现在竟动手，很意外，难道真如市上所说，皇帝已经停了考，不要秀才和举人了，因此赵家减了威风。因此他们也便小觑了他么？
segs.en: In the memory of Ah Q, this could be considered the greatest humiliation of his life. Because of Wang Hu’s scruffy beard, Ah Q had always been the one to mock him; never had he been mocked in return, let alone manhandled. And yet, here he was, actually laying hands on him! It was completely unexpected. Could it be, as the people in town were saying, that the Emperor had abolished the imperial examinations, that there were to be no more xiucai or juren, and that the Zhao family had consequently lost their prestige? And because of this, were they now starting to look down on him as well?

segs.zh: 阿Q无可适从的站着。
segs.en: Ah Q stood there, at a loss.

segs.zh: 远远的走来了一个人，他的对头又到了。这也是阿Q最厌恶的一个人，就是钱太爷的大儿子。他先前跑上城里去进洋学堂，不知怎么又跑到东洋去了，半年之后他回到家里来，腿也直了，辫子也不见了，他的母亲大哭了十几场，他的老婆跳了三回井。后来，他的母亲到处说，『这辫子是被坏人灌醉了酒翦去的。本来可以做大官，现在只好等留长再说了。』然而阿Q不肯信，偏称他『假洋鬼子』，也叫作『里通外国的人』，一见他，一定在肚子里暗暗的咒骂。
segs.en: In the distance, a figure approached—his nemesis had arrived again. This was the person Ah Q detested most: the eldest son of Mr. Qian. He had previously gone to the city to attend a foreign-style school, and somehow or other, had ended up in Japan. Half a year later, when he returned home, his legs were straight and his queue was gone. His mother had wept over it a dozen times, and his wife had jumped into the well three times. Later, his mother went around telling everyone, "That queue was cut off by some scoundrels who got him drunk. He could have been a high official, but now we have to wait for it to grow back." Ah Q, however, refused to believe this; he insisted on calling him the "Fake Foreign Devil," or a "traitor in league with foreigners," and whenever he caught sight of him, he was sure to curse him silently under his breath.

segs.zh: 阿Q尤其『深恶而痛绝之』的，是他的一条假辫子。辫子而至于假，就是没有了做人的资格；他的老婆不跳第四回井，也不是好女人。
segs.en: What Ah Q loathed and detested above all else was his false queue. To have a queue that was fake was to forfeit one’s very right to be a human being; and as for his wife, if she didn't jump down a well for the fourth time, she was no good woman either.

segs.zh: 这『假洋鬼子』近来了。
segs.en: The "Fake Foreign Devil" has been coming around lately.

segs.zh: 『秃儿。驴……』阿Q历来本只在肚子里骂，没有出过声，这回因为正气忿，因为要报仇，便不由的轻轻的说出来了。
segs.en: "Baldy! Ass..." Ah Q had always confined these insults to his own belly, never daring to utter them aloud. But this time, fueled by indignation and a burning desire for revenge, the words escaped his lips in a soft murmur.

segs.zh: 不料这秃儿却拿着一枝黄漆的棍子——就是阿Q所谓哭丧棒——大踏步走了过来。阿Q在这刹那，便知道大约要打了，赶紧抽紧筋骨，耸了肩膀等候着，果然，拍的一声，似乎确凿打在自己头上了。
segs.en: Unexpectedly, however, the bald fellow came striding over, brandishing a yellow-lacquered stick—the very thing Ah Q called a mourning staff. In that split second, Ah Q knew a beating was imminent; he braced his muscles and hunched his shoulders, waiting. Sure enough, with a sharp *thwack*, he felt it land squarely on his head.

segs.zh: 『我说他！』阿Q指着近旁的一个孩子，分辩说。
segs.en: "I'm talking about him!" Ah Q said, pointing to a child nearby by way of defense.

segs.zh: 拍！拍拍！
segs.en: Clap! Clap-clap!

segs.zh: 在阿Q的记忆上，这大约要算是生平第二件的屈辱。幸而拍拍的响了之后。于他倒似乎完结了一件事，反而觉得轻松些，而且『忘却』这一件祖传的宝贝也发生了效力，他慢慢的走，将到酒店门口，早已有些高兴了。
segs.en: In Ah Q’s memory, this might well have been the second greatest humiliation of his life. Fortunately, once the slapping sound had rung out, the matter seemed for him to be settled, and he felt all the lighter for it. Furthermore, that ancestral treasure of his, the faculty of "forgetfulness," began to work its effect; as he strolled along, by the time he reached the door of the Xianheng Tavern, he was already feeling quite cheerful again.

segs.zh: 但对面走来了静修庵里的小尼姑。阿Q便在平时，看见伊也一定要唾骂，而况在屈辱之后呢？他于是发生了回忆，又发生了敌忾了。
segs.en: But then, a young nun from the Quiet Cultivation Nunnery came walking from the opposite direction. Even in ordinary times, Ah Q would have been sure to spit and curse at the sight of her, and how much more so now, in the wake of his humiliation? Thus, memories were stirred within him, and with them, a surge of hostility.

segs.zh: 『我不知道我今天为什么这样晦气，原来就因为见了你！』他想。
segs.en: "I don't know why I'm having such rotten luck today; it must be because I ran into you!" he thought.

segs.zh: 他迎上去，大声的吐一口唾沫：
segs.en: He stepped up to meet him and spat out a loud mouthful of phlegm.
Hmph, open!

segs.zh: 小尼姑全不睬，低了头只是走。阿Q走近伊身旁，突然伸出手去摩着伊新剃的头皮，呆笑着，说：
segs.en: The little nun paid him no mind, but kept her head bowed and walked on. Ah Q drew up beside her, suddenly reached out to stroke her newly shaven scalp, and with a foolish grin, said:

segs.zh: 『秃儿！快回去，和尚等着你……』
segs.en: Baldy! Get back there, the monk is waiting for you...

segs.zh: 『你怎么动手动脚……』尼姑满脸通红的说，一面赶快走。
segs.en: "How can you be so handsy..." the nun said, her face flushing crimson as she hurried away.

segs.zh: 酒店里的人大笑了。阿Q看见自己的勋业得了赏识，便愈加兴高采烈起来：
segs.en: The people in the tavern burst into laughter. Seeing that his exploits had won their appreciation, Ah Q became even more exhilarated.

segs.zh: 『和尚动得，我动不得？』他扭住伊的面颊。
segs.en: "If the monk can touch her, why can't I?" He pinched her cheeks.

segs.zh: 酒店里的人大笑了。阿Q更得意，而且为满足那些赏鉴家起见，再用力的一拧，才放手。
segs.en: The people in the tavern burst into laughter. Ah Q, feeling even more pleased with himself and wishing to satisfy these connoisseurs, gave another forceful twist before finally letting go.

segs.zh: 他这一战，早忘却了王胡，也忘却了假洋鬼子，似乎对于今天的一切『晦气』都报了仇；而且奇怪，又仿佛全身比拍拍的响了之后更轻松，飘飘然的似乎要飞去了。
segs.en: After this fight, he had long since forgotten about Wang Hu, and forgotten about the Pseudo-Foreign Devil too; it was as if he had finally taken revenge for all the bad luck he had suffered that day. Strangely enough, he felt even lighter than he had after being slapped, as if his whole body were buoyant, ready to float away.

segs.zh: 『这断子绝孙的阿Q！』远远地听得小尼姑的带哭的声音。
segs.en: "That childless, damnable Ah Q!" came the sound of the little nun’s voice from afar, choked with tears.

segs.zh: 『哈哈哈！』阿Q十分得意的笑。
segs.en: “Ha, ha, ha!” Ah Q laughed, looking immensely pleased with himself.

segs.zh: 『哈哈哈！』酒店里的人也九分得意的笑。
segs.en: "Hahaha!" the people in the tavern also laughed, nine-tenths satisfied.

segs.zh: 有人说：有些胜利者，愿意敌手如虎，如鹰，他才感得胜利的欢喜；假使如羊，如小鸡，他便反觉得胜利的无聊。又有些胜利者，当克服一切之后，看见死的死了，降的降了，『臣诚惶诚恐死罪死罪』，他于是没有了敌人，没有了对手，没有了朋友，只有自己在上，一个，孤另另，凄凉，寂寞，便反而感到了胜利的悲哀。然而我们的阿Q却没有这样乏，他是永远得意的：这或者也是中国精神文明冠于全球的一个证据了。
segs.en: Some say that certain victors prefer their opponents to be like tigers or eagles, for only then do they truly savor the joy of triumph; should their foes be like sheep or chicks, they find victory rather dull. Others, having overcome all, seeing the dead buried and the living cowed—"Your servant is overwhelmed with terror, guilty of a capital crime, guilty of a capital crime"—find themselves without enemies, without rivals, and even without friends. Left all alone at the summit, solitary, desolate, and forlorn, they begin to feel the sorrow of victory. Yet our Ah Q is never so weary; he is eternally triumphant. This, perhaps, is further proof that China’s spiritual civilization stands unrivaled throughout the world.

segs.zh: 看哪，他飘飘然的似乎要飞去了！
segs.en: Look, he is drifting as if he might just float away!

segs.zh: 然而这一次的胜利，却又使他有些异样。他飘飘然的飞了大半天，飘进土谷祠，照例应该躺下便打鼾。谁知道这一晚，他很不容易合眼，他觉得自己的大拇指和第二指有点古怪：仿佛比平常滑腻些。不知道是小尼姑的脸上有一点滑腻的东西黏在他指上，还是他的指头在小尼姑脸上磨得滑腻了？……
segs.en: Yet this victory left him feeling somewhat strange. He drifted about, light as air, for the better part of the day, and when he floated into the Tutu Temple, he should by all rights have lain down and started snoring as usual. But who could have known that this night he would find it so hard to close his eyes? He felt that his thumb and forefinger were acting rather peculiar: they seemed, somehow, smoother than usual. He could not tell if there had been something slick on the little nun’s face that had clung to his fingers, or if it was his own fingers that had grown slick from rubbing against her face...

segs.zh: 『断子绝孙的阿Q！』
segs.en: Ah Q, you’ll die without an heir!

segs.zh: 阿Q的耳朵里又听到这句话。他想：不错，应该有一个女人，断子绝孙便没有人供一碗饭，……应该有一个女人。夫『不孝有三无后为大』，而『若敖之鬼馁而』，也是一件人生的大哀，所以他那思想，其实是样样合于圣经贤传的，只可惜后来有些『不能收其放心』了。
segs.en: The words rang in Ah Q’s ears once more. He thought to himself: That’s true, there ought to be a woman; if one’s line is broken, there will be no one to offer a bowl of rice... yes, there ought to be a woman. As the saying goes, "Of the three unfilial acts, the greatest is to have no offspring," and for the ghost of Ruo'ao to go hungry is indeed a great tragedy in life. Thus, his thinking was, in fact, entirely in accord with the teachings of the sages and worthies; it was only a pity that, later on, he was unable to "keep his heart and mind in check."

segs.zh: 『女人，女人！……』他想：
segs.en: "Women, women!..." he thought.

segs.zh: 『……和尚动得……女人，女人！……女人！』他又想：
segs.en: "The monk can touch... a woman, a woman!... a woman!" he thought again.

segs.zh: 我们不能知道这晚上阿Q在什么时候才打鼾。但大约他从此总觉得指头有些滑腻，所以他从此总有些飘飘然；『女……』他想：
segs.en: We cannot know at what hour that night Ah Q finally began to snore. But from that time on, he always felt as though his fingers were somewhat slick, and because of this, he always felt a little lightheaded; "Woman..." he thought.

segs.zh: 即此一端，我们便可以知道女人是害人的东西。
segs.en: From this single instance, we may know that women are pestilential creatures.

segs.zh: 中国的男人，本来大半都可以做圣贤，可惜全被女人毁掉了。商是妲己闹亡的；周是褒姒弄坏的；秦……虽然史无明文，我们也假定他因为女人，大约未必十分错；而董卓可是的确给貂蝉害死了。
segs.en: Chinese men, by nature, could almost all be sages, were it not for the fact that they are all ruined by women. The Shang Dynasty was brought to ruin by Daji; the Zhou by Baosi; as for the Qin... though the historical records are silent on the matter, we may assume it was also on account of a woman, and we likely wouldn't be far from the truth; and Dong Zhuo, as we know, was most certainly done in by Diaochan.

segs.zh: 阿Q本来也是正人，我们虽然不知道他曾蒙什么明师指授过，但他对于『男女之大防』却历来非常严；也很有排斥异端——如小尼姑及假洋鬼子之类——的正气。他的学说是：凡尼姑，一定与和尚私通，一个女人在外面走，一定想引诱野男人；一男一女在那里讲话，一定要有勾当了。为惩治他们起见，所以他往往怒目而视，或者大声说几句『诛心』话，或者在冷僻处，便从后面掷一块小石头。
segs.en: Ah Q was, in his own way, a righteous man. Though we have no way of knowing who his masters might have been, he had always held the "strict divide between the sexes" in the highest regard. He possessed a moral fervor that led him to ostracize all manner of heretics—such as the young nun or the "fake foreign devil." His philosophy was simple: every nun must be having an affair with a monk; any woman walking alone must be intent on seducing some man; and if a man and woman were seen talking together, they were surely up to no good. To punish such transgressions, he would often glare at them with righteous fury, shout a few "heart-piercing" insults, or, if the spot were sufficiently secluded, pelt them from behind with a small stone.

segs.zh: 谁知道他将到『而立』之年，竟被小尼姑害得飘飘然了。这飘飘然的精神，在礼教上是不应该有的，——所以女人真可恶，假使小尼姑的脸上不滑腻，阿Q便不至于被蛊，又假使小尼姑的脸上盖一层布，阿Q便也不至于被蛊了，——他五六年前，曾在戏台下的人丛中拧过一个女人的大腿，但因为隔一层裤，所以此后并不飘飘然，——而小尼姑并不然，这也足见异端之可恶。
segs.en: Who would have guessed that as he approached the age of thirty, he would be sent into a flutter by a little nun? Such a fluttering state of mind is, according to the rites, quite improper—which only goes to show how detestable women are. Had the little nun’s face not been so smooth, Ah Q would not have been bewitched; or had her face been covered by a piece of cloth, he would not have been bewitched either. Five or six years earlier, in a crowd beneath a theater stage, he had once pinched a woman’s thigh, but because there had been a layer of trousers in between, he had felt no such flutter afterwards. But the little nun was different, which further proves the detestable nature of such heresy.

segs.zh: 『女……』阿Q想。
segs.en: "Woman..." Ah Q thought.

segs.zh: 他对于以为『一定想引诱野男人』的女人，时常留心着，然而伊并不对他笑。他对于和他讲话的女人，也时常留心听，然而伊又并不提起关于什么勾当的话来。哦，这也是女人可恶之一节：伊们全都要装『假正经』的。
segs.en: He was always on the lookout for women he suspected of "surely wanting to lure some wild man," yet they never smiled at him. He was also always listening closely to the women who spoke to him, yet they never brought up anything scandalous. Oh, that is another detestable trait of women: they all insist on putting on a pretense of virtue.

segs.zh: 这一天，阿Q在赵太爷家里舂了一天米，吃过晚饭，便坐在厨房里吸旱烟。倘在别家，吃过晚饭本可以回去的了，但赵府上晚饭早，虽说定例不准掌灯，一吃完便睡觉，然而偶然也有一些例外：其一、是赵太爷未进秀才的时候，准其点灯读文章；其二、便是阿Q来做短工的时候，准其点灯舂米。因为这一条例外，所以阿Q在动手舂米之前，还坐在厨房里吸旱烟。
segs.en: That day, Ah Q had spent the entire time at Old Master Zhao’s house pounding rice. After dinner, he sat in the kitchen smoking his long-stemmed pipe. In any other household, he would have been free to go home after the evening meal, but the Zhao residence served dinner early. Although the standing rule forbade the lighting of lamps—meaning everyone was expected to turn in as soon as they had finished eating—there were, on rare occasions, exceptions: first, before Old Master Zhao had earned his xiucai degree, he had been permitted to light a lamp to study his essays; second, whenever Ah Q came to work as a day laborer, he was permitted to light a lamp to pound rice. It was precisely because of this second exception that Ah Q was still sitting in the kitchen, smoking his pipe, before setting to work.

segs.zh: 吴妈，是赵太爷家里唯一的女仆，洗完了碗碟，也就在长凳上坐下了，而且和阿Q谈闲天：
segs.en: Wu Ma, the only maid in Master Zhao’s household, would sit down on the bench once she had finished washing the dishes, and chat with Ah Q:

segs.zh: 『太太两天没有吃饭哩，因为老爷要买一个小的……』
segs.en: The mistress hasn't eaten for two days, all because the master wants to buy a little one...

segs.zh: 『女人……吴妈……这小孤孀……』阿Q想。
segs.en: "Woman... Wu Ma... this little widow..." Ah Q thought.

segs.zh: 『我们的少奶奶是八月里要生孩子了……』
segs.en: Our young mistress is due to give birth in August...

segs.zh: 『女人……』阿Q想。
segs.en: "Women..." Ah Q thought.

segs.zh: 阿Q放下烟管，站了起来。
segs.en: Ah Q put down his pipe and stood up.

segs.zh: 『我们的少奶奶……』吴妈还唠叨说。
segs.en: "Our young mistress..." Wu Ma kept on muttering.

segs.zh: 『我和你困觉，我和你困觉！』阿Q忽然抢上去，对伊跪下了。
segs.en: "I want to sleep with you, I want to sleep with you!" Ah Q suddenly lunged forward and fell to his knees before her.

segs.zh: 一刹时中很寂然。
segs.en: For an instant, there was a profound silence.

segs.zh: 『阿呀！』吴妈楞了一息，突然发抖，大叫着往外跑，且跑且嚷，似乎后来带哭了。
segs.en: "Ah!" Wu Ma froze for a second, then suddenly began to tremble. She let out a shriek and bolted for the door, wailing as she ran, until it sounded as if she had burst into tears.

segs.zh: 阿Q对了墙壁跪着也发楞，于是两手扶着空板凳，慢慢的站起来，仿佛觉得有些糟。他这时确也有些忐忑了，慌张的将烟管插在裤带上，就想去舂米。蓬的一声，头上着了很粗的一下，他急忙回转身去，那秀才便拿了一枝大竹杠站在他面前。
segs.en: Ah Q knelt facing the wall, dazed. He steadied himself against an empty bench and slowly stood up, feeling that things were taking a turn for the worse. He was truly flustered now; he hurriedly tucked his pipe into his waistband, intent on getting back to the rice-pounding. With a sharp *thwack*, he was struck a heavy blow on the head. He turned around in a panic to find the Scholar standing before him, brandishing a thick bamboo pole.

segs.zh: 『你反了，……你这……』
segs.en: You're rebelling, you... you...

segs.zh: 大竹杠又向他劈下来了。阿Q两手去抱头，拍的正打在指节上，这可很有一些痛。他冲出厨房门，仿佛背上又着了一下似的。
segs.en: The heavy bamboo pole came crashing down on him again. Ah Q threw both hands up to shield his head, but the blow landed squarely on his knuckles, which stung quite sharply. He bolted out of the kitchen door, feeling as if he had been struck once more across the back.

segs.zh: 『忘八蛋！』秀才在后面用了官话这样骂。
segs.en: "Son of a bitch!" the scholar cursed from behind, using the Mandarin dialect.

segs.zh: 阿Q奔入舂米场，一个人站着，还觉得指头痛，还记得『忘八蛋』，因为这话是未庄的乡下人从来不用，专是见过官府的阔人用的，所以格外怕，而印象也格外深。但这时，他那『女……』的思想却也没有了。而且打骂之后，似乎一件事也已经收束，倒反觉得一无挂碍似的，便动手去舂米。舂了一会，他热起来了，又歇了手脱衣服。
segs.en: Ah Q dashed into the husking ground and stood there alone, still feeling the sting in his fingers and still haunted by the words "bastard." Because this was an epithet never used by the country folk of Weizhuang—reserved exclusively for the wealthy who had brushed shoulders with officialdom—it filled him with a peculiar dread, and the impression it left was all the more indelible. Yet, for the moment, his thoughts of "woman..." had vanished. Furthermore, having been beaten and cursed, he felt as if a chapter had been closed, leaving him strangely unburdened, so he set to work husking the rice. After a while, he grew hot, paused, and stripped off his jacket.

segs.zh: 脱下衣服的时候，他听得外面很热闹，阿Q生平本来最爱看热闹，便即寻声走出去了。寻声渐渐的寻到赵太爷的内院里，虽然在昏黄中，却辨得出许多人，赵府一家连两日不吃饭的太太也在内，还有间壁的邹七嫂，真正本家的赵白眼，赵司晨。
segs.en: As he took off his clothes, he heard a great commotion outside. Ah Q had always been one to love a spectacle, so he followed the sound and headed out. He traced the noise until he reached the inner courtyard of Old Master Zhao’s house. Though it was dim, he could make out a crowd of people: the entire Zhao household, including the mistress, who had not eaten for two days; Mrs. Zou from next door; and his own kin, Zhao Baiyan and Zhao Sichen.

segs.zh: 少奶奶正拖着吴妈走出下房来，一面说：
segs.en: The young mistress was just dragging Wu Ma out of the servants' quarters, saying as she did so:

segs.zh: 『你到外面来，……不要躲在自己房里想……』
segs.en: You must go out into the world... stop hiding away in your room, brooding...

segs.zh: 『谁不知道你正经，……短见是万万寻不得的。』邹七嫂也从旁说：
segs.en: "Everyone knows you’re a respectable woman... but you must never, under any circumstances, think of doing anything rash." Auntie Zou added from the side.

segs.zh: 吴妈只是哭，夹些话，却不甚听得分明。
segs.en: Wu Ma did nothing but weep, interspersing her sobs with words that were, however, not very easy to make out.

segs.zh: 阿Q想：『哼，有趣，这小孤孀不知道闹着什么玩意儿了？』他想打听，走近赵司晨的身边。这时他猛然间看见赵太爷向他奔来，而且手里捏着一枝大竹杠。他看见这一枝大竹杠，便猛然间悟到自己曾经被打，和这一场热闹似乎有点相关。他翻身便走，想逃回舂米场，不图这枝竹杠阻了他的去路，于是他又翻身便走，自然而然的走出后门，不多工夫，已在土谷祠内了。
segs.en: Ah Q thought to himself, "Hmph, interesting. What on earth is this little widow up to?" Wishing to make inquiries, he edged closer to Zhao Sichen. Just then, he suddenly saw Old Master Zhao charging toward him, clutching a long bamboo pole. At the sight of that pole, it dawned on him that his previous thrashing might have had something to do with all this commotion. He turned and bolted, intending to flee back to the rice-pounding yard, but unexpectedly, the bamboo pole blocked his path. He turned again and scrambled away, naturally finding himself slipping out the back gate; in no time at all, he was back inside the Earth Temple.

segs.zh: 阿Q坐了一会，皮肤有些起栗，他觉得冷了，因为虽在春季，而夜间颇有馀寒，尚不宜于赤膊。他也记得布衫留在赵家，但倘若去取，又深怕秀才的竹杠。然而地保进来了。
segs.en: Ah Q sat for a while, his skin beginning to prickle; he felt the cold, for although it was spring, the nights still held a lingering chill, not yet fit for going shirtless. He remembered that his cloth tunic was still at the Zhao house, but he was deeply afraid that if he went to fetch it, he would run into the scholar’s bamboo pole. Just then, however, the village headman walked in.

segs.zh: 『阿Q，你的妈妈的！你连赵家的用人都调戏起来，简直是造反。害得我晚上没有觉睡，你的妈妈的！……』
segs.en: Ah Q, you son of a bitch! You’ve even started molesting the Zhao family’s maid—that’s practically treason. You’ve kept me awake half the night, you son of a bitch!...

segs.zh: 如是云云的教训了一通，阿Q自然没有话。临末，因为在晚上，应该送地保加倍酒钱四百文，阿Q正没有现钱，便用一顶毡帽做抵押，并且订定了五条件：
segs.en: After this long-winded lecture, Ah Q naturally had nothing to say. Finally, as it was already night, he was expected to pay the bailiff double the usual wine money—four hundred cash. Ah Q, having no ready cash on hand, offered his felt hat as security and agreed to the following five conditions:

segs.zh: 一、明天用红烛——要一斤重的——一对，香一封，到赵府上去赔罪。
segs.en: Tomorrow, you are to take a pair of red candles—the one-pound kind—and a packet of incense to the Zhao residence to offer an apology.

segs.zh: 二、赵府上请道士祓除缢鬼，费用由阿Q负担。
segs.en: Second, the Zhao household engaged a Taoist priest to exorcise the ghost of the hanged man, and the expenses were charged to Ah Q.

segs.zh: 三、阿Q从此不准踏进赵府的门槛。
segs.en: From that day on, Ah Q was forbidden to set foot across the threshold of the Zhao house.

segs.zh: 四、吴妈此后倘有不测，惟阿Q是问。
segs.en: 4. Should any misfortune befall Wu Ma hereafter, Ah Q alone shall be held to account.

segs.zh: 五、阿Q不准再去索取工钱和布衫。
segs.en: 5. Ah Q was forbidden from ever again asking for his wages or his tunic.

segs.zh: 阿Q自然都答应了，可惜没有钱。幸而已经春天，棉被可以无用，便质了二千大钱，履行条约。赤膊磕头之后，居然还剩几文，他也不再赎毡帽，统统喝了酒了。但赵家也并不烧香点烛，因为太太拜佛的时候可以用，留着了。那破布衫是大半做了少奶奶八月间生下来的孩子的衬尿布，那小半破烂的便都做了吴妈的鞋底。
segs.en: Ah Q, of course, agreed to everything, but unfortunately, he had no money. Fortunately, it was already spring and his cotton quilt was no longer needed, so he pawned it for two thousand cash to fulfill the terms of the agreement. After he had kowtowed bare-chested, he still had a few coins left over; he didn't bother to redeem his felt hat, but spent the lot on drink. The Zhao family, however, did not burn any incense or candles, as the mistress needed them for her own devotions and kept them for herself. As for the tattered tunic, the better part of it was made into diapers for the baby the young mistress had given birth to in the eighth month, while the remaining scraps were all used to make soles for Wu Ma’s shoes.

segs.zh: 阿Q礼毕之后，仍旧回到土谷祠，太阳下去了，渐渐觉得世上有些古怪。他仔细一想，终于省悟过来：其原因盖在自己的赤膊。他记得破夹袄还在，便披在身上，躺倒了，待张开眼睛，原来太阳又已经照在西墙上头了。他坐起身，一面说道：『妈妈的……』
segs.en: After completing his bows, Ah Q returned to the Earth Temple. The sun had gone down, and he began to feel that something in the world was slightly amiss. He pondered this carefully until it finally dawned on him: the reason lay in his being bare-chested. Remembering that his tattered lined jacket was still around, he draped it over his shoulders and lay down. When he opened his eyes again, the sun was already shining on the west wall. He sat up and muttered, "Damn it all..."

segs.zh: 他起来之后，也仍旧在街上逛，虽然不比赤膊之有切肤之痛，却又渐渐的觉得世上有些古怪了。仿佛从这一天起，未庄的女人们忽然都怕了羞，伊们一见阿Q走来，便个个躲进门里去。甚而至于将近五十岁的邹七嫂，也跟着别人乱钻，而且将十一岁的女儿都叫进去了。阿Q很以为奇，而且想：『这些东西忽然都学起小姐模样来了。这娼妇们……』
segs.en: Once he had risen, he went strolling through the streets as usual. Though it did not sting quite like a shirtless encounter, he began to feel, little by little, that there was something strange about the world. It was as if, from this day on, all the women in Weizhuang had suddenly become bashful; the moment they saw Ah Q approaching, they would each scurry behind their doors. Even Zou Qisao, who was nearing fifty, joined the others in this frantic hiding, and she even called her eleven-year-old daughter inside as well. Ah Q found this most peculiar, and he thought to himself: "These creatures have suddenly started acting like young ladies of high birth. The hussies..."

segs.zh: 但他更觉得世上有些古怪，却是许多日以后的事。其一、酒店不肯赊欠了；其二、管土谷祠的老头子说些废话，似乎叫他走；其三、他虽然记不清多少日，但确乎有许多日，没有一个人来叫他做短工。酒店不赊，熬着也罢了；老头子催他走，噜苏一通也就算了；只是没有人来叫他做短工，却使阿Q肚子饿：这委实是一件非常『妈妈的』的事情。
segs.en: It was only much later that he began to feel there was something strange about the world. For one thing, the tavern would no longer let him run a tab; for another, the old man who looked after the Earth Temple kept spouting nonsense, as if he wanted him to leave; and thirdly, although he could not remember exactly how many days it had been, it was certainly a long time since anyone had come to hire him for odd jobs. If the tavern wouldn’t give credit, he could endure the hunger; if the old man pestered him to leave, he could just brush it off with a few choice words; but the fact that no one came to hire him for odd jobs meant that Ah Q had an empty belly, and that, in truth, was a damnably "mother-of-a" situation.

segs.zh: 阿Q忍不下去了，他只好到老主顾的家里去探问，——但独不许踏进赵府的门槛，——然而情形也异样：一定走出一个男人来，现了十分烦厌的相貌，像回复乞丐一般的摇手道：——
segs.en: Ah Q could endure it no longer; he had no choice but to go to the homes of his regular customers to make inquiries—though he was strictly forbidden to cross the threshold of the Zhao residence. Yet, the situation was strange: invariably, a man would emerge, wearing an expression of intense annoyance, and wave his hand as if dismissing a beggar, saying:

segs.zh: 『没有没有！你出去！』
segs.en: No, no! Get out!

segs.zh: 阿Q愈觉得希奇了。他想，这些人家向来少不了要帮忙，不至于现在忽然都无事，这总该有些蹊跷在里面了。他留心打听，才知道他们有事都去叫小Don，这小D，是一个穷小子，又瘦又乏，在阿Q的眼睛里，位置是在王胡之下的，谁料这小子竟谋了他的饭碗去。所以阿Q这一气，更与平常不同，当气愤愤的走着的时候，忽然将手一扬，唱道：
segs.en: Ah Q found this increasingly strange. He reasoned that these families had always needed help, and it was unlikely they suddenly had nothing to be done; there had to be something fishy going on. He took care to make inquiries, only to discover that whenever they had work, they now called for Little D. This Little D was a scrawny, wretched pauper, someone whom Ah Q held in even lower regard than Wang Hu; who would have guessed that this brat had actually stolen his livelihood? Ah Q’s indignation was, therefore, unlike anything he had felt before. As he strode along, fuming, he suddenly flung up his hand and sang:

segs.zh: 『我手执钢鞭将你打！……』
segs.en: With a steel whip in my hand, I shall strike you down!...

segs.zh: 几天之后，他竟在钱府的照壁前遇见了小D。『仇人相见分外眼明』，阿Q便迎上去，小D也站住了。
segs.en: A few days later, he unexpectedly ran into Little D in front of the screen wall at the Qian residence. As the saying goes, "When enemies meet, their eyes are especially sharp." Ah Q strode up to him, and Little D stood his ground.

segs.zh: 『畜生！』阿Q怒目而视的说，嘴角上飞出唾沫来。
segs.en: "Beast!" Ah Q said, glaring with rage, flecks of spittle flying from the corners of his mouth.

segs.zh: 『我是虫豸，好么？……』小D说。
segs.en: "I'm a worm, am I? ... right?" said Little D.

segs.zh: 这谦逊反使阿Q更加愤怒起来，但他手里没有钢鞭，于是只得扑上去，伸手去拔小D的辫子。小D一手护住了自己的辫根，一手也来拔阿Q的辫子，阿Q便也将空着的一只手护住了自己的辫根。从先前的阿Q看来，小D本来是不足齿数的，但他近来挨了饿，又瘦又乏已经不下于小D，所以便成了势均力敌的现象，四只手拔着两颗头，都弯了腰，在钱家粉墙上映出一个蓝色的虹形，至于半点钟之久了。
segs.en: This humility only served to incense Ah Q all the more. Lacking a steel whip, he had no choice but to pounce and reach for Little D’s queue. Little D used one hand to protect the base of his own braid while reaching out with the other to grab Ah Q’s. Ah Q, in turn, used his free hand to shield the base of his own. To the Ah Q of old, Little D would have been beneath notice, but having recently suffered from hunger, Ah Q was now as lean and exhausted as his opponent, resulting in a perfectly matched struggle. With four hands tugging at two heads, both men bent low, casting a blue, rainbow-shaped shadow against the white-washed wall of the Qian household, where they remained locked in stalemate for fully half an hour.

segs.zh: 『好了，好了！』看的人们说，大约是解劝的。
segs.en: "All right, all right!" said the onlookers, presumably by way of mediation.

segs.zh: 『好，好！』看的人们说，不知道是解劝，是颂扬，还是煽动。
segs.en: "Good, good!" the onlookers said, though it was impossible to tell whether they were trying to intervene, offering praise, or merely egging him on.

segs.zh: 然而他们都不听。阿Q进三步，小D便退三步，都站着；小D进三步，阿Q便退三步，又都站着。大约半点钟，——未庄少有自鸣钟，所以很难说，或者二十分，——他们的头发里便都冒烟，额上便都流汗，阿Q的手放松了，在同一瞬间，小D的手也正放松了，同时直起，同时退开，都挤出人丛去。
segs.en: Yet neither of them would listen. Ah Q would advance three paces, and Little D would retreat three paces, then both would stand still; then Little D would advance three paces, and Ah Q would retreat three paces, and again both would stand still. After about half an hour—though as Weizhuang rarely possessed striking clocks it is hard to say, perhaps it was twenty minutes—steam began to rise from their hair and sweat to stream down their foreheads. Ah Q’s grip slackened, and at that very same instant, Little D’s grip also slackened. Simultaneously they straightened up, simultaneously they backed away, and both squeezed their way out of the crowd.

segs.zh: 『记着罢，妈妈的……』阿Q回过头去说。
segs.en: "Remember that, you son of a..." Ah Q said, turning his head back.

segs.zh: 『妈妈的，记着罢……』小D也回过头来说。
segs.en: "Damn it, you’d better remember that..." Little D said, turning his head back to add.

segs.zh: 这一场『龙虎斗』似乎并无胜败，也不知道看的人可满足，都没有发什么议论，而阿Q却仍然没有人来叫他做短工。
segs.en: This "Dragon-Tiger Fight" seemed to end in a draw, and whether the spectators were satisfied or not, no one offered any comment. As for Ah Q, there was still no one coming to hire him for odd jobs.

segs.zh: 有一日很温和，微风拂拂的颇有些夏意了，阿Q却觉得寒冷起来，但这还可担当，第一倒是肚子饿。棉被、毡帽、布衫早已没有了，其次就卖了棉袄；现在有裤子，却万不可脱的；有破夹袄，又除了送人做鞋底之外，决定卖不出钱。他早想在路上拾得一注钱，但至今还没有见；他想在自己的破屋里忽然寻到一注钱，慌张的四顾，但屋内是空虚而且了然。于是他决计出门求食去了。
segs.en: It was a very mild day, with a gentle breeze that carried a hint of summer, yet Ah Q felt a chill. This, however, he could still bear; his primary trouble was hunger. His quilt, felt hat, and cloth gown were long gone, followed by his cotton-padded jacket. He still had his trousers, but those he absolutely could not take off; as for his tattered lined jacket, it was fit for nothing but to be given away to serve as shoe-soles, and certainly could not be sold for a cent. He had long hoped to stumble upon a pile of money on the road, but none had appeared; he had hoped to suddenly discover a pile of money in his own dilapidated hut, and had looked around frantically, but the room was empty and bare. And so, he resolved to go out and beg for food.

segs.zh: 他在路上走着要『求食』，看见熟识的酒店，看见熟识的馒头，但他都走过了，不但没有暂停，而且并不想要。他所求的不是这类东西了；他求的是什么东西，他自己不知道。
segs.en: He walked along the road in search of "sustenance," seeing familiar taverns and familiar steamed buns, yet he passed them all by. He did not pause, nor did he feel the slightest desire for them. It was no longer such things that he sought; as for what it was he truly sought, he did not know himself.

segs.zh: 未庄本不是大村镇，不多时便走尽了。村外都是水田，满眼是新秧的嫩绿，夹着几个圆形的活动的黑点，便是耕田的农夫。阿Q并不赏鉴这田家乐，却只是走，因为他直觉的知道这与他的『求食』之道是很辽远的。但他终于走到静修庵的墙外了。
segs.en: Weizhuang was no great town, and in no time at all he had reached the end of it. Beyond the village lay paddy fields, a vast expanse of tender green rice shoots, broken only by a few round, moving black specks—the farmers at their plowing. Ah Q had no appreciation for this rustic idyll; he simply kept on walking, for he felt instinctively that such things were a far cry from his own "way of seeking a living." Yet, at long last, he arrived outside the wall of the Jingxiu Nunnery.

segs.zh: 庵周围也是水田，粉墙突出在新绿里，后面的低土墙里是菜园。阿Q迟疑了一会，四面一看，并没有人。他便爬上这矮墙去，扯着何首乌藤，但泥土仍然簌簌的掉，阿Q的脚也索索的抖；终于攀着桑树枝，跳到里面了。里面真是郁郁葱葱，但似乎并没有黄酒馒头，以及此外可吃的之类。靠西墙是竹丛，下面许多笋，只可惜都是并未煮熟的，还有油菜早经结子，芥菜已将开花，小白菜也很老了。
segs.en: The monastery was surrounded by paddy fields, its whitewashed walls standing out against the fresh green, while behind them lay a vegetable garden enclosed by a low earthen wall. Ah Q hesitated for a moment, glancing around; there was no one in sight. He scrambled up the low wall, clutching at the knotweed vines, but the earth kept crumbling away in rustles, and his legs trembled uncontrollably. Finally, he caught hold of a mulberry branch and vaulted inside. The place was indeed lush and verdant, but there seemed to be no sign of yellow wine, steamed buns, or anything else edible. Against the west wall grew a thicket of bamboo, beneath which stood many bamboo shoots, though unfortunately they were all raw; there were also rapeseed plants that had already gone to seed, mustard greens on the verge of flowering, and the bok choy was already far too old.

segs.zh: 阿Q仿佛文童落第似的觉得很冤屈，他慢慢走近园门去，忽而非常惊喜了，这分明是一畦老萝卜。他于是蹲下便拔，而门口突然伸出一个很圆的头来，又即缩回去了，这分明是小尼姑。小尼姑之流是阿Q本来视若草芥的，但世事须『退一步想』，所以他便赶紧拔起四个萝卜，拧下青叶，兜在大襟里。然而老尼姑已经出来了！
segs.en: Ah Q felt as aggrieved as a schoolboy who had failed his examinations. He walked slowly toward the garden gate, when suddenly, a surge of pleasant surprise washed over him: it was a patch of mature radishes. He squatted down and began to pull them up, but a round head suddenly poked out from the doorway, only to be as quickly withdrawn; it was clearly the little nun. Ah Q had always held the likes of little nuns in utter contempt, but worldly affairs, he reasoned, required one to "take a step back." He hurriedly pulled up four radishes, twisted off their green tops, and stuffed them into the front of his tunic. But the old nun had already come out!

segs.zh: 『阿弥陀佛，阿Q，你怎么跳进园里来偸萝卜！……阿呀，罪过呵，阿唷，阿弥陀佛！……』
segs.en: Amitabha! Ah Q, how could you jump into the garden to steal radishes! ... Oh, such a sin, oh dear, Amitabha!

segs.zh: 『我什么时候跳进你的园里来偸萝卜？』阿Q且看且走的说。
segs.en: "When did I ever jump into your garden to steal radishes?" Ah Q said, looking back as he walked away.

segs.zh: 『现在……这不是？』老尼姑指着他的衣兜。
segs.en: "Now... isn't that it?" The old nun pointed to his pocket.

segs.zh: 『这是你的？你能叫得他答应你么？你……』
segs.en: Is this yours? Can you make it answer you if you call it? You...

segs.zh: 阿Q没有说完话，拔步便跑；追来的是一匹很肥大的黑狗。这本来在前门的，不知怎的到后园来了。黑狗哼而且追，已经要咬着阿Q的腿，幸而从衣兜里落下一个萝卜来，那狗给一吓，略略一停，阿Q已经爬上桑树，跨到土墙，连人和萝卜都滚出墙外面了。只剩着黑狗还在对着桑树嗥，老尼姑念着佛。
segs.en: Ah Q did not finish his sentence; he turned and bolted. In pursuit was a very fat black dog. It had been at the front gate, but somehow or other it had made its way to the back garden. The black dog growled as it chased, and was just about to snap at Ah Q’s leg, when luckily a turnip tumbled from his pocket. Startled, the dog paused for a split second, giving Ah Q just enough time to scramble up the mulberry tree, vault onto the earthen wall, and go tumbling over to the other side, man and turnip alike. All that remained was the black dog baying at the mulberry tree, and the old nun chanting her prayers.

segs.zh: 阿Q怕尼姑又放出黑狗来，拾起萝卜便走。沿路又检了几块小石头，但黑狗却并不再出现。阿Q于是抛了石头，一面走一面吃，而且想道，这里也没有什么东西寻，不如进城去。……
segs.en: Fearing that the nun might release her black dog again, Ah Q snatched up the turnip and left. Along the way, he picked up a few small stones, but the black dog did not reappear. Ah Q tossed the stones aside, eating as he walked, and thought to himself, There is nothing worth looking for here; I might as well head into town.

segs.zh: 待三个萝卜吃完时，他已经打定了进城的主意了。
segs.en: By the time he had finished the three radishes, he had made up his mind to go to town.

segs.zh: 在未庄再看见阿Q出现的时候，是刚过了这年的中秋。人们都惊异，说是阿Q回来了，于是又回上去想道，他先前那里去了呢？阿Q前几回的上城，大抵早就兴高采烈的对人说，但这一次却并不，所以也没有一个人留心到。他或者也曾告诉过管土谷祠的老头子，然而未庄老例，只有赵太爷、钱太爷和秀才太爷上城才算一件事。假洋鬼子尚且不足数，何况是阿Q：因此老头子也就不替他宣传，而未庄的社会上也就无从知道了。
segs.en: When Ah Q reappeared in Weizhuang, it was just after the Mid-Autumn Festival of that year. Everyone was surprised, saying, "Ah Q is back!" and then they began to wonder where he had been all this time. Whenever Ah Q had gone to town in the past, he would generally have been in high spirits and told everyone about it, but this time he had not, and so no one had paid him any mind. He might have mentioned it to the old man who looked after the Tutu Temple, but according to the established customs of Weizhuang, only the visits to town by Mr. Zhao, Mr. Qian, or the Scholar counted as notable events. Even the Fake Foreign Devil hardly counted, let alone Ah Q; thus, the old man did not bother to spread the word, and the society of Weizhuang remained none the wiser.

segs.zh: 但阿Q这回的回来，却与先前大不同，确乎很值得惊异。天色将黑，他睡眼矇眬的在酒店门前出现了，他走近柜台，从腰间伸出手来，满把是银的和铜的，在柜上一扔说：『现钱！打酒来！』穿的是新夹袄，看去腰间还挂着一个大搭连，沈钿钿的将裤带坠成了很弯很弯的弧线。未庄老例，看见略有些醒目的人物，是与其慢也宁敬的，现在虽然明知道是阿Q，但因为和破夹袄的阿Q有些两样了，古人云：『士别三日便当刮目相待』，所以堂倌、掌柜、酒客、路人，便自然显出一种疑而且敬的形态来。掌柜既先之以点头，又继之以谈话：
segs.en: But this return of Ah Q was vastly different from before, and truly worthy of astonishment. As dusk began to fall, he appeared before the tavern, eyes bleary with sleep. He approached the counter, reached into his waistband, and flung a handful of silver and copper coins onto the wood, declaring, "Hard cash! Bring me wine!" He was wearing a new lined jacket, and hanging from his waist, one could see a large purse, so heavy it pulled his belt into a deep, sagging arc. According to the old custom of Weizhuang, when one encounters a person of some note, it is better to be respectful than dismissive; and though they knew perfectly well it was Ah Q, he was so unlike the Ah Q in the tattered jacket that the ancients' saying, "When a scholar has been away for three days, one must look at him with new eyes," naturally took hold. The waiter, the proprietor, the patrons, and the passersby all instinctively adopted an air of suspicion mingled with respect. The proprietor, having led the way with a nod, followed up with a conversation:

segs.zh: 『嚄，阿Q，你回来了！』
segs.en: Oh, Ah Q, you’re back!

segs.zh: 『回来了。』
segs.en: Back at last.

segs.zh: 『发财发财，你是——在……』
segs.en: Getting rich, getting rich, you are—at...

segs.zh: 『上城去了！』
segs.en: Off to the city!

segs.zh: 这一件新闻，第二天便传遍了全未庄。人人都愿意知道现钱和新夹袄的阿Q的中兴史，所以在酒店里，茶馆里，庙檐下，便渐渐的探听出来了。这结果，是阿Q得了新敬畏。
segs.en: This piece of news spread throughout Weizhuang by the following day. Everyone was eager to hear the story of Ah Q’s resurgence, complete with his ready cash and new lined jacket, so bit by bit, through inquiries made in the tavern, the teahouse, and under the temple eaves, the truth was ferreted out. The result was that Ah Q gained a new measure of awe.

segs.zh: 据阿Q说，他是在举人老爷家里帮忙，这一节，听的人都肃然了。这老爷本姓白，但因为合城里只有他一个举人，所以不必再冠姓，说起举人来就是他。这也不独在未庄是如此，便是一百里方圆之内也都如此，人们几乎多以为他的姓名就叫举人老爷的了。在这人的府上帮忙，那当然是可敬的。但据阿Q又说，他却不高兴再帮忙了，因为这举人老爷实在太『妈妈的』了。这一节，听的人都叹息而且快意，因为阿Q本不配在举人老爷家里帮忙，而不帮忙是可惜的。
segs.en: According to Ah Q, he had been working for the Ju-ren master, a claim that invariably filled his listeners with awe. The master’s actual surname was Bai, but as he was the only Ju-ren in the entire town, there was no need to prefix his name; to mention the Ju-ren was to speak of him. This was true not only in Weizhuang but for a hundred li around, to the point that people almost assumed his given name was "Ju-ren Master." To work in such a man’s household was, naturally, an honorable thing. Yet, according to Ah Q, he was no longer happy to work there, for the Ju-ren master was truly a "son-of-a-bitch." At this, his listeners would sigh while feeling a sense of satisfaction, for they felt that Ah Q had never been worthy of working in the Ju-ren master’s house in the first place, and yet it was a pity that he no longer did.

segs.zh: 据阿Q说，他的回来，似乎也由于不满意城里人，这就在他们将长凳称为条凳，而且煎鱼用葱丝，加以最近观察所得的缺点，是女人的走路也扭得不很好。然而也偶有大可佩服的地方，即如未庄的乡下人不过打三十二张的竹牌，只有假洋鬼子能够叉『麻酱』，城里却连小乌龟子都叉得精熟的。什么假洋鬼子，只要放在城里的十几岁的小乌龟子的手里，也就立刻是『小鬼见阎王』。这一节，听的人都赧然了。
segs.en: According to Ah Q, his return was also prompted by a dissatisfaction with the city folk. This included their habit of calling a bench a "long stool," their practice of garnishing fried fish with shredded scallions, and, as he had recently observed, the fact that their women did not sway their hips very gracefully when they walked. Yet, there were occasionally things worthy of great admiration; for instance, while the villagers of Weizhuang played only with thirty-two bamboo tiles, and only the Fake Foreign Devil knew how to play "mahjong," even the little runts in the city were masters of the game. Any so-called Fake Foreign Devil, once placed in the hands of one of these teenage city urchins, would immediately find himself like "a small ghost meeting the King of Hell." At this point, his listeners would all look rather ashamed.

segs.zh: 『你们可看见过杀头么？』阿Q说，『咳，好看。杀革命党。唉，好看好看，……』他摇摇头，将唾沫飞在正对面的赵司晨的脸上。这一节，听的人都凛然了。但阿Q又四面一看，忽然扬起右手，照着伸长脖子听得出神的王胡的后项窝上直劈下去道：
segs.en: "Have you ever seen a beheading?" Ah Q said. "Ah, it’s a fine sight. Executing revolutionaries. Yes, a fine sight, a fine sight..." He shook his head, spraying spittle onto the face of Zhao Sichen, who was sitting directly opposite him. At this, those listening were struck with a sudden chill. But Ah Q, glancing around at everyone, suddenly raised his right hand and brought it down in a sharp chop against the nape of Wang Hu's neck, who had been listening with his neck stretched out in rapt attention, and said:
Snap!

segs.zh: 王胡惊得一跳，同时电光石火似的赶快缩了头，而听的人又都悚然而且欣然了。从此王胡瘟头瘟脑的许多日，并且再不敢走近阿Q的身边；别的人也一样。
segs.en: Wang Hu jumped in alarm, his head snapping back like a flash of lightning, while the listeners were all struck with a thrill of both horror and delight. From that day on, Wang Hu went about in a daze for many days, and never again dared to go near Ah Q; the others felt the same.

segs.zh: 阿Q这时在未庄人眼睛里的地位，虽不敢说超过赵太爷，但谓之差不多，大约也就没有什么语病的了。
segs.en: At this time, in the eyes of the people of Weizhuang, Ah Q’s status, while perhaps not quite on par with that of Mr. Zhao, was certainly not far off; to say they were roughly equal would be no great distortion of the truth.

segs.zh: 然而不多久，这阿Q的大名忽又传遍了未庄的闺中。虽然未庄只有钱、赵两姓是大屋，此外十之九都是浅闺，但闺中究竟是闺中，所以也算得一件神异。女人们见面时一定说，邹七嫂在阿Q那里买了一条蓝绸裙，旧固然是旧的，但只化了九角钱。还有赵白眼的母亲——一说是赵司晨的母亲，待考，——也买了一件孩子穿的大红洋纱衫，七成新，只用三百大钱九二串。于是伊们都眼巴巴的想见阿Q，缺绸裙的想问他买绸裙，要洋纱衫的想问他买洋纱衫，不但见了不逃避，有时阿Q已经走过了，也还要追上去叫住他，问道：
segs.en: It was not long, however, before Ah Q’s great name suddenly spread among the boudoirs of Weizhuang. Although the Qian and Zhao families were the only ones in Weizhuang with grand houses, and nine out of ten of the others were mere modest dwellings, a boudoir is a boudoir after all, so it could be considered quite a marvel. Whenever the women met, they were sure to say that Seventh Sister-in-law Zou had bought a blue silk skirt from Ah Q; it was old, to be sure, but it had only cost her ninety cents. Then there was Zhao Baiyan’s mother—or perhaps it was Zhao Sichen’s mother, the point remains to be verified—who had also bought a bright red foreign-yarn tunic for a child, seven-tenths new, for a mere three hundred copper cash. Consequently, they all began to gaze longingly for a glimpse of Ah Q. Those in need of silk skirts wanted to ask him for silk skirts, and those wanting foreign-yarn tunics wanted to ask him for tunics. Not only did they no longer flee at the sight of him, but sometimes, even after Ah Q had already walked past, they would chase after him, call him back, and ask:

segs.zh: 『阿Q，你还有绸裙么？没有？纱衫也要的，有罢？』
segs.en: Ah Q, do you have any silk skirts left? No? What about a gauze shirt, surely you have one of those?

segs.zh: 后来这终于从浅闺传进深闺里去了，因为邹七嫂得意之馀，将伊的绸裙请赵太太去赏鉴，赵太太又告诉了赵太爷而且着实恭维了一番。赵太爷便在晚饭桌上，和秀才大爷讨论，以为阿Q实在有些古怪，我们门窗应该小心些；但他的东西，不知道可还有什么可买，也许有点好东西罢。加以赵太太也正想买一件价廉物美的皮背心。于是家族决议，便托邹七嫂即刻去寻阿Q，而且为此新辟了第三种的例外：这晚上也姑且特准点油灯。
segs.en: Eventually, this news traveled from the outer chambers into the inner sanctum, for Mrs. Zou, in her exuberance, had invited Mrs. Zhao to admire her silk skirt. Mrs. Zhao, in turn, relayed the story to Old Master Zhao, adding a generous measure of flattery. At the dinner table that evening, Old Master Zhao discussed the matter with the eldest scholar of the family, concluding that Ah Q was indeed a strange fellow and that they ought to be more careful with their doors and windows. Yet, he mused, one could not be sure what other wares he might have for sale—perhaps there were some decent things among them. Furthermore, Mrs. Zhao happened to be in the market for a fur vest that was both affordable and of good quality. Consequently, the family reached a resolution: they would commission Mrs. Zou to seek out Ah Q at once. To this end, they carved out a third exception to their rules: for this night only, they would permit the lighting of an oil lamp.

segs.zh: 油灯干了不少了，阿Q还不到。赵府的全眷都很焦急，打着呵欠，或恨阿Q太飘忽，或怨邹七嫂不上紧。赵太太还怕他因为春天的条件不敢来，而赵太爷以为不足虑：因为这是『我』去叫他的。果然，到底赵太爷有见识，阿Q终于跟着邹七嫂进来了。
segs.en: The oil lamp had burned quite low, but Ah Q had still not arrived. The entire household of the Zhao mansion was growing anxious; they stifled yawns, some cursing Ah Q for his flightiness, others complaining that Mrs. Zou Qi had not been diligent enough. Mrs. Zhao even worried that he might be too intimidated by the spring terms to show his face, but Master Zhao dismissed such fears, confident that since it was he who had sent for the man, there was nothing to worry about. And indeed, Master Zhao’s judgment proved sound: in the end, Ah Q finally followed Mrs. Zou Qi through the door.

segs.zh: 『他只说没有没有，我说你自己当面说去，他还要说，我说……』邹七嫂气喘吁吁的走着说。
segs.en: "He just kept saying 'no, no,' and I told him, 'Go and say that to their faces yourself.' But he still insisted on rambling, so I said..." Zou Qisao panted as she walked, talking all the way.

segs.zh: 『太爷！』阿Q似笑非笑的叫了一声，在檐下站住了。
segs.en: "Master!" Ah Q called out, a smirk on his face, and came to a halt under the eaves.

segs.zh: 『阿Q，听说你在外面发财。』赵太爷踱开去，眼睛打量着他的全身，一面说。『那很好，那很好的。这个，……听说你有些旧东西，……可以都拿来看一看，……这也并不是别的，因为我倒要……』
segs.en: Ah Q, I hear you’ve made a fortune out there." Old Master Zhao paced away, eyeing him up and down as he spoke. "That’s very good, very good indeed. This... I hear you have some old things... you might as well bring them over for me to have a look... it’s nothing else, really, it’s just that I happen to...

segs.zh: 『我对邹七嫂说过了。都完了。』
segs.en: I’ve already told Seventh Sister Zou. It’s all over.

segs.zh: 『完了？』赵太爷不觉失声的说，『那里会完得这样快呢？』
segs.en: Finished?" Master Zhao exclaimed involuntarily. "How could it be over so soon?

segs.zh: 『那是朋友的，本来不多。他们买了些。……』
segs.en: That belonged to a friend, and there wasn't much of it to begin with. They bought some of it. ...

segs.zh: 『总该还有一点罢。』
segs.en: There must still be a little left, surely.

segs.zh: 『现在，只剩了一张门幕了。』
segs.en: Now, only a door curtain remains.

segs.zh: 『就拿门幕来看看罢。』赵太太慌忙说。
segs.en: "Let's just take a look at the door curtain," Mrs. Zhao said in a flurry.

segs.zh: 『那么，明天拿来就是』，赵太爷却不甚热心了。『阿Q，你以后有什么东西的时候，你尽先送来给我们看，……』
segs.en: In that case, just bring it over tomorrow,” Master Zhao said, though he no longer seemed particularly interested. “Ah Q, from now on, whenever you have anything, you must bring it to us first to have a look at…

segs.zh: 『价钱决不会比别家出得少！』秀才说。秀才娘子忙一瞥阿Q的脸，看他感动了没有。
segs.en: "The price will certainly not be lower than what anyone else offers!" the scholar said. The scholar's wife immediately darted a glance at Ah Q's face, watching to see if he had been moved.

segs.zh: 『我要一件皮背心。』赵太太说。
segs.en: "I want a fur vest," Mrs. Zhao said.

segs.zh: 阿Q虽然答应着，却懒洋洋的出去了，也不知道他是否放在心上。这使赵太爷很失望，气忿而且担心，至于停止了打呵欠。秀才对于阿Q的态度也很不平，于是说，这忘八蛋要提防，或者竟不如吩咐地保，不许他住在未庄。但赵太爷以为不然，说这也怕要结怨，况且做这路生意的大概是『老鹰不吃窝下食』，本村倒不必担心的；只要自己夜里警醒的就是了。秀才听了这『庭训』，非常之以为然，便即刻撤消了驱逐阿Q的提议，而且叮嘱邹七嫂，请伊万不要向人提起这一段话。
segs.en: Ah Q gave his assent, but he wandered out sluggishly, and there was no telling whether he had taken it to heart. This greatly disappointed Master Zhao, leaving him so indignant and anxious that he even stopped yawning. The Xiucai was also incensed by Ah Q’s attitude, suggesting that the bastard ought to be watched, or perhaps they should simply instruct the bailiff to forbid him from living in Weizhuang. Master Zhao, however, disagreed; he said that might breed resentment, and besides, those in that line of work generally lived by the rule that a hawk does not prey on its own nest, so there was no need to worry about the village itself—one simply had to stay alert at night. Upon hearing this "paternal instruction," the Xiucai found it entirely reasonable; he immediately withdrew his proposal to expel Ah Q and cautioned Mrs. Zou not to mention a word of this conversation to anyone.

segs.zh: 但第二日，邹七嫂便将那蓝裙去染了皂，又将阿Q可疑之点传扬出去了，可是确没有提起秀才要驱逐他这一节。然而这已经于阿Q很不利。最先，地保寻上门了，取了他的门幕去，阿Q说是赵太太要看的，而地保也不还，并且要议定每月的孝敬钱。其次，是村人对于他的敬畏忽而变相了，虽然还不敢来放肆，却很有远避的神情，而这神情和先前的防他来『嚓』的时候又不同，颇混着『敬而远之』的分子了。
segs.en: By the following day, however, Mrs. Zou Qi had already had her blue skirt dyed black, and had begun spreading the word about Ah Q’s suspicious behavior—though she was careful not to mention the scholar’s demand that he be expelled. Even so, this was already highly disadvantageous to Ah Q. First, the village headman came knocking to seize his door-curtain. When Ah Q claimed Mrs. Zhao wanted to see it, the headman refused to return it, and furthermore demanded a monthly tribute. Next, the villagers’ attitude of awe and dread toward him underwent a subtle change. While they still dared not take liberties with him, they began to show a distinct air of keeping their distance—a manner quite different from their earlier fear that he might "snip" off their queues, now mixed with a heavy dose of "respectful avoidance."

segs.zh: 只有一班闲人们却还要寻根究底的去探阿Q的细底。阿Q也并不讳饰，傲然的说出他的经验来。从此他们才知道，他不过是一个小脚色，不但不能上墙，并且不能进洞，只站在门外接东西。有一夜，他刚才接到一个包，正手再进去，不一会，只听得里面大嚷起来，他便赶紧跑，连夜爬出城，逃回未庄来了，从此不敢再去做。然而这故事却于阿Q更不利，村人对于阿Q的『敬而远之』者，本因为怕结怨，谁料他不过是一个不敢再偸的偸儿呢？这实在是『斯亦不足畏也矣』。
segs.en: Only the idlers, however, insisted on getting to the bottom of Ah Q’s past. Ah Q, making no attempt to conceal anything, recounted his experiences with a haughty air. That was when they learned he was nothing more than a small-time operator: he could neither scale walls nor slip through holes, but merely stood outside the door to catch what was passed out to him. One night, he had just caught a bundle and was about to reach back in for more, when suddenly there was a great hue and cry from inside. He took to his heels at once, scrambled out of the city that very night, and fled back to Weizhuang, never daring to try his hand at it again. Yet this story did Ah Q even more harm. The villagers had previously kept their distance from him out of a wary respect, fearing he might bear a grudge; who would have thought he was merely a thief who no longer dared to steal? Truly, he was no longer to be feared.

segs.zh: 宣统三年九月十四日——即阿Q将搭连卖给赵白眼的这一天——三更四点，有一只大乌篷船到了赵府上的河埠头。这船从黑魆魆中荡来，乡下人睡得熟，都没有知道；出去时将近黎明，却很有几个看见的了。据探头探脑的调查来的结果，知道那竟是举人老爷的船！
segs.en: On the fourteenth day of the ninth month in the third year of the Xuantong reign—the very day Ah Q sold his money-belt to Zhao Baiyan—at the fourth watch past midnight, a large boat with a black awning pulled up to the Zhao family’s wharf. The boat had drifted in from the pitch-black darkness, and as the country folk were all sound asleep, no one noticed. By the time it left, near daybreak, quite a few people had seen it. According to the results of some prying and poking about, it turned out to have been none other than the boat of the Master Scholar himself!

segs.zh: 那船便将大不安载给了未庄，不到正午，全村的人心就很摇动。船的使命，赵家本来是很秘密的，但茶坊酒肆里却都说，革命党要进城，举人老爷到我们乡下来逃难了。惟有邹七嫂不以为然，说那不过是几口破衣箱，举人老爷想来寄存的，却已被赵太爷回复转去。其实举人老爷和赵秀才素不相能，在理本不能有『共患难』的情谊，况且邹七嫂又和赵家是邻居，见闻较为切近，所以大概该是伊对的。
segs.en: The boat brought great unease to Weizhuang, and by midday the entire village was in a state of agitation. The mission of the boat had been kept strictly secret by the Zhao family, yet in the teahouses and wine shops, everyone was saying that the revolutionaries were about to enter the city and the local Scholar had come to the countryside to seek refuge. Only Mrs. Zou Seventh disagreed, claiming it was nothing but a few trunks of old clothes the Scholar had wanted to store, only to be turned away by Master Zhao. In truth, the Scholar and Mr. Zhao had never gotten along, so there was logically no reason for them to share in each other’s misfortunes; moreover, as Mrs. Zou Seventh was a neighbor to the Zhao household and thus better informed, her version of events was likely the correct one.

segs.zh: 然而谣言很旺盛，说举人老爷虽然似乎没有亲到，却有一封长信，和赵家排了『转折亲』。赵太爷肚里一轮，觉得于他总不会有坏处，便将箱子留下了，现就塞在太太的床底下。至于革命党，有的说是便在这一夜进了城，个个白盔白甲：穿着崇正皇帝的素。
segs.en: Rumors, however, were flourishing. It was said that although the Master Scholar had not appeared in person, he had sent a long letter and arranged a "linked-kin" marriage with the Zhao family. Master Zhao turned the matter over in his mind and, deciding it could do him no harm, kept the trunk; it was now tucked away under his wife’s bed. As for the revolutionaries, some claimed they would enter the city that very night, all of them in white helmets and white armor—dressed in mourning for the Chongzhen Emperor.

segs.zh: 阿Q的耳朵里，本来早听到过革命党这一句话，今年又亲眼见过杀掉革命党。但他有一种不知从那里来的意见，以为革命党便是造反，造反便是与他为难，所以一向是『深恶而痛绝之』的。殊不料这却使百里闻名的举人老爷有这样怕，于是他未免也有些『神往』了，况且未庄的一群鸟男女的慌张的神情，也使阿Q更快意。
segs.en: Ah Q had long since heard the term "revolutionaries," and that very year he had even witnessed them being put to the sword. Yet, he harbored a notion—from where, he could not say—that to be a revolutionary was to be a rebel, and that to be a rebel was to be his enemy; thus, he had always held them in "deep loathing and bitter hatred." Little did he expect, however, that these same people would strike such terror into the heart of the venerable Scholar, whose reputation spanned a hundred miles. This left Ah Q feeling somewhat "enchanted," and the frantic expressions of the wretches in Weizhuang only added to his delight.

segs.zh: 『革命也好罢』，阿Q想，『革这伙妈妈的命，太可恶！太可恨！……便是我，也要投降革命党了。』
segs.en: Revolution, then, let it be," Ah Q thought. "To revolt against this lot of bastards—too detestable! Too hateful! ... I, too, shall surrender to the revolutionary party.

segs.zh: 阿Q近来用度窘，大约略略有些不平；加以午间吃了两碗空肚酒，愈加醉得快，一面想一面走，便又飘飘然起来。不知怎样一来，忽而似乎革命党便是自己，未庄人却都是他的俘虏了，他得意之馀，禁不住大声的嚷道：
segs.en: Lately, Ah Q had been short of money, which perhaps left him feeling a little disgruntled. To make matters worse, he had downed two bowls of wine on an empty stomach at noon, making him drunk all the faster. As he walked, he pondered, and soon he began to feel lightheaded and buoyant. Somehow, he suddenly imagined himself to be a revolutionary, and all the people of Weizhuang to be his captives. In the flush of his triumph, he could not help but shout at the top of his lungs:

segs.zh: 『造反了！造反了！』
segs.en: Rebellion! Rebellion!

segs.zh: 未庄人都用了惊惧的眼光对他看。这一种可怜的眼光，是阿Q从来没有见过的，一见之下，又使他舒服得如六月里喝了雪水。他更加高兴的走而且喊道：
segs.en: The people of Weizhuang looked at him with eyes full of dread. It was a pitiable sort of gaze, the likes of which Ah Q had never encountered before; the very sight of it made him feel as refreshed as if he had drunk iced water in the heat of June. More elated than ever, he walked on, shouting:

segs.zh: 『好，……我要什么就要什么，我欢喜谁就是谁。得得，锵锵！悔不该，酒醉了错斩了郑贤弟，悔不该，呀呀呀……得得，锵锵，得，锵令锵！我手执钢鞭将你打……』
segs.en: Fine... I’ll have whatever I want, I’ll fancy whoever I please. De-de, qiang-qiang! I regret that, in my drunken state, I wrongly slew Brother Zheng, I regret that, ya-ya-ya... De-de, qiang-qiang, de, qiang-ling-qiang! With steel whip in hand, I’ll strike you down...

segs.zh: 赵府上的两位男人和两个真本家，也正站在大门口论革命。阿Q没有见，昂了头直唱过去。
segs.en: The two men from the Zhao household and two of their true clansmen were also standing at the main gate, holding forth on the subject of the revolution. Ah Q, ignoring them, tilted his head back and sang loudly as he marched right past.
Clatter, clatter, …

segs.zh: 『老Q』，赵太爷怯怯的迎着低声的叫。
segs.en: "Ah Q," Master Zhao called out in a low, timid voice as he went to greet him.

segs.zh: 『锵锵』，阿Q料不到他的名字会和『老』字联结起来，以为是一句别的话，与己无干，只是唱。『得，锵，锵令锵，锵！』
segs.en: Clang-clang!" Ah Q, never expecting his name to be linked with the word "Old," assumed it was some other remark that had nothing to do with him, and simply went on singing, "Da, qiang, qiang-ling-qiang, qiang!

segs.zh: 『老Q。』
segs.en: Old Q.

segs.zh: 『悔不该……』
segs.en: I shouldn't have...

segs.zh: 『阿Q！』秀才只得直呼其名了。
segs.en: "Ah Q!" the Scholar had no choice but to call him by his name.

segs.zh: 阿Q这才站住，歪着头问道：『什么？』
segs.en: Ah Q finally came to a halt, cocked his head, and asked, "What?"

segs.zh: 『老Q，……现在……』赵太爷却又没有话，『现在……发财么？』
segs.en: Old Q... these days..." Grandfather Zhao hesitated, at a loss for words, then added, "These days... making any money?

segs.zh: 『发财？自然。要什么就是什么……』
segs.en: Get rich? Naturally. Anything you want, you shall have...

segs.zh: 『阿……Q哥，像我们这样穷朋友是不要紧的……』赵白眼惴惴的说，似乎想探革命党的口风。
segs.en: "Ah... Brother Q, as for us poor folk, it doesn't really matter..." Zhao Baiyan said uneasily, seemingly trying to sound out the revolutionary’s intentions.

segs.zh: 『穷朋友？你总比我有钱。』阿Q说着自去了。
segs.en: "Poor friend? You’re still better off than I am." Ah Q said this and went on his way.

segs.zh: 大家都怃然，没有话，赵太爷父子回家，晚上商量到点灯。赵白眼回家，便从腰间扯下搭连来，交给他女人藏在箱底里。
segs.en: Everyone fell into a disconsolate silence, no one speaking. Master Zhao and his son returned home and huddled in consultation until the lamps were lit. Zhao Baiyan went home, unhitched the money-belt from his waist, and handed it to his wife to hide at the bottom of a trunk.

segs.zh: 阿Q飘飘然的飞了一通，回到土谷祠，酒已经醒透了。这晚上，管祠的老头子也意外的和气，请他喝茶；阿Q便向他要了两个饼，吃完之后，又要了一枝点过的四两烛和一个树烛台，点起来，独自躺在自己的小屋里。他说不出的新鲜而且高兴，烛火像元夜似的闪闪的跳，他的思想也迸跳起来了：——
segs.en: Ah Q floated back to the Tutu Temple feeling as if he were walking on air, but by the time he arrived, his drunkenness had completely worn off. That night, the old man who looked after the temple was unexpectedly amiable and invited him to drink tea. Ah Q asked him for two cakes, and after finishing them, he requested a used four-ounce candle and a wooden candlestick. He lit the candle and lay alone in his small room. He felt an indescribable sense of novelty and joy; the candlelight flickered and danced like on the night of the Lantern Festival, and his thoughts began to leap along with it: —

segs.zh: 『造反？有趣，……来了一阵白盔白甲的革命党，都拿着板刀、钢鞭、炸弹、洋炮、三尖两刃刀、钩镰枪，走过土谷祠，叫道：「阿Q！同去同去！」于是一同去。……
segs.en: Revolt? Interesting... A band of revolutionaries in white helmets and white armor arrives, all carrying broadswords, steel whips, bombs, foreign rifles, three-pointed double-edged spears, and hooked lances. They walk past the Tutu Temple and call out, 'Ah Q! Come with us, come with us!' And so he goes with them...

segs.zh: 『这时未庄的一伙鸟男女才好笑哩，跪下叫道：「阿Q，饶命！」谁听他！第一个该死的是小D和赵太爷，还有秀才，还有假洋鬼子，……留几条么？王胡本来还可留，但也不要了。……
segs.en: It was at this point that that bunch of clowns in Weizhuang became truly hilarious; they knelt down and cried, "Ah Q, spare our lives!" But who would listen to them? The first ones who deserved to die were Little D and Grand-dad Zhao, then the Scholar, and the Fake Foreign Devil... Should I leave a few behind? I could have kept Wang Hu, perhaps, but no, better to finish him off too.

segs.zh: 『东西，……直走进去打开箱子来：元宝、洋钱、洋纱衫，……秀才娘子的一张宁式床先搬到土谷祠，此外便摆了钱家的桌椅，——或者也就用赵家的罢。自己是不动手的了，叫小D来搬，要搬得快，搬得不快打嘴巴。……
segs.en: Things... walk straight in and open the chests: silver ingots, foreign coins, foreign-style shirts... First, move the scholar’s wife’s Ningbo-style bed to the Earth Temple; as for the rest, arrange the Qian family’s tables and chairs—or perhaps just use the Zhao family’s instead. I won’t lift a finger myself; tell Little D to do the moving, and tell him to be quick about it—if he isn’t quick, slap him across the face.

segs.zh: 『赵司晨的妹子真丑。邹七嫂的女儿过几年再说。假洋鬼子的老婆会和没有辫子的男人睡觉，吓，不是好东西！秀才的老婆是眼胞上有疤的。……吴妈长久不见了，不知道在那里，——可惜脚太大。』
segs.en: Zhao Sichen’s sister is truly ugly. As for Zou Qisao’s daughter, well, that’s a matter for a few years from now. The fake foreign devil’s wife sleeps with men who have no queues—pah! A bad lot, that. The scholar’s wife has a scar on her eyelid. … Wu Ma hasn't been seen for a long time; no telling where she is—a pity about those big feet of hers.
