segs.zh: 阿Q没有想得十分停当，已经发了鼾声，四两烛还只点去了小半寸，红焰焰的光照着他张开的嘴。
segs.en: Ah Q had not yet finished his train of thought before he began to snore. The four-ounce candle had burned down only a scant half-inch, its crimson flame casting a glow upon his open mouth.

segs.zh: 『荷荷！』阿Q忽而大叫起来，抬了头仓皇的四顾。待到看见四两烛，却又倒头睡去了。
segs.en: "Ho-ho!" Ah Q suddenly cried out, lifting his head to gaze around in a panic. But once he caught sight of the four-ounce candle, he flopped back down and went to sleep.

segs.zh: 第二天他起得很迟，走出街上看时，样样都照旧。他也仍然肚饿，他想着，想不起什么来；但他忽而似乎有了主意了，慢慢的跨开步，有意无意的走到静修庵。
segs.en: The next day he rose very late. When he walked out into the street, everything was exactly as it had been. He was still hungry, and he tried to think, but could bring nothing to mind. Then, suddenly, he seemed to have an idea. With measured strides, he walked, whether by design or by chance, toward the Jingxiu Nunnery.

segs.zh: 庵和春天时节一样静，白的墙壁和漆黑的门。他想了一想，前去打门，一只狗在里面叫。他急急拾下几块断砖，再上去较为用力的打，打到黑门上生出许多麻点的时候，才听得有人来开门。
segs.en: The nunnery was as quiet as the spring season, with its white walls and pitch-black gate. He paused to think, then stepped forward to knock. A dog barked inside. He hurriedly picked up a few broken bricks and began to pound harder, and not until the black gate was pitted with marks did he hear someone coming to open it.

segs.zh: 阿Q连忙捏好砖头，摆开马步，准备和黑狗来开战。但庵门只开了一条缝，并无黑狗从中冲出，望进去只有一个老尼姑。
segs.en: Ah Q hurriedly gripped his brick and planted his feet in a horse stance, bracing himself for battle with the black dog. But the temple gate only creaked open a crack; no black dog charged out, and peering inside, he saw only an old nun.

segs.zh: 『你又来什么事？』伊大吃一惊的说。
segs.en: "What are you here for again?" she asked, looking thoroughly alarmed.

segs.zh: 『革命了……你知道？……』阿Q说得很含糊。
segs.en: "There’s a revolution... did you know?..." Ah Q said, very vaguely.

segs.zh: 『革命革命，革过一革的，……你们要革得我们怎么样呢？』老尼姑两眼通红的说。
segs.en: "Revolution, revolution—you've already had your revolution... what more do you want to do to us?" the old nun said, her eyes rimmed with red.

segs.zh: 『什么？……』阿Q诧异了。
segs.en: "What?..." Ah Q was taken aback.

segs.zh: 『你不知道，他们已经来革过了！』
segs.en: You don't know? They’ve already been here to stage a revolution!

segs.zh: 『谁？……』阿Q更其诧异了。
segs.en: "Who?..." Ah Q was even more bewildered.

segs.zh: 『那秀才和洋鬼子！』
segs.en: That scholar and that foreign devil!

segs.zh: 阿Q很出意外，不由的一错愕，老尼姑见他失了锐气，便飞速的关了门，阿Q再推时，牢不可开，再打时，没有回答了。
segs.en: Ah Q was taken completely by surprise; he could not help but falter. Seeing that his spirit had flagged, the old nun slammed the door shut in a flash. When Ah Q pushed against it again, it held fast, and when he knocked, there was no reply.

segs.zh: 那还是上午的事。赵秀才消息灵，一知道革命党已在夜间进城，便将辫子盘在顶上，一早去拜访那历来也不相能的钱洋鬼子。这是『咸与维新』的时候了，所以他们便谈得很投机，立刻成了情投意合的同志，也相约去革命。他们想而又想：才想出静修庵里有一块『皇帝万岁万万岁』的龙牌，是应该赶紧革掉的，于是又立刻同到庵里去革命。因为老尼姑来阻挡，说了三句话，他们便将伊当作满政府，在头上很给了不少的棍子和栗凿。尼姑待他们走后，定了神来检点，龙牌固然已经碎在地上了，而且又不见了观音娘娘座前的一个宣德𬬻。
segs.en: That had happened in the morning. Scholar Zhao, having a keen nose for news, learned that the revolutionaries had entered the city overnight. He immediately coiled his queue on top of his head and set off early to pay a visit to Qian the Foreign Devil, with whom he had never seen eye to eye. Since this was a time for "universal reform," they found common ground at once, becoming bosom comrades in a heartbeat, and pledged to join the revolution together. After much racking of their brains, they hit upon the idea that the tablet inscribed with "Long Live the Emperor, Long, Long Live the Emperor" in the Jingxiu Nunnery had to be revolutionized immediately, so they hurried there to carry out the task. Because the old nun tried to stop them and uttered three sentences, they treated her as the Manchu government and rained a flurry of blows and knuckle-raps upon her head. Once they had left and the nun had regained her composure to take stock of the situation, she found not only that the imperial tablet had been smashed to pieces on the floor, but that a Xuande censer from before the statue of the Goddess of Mercy had gone missing as well.

segs.zh: 这事阿Q后来才知道。他颇悔自己睡着，但也深怪他们不来招呼他。他又退一步想道：
segs.en: Ah Q only learned of this later. He regretted deeply that he had been asleep, yet he also blamed them severely for not coming to wake him. Then, taking a step back in his mind, he thought:

segs.zh: 『难道他们还没有知道我已经投降了革命党么？』
segs.en: Can it be that they haven't heard I've already surrendered to the revolutionaries?

segs.zh: 未庄的人心日见其安静了。据传来的消息，知道革命党虽然进了城，倒还没有什么大异样。知县大老爷还是原官，不过改称了什么，而且举人老爷也做了什么——这些名目，未庄人都说不明白——官，带兵的也还是先前的老把总。只有一件可怕的事是另有几个不好的革命党夹在里面捣乱，第二天便动手翦辫子，听说那邻村的航船七斤便着了道儿，弄得不像人样子了。但这却还不算大恐怖，因为未庄人本来少上城，即使偶有想进城的，也就立刻变了计，碰不着这危险。阿Q本也想进城去寻他的老朋友，一得这消息，也只得作罢了。
segs.en: The hearts of the people in Weizhuang grew calmer by the day. According to news that trickled in, although the revolutionaries had entered the city, there was nothing particularly unusual happening. The magistrate remained the same old official, though he had been given some new title or other, and the local scholar-gentry had also become something—the Weizhuang folk could not quite keep the names straight—while the military commander was still the same old sergeant as before. The only terrifying thing was that a few bad revolutionaries had wormed their way in to stir up trouble, and the very next day they began cutting off people’s queues. It was said that Seven-Pounder, the boatman from the next village, had fallen victim to them and been left looking like something not quite human. Yet this was not considered a major terror, for the people of Weizhuang seldom went to the city anyway; even those who had occasionally thought of going changed their minds immediately, so they never ran into this danger. Ah Q had also intended to go to the city to look up his old friends, but upon hearing this news, he had no choice but to give up the idea.

segs.zh: 但未庄也不能说是无改革。几天之后。将辫子盘在顶上的逐渐增加起来了，早经说过，最先自然是茂才公，其次便是赵司晨和赵白眼，后来是阿Q。倘在夏天，大家将辫子盘在头顶上或者打一个结，本不算什么稀奇事，但现在是暮秋，所以这『秋行夏令』的情形，在盘辫家不能不说是万分的英断，而在未庄也不能说无关于改革了。
segs.en: Weizhuang, however, could not be said to have remained entirely unreformed. A few days later, the number of people coiling their queues atop their heads began to increase. As already mentioned, the first to do so was, naturally, Master Maocai, followed by Zhao Sichen and Zhao Baiyan, and eventually, Ah Q. Had it been summer, coiling one’s queue atop one’s head or tying it in a knot would hardly have been a curious sight; but it was now late autumn. Thus, this "practicing summer customs in autumn" by those who coiled their queues could not be called anything less than an act of supreme decisiveness, and in Weizhuang, it could not be said to be unrelated to reform.

segs.zh: 赵司晨脑后空荡荡的走来，看见的人大嚷说：
segs.en: Zhao Sichen came walking along with the back of his head looking strangely empty, and those who saw him shouted:

segs.zh: 『嚄，革命党来了！』
segs.en: Oho, the revolutionaries are here!

segs.zh: 阿Q听到了很羡慕。他虽然早知道秀才盘辫的大新闻，但总没有想到自己可以照样做，现在看见赵司晨也如此，才有了学样的意思，定下实行的决心。他用一支竹筷将辫子盘在头顶上，迟疑多时，这才放胆的走去。
segs.en: Ah Q heard this with great envy. Although he had long since heard the big news about the scholar coiling his queue, it had never occurred to him that he might do the same; but now, seeing Zhao Sichen doing it too, he felt the urge to follow suit and made up his mind to put it into practice. Using a bamboo chopstick, he coiled his queue atop his head, hesitated for a long time, and then, emboldened, set off.

segs.zh: 他在街上走，人也看他，然而不说什么话，阿Q当初很不快，后来便很不平。他近来很容易闹脾气了；其实他的生活，倒也并不比造反之前反艰难，人见他也客气，店铺也不说要现钱。而阿Q总觉得自己太失意；既然革了命，不应该只是这样的。况且有一回看见小D，愈使他气破肚皮了。
segs.en: He walked down the street, and though people looked at him, they said nothing. At first, Ah Q was quite unhappy about this; later, he grew indignant. Lately, he had been prone to losing his temper. In truth, his life was no harder than it had been before the revolution—people were still polite to him, and the shops no longer insisted on cash payment. Yet Ah Q felt deeply frustrated; since he had made a revolution, it ought not to have ended like this. Moreover, catching sight of Little D one day made him so angry he felt as if he would burst.

segs.zh: 小D也将辫子盘在头顶上了，而且也居然用一支竹筷。阿Q万料不到他也敢这样做，自己也决不准他这样做！小D是甚么东西呢？他很想即刻揪住他，拗断他的竹筷，放下他的辫子，并且批他几个嘴巴，聊且惩罚他忘了生辰八字，他敢来做革命党的罪。但他终于饶放了，单是怒目而视的吐一口唾沫道：『呸！』
segs.en: Little D had also coiled his queue atop his head, and had even gone so far as to secure it with a bamboo chopstick. Ah Q had never imagined he would dare such a thing; he certainly would never permit it! Who did Little D think he was? Ah Q longed to seize him on the spot, snap his bamboo chopstick in two, pull down his queue, and slap his face a few times—a trifling punishment for his audacity in forgetting his own station and daring to pose as a revolutionary. But in the end, he let him go, merely glaring at him and spitting: "Pah!"

segs.zh: 这几日里，进城去的只有一个假洋鬼子。赵秀才本也想靠着寄存箱子的渊源，亲身去拜访举人老爷的，但因为有翦辫的危险，所以也就中止了。他写了一封『黄伞格』的信，托假洋鬼子带上城，而且托他给自己绍介绍介，去进自由党。假洋鬼子回来时，向秀才讨还了四块洋钱；秀才便有一块银桃子挂在大襟上了；未庄人都惊服，说这是柿油党的顶子，抵得一个翰林，赵太爷因此也骤然大阔，远过于他儿子初进秀才的时候，所以目空一切，见了阿Q，也就很有些不放在眼里了。
segs.en: In these last few days, the only person to go into town was the Fake Foreign Devil. Scholar Zhao had intended to pay a personal visit to the Provincial Graduate, counting on their old connection through the storage of the chests, but the danger of having his queue cut off forced him to abandon the plan. Instead, he wrote a letter in the "Yellow Umbrella" style and entrusted it to the Fake Foreign Devil to take into town, asking him at the same time to put in a good word for him so he might join the Freedom Party. When the Fake Foreign Devil returned, he demanded four silver dollars from the scholar. Soon after, the scholar had a silver peach dangling from his lapel. The people of Weizhuang were awestruck, whispering that this was the badge of the "Persimmon Oil Party," worth as much as a Hanlin academician. Old Master Zhao, as a result, suddenly became grander than ever—far more so than when his son had first earned his xiucai degree—and he began to look down on everyone, even treating Ah Q with utter disdain.

segs.zh: 阿Q正在不平，又时时刻刻感着冷落，一听得这银桃子的传说，他立即悟出自己之所以冷落的原因了：要革命，单说投降，是不行的；盘上辫子，也不行的；第一着仍然要和革命党去结识。他生平所知道的革命党只有两个，城里的一个早已『嚓』的杀掉了，现在只剩了一个假洋鬼子。他除却赶紧去和假洋鬼子商量之外，再没有别的道路了。
segs.en: Ah Q was feeling indignant, and at the same time, he was constantly troubled by a sense of being left out in the cold. But as soon as he heard the rumors about the silver peach, he immediately grasped the reason for his neglect: to make revolution, merely talking about surrender was not enough; winding his queue on top of his head would not do either; the first step was still to make connections with the revolutionaries. In his entire life, he had only known two revolutionaries: one in the city had long since been "snicked" off—executed—and now only the Fake Foreign Devil remained. He had no other choice but to hurry off and consult with him.

segs.zh: 钱府的大门正开着，阿Q便怯怯的躄进去。他一到里面，很吃了惊，只见假洋鬼子正站在院子的中央，一身乌黑的大约是洋衣，身上也挂着一块银桃子，手里是阿Q曾经领教过的棍子，已经留到一尺多长的辫子都拆开了披在肩背上，蓬头散发的像一个刘海仙。对面挺直的站着赵白眼和三个闲人，正在必恭必敬的听说话。
segs.en: The main gate of the Qian residence stood wide open, and Ah Q, trembling, shuffled inside. Once within, he was struck with astonishment: the Bogus Foreign Devil was standing in the center of the courtyard, dressed in a suit of pitch-black foreign clothes, a silver peach-shaped ornament hanging from his person, and in his hand the very stick Ah Q had once tasted. His queue, now grown over a foot long, had been unplaited and hung loose over his shoulders and back, leaving him looking as disheveled as a Taoist immortal. Opposite him stood Zhao Baiyan and three idlers, standing bolt upright and listening to him with the utmost reverence.

segs.zh: 阿Q轻轻的走进了，站在赵白眼的背后，心里想招呼，却不知道怎么说才好：叫他假洋鬼子固然是不行的了，洋人也不妥，革命党也不妥，或者就应该叫洋先生了罢。
segs.en: Ah Q walked up softly and stood behind Zhao Baiyan. He wanted to greet him, but could not decide how to address him: calling him "Fake Foreign Devil" was clearly out of the question, "Foreigner" didn't seem right, and "Revolutionary" was equally inappropriate. Perhaps, he thought, he should just call him "Mr. Foreign."

segs.zh: 洋先生却没有见他，因为白着眼睛讲得正起劲：
segs.en: The foreign gentleman, however, did not see him, for he was holding forth with his eyes rolled back, in the full heat of his discourse.

segs.zh: 『我是性急的，所以我们见面，我总是说：洪哥！我们动手罢！他却总说道No！——这是洋话，你们不懂的。否则早已成功了，然而这正是他做事小心的地方。他再三再四的请我上湖北，我还没有肯。谁愿意在这小县城里做事情。……』
segs.en: I am an impatient man, so whenever we met, I would always say, "Brother Hong! Let’s get to work!" But he would invariably reply, "No!"—that is a foreign word; you wouldn’t understand it. Otherwise, we would have succeeded long ago, though this is precisely what makes him so cautious in his dealings. Time and again he has urged me to go to Hubei, but I have yet to agree. Who would want to do anything in this wretched little county town? ...

segs.zh: 『唔，……这个……』阿Q候他略停，终于用十二分的勇气开口了，但不知道因为什么，又并不叫他洋先生。
segs.en: Well... this..." Ah Q waited until he had paused, then finally spoke with every ounce of courage he could muster, though for some reason he still could not bring himself to call him "Foreign Master.

segs.zh: 听着说话的四个人都吃惊的回顾他。洋先生也才看见：
segs.en: The four people listening turned their heads in surprise to look at him. The foreign gentleman, too, only then caught sight of him:

segs.zh: 『什么？』
segs.en: What?

segs.zh: 『出去！』
segs.en: Get out!

segs.zh: 『我要投……』
segs.en: I intend to...

segs.zh: 『滚出去！』洋先生扬起哭丧棒来了。
segs.en: "Get out!" the foreign gentleman shouted, brandishing his mourning staff.

segs.zh: 赵白眼和闲人便都吆喝道：『先生叫你滚出去，你还不听么！』
segs.en: Zhao Baiyan and the idlers all chimed in, "The gentleman told you to get out! Are you deaf?"

segs.zh: 阿Q将手向头上一遮，不自觉的逃出门外；洋先生倒也没有追。他快跑了六十多步，这才慢慢的走，于是心里便涌起了忧愁：洋先生不准他革命，他再没有别的路；从此决不能望有白盔白甲的人来叫他，他所有的抱负、志向、希望、前程；全被一笔勾销了。至于闲人们传扬开去，给小D、王胡等辈笑话，倒是还在其次的事。
segs.en: Ah Q shielded his head with his hand and instinctively bolted out the door; the foreign gentleman did not, however, pursue him. He ran for sixty paces or more before slowing to a walk, and then a wave of sorrow welled up within him: since the foreign gentleman would not permit him to make revolution, there was no other path left to him. From this day forth, he could never again hope to see men in white helmets and white armor coming to call for him; all his ambitions, aspirations, hopes, and prospects had been wiped out with a single stroke. As for the idlers spreading the news and the mockery he would endure from the likes of Little D and Wang Hu, that was, by comparison, a secondary matter.

segs.zh: 他似乎从来没有经验过这样的无聊。他对于自己的盘辫子。仿佛也觉得无意味，要侮蔑；为报仇起见，很想立刻放下辫子来，但也没有竟放。他游到夜间，赊了两碗酒，喝下肚去，渐渐的高兴起来了，思想里才又出现白盔白甲的碎片。
segs.en: He seemed never to have experienced such boredom before. He felt a sense of pointlessness, even contempt, for his own braided queue; in a fit of spite, he felt a strong urge to let it down at once, yet in the end, he did not. He wandered until nightfall, then bought two bowls of wine on credit. As he drank, his spirits gradually lifted, and fragments of white helmets and white armor began to flicker back into his thoughts.

segs.zh: 有一天，他照例的混到夜深，待酒店要关门，才踱回土谷祠去。
segs.en: One day, as was his custom, he lingered until late into the night, and only when the tavern was about to close did he saunter back to the Earth God’s Temple.
*Pa! Ba—!*

segs.zh: 他忽而听得一种异样的声音，又不是爆竹。阿Q本来是爱看热闹，爱管闲事的，便在暗中直寻过去。似乎前面有些脚步声；他正听，猛然间一个人从对面逃来了。阿Q一看见，便赶紧翻身跟着逃。那人转弯，阿Q也转弯，既转弯，那人站住了，阿Q也站住。他看后面并无什么，看那人便是小D。
segs.en: Suddenly, he heard an unusual sound, though it was not a firecracker. Ah Q, always one to enjoy a spectacle and meddle in others' affairs, started tracking the noise through the dark. There seemed to be footsteps ahead; just as he was listening, someone came bolting toward him. The moment Ah Q caught sight of the figure, he turned around and began to flee as well. The man turned a corner, and Ah Q turned too. Once they had rounded the corner, the man stopped, and Ah Q stopped with him. Looking back and seeing nothing, he realized the man was none other than Little D.

segs.zh: 『什么？』阿Q不平起来了。
segs.en: "What?" Ah Q began to bristle.

segs.zh: 『赵……赵家遭抢了！』小D气喘吁吁的说。
segs.en: "The... the Zhao house has been robbed!" Xiao D gasped, breathless.

segs.zh: 阿Q的心怦怦的跳了。小D说了便走；阿Q却跳而又停的两三回，但他究竟是做过『这路生意』的人，格外胆大，于是躄出路角，仔细的听，似乎有些嚷嚷，又仔细的看，似乎许多白盔白甲的人，络绎的将箱子抬出了，器具抬出了，秀才娘子的宁式床也抬出了，但是不分明，他还想上前，两只脚却没有动。
segs.en: Ah Q’s heart began to thump. Little D walked off as soon as he had spoken, but Ah Q paused and started two or three times. Still, having been in "this line of business" before, he was bolder than most; he hobbled out from the street corner and listened intently—there seemed to be a clamor. He looked intently too—there seemed to be a crowd of men in white helmets and white armor, carrying out chests in an endless stream, then household utensils, and even the Scholar’s wife’s Ningbo-style bed. But it was not quite clear. He wanted to press forward, yet his feet would not move.

segs.zh: 这一夜没有月，未庄在黑暗里很寂静，寂静到像羲皇时候一般太平。阿Q站着看到自己发烦，也似乎还是先前一样，在那里来来往往的搬，箱子抬出了，器具抬出了，秀才娘子的宁式床也抬出了，……抬得他自己有些不信他的眼睛了。但他决计不再上前，却回到自己的祠里去了。
segs.en: There was no moon that night. Weizhuang lay in the darkness, profoundly still—still as the age of Fuxi, a time of primordial peace. Ah Q stood watching until he grew weary. Everything seemed just as it had before; they were coming and going, carrying things out. Chests were carried out, utensils were carried out, even the Xiucai’s wife’s Ningbo-style bed was carried out... He could hardly believe his own eyes. Yet he resolved not to step forward again, and instead returned to his ancestral hall.

segs.zh: 土谷祠里更漆黑；他关好大门，摸进自己的屋子里。他躺了好一会，这才定了神，而且发出关于自己的思想来：白盔白甲的人明明到了，并不来打招呼，搬了许多好东西，又没有自己的份，——这全是假洋鬼子可恶，不准我造反，否则，这次何至于没有我的份呢？阿Q越想越气，终于禁不住满心痛恨起来，毒毒的点一点头：『不准我造反，只准你造反？妈妈的假洋鬼子，——好，你造反！造反是杀头的罪名呵，我总要告一状，看你抓进县里去杀头，——满门抄斩，——嚓！嚓！』
segs.en: The Tutu Temple was even darker; he bolted the heavy gate and groped his way into his room. He lay there for a long while before his racing heart finally settled, and he began to reflect upon his own situation: the men in white helmets and white armor had clearly arrived, yet they hadn't come to greet him. They had hauled away a great many fine things, and none of it had fallen to his share. It was all that wretched Fake Foreign Devil's fault for not allowing him to revolt; otherwise, how could he have been left out of this windfall? The more Ah Q thought about it, the angrier he became, until he could no longer contain his seething resentment. He nodded his head viciously. "You won't let me revolt, but you can revolt yourself? Damn you, you Fake Foreign Devil—fine, go ahead and revolt! Revolt is a capital offense, you know. I’ll go and lodge a complaint, and see you dragged off to the county seat to be beheaded—your whole clan wiped out—cha! cha!"

segs.zh: 赵家遭抢之后，未庄人大抵很快意而且恐慌，阿Q也很快意而且恐慌。但四天之后，阿Q在半夜里忽被抓进县城里去了。那时恰是暗夜，一队兵，一队团丁，一队警察，五个侦探，悄悄地到了未庄，乘昏暗围住土谷祠，正对门架好机关枪。然而阿Q不冲出。许多时没有动静，把总焦急起来了，悬了二十千的赏，才有两个团丁冒了险，逾垣进去，里应外合，一拥而入，将阿Q抓出来；直待擒出祠外面的机关枪左近，他才有些清醒了。
segs.en: After the Zhao household was robbed, the people of Weizhuang were, for the most part, both delighted and terrified; Ah Q was likewise both delighted and terrified. But four days later, in the dead of night, Ah Q was suddenly seized and hauled off to the county seat. It was a pitch-black night when a squad of soldiers, a squad of local militia, a squad of police, and five detectives crept into Weizhuang. Under the cover of darkness, they surrounded the Earth Temple and set up a machine gun directly facing the entrance. Yet Ah Q did not charge out. After a long wait with no movement, the captain grew anxious. It was only after he offered a reward of twenty thousand coppers that two militiamen braved the risk, scaled the wall, and, working from the inside to coordinate with those outside, swarmed in and dragged Ah Q out. It was not until he had been hauled out to the vicinity of the machine gun outside the temple that he finally began to come to his senses.

segs.zh: 到进城，已经是正午，阿Q见自己被搀进一所破衙门，转了五六个弯，便推在一间小屋里。他刚刚一跄踉，那用整株的木料做成的栅栏门便跟着他的脚跟阖上了，其馀的三面都是墙壁，仔细看时，屋角上还有两个人。
segs.en: By the time they reached the city, it was already noon. Ah Q saw that he was being ushered into a dilapidated yamen; after turning five or six corners, he was shoved into a small room. He had barely stumbled inside when the gate, made of whole logs, slammed shut at his heels. The other three sides were nothing but walls, and upon closer inspection, he saw two other men huddled in the corner.

segs.zh: 阿Q虽然有些忐忑，却并不很苦闷，因为他那土谷祠里的卧室，也并没有比这间屋子更高明。那两个也仿佛是乡下人，渐渐和他兜搭起来了，一个说是举人老爷要追他祖父欠下来的陈租，一个不知道为了什么事。他们问阿Q，阿Q爽利的答道，『因为我想造反。』
segs.en: Though Ah Q felt a little uneasy, he was not particularly distressed, for his sleeping quarters in the Tutu Temple were no better than this room. The other two men seemed to be country folk as well, and they gradually began to strike up a conversation with him. One said that the local scholar-gentry were hounding him for back rent owed by his grandfather, while the other’s reason was unclear. When they asked Ah Q about his own situation, he answered briskly, "Because I wanted to revolt."

segs.zh: 他下半天便又被抓出栅栏门去了，到得大堂，上面坐着一个满面剃得精光的老头子。阿Q疑心他是和尚，但看见下面站着一排兵，案旁又站着十几个长衫人物，也有满头剃得精光像这老头子的，也有将一尺来长的头发披在背后像那假洋鬼子的，都是一脸横肉，怒目而视的看他；他便知道这人一定有些来历，膝关节立刻自然而然的宽松，便跪了下去了。
segs.en: That afternoon he was hauled out through the barred gate again and taken to the main hall, where an old man with a completely shaven head sat high above. Ah Q suspected him of being a monk, but seeing a row of soldiers standing below, and a dozen or so men in long gowns standing by the bench—some with heads as completely shaven as the old man’s, others with a foot or more of hair hanging down their backs like the Fake Foreign Devil, all of them wearing fierce, scowling looks—he realized this person must be someone of consequence. His knees went limp of their own accord, and he promptly knelt down.

segs.zh: 『站着说！不要跪！』长衫人物都吆喝说。
segs.en: Stand up and talk! Don’t kneel! the long-gowned figures all shouted.

segs.zh: 阿Q虽然似乎懂得，但总觉得站不住，身不由己的蹲了下去，而且终于趁势改为跪下了。
segs.en: Although Ah Q seemed to understand, he still felt he could not hold his ground; his body gave way against his will, he squatted down, and finally, taking advantage of the posture, he sank to his knees.

segs.zh: 『奴隶性！……』长衫人物又鄙夷似的说，但也没有叫他起来。
segs.en: "Servility!..." the man in the long gown remarked, with a trace of disdain, though he made no move to tell him to get up.

segs.zh: 『你从实招来罢，免得吃苦。我早都知道了。招了可以放你』，那光头的老头子看定了阿Q的脸，沈静的清楚的说。
segs.en: "Fess up, and save yourself some grief. I’ve known everything for a long time. Confess, and you’ll be let go," the old man with the shaved head said, fixing his gaze on Ah Q’s face, his voice calm and clear.

segs.zh: 『招罢！』长衫人物也大声说。
segs.en: "Call him over!" the man in the long gown also shouted.

segs.zh: 『我本来要……来投……』阿Q胡里胡涂的想了一通，这才断断续续的说。
segs.en: "I originally intended to... come and join..." Ah Q thought muddledly for a moment, and only then said, stammering.

segs.zh: 『那么，为什么不来的呢？』老头子和气的问。
segs.en: "Then why doesn't he come?" the old man asked gently.

segs.zh: 『假洋鬼子不准我！』
segs.en: The Fake Foreign Devil won't allow me!

segs.zh: 『胡说！此刻说，也迟了。现在你的同党在那里？』
segs.en: Nonsense! It’s too late to talk now. Where are your accomplices?
What? ...

segs.zh: 『那一晚打劫赵家的一伙人。』
segs.en: The gang that raided the Zhao house that night.

segs.zh: 『他们没有来叫我。他们自己搬走了。』阿Q提起来便愤愤。
segs.en: "They didn't come to call for me. They just moved away on their own." Ah Q sounded indignant whenever he brought it up.

segs.zh: 『走到那里去了呢？说出来便放你了。』老头子更和气了。
segs.en: "Where have you gone to? Tell me, and I'll let you go." The old man's tone grew even milder.

segs.zh: 『我不知道，……他们没有来叫我……』
segs.en: I don't know... they didn't come to call me...

segs.zh: 然而老头子使了一个眼色，阿Q便又被抓进栅栏门里了。他第二次抓出栅栏门，是第二天的上午。
segs.en: However, the old man gave a wink, and Ah Q was hauled back inside the wicket gate. He was dragged out through the gate a second time the following morning.

segs.zh: 大堂的情形都照旧。上面仍然坐着光头的老头子，阿Q也仍然下了跪。
segs.en: The scene in the main hall remained exactly as it had been. The bald old man was still seated up there, and Ah Q was still on his knees.

segs.zh: 老头子和气的问道，『你还有什么话说么？』
segs.en: The old man asked gently, "Have you anything else to say?"

segs.zh: 阿Q一想，没有话，便回答说，『没有。』
segs.en: Ah Q pondered this, found nothing to say, and replied, "None."

segs.zh: 于是一个长衫人物拿了一张纸，并一支笔送到阿Q的面前，要将笔塞在他手里。阿Q这时很吃惊，几乎『魂飞魄散』了：因为他的手和笔相关，这回是初次。他正不知怎样拿；那人却又指着一处地方教他画花押。
segs.en: Then a man in a long gown produced a sheet of paper and a brush, which he thrust toward Ah Q, attempting to force the brush into his hand. Ah Q was so startled he nearly lost his wits; this was the first time his hand had ever had anything to do with a brush. Just as he was wondering how to hold it, the man pointed to a spot on the paper and told him to sign his mark.

segs.zh: 『我……我……不认得字。』阿Q一把抓住了笔，惶恐而且惭愧的说。
segs.en: "I... I... I don't know how to write," Ah Q said, clutching the brush, his expression one of panic and shame.

segs.zh: 『那么，便宜你，画一个圆圈！』
segs.en: Very well, I'll let you off easy—draw a circle!

segs.zh: 阿Q要画圆圈了，那手捏着笔却只是抖。于是那人替他将纸铺在地上。阿Q伏下去，使尽了平生的力画圆圈。他生怕被人笑话，立志要画得圆，但这可恶的笔不但很沉重，并且不听话，刚刚一抖一抖的几乎要合缝，却又向外一耸，画成瓜子模样了。
segs.en: Ah Q wanted to draw a circle, but his hand, clutching the brush, would not stop trembling. Seeing this, the man spread the paper on the ground for him. Ah Q crouched down and summoned every ounce of strength he possessed to draw that circle. He was terrified of being laughed at and was determined to make it perfect, but the wretched brush was not only heavy, it was also disobedient; just as he trembled his way toward closing the gap, the tip gave a sudden twitch outward, and the circle ended up looking like a melon seed.

segs.zh: 阿Q正羞愧自己画得不圆，那人却不计较，早已掣了纸笔去，许多人又将他第二次抓进栅栏门。
segs.en: Ah Q was still feeling ashamed that his circle had not been perfectly round, but the man did not seem to mind; he had already snatched up the paper and brush, and the crowd once again hauled him inside the wicket gate.

segs.zh: 他第二次进了栅栏，倒也并不十分懊恼。他以为人生天地之间，大约本来有时要抓进抓出，有时要在纸上画圆圈的，惟有圈而不圆！却是他『行状』上的一个污点。但不多时也就释然了，他想：孙子才画得很圆的圆圈呢。于是他睡着了。
segs.en: The second time he was thrown into the lockup, he was not particularly vexed. He reasoned that, living between heaven and earth, it was only natural for a man to be hauled in and out from time to time, and to be called upon to draw circles on paper—though to draw one that was not perfectly round! That, he felt, was a blot on his "record." Yet before long he felt at ease, thinking: Only a grandson would insist on drawing a perfectly round circle. With that, he fell asleep.

segs.zh: 然而这一夜，举人老爷反而不能睡：他和把总呕了气了。举人老爷主张第一要追赃，把总主张第一要示众。把总近来很不将举人老爷放在眼里了，拍案打凳的说道：『惩一儆百！你看，我做革命党还不上二十天，抢案就是十几件，全不破案，我的面子在那里？破了案，你又来迂。不成！这是我管的！』举人老爷窘急了，然而还坚持，说是倘若不追赃，他便立刻辞了帮办民政的职务。而把总却道：『请便罢！』于是举人老爷在这一夜竟没有睡，但幸而第二天倒也没有辞。
segs.en: Yet that night, the Jinshi gentleman found himself unable to sleep: he had fallen out with the battalion commander. The Jinshi insisted that the priority was to recover the stolen goods, while the commander argued that the priority was to make a public example of the culprits. The commander had lately begun to hold the Jinshi in low regard; he slammed his hand on the table and kicked a stool, shouting, "Punish one to warn a hundred! Look here, I’ve been a revolutionary for less than twenty days, and there have already been over a dozen robberies, none of them solved. Where does that leave my reputation? As soon as I try to solve them, you come along with your pedantry. It won't do! This is under my jurisdiction!" The Jinshi was flustered and desperate, yet he stood his ground, declaring that if the loot were not recovered, he would resign from his post as assistant administrator of civil affairs at once. To which the commander replied, "Please, do as you like!" And so, the Jinshi did not sleep that night, though fortunately, he did not actually resign the next day.

segs.zh: 阿Q第三次抓出栅栏门的时候，便是举人老爷睡不着的那一夜的明天的上午了。他到了大堂，上面还坐着照例的光头老头子；阿Q也照例的下了跪。
segs.en: The third time Ah Q was hauled out through the barred gate was on the morning following the night the venerable scholar could not sleep. When he arrived in the main hall, the same bald-headed old man was sitting in the seat of honor as always; and, as always, Ah Q knelt down.

segs.zh: 老头子很和气的问道，『你还有什么话么？』
segs.en: The old man asked very amiably, "Is there anything else you have to say?"

segs.zh: 阿Q一想，没有话，便回答说，『没有。』
segs.en: Ah Q pondered this, found nothing to say, and replied, "None."

segs.zh: 许多长衫和短衫人物，忽然给他穿上一件洋布的白背心，上面有些黑字。阿Q很气苦：因为这很像是带孝，而带孝是晦气的。然而同时他的两手反缚了，同时又被一直抓出衙门外去了。
segs.en: A crowd of men in long gowns and short jackets suddenly forced him into a white foreign-cloth vest, marked with black characters. Ah Q was deeply aggrieved, for it looked very much like mourning dress, and mourning was a sign of bad luck. Yet, at the same moment, his hands were bound behind his back, and he was dragged straight out of the yamen.

segs.zh: 阿Q被抬上了一辆没有篷的车，几个短衣人物也和他同坐在一处。这车立刻走动了，前面是一班背着洋炮的兵们和团丁，两旁是许多张着嘴的看客，后面怎样，阿Q没有见。但他突然觉到了；这岂不是去杀头么？他一急，两眼发黑，耳朵里喤的一声，似乎发昏了。然而他又没有全发昏，有时虽然着急，有时却也泰然；他意思之间，似乎觉得人生天地间，大约本来有时也未免要杀头的。
segs.en: Ah Q was hoisted onto a cart without a canopy, where several men in short coats were already seated. The cart lurched forward immediately. In front marched a squad of soldiers and local militiamen carrying foreign rifles, flanked by a crowd of onlookers with mouths agape. As for what lay behind, Ah Q could not see. But suddenly, it dawned on him: was this not the way to the execution ground? Panic surged, his vision blurred, and a loud ringing filled his ears; he felt as though he were fainting. Yet, he did not lose consciousness entirely. Though anxious at times, at others he felt strangely composed; it occurred to him that, in this world, human life was perhaps destined, now and then, to be cut short by the executioner's blade.

segs.zh: 他还认得路，于是有些诧异了：怎么不向着法场走呢？他不知道这是在游街，在示众。但即使知道也一样，他不过以为人生天地间，大约本来有时也未免要游街要示众罢了。
segs.en: He still knew the way, so he felt a touch of surprise: why were they not heading toward the execution ground? He did not know that this was a parade, a public exhibition. But even if he had known, it would have made no difference; he merely thought that, living between heaven and earth, it was perhaps only natural that one should, now and then, be paraded through the streets and put on public display.

segs.zh: 他省悟了，这是绕到法场去的路，这一定是『嚓』的去杀头。他惘惘的向左右看，全跟着蚂蚁似的人，而在无意中，却在路旁的人丛中发见了一个吴妈。很久违，伊原来在城里做工了。阿Q忽然很羞愧自己没志气：竟没有唱几句戏。他的思想仿佛旋风似的在脑里一回旋：小孤孀上坟欠堂皇，龙虎斗里的『悔不该……』也太乏，还是『手执钢鞭将你打』罢。他同时将手一扬，才记得这两手原来都捆着，于是『手执钢鞭』也不唱了。
segs.en: He realized now that this was the road to the execution ground, and that this must be the end, the sudden "slash" of the blade. He looked about him in a daze; the people following him were like a trail of ants. Quite by chance, he spotted Wu Ma in the crowd lining the road. It had been a long time; he had not realized she was working in the city now. Suddenly, Ah Q felt ashamed of his own lack of spirit: he had not even sung a few lines from an opera. His thoughts whirled through his mind like a cyclone: the widow visiting her husband’s grave was not dignified enough, and the "How I regret..." from *The Dragon and the Tiger* was too wearying—better to sing "With steel whip in hand, I shall strike you down." He raised his hand to suit the gesture, only to remember that his hands were bound, and so he did not sing "With steel whip in hand" after all.

segs.zh: 『过了二十年又是一个……』阿Q在百忙中，『无师自通』的说出半句从来不说的话。
segs.en: "In twenty years' time, there will be another..." Ah Q said, in the midst of his frantic rush, uttering half a sentence he had never said before, as if he had been inspired by some inner wisdom.

segs.zh: 『好！』从人丛里，便发出豺狼的嗥叫一般的声音来。
segs.en: "Good!" came a cry from the crowd, sounding for all the world like the howl of a jackal.

segs.zh: 车子不住的前行，阿Q在喝采声中，轮转眼睛去看吴妈，似乎伊一向并没有见他，却只是出神的看着兵们背上的洋炮。
segs.en: The cart kept rolling forward. Amidst the cheering, Ah Q rolled his eyes to look at Wu Ma, but she seemed not to have seen him at all, staring instead, lost in thought, at the foreign rifles slung across the soldiers' backs.

segs.zh: 阿Q于是再看那些喝采的人们。
segs.en: Ah Q then turned his gaze back to those who were cheering.

segs.zh: 这刹那中，他的思想又仿佛旋风似的在脑里一回旋了。四年之前，他曾在山脚下遇见一只饿狼，永是不近不远的跟定他，要吃他的肉。他那时吓得几乎要死，幸而手里有一柄斫柴刀，才得仗这壮了胆，支持到未庄；可是永远记得那狼眼睛，又凶又怯，闪闪的像两颗鬼火，似乎远远的来穿透了他的皮肉。而这回他又看见从来没有见过的更可怕的眼睛了，又钝又锋利，不但已经咀嚼了他的话，并且还要咀嚼他皮肉以外的东西，永是不远不近的跟他走。
segs.en: In that instant, his thoughts seemed to whirl through his mind like a cyclone. Four years ago, he had encountered a starving wolf at the foot of a mountain, a creature that trailed him at a constant, nerve-wracking distance, intent on devouring his flesh. He had been terrified, nearly to the point of death, and it was only by brandishing a wood-chopping axe that he had summoned the courage to make it all the way to Weizhuang. Yet, he could never forget those lupine eyes: fierce and cowardly, flickering like two ghostly flames, seemingly piercing through his very skin from afar. But now, he saw eyes far more terrifying than any he had ever known—eyes both dull and razor-sharp, which had not only chewed up his words but were poised to devour something even deeper than his flesh, trailing him always at that same, inescapable distance.

segs.zh: 这些眼睛们似乎连成一气，已经在那里咬他的灵魂。
segs.en: These eyes seemed to unite as one, already there, gnawing at his soul.
Save me, ......

segs.zh: 然而阿Q没有说。他早就两眼发黑，耳朵里嗡的一声，觉得全身仿佛微尘似的迸散了。
segs.en: Yet Ah Q said nothing. His vision had long since gone dark, a buzzing filled his ears, and he felt his whole body scattering like specks of dust.

segs.zh: 至于当时的影响，最大的倒反在举人老爷，因为终是没有追赃，他全家都号咷了。其次是赵府，非特秀才因为上城去报官，被不好的革命党翦了辫子，而且又破费了二十千的赏钱，所以全家也号咷了。从这一天以来，他们便渐渐的都发生了遗老的气味。
segs.en: As for the impact at the time, the greatest was ironically felt by the Master Scholar. Because the stolen goods were never recovered, his entire household wailed in lamentation. Next came the Zhao residence; not only had the son, a budding scholar, gone to the city to report the matter to the authorities only to have his queue shorn off by some unruly revolutionaries, but the family had also been forced to shell out twenty thousand cash in rewards, leaving them, too, to wail in lamentation. From that day on, a faint scent of the loyalist relic began to cling to them all.

segs.zh: 至于舆论，在未庄是无异议，自然都说阿Q坏，被枪毙便是他的坏的证据；不坏又何至于被枪毙呢？而城里的舆论却不佳，他们多半不满足，以为枪毙并无杀头这般好看；而且那是怎样的一个可笑的死囚呵，游了那么久的街，竟没有唱一句戏：他们白跟一趟了。
segs.en: As for public opinion, in Weizhuang it was unanimous. Naturally, everyone said Ah Q was bad, and his execution was simply proof of his badness; if he hadn’t been bad, why would he have been shot? But in the city, public opinion was less favorable. Most people were dissatisfied, feeling that a shooting was not nearly as entertaining as a beheading. Moreover, what a laughable prisoner he had been—paraded through the streets for so long, and he hadn't even sung a single line from an opera. They had followed him for nothing.

segs.zh: （一九二一年十二月。）
segs.en: (December, 1921.)

segs.zh: 这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may also be in the public domain in countries and regions where the copyright term is based on the life of the author plus an additional number of years, but which apply a shorter term to foreign works.

segs.zh: 据《中华人民共和国著作权法释义》，法人作品应满足三点要求：（1）由法人（或其他组织，以下简称“法人”）主持创作，而非工作人员自发进行；（2）创作思想及表达方式体现法人意志；（3）由法人负责，而非执笔者。（详情）

(Details)

chapters.note: 鲁迅笔下最不朽的形象。「精神胜利法」四字，自此成了一个民族的镜子。从「优胜记略」到「大团圆」，写尽看客与被看者的悲喜。

segs.zh: 方玄绰近来爱说『差不多』这一句话，几乎成了『口头禅』似的；而且不但说，的确也盘据在他脑里了。他最初说的是『都一样』，后来大约觉得欠稳当了，便改为『差不多』，一直使用到现在。
segs.en: Fang Xuanchuo has recently taken a liking to the expression "pretty much," which has become something of a catchphrase for him. And it is not just that he says it; the phrase has truly taken root in his mind. At first, he used to say "it's all the same," but later, perhaps feeling that was not quite precise enough, he switched to "pretty much," and has been using it ever since.

segs.zh: 他自从发见了这一句平凡的警句以后，虽然引起了不少的新感慨，同时却也得到许多新慰安。譬如看见老辈威压青年，在先是要愤愤的，但现在却就转念道，将来这少年有了儿孙时，大抵也要摆这架子的罢，便再没有什么不平了。又如看见兵士打车夫，在先也要愤愤的，但现在也就转念道，倘使这车夫当了兵，这兵拉了车，大抵也就这么打，便再也不放在心上了。他这样想着的时候，有时也疑心是因为自己没有和恶社会奋斗的勇气，所以瞒心昧己的故意造出来的一条逃路，很近于『无是非之心』。远不如改正了好。然而这意见，总反而在他脑里生长起来。
segs.en: Ever since he had discovered this simple aphorism, it had stirred in him many new reflections, yet at the same time, it brought him a great deal of new comfort. For instance, when he saw the older generation browbeating the young, he would once have been indignant, but now he would think to himself, "When this youth has children and grandchildren of his own, he will likely put on the same airs," and with that, he would no longer feel any resentment. Or, seeing a soldier beating a rickshaw puller, he would once have been incensed, but now he would tell himself, "If this puller were a soldier, and this soldier a puller, they would likely be beating each other in just the same way," and so he would cease to take it to heart. At times, he suspected that this line of thought was merely a path of escape he had willfully invented to deceive his own conscience, stemming from a lack of courage to struggle against a wicked society—something dangerously close to a total absence of moral judgment. He felt it would be far better to mend his ways. Yet, despite this, the notion only continued to take deeper root in his mind.

segs.zh: 他将这『差不多说』最初公表的时候是在北京首善学校的讲堂上，其时大概是提起关于历史上的事情来，于是说到『古今人不相远』，说到各色人等的『性相近』，终于牵扯到学生和官僚身上，大发其议论道：
segs.en: The first time he publicly aired his theory of "near-enough-ism" was in the lecture hall of the Shoushan School in Beijing. It was likely brought on by a discussion of historical events, which led to the observation that "the ancients and the moderns are not so far apart," and that the "natures of all sorts of people are much the same." This eventually drew the students and bureaucrats into the fray, prompting him to hold forth at great length:

segs.zh: 『现在社会上时髦的都通行骂官僚，而学生骂得尤利害。然而官僚并不是天生的特别种族，就是平民变就的。现在学生出身的官僚就不少，和老官僚有什么两样呢？「易地则皆然」，思想、言论举动、丰采都没有什么大区别……便是学生团体新办的许多事业，不是也已经难免出弊病，大半烟消火灭了么？差不多的。但中国将来之可虑就在此。……』
segs.en: Fashionable society nowadays is all for railing against bureaucrats, and students are the most vehement of all. Yet bureaucrats are not a special species born of nature; they are merely commoners transformed. There are already many bureaucrats who started out as students—how are they any different from the old-school ones? "Change the setting, and the result is the same." Their thinking, their rhetoric, their actions, their bearing—there is no real difference. Even the many new enterprises launched by student organizations, have they not already succumbed to corruption and, for the most part, flickered out into nothingness? It is much the same. But therein lies the true cause for anxiety regarding China’s future.

segs.zh: 散坐在讲堂里的二十多个听讲者，有的怅然了，或者是以为这话对；有的勃然了，大约是以为侮辱了神圣的青年；有几个却对他微笑了，大约以为这是他替自己的辩解：因为方玄绰就是兼做官僚的。
segs.en: The twenty or so listeners scattered about the lecture hall reacted in various ways. Some were crestfallen, perhaps feeling there was truth in what he said; others were indignant, likely offended that he had insulted the sanctity of youth. A few, however, smiled at him, presumably viewing his words as a self-justification, for Fang Xuanchuo was, after all, a man who also served as a bureaucrat.

segs.zh: 而其实却是都错误。这不过是他的一种新不平；虽说不平，又只是他的一种安分的空论。他自己虽然不知道是因为懒，还是因为无用，总之觉得是一个不肯运动，十分安分守己的人。总长冤他有神经病，只要地位还不至于动摇，他决不开一开口；教员的薪水欠到大半年了，只要别有官俸支持，他也决不开一开口。不但不开口，当教员联合索薪的时候，他还暗地里以为欠斟酌，太嚷嚷；直到听得同寮过分的奚落他们了，这才略有些小感慨，后来一转念，这或者因为自己正缺钱，而别的官并不兼做教员的缘故罢，于是就释然了。
segs.en: But in truth, all of this was wrong. It was merely a new kind of grievance of his; and though he called it a grievance, it was nothing more than a bit of idle, submissive speculation. He himself did not know whether it was out of laziness or sheer incompetence, but he felt he was a man who refused to bestir himself and was, in every sense, a law-abiding soul. When the Minister wrongly accused him of being mentally ill, he would not utter a single word so long as his position remained secure; when his salary as a teacher went unpaid for more than half a year, he would not utter a single word so long as he had his official stipend to fall back on. Not only did he keep silent, but when the other teachers banded together to demand their pay, he secretly felt it was ill-considered and far too boisterous. It was not until he heard his colleagues heaping excessive ridicule upon them that he felt a slight pang of sympathy, though a moment later he reconsidered: perhaps this was simply because he himself was short of cash, whereas the other officials did not hold teaching posts on the side. And with that, he was at peace once more.

segs.zh: 他虽然也缺钱，但从没有加入教员的团体内，大家议决罢课，可是不去上课了。政府说『上了课才给钱』，他才略恨他们的类乎用果子耍猴子；一个大教育家说道：『教员一手挟书包一手要钱不高尚。』他才对于他的太太正式的发牢骚了。
segs.en: Though he too was short of money, he had never joined the teachers' union; when the others voted to strike and simply stopped showing up to class, he did not. It was only when the government declared, "No classes, no pay," that he felt a flicker of resentment, as if he were a monkey being teased with a piece of fruit. Then, a great educator remarked, "It is ignoble for a teacher to hold a satchel in one hand and demand money with the other." Only then did he begin to air his grievances formally to his wife.

segs.zh: 「喂，怎么只有两盘？』听了『不高尚说』这一日的晚餐时候，他看着菜蔬说。
segs.en: "Hey, how come there are only two dishes?" he said at dinner that day, after having heard the "not-so-noble" theory, eyeing the vegetables as he spoke.

segs.zh: 他们是没有受过新教育的，太太并无学名或雅号，所以也就没有什么称呼了，照老例虽然也可以叫『太太』，但他又不愿意太守旧，于是就发明了一个『喂』字。太太对他却连『喂』字也没有，只要脸向着他说话，依据习惯法，他就知道这话是对他而发的。
segs.en: They had never received a modern education, and since the lady had no scholarly name or elegant sobriquet, she lacked any formal title of address. While by old custom she could have been called "Madam," he was unwilling to be so hidebound, and thus he invented the word "Hey." As for the lady, she did not even use "Hey" for him; she would simply turn her face toward him when she spoke, and by common law, he knew the words were intended for him.

segs.zh: 『可是上月领来的一成半都完了……昨天的米，也还是好容易才赊来的呢。』伊站在桌旁，脸对着他说。
segs.en: "But the fifteen percent I drew last month is all gone... Even yesterday's rice was only obtained on credit with the greatest difficulty." She stood by the table, her face turned toward him as she spoke.

segs.zh: 『你看，还说教书的要薪水是卑鄙哩。这种东西似乎连人要吃饭，饭要米做，米要钱买这一点粗浅事情都不知道……』
segs.en: Look at that," he said. "And he even has the nerve to claim it’s base for a teacher to ask for his wages. The man seems not to grasp even the most elementary facts of life—that people need to eat, that food is made of rice, and that rice must be bought with money...

segs.zh: 『对啦。没有钱怎么买米，没有米怎么煮……』他两颊都鼓起来了，仿佛气恼这答案正和他的议论『差不多』，近乎随声附和模样；接着便将头转向别一面去了，依据习惯法，这是宣告讨论中止的表示。
segs.en: "Exactly. If you haven't any money, how can you buy rice? And if you haven't any rice, how can you cook it..." His cheeks puffed out, as if he were annoyed that this answer was 'much the same' as his own argument, appearing little more than a sycophantic echo. He then turned his head away; by the common law of his habit, this was a signal that the discussion was closed.

segs.zh: 待到凄风冷雨这一天，教员们因为向政府去索欠薪，在新华门前烂泥里被国军打得头破血出之后，倒居然也发了一点薪水。方玄绰不费一举手之劳的领了钱，酌还些旧债，却还缺一大笔款，这是因为官俸也颇有些拖欠了。当是时，便是廉吏清官们也渐以为薪之不可不索，而况兼做教员的方玄绰，自然更表同情于学界起来，所以大家主张继续罢课的时候，他虽然仍未到场，事后却尤其心悦诚服的确守了公共的决议。
segs.en: When the day of bitter winds and cold rain arrived, the teachers, having gone to petition the government for their back pay, were beaten until their heads were bloodied in the mud before Xinhua Gate by the national troops; yet, remarkably, this actually resulted in the payment of a portion of their salaries. Fang Xuanchuo collected his money without lifting a finger and used it to pay off some old debts, though a large deficit remained, as his official stipend had also been in arrears for some time. By then, even the most incorruptible and upright officials were beginning to feel that salaries must be demanded, and Fang Xuanchuo, who also worked as a teacher, naturally grew even more sympathetic toward the academic community. Thus, when everyone advocated for a continued strike, although he still did not attend the meetings, he was, in the aftermath, particularly heartfelt and submissive in his strict observance of the collective resolution.

segs.zh: 然而政府竟又付钱，学校也就开课了。但在前几天，却有学生总会上一个呈文给政府，说：『教员倘若不上课，便不要付欠薪。』这虽然并无效，而方玄绰却忽而记起前回政府所说的『上了课才给钱』的话来，『差不多』这一个影子在他眼前又一幌，而且并不消灭，于是他便在讲堂上公表了。
segs.en: Yet the government did, in fact, pay up, and the school resumed classes. A few days earlier, however, a student union had submitted a petition to the government stating: "If the teachers do not hold classes, they should not be paid their back wages." Though this had no effect, Fang Xuanchuo suddenly recalled the government's previous ultimatum that "pay would only be issued upon the resumption of classes." The specter of "good enough" flickered before his eyes once more, and, refusing to fade, it prompted him to make a public announcement of it in the lecture hall.

segs.zh: 准此，可见如果将『差不多说』锻炼罗织起来，自然也可以判作一种挟带私心的不平，但总不能说是专为自己做官的辩解。只是每到这些时，他又常常喜欢拉上中国将来的命运之类的问题，一不小心，便连自己也以为是一个忧国的志士：人们是每苦于没有『自知之明』的。
segs.en: From this, it is evident that if one were to forge and weave these "near-enough" remarks together, they could naturally be construed as a grievance born of self-interest; yet, one could never claim they were intended solely as a defense for one’s own official career. It is just that, whenever these moments arise, he is fond of dragging in questions regarding the future destiny of China, and if he is not careful, he even begins to believe himself a patriot concerned for the nation: people, after all, often suffer from a lack of self-knowledge.

segs.zh: 但是『差不多』的事实又发生了，政府当初虽只不理那些招人头痛的教员，后来竟不理到无关痛痒的官吏，欠而又欠，终于逼得先前鄙薄教员要钱的好官，也很有几员化为索薪大会里的骁将了。惟有几种日报上却很发了些鄙薄讥笑他们的文字。方玄绰也毫不为奇，毫不介意，因为他根据了他的『差不多说』，知道这是新闻记者还未缺少润笔的缘故，万一政府或是阔人停了津贴，他们多半也要开大会的。
segs.en: Yet the phenomenon of "near enough" soon repeated itself. While the government had initially ignored only those troublesome teachers, it eventually grew so indifferent that it began to ignore even the most inconsequential of bureaucrats. Paychecks were delayed again and again, until finally, even several of those upright officials who had previously looked down on teachers for demanding money were transformed into valiant champions at the rallies for back pay. Only a few daily newspapers published articles sneering at and belittling them. Fang Xuanchuo, however, was neither surprised nor bothered; for, based on his "theory of near enough," he knew this was simply because those journalists had not yet run out of pocket money. Should the government or the wealthy patrons ever cut off their subsidies, they, too, would most likely be holding rallies of their own.

segs.zh: 他既已表同情于教员的索薪，自然也赞成同寮的索俸，然而他仍然安坐在衙门中，照例的并不一同去讨债。至于有人疑心他孤高，那可也不过是一种误解罢了。他自己说，他是自从出世以来，只有人向他来要债，他从没有向人去讨过债，所以这一端是『非其所长』。而且他最不敢见手握经济之权的人物，这种人待到失了权势之后，捧着一本大乘起信论讲佛学的时候，固然也很是『蔼然可亲』的了，但还在宝座上时，却总是一副阎王脸，将别人都当奴才看，自以为手操着你们这些穷小子们的生杀之权。他因此不敢见，也不愿见他们。这种脾气，虽然有时连自己也觉得是孤高，但往往同时也疑心这其实是没本领。
segs.en: Since he had already expressed sympathy for the teachers demanding their salaries, it was only natural that he should also approve of his colleagues demanding their pay. Nevertheless, he remained comfortably seated in the yamen, as usual, and did not join them in dunning for debt. As for those who suspected him of being aloof, that was merely a misunderstanding. He himself claimed that ever since he was born, he had only ever been dunned for debts by others; he had never once gone to dun anyone else, and therefore this sort of thing was "not his forte." Furthermore, he was terrified of meeting those who held the purse strings. When such men eventually lost their power and sat clutching a copy of the *Awakening of Faith in the Mahayana* to lecture on Buddhism, they were, to be sure, "amiable and approachable" enough; but while they were still on their pedestals, they always wore the faces of Kings of Hell, treating everyone else like lackeys and fancying themselves in possession of the power of life and death over poor devils like us. For this reason, he dared not see them, nor did he wish to. Though at times he felt this temperament of his might indeed be a form of aloofness, he often suspected, simultaneously, that it was actually nothing more than a lack of ability.

segs.zh: 大家左索右索，总算一节一节的挨过去了，但比起先前来，方玄绰究竟是万分的拮据，所以使用的小厮和交易的店家不消说，便是方太太对于他也渐渐的缺了敬意，只要看伊近来不很附和，而且常常提出独创的意见，有些唐突的举动，也就可以了然了。到了阴历五月初四的午前，他一回来，伊便将一叠帐单塞在他的鼻子跟前，这也是往常所没有的。
segs.en: They scraped and scrambled, somehow managing to get by day after day, but compared to before, Fang Xuanchuo was now in dire financial straits. It went without saying that his servants and the local tradesmen had lost their respect for him; even Mrs. Fang had begun to treat him with less deference. One had only to observe how she rarely agreed with him lately, frequently offered unsolicited opinions, and acted with such sudden, jarring insolence to see that clearly enough. On the morning of the fourth day of the fifth lunar month, no sooner had he stepped through the door than she thrust a stack of bills right under his nose—something that had never happened before.

segs.zh: 『一总总得一百八十块钱才够开消……发了么？』伊并不对着他看的说。
segs.en: "Altogether, it’s going to take a hundred and eighty dollars just to cover the expenses... Have you got it yet?" she asked, not looking at him.

segs.zh: 『哼，我明天不做官了。钱的支票是领来的了，可是索薪大会的代表不发放，先说是没有同去的人都不发，后来又说是要到他们跟前去亲领。他们今天单捏着支票，就变了阎王脸了，我实在怕看见……我钱也不要了，官也不做了，这样无限量的卑屈……』
segs.en: Hmph, I’m done being an official tomorrow. The check for my salary has been issued, but the representatives at the pay-demand meeting won't hand it over. At first, they claimed they wouldn't give it to anyone unless we all went together, and later they insisted we had to go and collect it from them in person. Today, just by holding onto that check, they’ve already turned into the faces of Yama. I’m truly afraid to see them... I don't want the money, and I don't want the post either. Such boundless humiliation...

segs.zh: 方太太见了这少见的义愤，倒有些愕然了，但也就沈静下来。
segs.en: Mrs. Fang, witnessing this rare display of righteous indignation, was taken aback for a moment, but she soon grew quiet.

segs.zh: 『我想，还不如去亲领罢，这算什么呢。』伊看着他的脸说。
segs.en: "I think it would be better to go and claim it yourself; what is the meaning of this?" she said, looking at his face.

segs.zh: 『我不去！这是官俸，不是赏钱，照例应该由会计科送来的。』
segs.en: I won't go! This is a government salary, not a tip; by regulation, it should be delivered by the accounting department.

segs.zh: 『可是不送来又怎么好呢……哦，昨夜忘记说了，孩子们说那学费，学校里已经催过好几次了，说是倘若再不缴……』
segs.en: But what else can be done if we don't send it? Oh, I forgot to mention last night: the children said the school has been pressing for the tuition again. They say if it isn't paid soon...

segs.zh: 『胡说！做老子的办事教书都不给钱，儿子去念几句书倒要钱？』
segs.en: Nonsense! I’m a father who does all this work and teaching without getting paid a cent, yet my son has to pay just to read a few books?

segs.zh: 伊觉得他已经不很顾忌道理，似乎就要将自己当作校长来出气，犯不上，便不再言语了。
segs.en: She sensed that he no longer felt bound by reason and seemed on the verge of venting his frustrations on her as if she were the principal; it wasn't worth the trouble, so she held her tongue.

segs.zh: 两个默默的吃了午饭。他想了一会，又懊恼的出去了。
segs.en: The two of them ate their midday meal in silence. After pondering for a while, he went out again, looking vexed.

segs.zh: 照旧例，近年是每逢节根或年关的前一天，他一定须在夜里的十二点钟才回家，一面走，一面掏着怀中，一面大声的叫道：『喂，领来了！』于是递给伊一叠簇新的中交票，脸上很有些得意的形色。谁知道初四这一天却破了例，他不到七点钟便回家来。方太太很惊疑，以为他竟已辞了职了，但暗暗地察看他脸上，却也并不见有什么格外倒运的神情。
segs.en: By established custom, for the past few years, he would invariably return home only at the stroke of midnight on the eve of a festival or the lunar New Year. As he walked, he would fumble in his breast pocket and call out loudly, "Hey, I’ve brought the pay!" Then he would hand her a stack of crisp Bank of Communications notes, his face wearing a look of distinct self-satisfaction. Who would have guessed that this fourth day of the month would break the pattern? He was home before seven o’clock. Mrs. Fang was deeply alarmed, suspecting he had been dismissed from his post, yet as she scrutinized his face in secret, she could see no sign of any particular misfortune.

segs.zh: 『怎么了？……这样早？……』伊看定了他说。
segs.en: "What’s wrong? ... So early? ..." She fixed her eyes on him as she spoke.

segs.zh: 『发不及了，领不出了，银行已经关了门，得等初八。』
segs.en: It's too late, I can't draw it out; the bank has already closed its doors and won't reopen until the eighth.

segs.zh: 『亲领？……』伊惴惴的问。
segs.en: "Personally collect it?..." she asked, trembling.

segs.zh: 『亲领这一层，倒也已经取消了，听说仍旧由会计科分送。可是银行今天已经关了门，休息三天，得等到初八的上午。』他坐下，眼睛看着地面了，喝过一口茶，才又慢慢的开口说：『幸而衙门里也没有什么问题了，大约到初八就准有钱……向不相干的亲戚朋友去借钱，实在是一件烦难事。我午后硬着头皮去寻金永生，谈了一会，他先恭维我不去索薪，不肯亲领，非常之清高，一个人正应该这样做；待到知道我想要向他通融五十元，就像我在他嘴里塞了一大把盐似的，凡有脸上可以打皱的地方都打起皱来，说房租怎样的收不起，买卖怎样的赔本，在同事面前亲身领款，也不算什么的，即刻将我支使出来了。』
segs.en: As for collecting it in person, that has actually been cancelled; I hear it will be distributed by the accounting department as before. But the bank is closed today for a three-day holiday, so we have to wait until the morning of the eighth." He sat down, eyes fixed on the floor. After a sip of tea, he began to speak again, slowly: "Fortunately, there shouldn't be any trouble at the office; I’m certain the money will be there by the eighth... Borrowing from friends and relatives who have nothing to do with this is truly an arduous business. This afternoon, I steeled myself and went to look for Jin Yongsheng. We chatted for a while, and he first complimented me on being so noble for refusing to demand my salary or collect it in person—saying that is exactly how a man should behave. But the moment he realized I wanted to borrow fifty dollars from him, it was as if I’d stuffed a handful of salt into his mouth. Every wrinkle that could possibly appear on his face appeared at once. He went on about how impossible it is to collect rent, how his business is losing money, and how there’s really nothing wrong with collecting one's pay in front of colleagues—and just like that, he sent me packing.

segs.zh: 『这样紧急的节根，谁还肯借出钱去呢。』方太太却只淡淡的说，并没有什么慨然。
segs.en: “At a time like this, who would be willing to lend out money?” Mrs. Fang said coolly, without a hint of sympathy.

segs.zh: 方玄绰低下头来了，觉得这也无怪其然的，况且自己和金永生本来很疏远，他接着就记起去年年关的事来，那时有一个同乡来借十块钱，他其时明明已经收到了衙门的领款凭单的了，因为恐怕这人将来未必会还钱，便装了一副为难的神色，说道衙门里既然领不到俸钱，学校里又不发薪水，实在『爱莫能助』，将他空手送走了。他虽然自己并不看见装了怎样的脸，但此时却觉得很局促，嘴唇微微一动，又摇一摇头。
segs.en: Fang Xuanchuo lowered his head, feeling that this was only to be expected; besides, he and Jin Yongsheng had always been quite distant. He then recalled an incident from the end of the previous year, when a fellow townsman had come to borrow ten dollars. At the time, he had clearly already received his salary voucher from the yamen, but fearing that the man might not pay him back, he had put on a troubled expression and said that since he couldn't collect his stipend from the yamen and the school wasn't paying his salary either, he was truly "powerless to help." He had sent the man away empty-handed. Though he had not seen his own face at the time, he now felt quite ill at ease; his lips twitched slightly, and he shook his head.

segs.zh: 然而不多久，他忽而恍然大悟似的发命令了：叫小厮即刻上街去赊一甁莲花白。他知道店家希图明天多还帐，大抵是不敢不赊的，假如不赊，则明天分文不还，正是他们应得的惩罚。
segs.en: Yet before long, he seemed to have a sudden epiphany and issued a command: he told the boy to go to the street immediately and buy a bottle of Lianhuabai on credit. He knew that since the shopkeeper was hoping for a larger payment tomorrow, he would hardly dare refuse; and if he did refuse, then not a single cent would be paid tomorrow—which would be exactly the punishment he deserved.

segs.zh: 莲花白竟赊来了，他喝了两杯，青白色的脸上泛了红，吃完饭，又颇有些高兴了，他点上一枝大号哈得门香烟，从桌上抓起一本尝试集来，躺在床上就要看。
segs.en: He had actually managed to get the Lotus White on credit. After two cups, a flush spread over his sallow face, and by the time he finished his meal, he was in quite high spirits. He lit a large Hartman cigarette, snatched up a copy of *Experiments* from the table, and lay down on the bed to read.

segs.zh: 『那么，明天怎么对付店家呢？』方太太追上去，站在床面前，看着他的脸说。
segs.en: “Then how are we going to deal with the shopkeeper tomorrow?” Mrs. Fang hurried after him, stood before the bed, and looked him in the face as she spoke.

segs.zh: 『店家？……教他们初八的下半天来。』
segs.en: Innkeeper? ... Tell them to come on the afternoon of the eighth.

segs.zh: 『我可不能这么说。他们不相信，不答应的。』
segs.en: I really couldn't say that. They wouldn't believe me, and they wouldn't agree to it.

segs.zh: 『有什么不相信。他们可以问去，全衙门里什么人也没有领到，都得初八！』他戟着第二个指头在帐子里的空中画了一个半圆，方太太跟着指头也看了一个半圆，只见这手便去翻开了尝试集。
segs.en: "Why shouldn't I be believed? They can go and ask around; not a single person in the entire yamen has received their pay, everyone has to wait until the eighth!" He pointed his second finger into the air inside the mosquito net and traced a semicircle. Mrs. Fang’s eyes followed the finger through its arc, only to see the hand then reach out and flip open the *Collection of Experiments*.

segs.zh: 方太太见他强横到出乎情理之外了，也暂时开不得口。
segs.en: Mrs. Fang, seeing that his arrogance had reached such an unreasonable pitch, was for the moment struck dumb.

segs.zh: 『我想，这模样是闹不下去的，将来总得想点法，做点什么别的事……』伊终于寻到了别的路，说。
segs.en: I suppose," she said, having finally found another way, "that things can't go on like this. Sooner or later, I'll have to think of something else—to find some other line of work...

segs.zh: 『什么法呢？我「文不像誊录生武不像救火兵」，别的做什么？』
segs.en: What else can I do? I'm neither a scribe in my writing nor a fireman in my fighting—what else is there for me?

segs.zh: 『你不是给上海的书铺子做过文章么？』
segs.en: Didn't you write articles for those publishing houses in Shanghai?

segs.zh: 『上海的书铺子？买稿要一个一个的算字，空格不算数。你看我做在那里的白话诗去，空白有多少，怕只值三百大钱一本罢。收版权税又半年六月没消息，「远水救不得近火」，谁耐烦。』
segs.en: Shanghai bookstores? They pay for manuscripts by the character, and they don't count the spaces. You look at those vernacular poems I wrote for them—with all those blank spaces, I’m afraid a whole book wouldn't fetch more than three hundred coppers. And as for royalties? Six months have passed without a word. ‘Distant water cannot put out a nearby fire,’ and who has the patience for that?

segs.zh: 『那么，给这里的报馆里……』
segs.en: In that case, why don't you send it to the newspaper office here...

segs.zh: 『给报馆里？便在这里很大的报馆里，我靠着一个学生在那里做编辑的大情面，一千字也就是这几个钱，即使一早做到夜，能够养活你们么？况且我肚子里也没有这许多文章。』
segs.en: Send it to a newspaper? Even at the big newspapers here, thanks to the pull of a student I know who works as an editor, a thousand words only fetches this much money. Even if I were to write from dawn till dusk, could I support you all on that? Besides, I don’t have that many articles in me.

segs.zh: 『那么，过了节怎么办呢？』
segs.en: Well then, what are we to do after the festival?

segs.zh: 『过了节么？——仍旧做官……明天店家来要钱，你只要说初八的下午。』
segs.en: Is the festival over? Still serving as an official, I see... When the shopkeeper comes for money tomorrow, just tell him it will be the afternoon of the eighth.

segs.zh: 他又要看尝试集了。方太太怕失了机会，连忙吞吞吐吐的说：
segs.en: He wanted to see the *Experiment* collection again. Fearing she might miss her chance, Mrs. Fang hurriedly stammered:

segs.zh: 『我想，过了节，到了初八，我们……倒不如去买一张彩票……』
segs.en: I was thinking, once the festival is over, come the eighth, we... we might as well go buy a lottery ticket...

segs.zh: 『胡说！会说出这样无教育的……』
segs.en: Nonsense! How could you say something so uneducated...

segs.zh: 这时候，他忽而又记起被金永生支使出来以后的事了。那时他惘惘的走过稻香村，看见店门口竖着许多斗大的字的广告道『头彩几万元』，仿佛记得心里也一动，或者也许放慢了脚步的罢，但似乎因为舍不得皮夹里仅存的六角钱，所以竟也毅然决然的走远了。他脸色一变，方太太料想他是在恼着伊的无教育，便赶紧退开，没有说完话。方玄绰也没有说完话，将腰一伸，咿咿呜呜的就念尝试集。
segs.en: At this moment, he suddenly recalled what had happened after Jin Yongsheng had sent him out on an errand. He had been walking aimlessly past Daoxiangcun when he saw a sign at the shop entrance with characters as large as peck-measures, proclaiming: "First Prize: Tens of Thousands." He remembered, or perhaps just imagined, that his heart had skipped a beat, and that he might even have slowed his pace. But, seemingly unable to part with the last sixty cents in his wallet, he had resolutely walked away. His expression shifted. Mrs. Fang, assuming he was annoyed by her lack of education, quickly backed away, leaving her sentence unfinished. Fang Xuanchuo did not finish his, either; he stretched his waist, and began to chant, in a low, droning hum, from the *Experiment Collection*.

segs.zh: （一九二二年六月。）
这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may also be in the public domain in countries and regions where the copyright term is based on the life of the author plus an additional term, but which apply a shorter term rule to foreign works.

segs.zh: 据《中华人民共和国著作权法释义》，法人作品应满足三点要求：（1）由法人（或其他组织，以下简称“法人”）主持创作，而非工作人员自发进行；（2）创作思想及表达方式体现法人意志；（3）由法人负责，而非执笔者。（详情）
segs.en: According to the Interpretation of the Copyright Law of the People's Republic of China, a work created by a legal entity must satisfy three requirements: (1) it is created under the direction of the legal entity (or other organization, hereinafter referred to as the "legal entity"), rather than being a spontaneous effort by staff members; (2) the creative ideas and the manner of expression reflect the will of the legal entity; and (3) the legal entity, rather than the individual writer, bears the responsibility. (Details)

segs.zh: 陈士成看过县考的榜，回到家里的时候，已经是下午了。他去得本很早，一见榜，便先在这上面寻陈字。陈字也不少，似乎也都争先恐后的跳进他眼睛里来，然而接着的却全不是士成这两个字。他于是重新再在十二张榜的圆图里细细地搜寻，看的人全已散尽了，而陈士成在榜上终于没有见，单站在试院的照壁的面前。
segs.en: It was already afternoon by the time Chen Shicheng returned home after checking the results of the county examination. He had gone early, and the moment he saw the posted list, he began searching for the character "Chen." There were plenty of Chens, and they seemed to leap into his eyes, jostling for attention, but the characters that followed were never "Shicheng." He searched through the twelve sheets of the list again, meticulously, line by line. By then, the crowd had dispersed entirely, yet Chen Shicheng still had not found his name; he stood alone, staring blankly at the screen wall of the examination hall.

segs.zh: 凉风虽然拂拂的吹动他斑白的短发，初冬的太阳却还是很温和的来晒他。但他似乎被太阳晒得头晕了，脸色越加变成灰白，从劳乏的红肿的两眼里，发出古怪的闪光。这时他其实早已不看到什么墙上的榜文了，只见有许多乌黑的圆圈，在眼前泛泛的游走。
segs.en: Though a cool breeze stirred his grizzled, short hair, the early winter sun shone down upon him with a gentle warmth. Yet he seemed dizzied by the sun, his face turning an even ashier grey, while from his weary, inflamed eyes shot a strange and peculiar gleam. In truth, he was no longer seeing the notice on the wall; all he could see were countless black circles drifting aimlessly before his eyes.

segs.zh: 隽了秀才，上省去乡试，一径联捷上去，……绅士们既然千方百计的来攀亲，人们又都像看见神明似的敬畏，深悔先前的轻薄，发昏，……赶走了租住在自己破宅门里的杂姓——那是不劳说赶，自己就搬的，——屋宇全新了，门口是旗竿和扁额，……要清高可以做京官，否则不如谋外放。……他平日安排停当的前程，这时候又像受潮的糖塔一般，刹时倒塌，只剩下一堆碎片了。他不自觉的旋转了觉得涣散了的身躯，惘惘的走向归家的路。
segs.en: Once he had passed the xiucai examinations, he headed to the provincial capital for the triennial exams, succeeding in one unbroken streak of victories... The local gentry, having moved heaven and earth to forge ties of kinship with him, now looked upon him with the fearful reverence one might reserve for a deity, deeply regretting their earlier slights and folly... He drove out the various families who had been renting rooms in his dilapidated ancestral home—though, truth be told, they moved out of their own accord—the buildings were renovated, and flagpoles and inscribed plaques were erected at the gate... If he wished to maintain his prestige, he could take a post in the capital; otherwise, a provincial appointment would be just as well... The future he had so carefully mapped out in his mind now crumbled in an instant, like a sugar pagoda left out in the damp, leaving behind nothing but a heap of shards. Unconsciously, he turned his weary, listless body around and began the dazed journey home.

segs.zh: 他刚到自己的房门口，七个学童便一齐放开喉咙，吱的念起书来。他大吃一惊，耳朵边似乎敲了一声磬，只见七个头拖了小辫子在眼前幌，幌得满房，黑圈子也夹着跳舞。他坐下了，他们送上晚课来，脸上都显出小觑他的神色。
segs.en: He had just reached his door when the seven pupils all let out their voices at once, screeching their lessons. He was taken aback, as if a chime had been struck right beside his ear; all he could see were seven heads with little pigtails swinging before his eyes, filling the room, while dark circles danced along with them. He sat down, and they brought him their evening lessons, all wearing expressions that clearly showed their contempt for him.

segs.zh: 『回去罢。』他迟了片时疑，这才悲惨的说。
segs.en: "Go back, then." He hesitated for a moment, then spoke the words in a voice of utter misery.

segs.zh: 他们胡乱的包了书包，挟着，一溜烟跑走了。
segs.en: They bundled up their books in a haphazard fashion, tucked them under their arms, and took to their heels in a flash.

segs.zh: 陈士成还看见许多小头夹着黑圆圈在眼前跳舞，有时杂乱，有时也排成异样的阵图，然而渐渐的减少，模胡了。
segs.en: Chen Shicheng saw many small heads with black circles dancing before his eyes; sometimes they swirled in a jumble, sometimes they aligned into strange, formation-like patterns, until they gradually thinned and faded into a blur.

segs.zh: 『这回又完了！』
segs.en: This time it's all over again!

segs.zh: 他大吃一惊，直跳起来，分明就在耳朵边的话，回过头去却并没有什么人，仿佛又听得嗡的敲了一声磬，自己的嘴也说道：
segs.en: He was taken aback and leapt straight up; it was as if the words had been spoken right beside his ear, yet when he turned his head, there was no one there. It was as if he had heard the dull chime of a stone bell, and his own mouth spoke:

segs.zh: 『这回又完了！』
segs.en: Finished again!

segs.zh: 他忽而举起一只手来，屈指计数着想，十一，十三回，连今年是十六回，竟没有一个考官懂得文章，有眼无珠，也是可怜的事，便不由嘻嘻的失了笑。然而他愤然了，蓦地从书包布底下抽出誊真的制艺和试帖来，拿着往外走，刚近房门，却看见满眼都明亮，连一群鸡也正在笑他，便禁不住的头突突的狂跳，只好缩回里面了。
segs.en: He suddenly raised a hand, counting on his fingers as he mused: eleven, thirteen—including this year, sixteen times. Not a single examiner had understood his essays; blind, utterly blind, the poor wretches. At this, he could not help but let out a thin, mocking titter. Yet, suddenly incensed, he whipped his fair-copied examination essays and poems from beneath his book-wrapper and strode toward the door. But as he neared the threshold, the world outside seemed to blaze with light, and even a flock of chickens appeared to be laughing at him. His heart began to hammer wildly against his temples, and he had no choice but to retreat back into the room.

segs.zh: 他又就了坐，眼光格外的闪烁；他目睹着许多东西，然而很模胡，——是到塌了的糖塔一般的前程躺在他面前，这前程又只是广大起来，阻住了他的一切路。
segs.en: He sat down again, his eyes gleaming with an extraordinary intensity; he stared at many things, yet all was blurred—a future like a collapsed sugar pagoda lay before him, and this future only kept expanding, blocking every path he might have taken.

segs.zh: 别家的炊烟早消歇了，碗筷也洗过了，而陈士成还不去做饭。寓在这里的杂姓是知道老例的，凡遇到县考的年头，看见发榜后的这样的眼光，不如及早关了门，不要多管事。最先就绝了人声，接着是陆续的熄了灯火，独有月亮，却缓缓的出现在寒夜的空中。
segs.en: The smoke from other houses had long since vanished, and the dishes had been washed, yet Chen Shicheng still made no move to cook. The various families boarding here knew the custom: whenever a year of the county examinations came around, the sight of that look in a man’s eyes after the lists were posted was a sign to bolt one’s door early and mind one’s own business. First, the human voices ceased, then one by one the lamps were extinguished, leaving only the moon to emerge slowly into the cold night sky.

segs.zh: 空中青碧到如一片海，略有些浮云，仿佛有谁将粉笔洗在笔洗里似的摇曳。月亮对着陈士成注下寒冷的光波来，当初也不过像是一面新磨的铁镜罢了，而这镜却诡秘的照透了陈士成的全身，就在他身上映出铁的月亮的影。
segs.en: The sky was a deep, oceanic blue, streaked with thin, drifting clouds that swayed like chalk-water swirled in a basin. The moon cast a cold, shimmering light down upon Chen Shicheng—at first glance, it seemed no more than a freshly polished iron mirror. Yet this mirror shone with a sinister clarity, piercing through him and imprinting the reflection of an iron moon upon his very body.

segs.zh: 他还在房外的院子里徘徊，眼里颇清净了，四近也寂静。但这寂静忽又无端的纷扰起来，他耳边又确凿听到急促的低声说：
segs.en: He was still pacing the courtyard outside the room; his eyes felt quite clear now, and all around was silent. But this silence was suddenly, inexplicably disturbed, and he heard, quite distinctly, a hurried whisper in his ear:

segs.zh: 『左弯右弯……』
segs.en: Twisting and turning...

segs.zh: 他耸然了，倾耳听时，那声音却又提高的复述道：
segs.en: He stiffened, and as he inclined his head to listen, the voice repeated itself, only louder:

segs.zh: 『右弯！』
segs.en: Right turn!

segs.zh: 他记得了。这院子，是他家还未如此雕零的时候，一到夏天的夜间，夜夜和他的祖母在此纳凉的院子。那时他不过十岁有零的孩子，躺在竹榻上，祖母便坐在榻旁边，讲给他有趣的故事听。伊说是曾经听得伊的祖母说，陈氏的祖宗是钜富的，这屋子便是祖基，祖宗埋着无数的银子，有福气的子孙一定会得到的罢，然而至今还没有现。至于处所，那是藏在一个谜语的中间：
segs.en: He remembered. This courtyard was the very place where, before his family had fallen into such ruin, he would sit every summer night to catch the breeze with his grandmother. He was barely ten years old then, lying on a bamboo couch while his grandmother sat beside him, telling him fascinating stories. She said she had heard from her own grandmother that the Chen ancestors had been men of immense wealth, and that this house was built upon the family foundation, beneath which lay buried untold amounts of silver. Surely, she would say, a fortunate descendant would one day find it, though to this day it had yet to appear. As for the exact location, it remained hidden within a riddle:

segs.zh: 『左弯右弯，前走后走，量金量银不论斗。』
segs.en: Turn left, turn right, go forward, go back; weighing gold and silver, never by the peck.

segs.zh: 对于这谜语，陈士成便在平时，本也常常暗地里加以揣测的，可惜大抵刚以为可通，却又立刻觉得不合了。有一回，他确有把握，知道这是在租给唐家的房底下的了，然而总没有前去发掘的勇气；过了几时，可又觉得太不相像了。至于他自己房子里的几个掘过的旧痕迹，那却全是先前几回下第以后的发了怔忡的举动，后来自己一看到，也还感到惭愧而且羞人。
segs.en: As for this riddle, Chen Shicheng had often pondered it in secret, though it was a pity that just as he thought he had hit upon a solution, he would immediately find it did not fit. Once, he had been absolutely certain that the treasure lay beneath the foundations of the house he rented to the Tang family, yet he could never summon the courage to go and dig it up; after a while, he began to feel that the theory did not hold water after all. As for the few old pits in his own house, they were merely the fruit of his distracted frenzies following his previous failures in the examinations—scars that, whenever he caught sight of them later, filled him with a sense of shame and humiliation.

segs.zh: 但今天铁的光罩住了陈士成，又软软的来劝他了，他或者偶一迟疑，便给他正经的证明，又加上阴森的催逼，使他不得不又向自己的房里转过眼光去。
segs.en: But today, the iron cowl had settled over Chen Shicheng, pressing down upon him with a soft, insistent persuasion. If he so much as hesitated, it would offer him solemn proofs and add to them a chilling urgency, leaving him no choice but to turn his gaze back toward his own room.

segs.zh: 白光如一柄白团扇，摇摇摆摆的闪起在他房里了。
segs.en: A white light, like a round white fan, began to flicker and sway in his room.

segs.zh: 『也终于在这里！』
segs.en: So it finally ends here!

segs.zh: 他说着，狮子似的赶快走进那房里去，但跨进里面的时候，便不见了白光的影踪，只有莽苍苍的一间旧房，和几个破书桌都没在昏暗里。他爽然的站着，慢慢的再定睛，然而白光却分明的又起来了，这回更广大，比硫黄火更白净，比朝雾更霏微，而且便在靠东墙的一张书桌下。
segs.en: He spoke, then lunged into the room like a lion, but the moment he crossed the threshold, the white light had vanished. There was only a vast, desolate room, and a few broken desks submerged in the gloom. He stood there, struck with sudden disappointment, and slowly steadied his gaze; yet the white light clearly rose again, this time more expansive, whiter and purer than a sulfur flame, finer and more ethereal than the morning mist, and it was right there, beneath a desk against the eastern wall.

segs.zh: 陈士成狮子似的奔到门后边，伸手去摸锄头，撞着一条黑影。他不知怎的有些怕了，张惶的点了灯，看锄头无非倚着。他移开桌子，用锄头一气掘起四块大方砖，蹲身一看，照例是黄澄澄的细沙，揎了袖爬开细沙，便露出下面的黑土来。他极小心的，幽静的，一锄一锄往下掘，然而深夜究竟太寂静了，尖铁触土的声音，总是钝重的不肯瞒人的发响。
segs.en: Chen Shicheng rushed behind the door like a lion, reaching out to grab his hoe, only to collide with a dark shadow. For some reason, he felt a sudden tremor of fear. Flustered, he lit the lamp, but the hoe was merely leaning there as always. He pushed the table aside and, with his hoe, pried up four large square bricks in one go. Crouching down to look, he saw, as expected, only fine, golden sand. Rolling up his sleeves, he shoveled the sand away to reveal the black earth beneath. He dug down, one stroke after another, with extreme care and in profound silence; yet, the night was so still that the sound of the sharp iron striking the earth rang out with a dull, heavy persistence that refused to be hidden.

segs.zh: 土坑深到二尺多了，并不见有瓮口，陈士成正心焦，一声脆响，颇震得手腕痛，锄尖碰着什么坚硬的东西了；他急忙抛下锄头，摸索着看时，一块大方砖在下面。他的心抖得很利害，聚精会神的挖起那方砖来，下面也满是先前一样的黑土，爬松了许多土，下面似乎还无穷。但忽而又触着坚硬的小东西了，圆的，大约是一个锈铜钱；此外也还有几片破碎的磁片。
segs.en: The pit was already over two feet deep, yet there was still no sign of an earthenware jar. Just as Chen Shicheng was growing anxious, a crisp clink—sharp enough to make his wrist ache—signaled that his hoe had struck something hard. He hurriedly cast the tool aside and reached down to feel about; it was a large, square brick. His heart hammered violently as he set to work with intense concentration to pry it up. Beneath it lay the same black earth as before; he loosened a great deal of it, but it seemed to go on forever. Suddenly, he struck another small, hard object—something round, likely a rusted copper coin—along with a few jagged shards of porcelain.

segs.zh: 陈士成心里仿佛觉得空虚了，浑身流汗，急躁的只爬搔；这其间，心在空中一抖动，又触着一种古怪的小东西了，这似乎约略有些马掌形的，但触手很松脆。他又聚精会神的挖起那东西来，谨慎的撮着，就灯光下仔细的看时，那东西斑斑剥剥的像是烂骨头，上面还带着一排零落不全的牙齿。他已经悟到这许是下巴骨了，而那下巴骨也便在他手里索索的动弹起来，而且笑吟吟的显出笑影，终于听得他开口道：
segs.en: Chen Shicheng felt a sudden void open up inside him; he broke into a sweat, his fingers twitching with frantic impatience. In the midst of this, his heart gave a jolt as he brushed against some strange little object—something roughly horseshoe-shaped, yet crumbly to the touch. He began to dig it out with rapt intensity, cupping it gingerly in his hands to inspect it under the lamplight. It was a mottled, decaying thing, like a piece of rotted bone, and clinging to it was a jagged, incomplete row of teeth. He realized then that it must be a jawbone, and even as he held it, the jaw began to quiver in his hands, wearing a faint, mirthful grin, until at last he heard it open its mouth and say:

segs.zh: 『这回又完了！』
segs.en: This time, it's over again!

segs.zh: 他栗然的发了大冷，同时也放了手，下巴骨轻飘飘的回到坑底里不多久，他也就逃到院子里了。他偷看房里面，灯火如此辉煌，下巴骨如此嘲笑，异乎寻常的怕人，便再不敢向那边看。他躺在远处的檐下的阴影里，觉得较为平安了；但在这平安中，忽而耳朵边又听得窃窃的低声说：
segs.en: A sudden, violent shivering seized him, and his grip slackened. Before long, his jawbone rattled back into its socket, and he fled into the courtyard. Peering inside, he saw the room ablaze with light and the jawbone mocking him with a terror beyond reason; he dared not look again. He lay down in the shadow of the eaves some distance away, feeling a measure of safety; yet even in this safety, he suddenly heard, right beside his ear, a low, whispered murmuring:

segs.zh: 『这里没有……到山里去……』
segs.en: There’s nothing here… go into the mountains…

segs.zh: 陈士成似乎记得白天在街上也曾听得有人说这种话，他不待再听完，已经恍然大悟了。他突然仰面向天，月亮已向西高峰这方面隐去，远想离城三十五里的西高峰正在眼前，朝笏一般黑魆魆的挺立着，周围便放出浩大闪烁的白光来。
segs.en: Chen Shicheng seemed to recall having heard someone say something similar on the street earlier that day. Without waiting to hear more, he was suddenly struck by a flash of realization. He abruptly tilted his head back toward the sky. The moon had begun to sink toward the West Peak, and in his mind’s eye, the peak—thirty-five li from the city—loomed directly before him, standing stark and black like a ceremonial tablet, casting a vast, shimmering white light all around.

segs.zh: 而且这白光又远远的就在前面了。
segs.en: And that white light was already there, gleaming in the distance just ahead.

segs.zh: 『是的，到山里去！』
segs.en: Yes, to the mountains!

segs.zh: 他决定的想，惨然的奔出去了。几回的开门声之后，门里面便再不闻一些声息。灯光结了大灯花照着空屋和坑洞，毕毕剥剥的炸了几声之后，便渐渐的缩小以至于无有，那是残油已经烧尽了。
segs.en: He reached a firm decision, then rushed out in misery. After the door had opened and shut a few times, no further sound came from within. The lamp, crowned with a large snuff, cast its light over the empty room and the hollows of the kang; after sputtering and crackling a few times, the flame gradually shrank until it vanished altogether—the last of the oil had burned away.

segs.zh: 『开城门来～～』
segs.en: Open the city gates!

segs.zh: 含着大希望的恐怖的悲声，游丝似的在西关门前的黎明中，战战兢兢的叫喊。
segs.en: A cry of terror, heavy with great hope, drifted like a gossamer thread through the dawn before the West Gate, trembling as it wailed.

segs.zh: 第二天的日中，有人在离西门十五里的万流湖里看见一个浮尸，当即传扬开去，终于传到地保的耳朵里了，便叫乡下人捞将上来。那是一个男尸，五十多岁，『身中面白无须』，浑身也没有什么衣裤。或者说这就是陈士成。但邻居懒得去看，也并无尸亲认领，于是经县委员相验之后，便由地保抬埋了。至于死因，那当然是没有问题的，剥取死尸的衣服本来是常有的事，够不上疑心到谋害去；而且仵作也证明是生前的落水，因为他确凿曾在水底里挣命，所以十个指甲里都满嵌着河底泥。
segs.en: At noon the following day, someone spotted a floating corpse in Wanliu Lake, fifteen li from the West Gate. The news spread instantly, eventually reaching the ears of the local bailiff, who ordered some villagers to drag it ashore. It was the body of a man, over fifty years old, “fair-complexioned and beardless,” and entirely devoid of clothing. People surmised it was Chen Shicheng. But the neighbors couldn’t be bothered to go and look, and since no relatives came to claim the body, the county official conducted an inspection, after which the bailiff had it buried. As for the cause of death, that was naturally beyond question. Stripping a corpse of its clothes was a common enough occurrence; it hardly warranted suspicion of foul play. Besides, the coroner confirmed it was a case of drowning while alive, for the man had clearly struggled in the depths—his ten fingernails were packed tight with riverbed silt.

segs.zh: （一九二二年六月。）
