segs.zh: 住在我们后进院子里的三太太，在夏间买了一对白兔，是给伊的孩子们看的。
segs.en: The Third Mistress, who lived in our rear courtyard, bought a pair of white rabbits during the summer to amuse her children.

segs.zh: 这一对白兔，似乎离娘并不久，虽然是异类，也可以看出他们的天真烂熳来。但也竖直了小小的通红的长耳朵，动著鼻子，眼睛里颇现些惊疑的神色，大约究竟觉得人地生疏，没有在老家时候的安心了。这种东西，倘到庙会日期自己出去买，每个至多不过两吊钱，而三太太却花了一元，因为是叫小使上店买来的。
segs.en: This pair of white rabbits seemed to have been separated from their mother only recently; though they were of a different species, one could still discern a certain innocence and artlessness in them. Yet, they held their small, translucent, crimson ears bolt upright and twitched their noses, their eyes betraying a look of considerable alarm and bewilderment—evidently, they felt like strangers in a strange land and lacked the sense of security they had known back home. Had one gone to the temple fair to buy such creatures in person, they would have cost no more than two strings of cash each at most, but the Third Mistress had paid a full dollar, simply because she had sent a servant to the shop to fetch them.

segs.zh: 孩子们自然大得意了，嚷著围住了看；大人也都围著看；还有一匹小狗名叫S的也跑来，闯过去一嗅，打了一个喷嚏，退了几步。三太太吆喝道，“S，听著，不准你咬他！”于是在他头上打了一拳，S便退开了，从此并不咬。
segs.en: The children were naturally delighted, clamoring as they crowded around to watch; the adults also gathered round to see. Even a small dog named S came running over, pushed its way through to sniff, let out a sneeze, and retreated a few paces. The Third Mistress shouted, "S, listen to me, you are not allowed to bite him!" With that, she gave it a punch on the head, and S backed away, never to bite him again.

segs.zh: 这一对兔总是关在后窗后面的小院子里的时候多，听说是因为太喜欢撕壁纸，也常常啃木器脚。这小院子里有一株野桑树，桑子落地，他们最爱吃，便连喂他们的菠菜也不吃了。乌鸦喜鹊想要下来时，他们便躬著身子用后脚在地上使劲的一弹，砉的一声直跳上来，像飞起了一团雪，鸦鹊吓得赶紧走，这样的几回，再也不敢近来了。三太太说，鸦鹊到不打紧，至多也不过抢吃一点食料，可恶的是一匹大黑猫，常在矮墙上恶狠狠的看，这却要防的，幸而S和猫是对头，或者还不至于有什么罢。
segs.en: Most of the time, this pair of rabbits was kept in the small courtyard behind the back window; I heard it was because they were too fond of tearing the wallpaper and frequently gnawed on the legs of the furniture as well. There was a wild mulberry tree in this small courtyard, and when the mulberries fell to the ground, the rabbits loved them best, forsaking even the spinach fed to them. Whenever crows or magpies tried to swoop down, the rabbits would arch their backs, give a vigorous shove with their hind legs, and with a crisp snap, spring straight up like a tuft of snow taking flight. The birds, startled, would hurry away, and after a few such encounters, they dared not come near again. The Third Mistress said that the birds were of no great consequence—at most, they would only snatch a bit of feed—but the truly detestable thing was a large black cat that often glared viciously from atop the low wall. That, she said, was what they had to guard against, though fortunately S was the cat’s sworn enemy, so perhaps, in the end, there would be nothing to worry about.

segs.zh: 孩子们时时捉他们来玩耍；他们很和气，竖起耳朵，动著鼻子，驯良的站在小手的圈子里，但一有空，却也就溜开去了。他们夜里的卧榻是一个小木箱，里面铺些稻草，就在后窗的房檐下。
segs.en: The children often caught them to play with. They were very docile, pricking up their ears and twitching their noses, standing tamely within the circle of small hands, but at the first opportunity, they would slip away. Their bed at night was a small wooden crate lined with straw, kept under the eaves of the back window.

segs.zh: 这样的几个月之后，他们忽而自己掘土了，掘得非常快，前脚一抓，后脚一踢，不到半天，已经掘成一个深洞。大家都奇怪，后来仔细看时，原来一个的肚子比别一个的大得多了。他们第二天便将干草和树叶衔进洞里去，忙了大半天。
segs.en: A few months later, they suddenly took to digging the earth themselves. They dug with remarkable speed, scratching with their front paws and kicking with their hind ones; in less than half a day, they had already excavated a deep burrow. Everyone was astonished, but upon closer inspection, it became clear that one of them had a much larger belly than the others. The next day, they spent the better part of the morning carrying dried grass and leaves into the hole.

segs.zh: 大家都高兴，说又有小兔可看了；三太太便对孩子们下了戒严令，从此不许再去捉。我的母亲也很喜欢他们家族的繁荣，还说待生下来的离了乳，也要去讨两匹来养在自己的窗外面。
segs.en: Everyone was delighted, saying there would be little rabbits to look at again; the Third Mistress promptly issued a martial-law decree to the children, forbidding them from ever going to catch them again. My mother was also pleased by the prosperity of their family, and even remarked that once the newborns were weaned, she would ask for a pair to keep outside her own window.

segs.zh: 他们从此便住在自造的洞府里，有时也出来吃些食，后来不见了，可不知道他们是预先运粮存在里面呢还是竟不吃。过了十多天，三太太对我说，那两匹又出来了，大约小兔是生下来又都死掉了，因为雌的一匹的奶非常多，却并不见有进去哺养孩子的形迹。伊言语之间颇气愤，然而也没有法。
segs.en: From then on, they lived in the burrow they had dug themselves, emerging only occasionally to forage for food. Later, they disappeared entirely, though whether they had stockpiled enough grain inside or simply stopped eating altogether, I could not say. More than ten days passed before the Third Mistress told me that the pair had appeared again. The little rabbits must have been born and then died, for the female was heavy with milk, yet there was no sign of her ever going back in to nurse them. She spoke with considerable indignation, but there was nothing to be done.

segs.zh: 有一天，太阳很温暖，也没有风，树叶都不动，我忽听得许多人在那里笑，寻声看时，却见许多人都靠著三太太的后窗看：原来有一个小兔，在院子里跳跃了。这比他的父母买来的时候还小得远，但也已经能用后脚一弹地，迸跳起来了。孩子们争著告诉我说，还看见一个小兔到洞口来探一探头，但是即刻便缩回去了，那该是他的弟弟罢。
segs.en: One day, the sun was warm and there was no wind; the leaves on the trees were perfectly still. Suddenly, I heard many people laughing. Following the sound, I saw a crowd gathered by the Third Mistress’s back window: it turned out a little rabbit was hopping about in the courtyard. It was far smaller than when its parents were first bought, yet it could already push off with its hind legs and spring into the air. The children vied to tell me that they had also seen another little rabbit poke its head out from the burrow, only to pull it back in again immediately—that must have been its brother.

segs.zh: 那小的也捡些草叶吃，然而大的似乎不许他，往往夹口的抢去了，而自己并不吃。孩子们笑得响，那小的终于吃惊了，便跳著钻进洞里去；大的也跟到洞门口，用前脚推著他的孩子的脊梁，推进之后，又爬开泥土来封了洞。
segs.en: The smaller one also picked at some blades of grass to eat, but the larger one seemed not to allow it, often snatching them away from its mouth, though it did not eat them itself. The children laughed loudly, which finally startled the little one, and it hopped into the hole; the larger one followed to the entrance, pushed its offspring’s back with its forepaws, and once it was inside, scraped up earth to seal the hole.

segs.zh: 从此小院子里更热闹，窗口也时时有人窥探了。
segs.en: From then on, the little courtyard became even livelier, and people were constantly peeking in through the windows.

segs.zh: 然而竟又全不见了那小的和大的。这时是连日的阴天，三太太又虑到遭了那大黑猫的毒手的事去。我说不然，那是天气冷，当然都躲著，太阳一出，一定出来的。
segs.en: Yet, in the end, neither the small one nor the big one was to be seen anywhere. It had been overcast for days, and the Third Mistress was again fretting that they had fallen victim to that big black cat. I told her it was not so; it was only the cold weather that had driven them into hiding, and they would surely come out as soon as the sun appeared.

segs.zh: 太阳出来了，他们却都不见。于是大家就忘却了。
segs.en: The sun had come out, yet they were nowhere to be seen. And so, everyone simply forgot about them.

segs.zh: 惟有三太太是常在那里喂他们菠菜的，所以常想到。伊有一回走进窗后的小院子去，忽然在墙角发见了一个别的洞，再看旧洞口，却依稀的还见有许多爪痕。这爪痕倘说是大兔的，爪该不会有这样大，伊又疑心到那常在墙上的大黑猫去了，伊于是也就不能不定下发掘的决心了。伊终于出来取了锄子，一路掘下去，虽然疑心，却也希望著意外的见了小白兔的，但是待到底，却只见一堆烂草夹些兔毛，怕还是临蓐时候所铺的罢，此外是冷清清的，全没有什么雪白的小兔的踪迹，以及他那只一探头未出洞外的弟弟了。
segs.en: Only the Third Mistress, who often fed them spinach, kept them in her thoughts. One day, she walked into the small courtyard behind the window and suddenly discovered another hole in the corner of the wall. Looking back at the old entrance, she could still faintly make out a number of claw marks. If these were from the big rabbit, the claws surely shouldn't be that large; she began to suspect the big black cat that was always prowling the wall, and so she felt she had no choice but to resolve to dig it up. She finally went out to get a hoe and dug all the way down. Though full of doubt, she still hoped to catch a glimpse of the little white rabbit unexpectedly. But when she reached the bottom, she found nothing but a pile of rotten grass mixed with rabbit fur—likely bedding from when the litter was born—and beyond that, it was desolate. There was absolutely no trace of the snow-white little rabbit, nor of its brother, who had once poked his head out but never emerged from the hole.

segs.zh: 气愤和失望和凄凉，使伊不能不再掘那墙角上的新洞了。一动手，那大的两匹便先窜出洞外面。伊以为他们搬了家了，很高兴，然而仍然掘，待见底，那里面也铺著草叶和兔毛，而上面却睡著七个很小的兔，遍身肉红色，细看时，眼睛全都没有开。
segs.en: Anger, disappointment, and a sense of desolation compelled her to dig yet another new hole in the corner of the wall. No sooner had she begun than the two large ones darted out. Thinking they had moved house, she was quite pleased, yet she went on digging. When she reached the bottom, she found it lined with blades of grass and rabbit fur, and resting upon it were seven tiny rabbits, their bodies a raw, fleshy red; peering closely, she saw that none of their eyes had yet opened.

segs.zh: 一切都明白了，三太太先前的预料果不错。伊为预防危险起见，便将七个小的都装在木箱中，搬进自己的房里，又将大的也捺进箱里面，勒令伊去哺乳。
segs.en: Everything was clear now; the Third Mistress’s earlier premonition had been correct indeed. As a precautionary measure against danger, she had packed all seven of the little ones into wooden chests and moved them into her own room, then shoved the eldest in as well, ordering her to suckle them.

segs.zh: 三太太从此不但深恨黑猫，而且颇不以大兔为然了。据说当初那两个被害之先，死掉的该还有，因为他们生一回，决不至于只两个，但为了哺乳不匀，不能争食的就先死了。这大概也不错的，现在七个之中，就有两个很瘦弱。所以三太太一有闲空，便捉住母兔，将小兔一个一个轮流的摆在肚子上来喝奶，不准有多少。
segs.en: From then on, the Third Mistress not only harbored a deep hatred for the black cat, but also grew quite dissatisfied with the mother rabbit. It was said that before those two were killed, others must have already perished; for a litter was never just two, but because of uneven nursing, the ones unable to compete for food had died off first. This was likely true, as even now, two of the seven were notably scrawny. Consequently, whenever the Third Mistress had a spare moment, she would seize the mother rabbit and, one by one, place the little ones on her belly in turns to nurse, ensuring none received more than their share.

segs.zh: 母亲对我说，那样麻烦的养兔法，伊历来连听也未曾听到过，恐怕是可以收入《无双谱》的。
segs.en: Mother remarked that such a troublesome way of raising rabbits was something she had never even heard of in her life; it was surely rare enough, she said, to be included in the Gallery of Peerless Figures.

segs.zh: 白兔的家族更繁荣；大家也又都高兴了。
segs.en: The family of white rabbits grew even more prosperous; everyone was happy once again.

segs.zh: 但自此之后，我总觉得凄凉。夜半在灯下坐著想，那两条小性命，竟是人不知鬼不觉的早在不知什么时候丧失了，生物史上不著一些痕迹，并S也不叫一声。我于是记起旧事来，先前我住在会馆里，清早起身，只见大槐树下一片散乱的鸽子毛，这明明是膏于鹰吻的了，上午长班来一打扫，便什么都不见，谁知道曾有一个生命断送在这里呢？我又曾路过西四牌楼，看见一匹小狗被马车轧得快死，待回来时，什么也不见了，搬掉了罢，过往行人憧憧的走著，谁知道曾有一个生命断送在这里呢？夏夜，窗外面，常听到苍蝇的悠长的吱吱的叫声，这一定是给蝇虎咬住了，然而我向来无所容心于其间，而别人并且不听到……
segs.en: But ever since then, a sense of desolation has haunted me. Sitting beneath the lamp in the dead of night, I reflect on those two little lives, lost so quietly, so unbeknownst to anyone, without a trace left in the annals of biology, not even a cry. This brings back old memories. When I lived at the guild hall, I would rise early in the morning to find a scattering of pigeon feathers beneath the great locust tree—the clear remains of a feast for a hawk. By mid-morning, when the attendant came to sweep, everything was gone. Who would know a life had been snuffed out there? I once passed by Xisi Pailou and saw a puppy crushed by a carriage, left there dying; when I returned, it was gone—removed, I suppose. The passersby hurried to and fro, and who would know a life had been snuffed out there? On summer nights, outside my window, I often hear the long, drawn-out buzzing of a fly—it must have been seized by a jumping spider. Yet, I have always remained indifferent to such things, and others do not even hear them at all...

segs.zh: 假使造物也可以责备，那么，我以为他实在将生命造得太滥了，毁得太滥了。
segs.en: If the Creator could be held to account, I would say that He has been altogether too profligate in the creation of life, and just as profligate in its destruction.

segs.zh: 嗥的一声，又是两条猫在窗外打起架来。
segs.en: With a howl, two more cats started fighting outside the window.

segs.zh: “迅儿！你又在那里打猫了？”
segs.en: Xun'er! Are you back to beating the cat again?

segs.zh: “不，他们自己咬。他那里会给我打呢。”
segs.en: No, they bite each other. How would he ever hit me?

segs.zh: 我的母亲是素来很不以我的虐待猫为然的，现在大约疑心我要替小兔抱不平，下什么辣手，便起来探问了。而我在全家的口碑上，却的确算一个猫敌。我曾经害过猫，平时也常打猫，尤其是在他们配合的时候。但我之所以打的原因并非因为他们配合，是因为他们嚷，嚷到使我睡不著，我以为配合是不必这样大嚷而特嚷的。
segs.en: My mother had always disapproved of my maltreatment of cats, and now, suspecting I might be seeking vengeance for the little rabbits and was about to do something ruthless, she got up to investigate. In the eyes of my whole family, I was indeed an enemy of cats. I had harmed them in the past, and I often beat them, especially when they were mating. But the reason I beat them was not because they were mating, but because they were caterwauling—screaming so loudly that I couldn't sleep. I felt that mating really didn't require such an incessant, ear-splitting racket.

segs.zh: 况且黑猫害了小兔，我更是“师出有名”的了。我觉得母亲实在太修善，于是不由的就说出模棱的近乎不以为然的答话来。
segs.en: Besides, the black cat had harmed the little rabbit, which gave me all the more reason to take action. Feeling that my mother was altogether too soft-hearted, I couldn't help but offer a vague reply that bordered on disapproval.

segs.zh: 造物太胡闹，我不能不反抗他了，虽然也许是倒是帮他的忙……
segs.en: The Creator is altogether too perverse, and I have no choice but to rebel against Him, though I suspect that in doing so, I may only be playing right into His hands...

segs.zh: 那黑猫是不能久在矮墙上高视阔步的了，我决定的想，于是又不由的一瞥那藏在书箱里的一瓶青酸钾。
segs.en: That black cat could no longer be allowed to strut and stare down from the low wall, I decided, and at that, I could not help but cast a glance at the bottle of potassium cyanide hidden in my bookcase.

segs.zh: 一九二二年十月
segs.en: October 1922

segs.zh: 这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may also be in the public domain in countries and regions where the copyright term is longer for domestic works but shorter for foreign works.

segs.zh: 俄国的盲诗人爱罗先珂君带了他那六弦琴到北京之后不久，便向我诉苦说：
segs.en: Not long after the blind Russian poet Mr. Eroshenko arrived in Beijing with his balalaika, he came to me to pour out his grievances:

segs.zh: “寂寞呀，寂寞呀，在沙漠上似的寂寞呀！”
segs.en: Loneliness, oh, loneliness! It is a loneliness like that of the vast desert!

segs.zh: 这应该是真实的，但在我却未曾感得；我住得久了，“入芝兰之室，久而不闻其香”，只以为很是嚷嚷罢了。然而我之所谓嚷嚷，或者也就是他之所谓寂寞罢。
segs.en: This must be true, yet I have never felt it myself; having lived here so long, like one who "enters a room of sweet orchids and after a time ceases to smell their scent," I merely found it all rather clamorous. Yet what I call clamor may, perhaps, be what he calls loneliness.

segs.zh: 我可是觉得在北京仿佛没有春和秋。老于北京的人说，地气北转了，这里在先是没有这么和暖。只是我总以为没有春和秋；冬末和夏初衔接起来，夏才去，冬又开始了。
segs.en: I, for one, feel as though Beijing has neither spring nor autumn. Old hands in Beijing will tell you that the earth’s climate has shifted north, and that in the past, it was never this mild. Yet I cannot help but feel there is no spring or autumn here; late winter just segues into early summer, and no sooner has summer departed than winter begins again.

segs.zh: 一日就是这冬末夏初的时候，而且是夜间，我偶而得了闲暇，去访问爱罗先珂君。他一向寓在仲密君的家里；这时一家的人都睡了觉了，天下很安静。他独自靠在自己的卧榻上，很高的眉棱在金黄色的长发之间微蹙了，是在想他旧游之地的缅甸，缅甸的夏夜。
segs.en: It was one of those days toward the end of winter and the beginning of summer, and late at night, when I happened to have some leisure and went to visit Mr. Eroshenko. He had been staying at the home of Mr. Zhongmi; by then, the whole household was asleep, and everything under heaven was very quiet. He was leaning alone against his couch, his prominent brow slightly furrowed amidst his long, golden hair, as he thought of Burma, the land of his former travels, and of the summer nights in Burma.

segs.zh: “这样的夜间，”他说，“在缅甸是遍地是音乐。房里，草间，树上，都有昆虫吟叫，各种声音，成为合奏，很神奇。其间时时夹著蛇鸣：'嘶嘶！'可是也与虫声相和协……”他沉思了，似乎想要追想起那时的情景来。
segs.en: "On nights like this," he said, "in Burma, the air is filled with music. In the rooms, among the grass, up in the trees—everywhere there are insects chirping. All these different sounds blend into a symphony; it’s truly magical. Now and then, you hear the hiss of a snake: 'Hiss! Hiss!'—but even that seems to harmonize with the chorus of the insects..." He sank into thought, as if trying to conjure up the scene as it was then.

segs.zh: 我开不得口。这样奇妙的音乐，我在北京确乎未曾听到过，所以即使如何爱国，也辩护不得，因为他虽然目无所见，耳朵是没有聋的。
segs.en: I could not bring myself to speak. Such wondrous music I had certainly never heard in Beijing, so no matter how patriotic I might be, I could not offer a defense; for although he saw nothing, his ears were by no means deaf.

segs.zh: “北京却连蛙鸣也没有……”他又叹息说。
segs.en: "But in Beijing, there isn't even the sound of frogs," he sighed again.

segs.zh: “蛙鸣是有的！”这叹息，却使我勇猛起来了，于是抗议说，“到夏天，大雨之后，你便能听到许多虾蟆叫，那是都在沟里面的，因为北京到处都有沟。”
segs.en: There are frog songs, you know!" This sigh, however, emboldened me, and I protested: "In summer, after a heavy rain, you can hear plenty of toads croaking; they are all in the ditches, because there are ditches everywhere in Beijing.
Oh...

segs.zh: 过了几天，我的话居然证实了，因为爱罗先珂君已经买到了十几个蝌蚪子。他买来便放在他窗外的院子中央的小池里。那池的长有三尺，宽有二尺，是仲密所掘，以种荷花的荷池。从这荷池里，虽然从来没有见过养出半朵荷花来，然而养虾蟆却实在是一个极合式的处所。
segs.en: A few days later, my words were indeed confirmed, for Mr. Eroshenko had managed to buy a dozen or so tadpoles. He brought them home and placed them in a small pond in the middle of the courtyard outside his window. The pond was three feet long and two feet wide, originally dug by Zhongmi for the purpose of growing lotus flowers. Although not a single lotus had ever been seen to bloom in it, it was, in truth, a perfectly suitable place for raising toads.

segs.zh: 蝌蚪成群结队的在水里面游泳；爱罗先珂君也常常踱来访他们。有时候，孩子告诉他说，“爱罗先珂先生，他们生了脚了。”他便高兴的微笑道，“哦！”
segs.en: Tadpoles swam about in shoals; Eroshenko would often stroll over to visit them. Sometimes, the children would tell him, "Mr. Eroshenko, they’ve grown legs." He would smile happily and say, "Oh!"

segs.zh: 然而养成池沼的音乐家却只是爱罗先珂君的一件事。他是向来主张自食其力的，常说女人可以畜牧，男人就应该种田。所以遇到很熟的友人，他便要劝诱他就在院子里种白菜；也屡次对仲密夫人劝告，劝伊养蜂，养鸡，养猪，养牛，养骆驼。后来仲密家果然有了许多小鸡，满院飞跑，啄完了铺地锦的嫩叶，大约也许就是这劝告的结果了。
segs.en: Yet cultivating a musician for the pond was only one of Eroshenko’s eccentricities. He had always been an advocate of self-reliance, often saying that if women could raise livestock, men ought to till the fields. Consequently, whenever he met a close friend, he would try to persuade him to grow cabbage in his courtyard; he also repeatedly urged Mrs. Zhongmi to take up beekeeping, chicken farming, pig rearing, cattle herding, and even camel breeding. Later on, the Zhongmi household did indeed acquire a brood of chicks that scurried all over the yard, pecking away at the tender leaves of the carpet bugle—a result, perhaps, of his persistent exhortations.

segs.zh: 从此卖小鸡的乡下人也时常来，来一回便买几只，因为小鸡是容易积食，发痧，很难得长寿的；而且有一匹还成了爱罗先珂君在北京所作唯一的小说《小鸡的悲剧》里的主人公。有一天的上午，那乡下人竟意外的带了小鸭来了，咻咻的叫著；但是仲密夫人说不要。爱罗先珂君也跑出来，他们就放一个在他两手里，而小鸭便在他两手里咻咻的叫。他以为这也很可爱，于是又不能不买了，一共买了四个，每个八十文。
segs.en: From then on, the countryman who sold chicks came often, and each time I bought a few, for chicks are prone to indigestion and cholera, and rarely live long; one of them even became the protagonist of Mr. Eroshenko’s only story written in Beijing, "The Tragedy of the Chicks." One morning, the countryman unexpectedly brought some ducklings along, which kept up a constant peeping. Madam Zhongmi, however, said she didn't want any. Mr. Eroshenko ran out, and when they placed one in his hands, it went on peeping there, too. Finding this quite endearing, he felt he had no choice but to buy them; he bought four in all, at eighty cash apiece.

segs.zh: 小鸭也诚然是可爱，遍身松花黄，放在地上，便蹒跚的走，互相招呼，总是在一处。大家都说好，明天去买泥鳅来喂他们罢。爱罗先珂君说，“这钱也可以归我出的。”
segs.en: The ducklings were certainly adorable, covered in fluff the color of pine pollen. When placed on the ground, they would waddle about, calling to one another, never straying far apart. Everyone agreed they were wonderful and suggested buying loaches tomorrow to feed them. Eroshenko said, "I can chip in for that, too."

segs.zh: 他于是教书去了；大家也走散。不一会，仲密夫人拿冷饭来喂他们时，在远处已听得泼水的声音，跑到一看，原来那四个小鸭都在荷池里洗澡了，而且还翻觔斗，吃东西呢。等到拦他们上了岸，全池已经是浑水，过了半天，澄清了，只见泥里露出几条细藕来；而且再也寻不出一个已经生了脚的蝌蚪了。
segs.en: He went off to teach then; the others scattered too. A little later, when Mrs. Zhongmi brought cold rice to feed them, she heard the splashing of water from a distance. She hurried over to look, and there were the four ducklings, bathing in the lotus pond, turning somersaults and snapping up food. By the time she managed to shoo them onto the bank, the whole pond was a churn of muddy water. After a long while, when it finally cleared, all that could be seen in the muck were a few slender lotus roots; and not a single tadpole with legs could be found anywhere.

segs.zh: “伊和希珂先，没有了，虾蟆的儿子。”傍晚时候，孩子们一见他回来，最小的一个便赶紧说。
segs.en: “He and Xi-Ke-Xian, they’re gone, the toad’s spawn.” As evening fell, the moment the children saw him return, the youngest one hurried to say.

segs.zh: “唔，虾蟆？”
segs.en: Hmm, a toad?

segs.zh: 仲密夫人也出来了，报告了小鸭吃完蝌蚪的故事。
segs.en: Mrs. Zhongmi came out too, and recounted the story of how the duckling had finished off the tadpoles.
"Sigh, sigh!..." he said.

segs.zh: 待到小鸭褪了黄毛，爱罗先珂君却忽而渴念著他的“俄罗斯母亲”了，便匆匆的向赤塔去。
segs.en: By the time the ducklings had shed their yellow down, Mr. Eroshenko suddenly began to yearn for his "Russian mother," and so he departed in haste for Chita.

segs.zh: 待到四处蛙鸣的时候，小鸭也已经长成，两个白的，两个花的，而且不复咻咻的叫，都是“鸭鸭”的叫了。荷花池也早已容不下他们盘桓了，幸而仲密的住家的地势是很低的，夏雨一降，院子里满积了水，他们便欣欣然，游水，钻水，拍翅子，“鸭鸭”的叫。
segs.en: By the time the frogs began their chorus everywhere, the ducklings had grown up. Two were white, two were mottled, and they no longer let out those little peeps, but had started to quack instead. The lotus pond could no longer contain their wanderings, but fortunately, Zhongmi’s house sat on low-lying ground; whenever the summer rains fell, the courtyard would fill with water, and they would be filled with delight—swimming, diving, flapping their wings, and calling out in their duck-talk.

segs.zh: 现在又从夏末交了冬初，而爱罗先珂君还是绝无消息，不知道究竟在那里了。
segs.en: Now we have passed from late summer into early winter, and still there is no news of Mr. Eroshenko; I have no idea where he might be.

segs.zh: 只有四个鸭，却还在沙漠上“鸭鸭”的叫。
segs.en: There were only four ducks, yet they still kept calling out "quack, quack" across the desert.

segs.zh: 一九二二年十月
segs.en: October 1922

segs.zh: 这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may also be in the public domain in countries and regions where the copyright term is longer for domestic works but shorter for foreign ones, due to the application of the rule of the shorter term.

chapters.label: 十四

segs.zh: 我在倒数上去的二十年中，只看过两回中国戏，前十年是绝不看，因为没有看戏的意思和机会，那两回全在后十年，然而都没有看出什么来就走了。
segs.en: In the twenty years I look back upon, I have seen Chinese opera only twice. During the first decade, I saw none at all, having neither the inclination nor the opportunity; both times were in the latter decade, yet I left each performance without having made head or tail of what I had seen.

segs.zh: 第一回是民国元年我初到北京的时候，当时一个朋友对我说，北京戏最好，你不去见见世面么？我想，看戏是有味的，而况在北京呢。于是都兴致勃勃的跑到什么园，戏文已经开场了，在外面也早听到咚咚地响。我们挨进门，几个红的绿的在我的眼前一闪烁，便又看见戏台下满是许多头，再定神四面看，却见中间也还有几个空座，挤过去要坐时，又有人对我发议论，我因为耳朵已经喤喤的响着了，用了心，才听到他是说“有人，不行！”
segs.en: The first time was in the first year of the Republic, when I had just arrived in Beijing. A friend said to me, "Beijing has the best theater; shouldn't you go and broaden your horizons?" I thought to myself, theater is an interesting pastime, and all the more so in Beijing. And so, full of high spirits, we rushed off to some garden or other. The performance had already begun; even from outside, we could hear the constant booming of drums. We squeezed through the door, and as a blur of red and green costumes flashed before my eyes, I saw that the area below the stage was packed with heads. Focusing my gaze and looking around, I spotted a few empty seats in the middle. Just as I was pushing my way over to sit down, someone began to lecture me. My ears were already ringing with the din, and it took a concentrated effort to hear him say, "Taken. You can't sit here!"

segs.zh: 我们退到后面，一个辫子很光的却来领我们到了侧面，指出一个地位来。这所谓地位者，原来是一条长凳，然而他那坐板比我的上腿要狭到四分之三，他的脚比我的下腿要长过三分之二。我先是没有爬上去的勇气，接着便联想到私刑拷打的刑具，不由的毛骨悚一作“耸”然的走出了。
segs.en: We retreated to the back, but a man with a very shiny queue came to lead us to the side, pointing out a place for us. This so-called place turned out to be a long bench; yet its seat was three-quarters narrower than my thighs, and its legs were two-thirds longer than my shins. At first, I lacked the courage to scramble up, and then, immediately associating it with an instrument of torture, I could not help but walk out, my hair standing on end.

segs.zh: 走了许多路，忽听得我的朋友的声音道，“究竟怎的？”我回过脸去，原来他也被我带出来了。他很诧异的说，“怎么总是走，不答应？”我说，“朋友，对不起，我耳朵只在咚咚喤喤的响，并没有听到你的话。”
segs.en: I had walked quite a distance when suddenly I heard my friend’s voice: "What on earth is the matter?" I turned around to find that he, too, had been led out by me. He asked in astonishment, "Why do you keep walking without answering?" I replied, "My friend, I apologize. My ears have been ringing with such a thumping din that I didn't hear a word you said."

segs.zh: 后来我每一想到，便很以为奇怪，似乎这戏太不好，——否则便是我近来在戏台下不适于生存了。
segs.en: Whenever I think of it now, I find it quite strange; it seems that the play was not very good—or perhaps it is simply that, of late, I have become ill-suited for survival beneath the theater stage.

segs.zh: 第二回忘记了那一年，总之是募集湖北水灾捐而谭叫天还没有死。捐法是两元钱买一张戏票，可以到第一舞台去看戏，扮演的多是名角，其一就是小叫天。我买了一张票，本是对于劝募人聊以塞责的，然而似乎又有好事家乘机对我说了些叫天不可不看的大法要了。我于是忘了前几年的咚咚喤喤之灾，竟到第一舞台去了，但大约一半也因为重价购来的宝票，总得使用了才舒服。我打听得叫天出台是迟的，而第一舞台却是新式构造，用不着争座位，便放了心，延宕到九点钟才出去，谁料照例，人都满了，连立足也难，我只得挤在远处的人丛中看一个老旦在台上唱。那老旦嘴边插着两个点火的纸捻子，旁边有一个鬼卒，我费尽思量，才疑心他或者是目连的母亲，因为后来又出来了一个和尚。然而我又不知道那名角是谁，就去问挤小在我的左边的一位胖绅士。他很看不起似的斜瞥了我一眼，说道，“龚云甫！”我深愧浅陋而且粗疏，脸上一热，同时脑里也制出了决不再问的定章，于是看小旦唱，看花旦唱，看老生唱，看不知什么角色唱，看一大班人乱打，看两三个人互打，从九点多到十点，从十点到十一点，从十一点到十一点半，从十一点半到十二点，——然而叫天竟还没有来。
segs.en: I forget which year it was, but it was certainly when funds were being raised for the Hubei flood relief, and Tan Jiaotian was still alive. The method of donation was to pay two yuan for a theater ticket, which allowed one to see a performance at the First Theater. Most of the actors were famous, among them Little Jiaotian. I bought a ticket, initially just to satisfy the solicitor, but some busybody seized the opportunity to lecture me on the vital importance of seeing Jiaotian perform. I promptly forgot the cacophonous ordeals of previous years and actually went to the First Theater—though, in truth, I suppose half the reason was that having paid a high price for the precious ticket, I wouldn't feel comfortable unless I used it. I had inquired and learned that Jiaotian would be on late, and since the First Theater was of modern construction where one didn't need to scramble for seats, I felt at ease and delayed my departure until nine o'clock. Unexpectedly, as is usually the case, the place was packed; it was difficult even to find a place to stand. I had no choice but to squeeze into the crowd far back and watch an old woman on stage. She had two lighted paper tapers stuck to the corners of her mouth, and beside her stood a ghostly attendant. After racking my brains, I suspected she might be the mother of Mulian, as a monk appeared shortly after. Not knowing who the famous actor was, I asked a fat gentleman squeezed in on my left. He cast a sidelong glance at me, looking thoroughly contemptuous, and said, "Gong Yunfu!" I felt deeply ashamed of my shallowness and lack of refinement; my face burned, and I instantly made a rule for myself never to ask another question. So I watched the young maid sing, watched the coquette sing, watched the old man sing, watched I-don't-know-what-kind-of-character sing, watched a great crowd of people brawl, watched two or three people spar—from after nine to ten, from ten to eleven, from eleven to eleven-thirty, from eleven-thirty to twelve—yet Jiaotian still had not appeared.

segs.zh: 我向来没有这样忍耐的等候过什么事物，而况这身边的胖绅士的吁吁的喘气，这台上的咚咚喤喤的敲打，红红绿绿的晃荡，加之以十二点，忽而使我省悟到在这里不适于生存了。我同时便机械的拧转身子，用力往外只一挤，觉得背后便已满满的，大约那弹性的胖绅士早在我的空处胖开了他的右半身了。我后无回路，自然挤而又挤，终于出了大门。街上除了专等看客的车辆之外，几乎没有什么行人了，大门口却还有十几个人昂着头看戏目，别有一堆人站着并不看什么，我想：他们大概是看散戏之后出来的女人们的，而叫天却还没有来……
segs.en: I had never before waited for anything with such patience, and what with the heavy, wheezing breath of the fat gentleman beside me, the incessant clanging and banging on the stage, the dizzying shimmer of reds and greens, and the sudden realization that it was already midnight, I came to the sharp awareness that this was no place for me to survive. Mechanically, I twisted my body and shoved my way out with all my might; I felt the space behind me fill up instantly, as if that resilient fat gentleman had long since expanded his right side into the void I had left behind. With no way back, I naturally squeezed and squeezed again until at last I emerged through the main gate. On the street, save for the carriages waiting for the theatergoers, there was hardly a soul to be seen. Yet at the entrance, a dozen or so people stood with heads craned, staring at the playbill, while another group stood idly by, watching nothing at all. I thought to myself: they are likely waiting for the women to come out after the show, though the night-watchman has yet to make his rounds...

segs.zh: 然而夜气很清爽，真所谓“沁人心脾”，我在北京遇着这样的好空气，仿佛这是第一遭了。
segs.en: Yet the night air was crisp and refreshing—truly what one might call "invigorating to the heart and spirit." It felt as if this were the very first time I had encountered such fine air since arriving in Beijing.

segs.zh: 这一夜，就是我对于中国戏告了别的一夜，此后再没有想到他，即使一作“即便”偶而经过戏园，我们也漠不相关，精神上早已一在天之南一在地之北了。
segs.en: That night was the night I bade farewell to Chinese opera. From then on, I never thought of it again; even when I happened to pass a theater, we remained as indifferent to each other as strangers, our spirits already worlds apart, one at the southern end of the sky and the other at the northern.

segs.zh: 但是前几天，我忽在无意之中看到一本日本文的书，可惜忘记了书名和著者，总之是关于中国戏的。其中有一篇，大意仿佛说，中国戏是大敲，大叫，大跳，使看客头昏脑眩，很不适于剧场，但若在野外散漫的所在，远远的看起来，也自有他的风致。我当时觉着一作“觉得”这正是说了在我意中而未曾想到的话，因为我确记得在野外看过很好的好戏，到北京以后的连进两回戏园去，也许还是受了那时的影响哩。可惜我不知道怎么一来，竟将书名忘却了。
segs.en: But a few days ago, I happened upon a Japanese book—though, regrettably, I have forgotten both its title and author—which dealt with Chinese theater. One passage, as I recall, remarked that Chinese opera consists of loud clanging, shrill shouting, and frantic leaping, leaving the audience dizzy and bewildered, and that it is quite unsuited for the theater. Yet, if watched from a distance in some sprawling, open field, it possesses a certain charm of its own. At the time, I felt this was exactly what I had been thinking but had never been able to articulate; I distinctly remember having seen excellent plays performed in the open air, and perhaps it was that early influence that led me to visit the playhouses twice after arriving in Beijing. It is a pity that, somehow or other, I have managed to forget the title of the book.

segs.zh: 至于我看那好戏的时候，却实在已经是“远哉遥遥”的了，其时恐怕我还不过十一二岁。我们鲁镇的习惯，本来是凡有出嫁的女儿，倘自己还未当家，夏间便大抵回到母家去消夏。那时我的祖母虽然还康健，但母亲也已分担了些家务，所以夏期便不能多日的归省了，只得在扫墓完毕之后，抽空去住几天，这时我便每年跟了我的母亲住在外祖母的家里。那地方叫平桥村，是一个离海边不远，极偏僻的，临河的小村庄；住户不满三十家，都种田，打鱼，只有一家很小的杂货店。但在我是乐土：因为我在这里不但得到优待，又可以免念“秩秩斯干幽幽南山”了。
segs.en: As for when I actually watched those plays, that was truly a "long, long time ago"—at the time, I could not have been more than eleven or twelve. It was the custom in our Lu Town that any daughter who had married but was not yet mistress of her own household would generally return to her mother's home to spend the summer. My grandmother was still in good health back then, but my mother had already begun to share in the management of our household, so she could no longer spend long periods visiting her parents. She could only steal a few days to stay after the tomb-sweeping was finished, and each year, I would accompany her to stay at my grandmother’s house. That place was called Pingqiao Village, a remote little hamlet by a river, not far from the sea. There were fewer than thirty households, all of whom farmed or fished, and there was only one tiny general store. Yet for me, it was paradise: not only was I treated as an honored guest there, but I was also exempt from having to recite "The flowing stream, the secluded southern hill."

segs.zh: 和我一同玩的是许多小朋友，因为有了远客，他们也都从父母那里得了减少工作的许可，伴我来游戏。在小村里，一家的客，几乎也就是公共的。我们年纪都相仿，但论起行辈来，却至少是叔子，有几个还是太公，因为他们合村都同姓，是本家。然而我们是朋友，即使偶而吵闹起来，打了太公，一村的老老小小，也决没有一个会想出“犯上”这两个字来，而他们也百分之九十九不识字。
segs.en: I played with many of the local children. Because of the arrival of a guest from afar, they had all been granted permission by their parents to shirk their chores and keep me company. In our small village, a guest of one family was, in a sense, a guest of the entire community. We were all of a similar age, but in terms of seniority, they were at least my uncles, and some were even my grand-uncles, for everyone in the village shared the same surname and belonged to the same clan. Yet, we were friends; even if we happened to squabble and I struck a grand-uncle, not a single soul among the village elders or children would ever think to utter the phrase "insubordination"—and, in any case, ninety-nine percent of them were illiterate.

segs.zh: 我们每天的事情大概是掘蚯蚓，掘来穿在铜丝做的小钩上，伏在河沿上去钓虾。虾是水世界里的呆子，决不惮用了自己的两个钳捧着钩尖送到嘴里去的，所以不半天便可以钓到一大碗。这虾照例是归我吃的。其次便是一同去放牛，但或者因为高等动物了的缘故罢，黄牛水牛都欺生，敢于欺侮我，因此我也总不敢走近身，只好远远地跟着，站着。这时候，小朋友们便不再原谅我会读“秩秩斯干”，却全都嘲笑起来了。
segs.en: Our daily routine mostly consisted of digging for earthworms, which we would thread onto small hooks made of copper wire, then lie in wait along the riverbank to fish for shrimp. Shrimp are the simpletons of the aquatic world, never hesitating to clasp the point of the hook in their two pincers and deliver it straight to their own mouths, so that within half a day one could easily catch a large bowlful. As a rule, these shrimp were mine to eat. Our next task was to go out together to graze the oxen; but perhaps because I was considered a "higher animal," the yellow oxen and water buffalo were all wary of a stranger and dared to bully me, so I never dared to get close, having to follow and stand at a distance. At such times, my little playmates would no longer forgive me for being able to recite "Zhi zhi si gan," but would instead all burst out laughing at me.

segs.zh: 至于我在那里所第一盼望的，却在到赵庄去看戏。赵庄是离平桥村五里的较大的村庄；平桥村太小，自己演不起戏，每年总付给赵庄多少钱，算作合做的。当时我并不想到他们为什么年年要演戏。现在想，那或者是春赛，是社戏了。
segs.en: As for what I looked forward to most of all while I was there, it was going to Zhaozhuang to see the plays. Zhaozhuang was a larger village, five miles away from Pingqiao Village. Pingqiao was too small to afford its own theatrical performances, so every year it would pay a certain sum to Zhaozhuang to be included in the venture. At the time, I never wondered why they insisted on putting on plays every year. Looking back now, I suppose it must have been the spring festival, the village opera.

segs.zh: 就在我十一二岁时候的这一年，这日期也看看等到了。不料这一年真可惜，在早上就叫不到船。平桥村只有一只早出晚归的航船是大船，决没有留用的道理。其馀的都是小船，不合用；央人到邻村去问，也没有，早都给别人定下了。外祖母很气恼，怪家里的人不早定，絮叨起来。母亲便宽慰伊，说我们鲁镇的戏比小村里的好得多，一年看几回，今天就算了。只有我急得要哭，母亲却竭力的嘱咐我，说万不能装模装样，怕又招外祖母生气，又不准和别人一同去，说是怕外祖母要担心。
segs.en: That year, when I was eleven or twelve, the day finally drew near. Yet, as luck would have it, we could not find a boat that morning. In Pingqiao Village, there was only one large ferry boat that went out early and returned late, and there was no way it could be spared for us. All the other craft were tiny, quite unsuitable. When we begged others to check in the neighboring villages, there were none to be had; they had all been booked long before. My grandmother was deeply vexed, blaming the family for not having made arrangements sooner, and she began to grumble incessantly. My mother tried to soothe her, saying that the operas in our Lu Town were far better than those in small villages, and since we saw them several times a year, we might as well let this one go. I alone was so anxious I was on the verge of tears, yet my mother exhorted me with all her might not to put on a show of temper, fearing it would only anger my grandmother further. At the same time, she forbade me from going with anyone else, saying she was afraid it would cause my grandmother unnecessary worry.

segs.zh: 总之，是完了。到下午，我的朋友都去了，戏已经开场了，我似乎听到锣鼓的声音，而且知道他们在戏台下买豆浆喝。
segs.en: In short, it was all over. By the afternoon, my friends had all left and the play had already begun; I could almost hear the sound of the gongs and drums, and I knew they were down by the stage, buying soy milk to drink.

segs.zh: 这一天我不钓虾，东西也少吃。母亲很为难，没有法子想。到晚饭时候，外祖母也终于觉察了，并且说我应当不高兴，他们太怠慢，是待客的礼数里从来所没有的。吃饭之后，看过戏的少年们也都聚拢来了，高高兴兴的来讲戏。只有我不开口；他们都叹息而且表同情。忽然间，一个最聪明的双喜大悟似的提议了，他说，“大船？八叔的航船不是回来了么？”十几个别的少年也大悟，立刻撺掇起来，说可以坐了这航船和我一同去。我高兴了。然而外祖母又怕都是孩子们，不可靠；母亲又说是若叫大人一同去，他们白天全有工作，要他熬夜，是不合情理的。在这迟疑之中，双喜可又看出底细来了，便又大声的说道，“我写包票！船又大；迅哥儿向来不乱跑；我们又都是识水性的！”
segs.en: That day I did not fish for shrimp, and I ate very little. My mother was at a loss, unable to think of a way to help. By suppertime, my grandmother finally noticed, saying that I must be upset because they had been too negligent—a breach of hospitality she had never seen before. After the meal, the boys who had been to the play gathered around, eagerly talking about it. I alone kept silent; they all sighed and expressed their sympathy. Suddenly, the brightest of them, Shuangxi, seemed to have an epiphany and suggested, "The big boat? Isn't Eighth Uncle's passenger boat back?" A dozen other boys realized it too, and immediately began egging me on, saying they could take that boat and go with me. I was delighted. But my grandmother was afraid that since we were all children, it wouldn't be safe; and my mother argued that if we asked the adults to go along, they all had work to do during the day, and it would be unreasonable to make them stay up all night. Amidst this hesitation, Shuangxi again saw through to the heart of the matter and spoke up loudly, "I'll guarantee it! The boat is big; Brother Xun never runs off recklessly; and we are all good swimmers!"

segs.zh: 诚然！这十多个少年，委实没有一个不会凫水的，而且两三个还是弄潮的好手。
segs.en: Indeed! Of those dozen or so lads, there was truly not one who could not swim, and two or three of them were even expert tide-riders.

segs.zh: 外祖母和母亲也相信，便不再驳回，都微笑了。我们立刻一哄的出了门。
segs.en: My grandmother and mother believed it too, so they raised no further objections, both of them smiling. We swarmed out of the door at once.

segs.zh: 我的很重的心忽而轻松了，身体也似乎舒展到说不出的大。一出门，便望见月下的平桥内泊着一只白篷的航船，大家跳下船，双喜拔前篙，阿发拔后篙，年幼的都陪我坐在舱中，较大的聚在船尾。母亲送出来吩咐“要小心”的时候，我们已经点开船，在桥石上一磕，退后几尺，即又上前出了桥。于是架起两枝橹，一枝两人，一里一换，有说笑的，有嚷的，夹着潺潺的船头激水的声音，在左右都是碧绿的豆麦田地的河流中，飞一般径向赵庄前进了。
segs.en: My heavy heart suddenly lightened, and my body felt as if it had expanded to an indescribable size. As soon as I stepped out, I saw a boat with a white awning moored under Pingqiao Bridge in the moonlight. Everyone jumped aboard; Shuangxi pushed off the front pole, Afa the rear, while the younger ones sat with me in the cabin and the older boys gathered at the stern. By the time my mother came out to call after us to "be careful," we had already pushed off. With a bump against the bridge pier, the boat backed a few feet, then surged forward, clearing the bridge. Two oars were set, each manned by two boys, who took turns every mile. Amidst the laughter, the shouting, and the rhythmic splashing of water against the bow, we flew through the river, flanked on both sides by emerald fields of beans and wheat, heading straight for Zhaozhuang.

segs.zh: 两岸的豆麦和河底的水草所发散出来的清香，夹杂在水气中扑面的吹来；月色便朦胧一作“蒙胧”在这水气里。淡黑的起伏的连山，仿佛是踊跃的铁的兽脊似的，都远远地向船尾跑去了，但我却还以为船慢。他们换了四回手，渐望见依稀的赵庄，而且似乎听到歌吹了，还有几点火，料想便是戏台，但或者也许是渔火。
segs.en: The fragrance of bean and wheat from the banks and the water-weeds from the riverbed drifted toward us, mingled with the mist that brushed against our faces; the moonlight grew hazy within this vapor. The undulating, dark-black silhouettes of the distant mountains looked like the leaping spines of iron beasts, all racing toward the stern of the boat, yet I still felt we were moving too slowly. Four times the oarsmen took turns, and gradually we caught a dim glimpse of Zhao Village; it seemed I could even hear the sound of singing and pipes, and a few flickering lights—I guessed it must be the opera stage, though perhaps, just perhaps, they were only fishing fires.

segs.zh: 那声音大概是横笛，宛转，悠扬，使我的心也沈静，然而又自失起来，觉得要和他濔散在含着豆麦蕴藻之香的夜气里。
segs.en: The sound was likely from a horizontal flute, winding and melodious, soothing my heart, yet at the same time making me lose myself, as if I were about to dissolve with it into the night air, heavy with the fragrance of beans, wheat, and river weeds.

segs.zh: 那火接近了，果然是渔火；我才记得先前望见的也不是赵庄。那是正对船头的一丛松柏林，我去年也曾经去游玩过，还看见破的石马倒在地下，一个石羊蹲在草里呢。过了那林，船便弯进了叉港，于是赵庄便真在眼前了。
segs.en: The light drew nearer, and it was indeed a fisherman’s lamp; only then did I recall that what I had seen earlier was not Zhao Village after all. It was a cluster of pine and cypress trees facing the bow of the boat, a place I had visited the year before, where I had seen a broken stone horse lying on the ground and a stone sheep crouching in the grass. Once we had passed the woods, the boat veered into a side channel, and there, at last, Zhao Village appeared before my eyes.

segs.zh: 最惹眼的是屹立在庄外临河的空地上的一座戏台，模胡在远处的月夜中，和空间几乎分不出界限，我疑心画上见过的仙境，就在这里出现了。这时船走得更快，不多时，在台上显出人物来，红红绿绿的动，近台的河里一望乌黑的是看戏的人家的船篷。
segs.en: The most striking sight was the stage standing on the open ground by the river outside the village. Blurred in the moonlight of the distance, it seemed to merge almost indistinguishably with the void, and I suspected that the fairylands I had seen in paintings had manifested right here. The boat moved faster now, and before long, figures appeared on the stage, moving in flashes of red and green; in the river near the stage, the black, crowded boat-awnings of the spectators stretched as far as the eye could see.

segs.zh: “近台没有什么空了，我们远远的看罢。”阿发说。
segs.en: "There’s no room left near the stage; let’s watch from further back," Ah Fa said.

segs.zh: 这时船慢了，不久就到，果然近不得台旁，大家只能下了篙，比那正对戏台的神棚还要远。其实我们这白篷的航船，本也不愿意和乌篷的船在一处，而况并没有空地呢……
segs.en: The boat slowed down, and before long we reached our destination. As expected, we could not get close to the stage; everyone had to lower their poles, mooring even further away than the shrine-shed that faced the theatre directly. In truth, our white-awning boat had no desire to mix with the black-awning ones, and besides, there was no room left anyway.

segs.zh: 在停船的匆忙中，看见台上有一个黑的长胡子的背上插着四张旗，捏着长枪，和一群赤膊的人正打仗。双喜说，那就是有名的铁头老生，能连翻八十四个筋斗，他日里亲自数过的。
segs.en: In the hurry of mooring the boat, I saw on the stage a man with a long black beard, four flags stuck into his back, clutching a long spear and battling a group of bare-chested men. Shuangxi said that was the famous Iron-Headed Old Actor, who could turn eighty-four somersaults in a row—he had counted them himself that day.

segs.zh: 我们便都挤在船头上看打仗，但那铁头老生却又并不翻筋斗，只有几个赤膊的人翻，翻了一阵，都进去了，接着走出一个小旦来，咿咿呀呀的唱。双喜说，“晚上看客少，铁头老生也懈了，谁肯显本领给白地看呢？”我相信这话对，因为其时台下已经不很有人，乡下人为了明天的工作，熬不得夜，早都睡觉去了，疏疏朗朗的站着的不过是几十个本村和邻村的闲汉。乌篷船里的那些土财主的家眷固然在，然而他们也不在乎看戏，多半是专到戏台下来吃糕饼水果和瓜子的。所以简直可以算白地。
segs.en: We all crowded to the bow of the boat to watch the fighting on stage, but the "Iron-Headed Elder" did not turn any somersaults; only a few bare-chested men were doing so. After a while, they all went inside, and a young *dan* actress stepped out, singing in a high-pitched, warbling voice. Shuangxi remarked, "The audience is thin tonight, so even the Iron-Headed Elder has slacked off. Who would want to show off their skills to an empty house?" I believed he was right, for there were very few people left in front of the stage. The country folk, needing to work the next day, could not stay up late and had long since gone to bed; the few dozen idlers from our village and the neighboring ones who remained were standing about, scattered and sparse. The families of the local wealthy landowners were indeed still in their black-awning boats, but they did not care much for the opera; for the most part, they had come to the stage simply to eat cakes, fruit, and melon seeds. Thus, it could practically be called an empty house.

segs.zh: 然而我的意思却也并不在乎看翻筋斗。我最愿意看的是一个人蒙了白布，两手在头上捧着一支棒似的蛇头的蛇精，其次是套了黄布衣跳老虎。但是等了许多时都不见，小旦虽然进去了，立刻又出来了一个很老的小生。我有些疲倦了，托桂生买豆浆去。他去了一刻，回来说，“没有。卖豆浆的聋子也回去了。日里倒有，我还喝了两碗呢。现在去舀—瓢水来给你喝罢。”
segs.en: But I did not really care about watching acrobatic somersaults. What I most wanted to see was someone covered in a white cloth, holding a snake-spirit with a rod-like snake’s head above their head with both hands, or perhaps someone in a yellow tiger-skin suit leaping about. But after waiting for a long time, nothing appeared. The young actress had gone in, only for an elderly young-male lead to come out instead. Feeling a bit tired, I asked Guisheng to go buy some soy milk. He was gone for a moment, then returned to say, "There isn't any. The deaf man who sells it has already gone home. He was there during the day—I even had two bowls myself. I’ll go scoop you a dipper of water to drink instead."

segs.zh: 我不喝水，支撑着仍然看，也说不出见了些什么，只觉得戏子的脸都渐渐的有些稀奇了，那五官渐不明显，似乎融成一片的再没有什么高低。年纪小的几个多打呵欠了，大的也各管自己谈话。忽而一个红衫的小丑被绑在台柱子上，给一个花白胡子的用马鞭打起来了，大家才又振作精神的笑着看。在这一夜里，我以为这实在要算是最好的一折。
segs.en: I did not drink any water, but kept watching, propping myself up. I could not say exactly what I was seeing; it only felt as if the actors’ faces were gradually becoming strange, their features blurring until they seemed to melt into a single, flat surface, devoid of any depth. The younger children were yawning, and the older ones were busy talking among themselves. Suddenly, a clown in a red tunic was tied to a stage pillar and lashed with a horsewhip by a man with a grizzled beard, and everyone perked up, laughing as they watched. That night, I thought, this was truly the best scene of all.

segs.zh: 然而老旦终于出台了。老旦本来是我所最怕的东西，尤其是怕他坐下了唱。这时候，看见大家也都很扫兴，才知道他们的意见是和我一致的。那老旦当初还只是踱来踱去的唱，后来竟在中间的一把交椅上坐下了。我很担心；双喜他们却就破口喃喃的骂。我忍耐的等着，许多工夫，只见那老旦将手一抬，我以为就要站起来了，不料他却又慢慢的放下在原地方，仍旧唱。全船里几个人不住的吁气，其馀的也打起呵欠来。双喜终于熬不住了，说道，怕他会唱到天明一作“明天”还不完，还是我们走的好罢。大家立刻都赞成，和开船时候一样踊跃，三四人径奔船尾，拔了篙，点退几丈，回转船头，架起橹，骂着老旦，又向那松柏林前进了。
segs.en: At last, the old woman made her entrance. The old woman was the one thing I feared most, especially when she sat down to sing. Seeing that everyone else was equally disappointed, I realized their opinion matched my own. At first, she had only paced back and forth as she sang, but then she actually sat down in a folding chair in the center of the stage. I was deeply worried, while Shuangxi and the others began to mutter curses under their breaths. I waited patiently for a long time, until finally I saw the old woman raise her hand. I thought she was about to stand up, but to my dismay, she slowly lowered it back to its original position and continued to sing. A few people on the boat kept letting out heavy sighs, and the rest began to yawn. Shuangxi finally lost his patience. "I'm afraid she'll keep singing until daybreak," he said. "We’d better just leave." Everyone agreed immediately, as eager as they had been when the boat first set off. Three or four of them rushed to the stern, pulled up the pole, pushed off a few yards, turned the boat around, and set the sculls in motion. Cursing the old woman, we headed back toward the pine and cypress grove.

segs.zh: 月还没有落，仿佛看戏也并不很久似的，而一离赵庄，月光又显得格外的皎洁。回望戏台在灯火光中，却又如初来未到时候一般，又漂渺得像一座仙山褛阁，满被红霞罩着了。吹到耳边来的又是横笛，很悠扬；我疑心老旦已经进去了，但也不好意思说再回去看。
segs.en: The moon had not yet set, and it felt as if we had not been watching the play for very long; once we were clear of Zhao Village, the moonlight seemed exceptionally bright. Looking back, the stage, bathed in lamplight, appeared just as it had when we first arrived—ethereal, like a fairy pavilion adrift in the clouds, shrouded in a rosy mist. The sound of a flute drifted to my ears, long and melodious. I suspected the old woman had already finished her performance, but I felt too embarrassed to suggest we go back to see.

segs.zh: 不多久，松柏林早在船后了，船行也并不慢，但周围的黑暗只是浓，可知已经到了深夜。他们一面议论着戏子，或骂，或笑，一面加紧的摇船。这一次船头的激水声更其响亮了，那航船，就像一条大白鱼背着一群孩子在浪花里蹿，连夜渔的几个老渔父，也停了艇子看着喝采起来。
segs.en: Before long, the pine and cypress grove was far behind the boat. Though the boat was moving quite fast, the surrounding darkness only grew thicker, indicating that it was already the dead of night. Chatting about the actors—sometimes cursing, sometimes laughing—they rowed even harder. The sound of the water churning at the bow grew louder; the boat seemed like a great white fish carrying a group of children as it darted through the spray, and even a few old night-fishermen stopped their skiffs to watch and cheer them on.

segs.zh: 离平桥村还有一里模样，船行却慢了，摇船的都说很疲乏，因为太用力，而且许久没有东西吃。这回想出来的是桂生，说是罗汉豆正旺相，柴火又现成，我们可以偸一点来煮吃的。大家都赞成，立刻近岸停了船；岸上的田里，乌油油的便都是结实的罗汉豆。
segs.en: When we were still about a li away from Pingqiao Village, the boat began to slow down. The rowers complained of exhaustion, saying they had been pulling too hard and had not eaten for a long time. It was Guisheng who came up with the idea: he said the broad beans were at their peak, and since there was plenty of firewood at hand, we could filch some to cook. Everyone agreed, and we immediately steered the boat toward the bank; there, in the fields along the shore, the dark, lush plants were heavy with plump broad beans.

segs.zh: “阿阿，阿发，这边是你家的，这边是老六一家的，我们偷那一边的呢？”双喜先跳下去了，在岸上说。
segs.en: "Ah, Ah Fa, this side is your family's, and this side belongs to Lao Liu's family. Which side shall we steal from?" Shuangxi jumped down first and called out from the bank.

segs.zh: 我们也都跳上岸。阿发一面跳，一面说道，“且慢，让我来看一看罢，”他于是往来的摸了一回，直起身来说道，“偷我们的罢，我们的大得多呢。”一声答应，大家便散开在阿发家的豆田里，各摘了一大捧，抛入船舱中。双喜以为再多偷，倘给阿发的娘知道是要哭骂的，于是各人便到六一公公的田里又各偷了一大捧。
segs.en: We all jumped onto the bank as well. As he leapt, A Fa said, "Wait, let me take a look." He felt around for a moment, then straightened up and declared, "Let's steal ours; ours are much bigger." With a shout of agreement, everyone scattered into A Fa's bean field, each picking a large handful and tossing it into the boat. Shuangxi figured that if we stole any more, and A Fa's mother found out, she would surely cry and scold us, so we all went over to Old Man Liu Yi's field and stole another large handful each.

segs.zh: 我们中间几个年长的仍然慢慢的摇着船，几个到后舱去生火，年幼的和我都剥豆。不久豆熟了，便任凭航船浮在水面上，都围起来用手撮着吃。吃完豆，又开船，一面洗器具，豆荚豆壳全抛在河水里，什么痕迹也没有了。双喜所虑的是用了八公公船上的盐和柴，这老头子很细心，一定要知道，会骂的。然而大家议论之后，归结是不怕。他如果骂，我们便要他归还去年在岸边拾去的一枝枯桕树，而且当面叫他“八癞子”。
segs.en: The older boys among us continued to row the boat at a leisurely pace, while a few went to the stern to light a fire; the younger ones and I sat shelling beans. Soon the beans were cooked, and letting the boat drift aimlessly on the water, we all gathered around to scoop them up with our hands and eat. When we had finished, we resumed rowing. As we washed the utensils, we tossed the pods and shells into the river, leaving no trace behind. Shuangxi was worried that we had used salt and firewood belonging to Old Eighth, a man so fastidious that he would surely notice and give us a scolding. But after some discussion, we concluded there was nothing to fear. If he dared to scold us, we would demand he return the withered tallow-tree branch he had picked up on the bank last year, and we would call him “Old Eighth the Scabby” right to his face.

segs.zh: “都回来了！那里会错。我原说过写包票的！”双喜在船头上忽而大声的说。
segs.en: "They're all back! There's no mistake. I told you I'd stake my life on it!" Shuangxi suddenly shouted from the bow of the boat.

segs.zh: 我向船头一望，前面已经是平桥。桥脚上站着一个人，却是我的母亲，双喜便是对伊说着话。我走出前舱去，船也就进了平桥了，停了船，我们纷纷都上岸。母亲颇有些生气，说是过了三更了，怎么回来得这样迟，但也就高兴了，笑着邀大家去吃炒米。
segs.en: I looked toward the bow; ahead lay Pingqiao. Standing at the foot of the bridge was my mother, and Shuangxi was talking to her. I stepped out of the front cabin just as the boat reached the bridge. We moored and scrambled ashore. Mother was rather annoyed, asking why we were so late—it was past midnight—but she was pleased nonetheless, and with a smile, invited everyone to come and have some fried rice.

segs.zh: 大家都说已经吃了点心，又渴睡，不如及早睡的好，各自回去了。
segs.en: Everyone agreed that they had already had their fill of snacks and were feeling drowsy, so they decided it would be better to turn in early, and each went their separate ways.

segs.zh: 第二天，我向午才起来，并没有听到什么关系八公公盐柴事件的纠葛，下午仍然去钓虾。
segs.en: I did not rise until noon the next day, and hearing nothing further of the complications surrounding Eighth Master Gong’s firewood and salt, I went fishing for shrimp again in the afternoon.

segs.zh: “双喜，你们这班小鬼，昨天偷了我的豆了罢？又不肯好好的摘，踏坏了不少。”我抬头看时，是六一公公棹着小船，卖了豆回来了，船肚里还有賸下的一堆豆。
segs.en: "Shuangxi, you little rascals, you stole my beans yesterday, didn't you? And you didn't even have the decency to pick them properly—trampled quite a few, too." I looked up to see Old Man Liu Yi sculling his small boat back from market; there was still a heap of beans left in the hold.

segs.zh: “是的。我们请客。我们当初还不要你的呢。你看，你把我的虾吓跑了！”双喜说。
segs.en: "Yes, we're the ones hosting. We didn't even want you along in the first place. Look, you've scared all my shrimp away!" Shuangxi said.

segs.zh: 六一公公看见我，便停了楫，笑道，“请客？——这是应该的。”于是对我说，“迅哥儿，昨天的戏可好么？”
segs.en: Old Gong-yi, seeing me, rested his oar and said with a smile, "Treating guests? That’s only right." He then turned to me and asked, "Brother Xun, was the opera good yesterday?"

segs.zh: 我点一点头，说道，“好。”
segs.en: I nodded and said, "Very well."

segs.zh: “豆可中吃呢？”
segs.en: Are the beans any good?

segs.zh: 我又点一点头，说道，“很好。”
segs.en: I nodded again and said, "Very good."

segs.zh: 不料六一公公竟非常感激起来，将大拇指一翘，得意的说道，“这真是大市镇里出来的读过书的人才识货！我的豆种是粒粒挑选过的，乡下人不识好歹，还说我的豆比不上别人的呢。我今天也要送些给我们的姑奶奶尝尝去……”他于是打着楫子过去了。
segs.en: To my surprise, Uncle Liu Yi was immensely gratified. He stuck up his thumb and said with a smug air, "Only a scholar from the big city like you knows quality! I picked every single one of these beans myself. Those country bumpkins don’t know any better; they even had the gall to say my beans weren't as good as everyone else's. I’m going to send some to my aunt to taste today, too..." With that, he bowed and went on his way.

segs.zh: 待到母亲叫我回去吃晚饭的时候，桌上便有一大碗煮熟了的罗汉豆，就是六一公公送给母亲和我吃的。听说他还对母亲极口夸奖我，说“小小年纪便有见识，将来一定要中状元。姑奶奶，你的福气是可以写包票的了。”但我吃了豆，却并没有昨夜的豆那么好。
segs.en: When my mother called me back for supper, there was a large bowl of boiled broad beans on the table—the very ones Old Man Liuyi had sent over for us. I heard that he had even been heaping praise upon me to my mother, saying, "He has so much sense for one so young; he's sure to be a top scholar one day. You’re in for a life of ease, ma’am, I can guarantee it." But as I ate the beans, they didn't taste nearly as good as the ones I’d had the night before.

segs.zh: 真的，一直到现在，我实在再没有吃到那夜似的好豆，——也不再看到那夜似的好戏了。
segs.en: Truth be told, to this very day, I have never again tasted beans as delicious as those, nor have I ever again seen a play as wonderful as that one.

segs.zh: 一九二二年十月。
segs.en: October, 1922.

segs.zh: 这部作品也可能在本国本地版权期限更长，但对外国外地作品应用较短期限规则的国家以及地区，属于公有领域。
segs.en: This work may also be in the public domain in countries and regions where the copyright term is based on the life of the author plus an additional number of years, but where the term for foreign works is shorter.

chapters.note: 全书最温润的收束。月夜行船、偷豆煮食——「真的，一直到现在，我实在再没有吃到那夜似的好豆，——也不再看到那夜似的好戏了。」
